framing: You recently changed your password on a few accounts.

message.subject: Unusual activity on your account — please review
message.body:
Dear Helen,

We noticed a transaction that may be unusual for your account:

$214.00 — TechGadgets Online — Feb 12

If you recognize this transaction, no action is needed. If you don't, please call the number on the back of your debit card or log in at bluepeak.com to dispute it.

Do not reply to this email.

BluePeak Fraud Prevention

explanation.short: This is a legitimate fraud alert — it comes from the bank's official domain and directs you to call the number on your card rather than clicking a link.
tells: Sent from the bank's official domain — the sender address matches the bank's website
Directs you to the number on your card or the bank's own website — not a separate support number embedded in the message
Says 'do not reply to this email' — a real security measure, not a redirect to a scam phone line
No login link embedded in the email, no urgency, no threat of account closure
explanation.safe_move: If you recognize the transaction, no action is needed. If not, call the number on the back of your debit card to dispute it.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email. Acting on it promptly if the charge is fraudulent could protect your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate bank fraud emails reference your card's back number — they don't embed a call-now link to an unknown number.

green_flags.label: No embedded call-now or login link
Directs you to number on back of your card

message.subject: Overdue items on your library account
message.body:
Dear Patron,

You have overdue items on your library account:

- 'The Garden of Lost Souls' (due Jan 28) — $0.50/day

Please return the item or renew online at citylibraryohio.gov/account. Your total fine is currently $4.00.

Thank you,
City Library System

red_flags.label: Library fine you may not have expected

explanation.short: This is a legitimate library overdue notice — it comes from a .gov domain and contains specific, accurate account details.
tells: Sent from a .gov domain — consistent with a public library or government institution
Contains a specific book title and a small, realistic fine amount
Directs you to the library's own official website — not a third-party payment link
No urgency, no threats, no unusual requests
explanation.safe_move: Return the book or renew it through the library's official website. The fine is small and easy to resolve through the official site.
explanation.consequence: No risk — just a routine overdue notice.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government and public institution emails use .gov domains and include specific details matching your actual account.

framing: Your elderly parent got this and is scared.

message.body: National Health Benefits: Your annual wellness visit reminder is due. Schedule at medicare.gov or call 1-800-MEDICARE. Reply STOP to opt out of reminders.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate government wellness reminder — it comes from a short code and directs you only to an official .gov website or a known government helpline.
tells: Sent from a short code, consistent with official government SMS notification programs
Only directs to a .gov website and a well-known official government phone number — no other links
No request for payment, personal information, or urgency
Offers opt-out via STOP — standard practice for legitimate government SMS programs
explanation.safe_move: Schedule your wellness visit through the official .gov website if you haven't had one this year.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a health reminder you can act on or ignore.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government texts from short codes directing only to .gov websites and known official numbers are legitimate — scam texts typically use 10-digit numbers and unfamiliar links.

green_flags.label: No payment or personal info requested

message.subject: Your PeachDevice account has been locked — call us now
message.body: We have locked your PeachDevice account after detecting unauthorized access. To unlock your account and recover your photos, call our Senior Assistance Line at 1-888-555-0774. Our technicians are available 24/7. Do not attempt to log in until you call us.

red_flags.label: Unsolicited account lock with call-now instruction

explanation.short: This is a tech support scam — the sender domain is not the company's official domain, and the message uses fear tactics to push you toward a phone number rather than the real website.
tells: The sending domain contains extra words and hyphens — legitimate companies send account notices from their own official domain, not lookalike addresses
A 'Senior Assistance Line' label is a social engineering tactic commonly used to target older adults specifically
'Do not log in until you call' is designed to prevent you from verifying the claim yourself through the real website
Legitimate tech companies resolve account issues through their own official websites — they do not ask you to call a separate phone line to unlock your account
explanation.safe_move: Ignore the email. Go directly to the company's official website by searching for it and sign in to check your actual account status.
explanation.consequence: Calling this type of number could connect you to scammers who may instruct you to grant remote device access, potentially leading to theft of your account credentials and financial information.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When told not to log in and to call instead, that's the scam — always verify account issues yourself through the official website.

framing: Your parent got this and asked you to check if it is safe.

message.subject: Congratulations! You've been selected for a remote position — $85/hr
message.body: Hi! We reviewed your profile and you're a great fit for our Remote Data Entry Specialist role. Starting pay is $85/hr, fully remote, flexible hours. No experience necessary — we provide full training. Reply with your full name, address, and date of birth to begin the onboarding process.

red_flags.label: Requests full name, address, DOB upfront

explanation.short: No legitimate employer pays $85/hr for no-experience data entry — and real jobs never request your date of birth over email.
tells: $85/hr for data entry requiring no experience is far above any real market rate — inflated pay is designed to attract and then exploit job seekers
Legitimate employers collect personal information through secure onboarding portals, not email replies
You were 'selected' without applying or interviewing — real hiring processes require both
The sender domain does not match any official recruitment platform's primary domain — lookalike domains are a common scam signal
explanation.safe_move: Do not reply. If you are interested in legitimate roles with this type of platform, search for it directly and report this solicitation as spam.
explanation.consequence: Sharing your name, address, and date of birth could be used for identity theft, or you may later receive a fraudulent check as part of a check overpayment scam.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Unsolicited job offers with high pay and no interview are bait — real employers post jobs and hold interviews.

message.subject: Welcome aboard — your first check is attached
message.body: Congratulations on joining our remote bookkeeping team! Your signing bonus of $3,800 is attached as a check. Please deposit it and use $2,200 to purchase office supplies from our approved vendor (we'll send the link). Keep the remaining $1,600 as your first week's advance. Let us know once deposited!

red_flags.label: Deposit check and buy supplies — classic check scam
Told to keep part of the check

explanation.short: This is a check overpayment scam — the check will bounce after you have already spent money on fake 'supplies.'
tells: Legitimate employers never send checks before you start and ask you to forward a portion of the money elsewhere
The check may appear to clear initially, but could bounce one to two weeks later — you would be liable for the full amount
The 'approved vendor' is controlled by the scammer — any money sent goes directly to them
No real employer onboards a new hire by emailing a check attachment before the first day of work
explanation.safe_move: Do not deposit any check received this way. Report the email as fraud and block the sender.
explanation.consequence: If you deposit the check and send money to the vendor, the check may later bounce, leaving you responsible for the negative balance.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any job that mails you a check before you start and asks you to send part of it elsewhere is always a scam.

framing: You've been job hunting for 3 months and are getting desperate.

message.from_name: Sarah Chen — Talent Partner at Apex
message.body: Hi! I came across your profile and think you'd be a great fit for a Senior UX Role at Apex Technologies ($130k+, remote). We're moving fast on this — can you hop on a quick CloudMeet today? I'll send a link. Just need a soft copy of your resume to share with the hiring manager first.

red_flags.label: Recruiter contacting via personal DM handle

explanation.short: Legitimate corporate recruiters contact candidates through verified company accounts or official platforms — not personal-looking DM handles.
tells: A real recruiter at a legitimate company would message from a verified company profile or official email, not a personal-style social handle
'Moving fast' is an urgency tactic designed to prevent you from taking time to verify the recruiter's identity
Sending your resume via DM bypasses any official application system — your personal information could be harvested for identity theft
A personal-style username appended with a company name is not the same as a verified company presence
explanation.safe_move: Don't send your resume. Search for the role on the company's official website. If interested, apply through the official channel.
explanation.consequence: Your resume contains your home address, phone number, and work history — sharing it this way could make it valuable for identity theft or targeted phishing attempts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verify recruiter identity through the company's official website before sharing any documents.

message.subject: Action Required: Purchase your remote work equipment
message.body:
Welcome to BrightPath! Before your start date, you'll need to purchase your home office equipment through our certified supplier. Total cost: $847. You'll be fully reimbursed on your first paycheck. Purchase link: brightpath-equip.com/order

Please complete this within 48 hours to hold your position.

red_flags.label: Equipment from a separate third-party link

explanation.short: Legitimate remote employers ship equipment to you or reimburse through official expense systems — they never ask new hires to buy from a third-party link.
tells: Real companies either ship hardware directly or set up corporate accounts — they don't ask new hires to buy equipment out-of-pocket upfront
Reimbursement promises on equipment purchases made before your first day are a common scam tactic — payment is sent, and contact vanishes
An equipment purchase link on a completely separate domain from the employer is a hallmark of a fake vendor setup
.co domains are cheap and commonly used in job scam impersonation attempts
explanation.safe_move: Do not purchase anything. Search for the company independently using a trusted search engine and contact them through a verified phone number or email found on their official website — not through any link or contact in this message.
explanation.consequence: Victims who pay typically lose the full amount — the equipment never arrives, and the sender becomes unreachable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any job that asks you to pay for equipment before starting — with a reimbursement promise — is a scam.

framing: Your friend says they got the same message.

message.subject: 3 employers viewed your profile — verify to see who
message.body:
Great news! 3 recruiters viewed your JobBridge profile in the last 24 hours. Log in to see who's interested and send them a message before they move on. Your profile visibility expires in 12 hours without verification.

Verify and see who viewed you: jobbridge-verify.com/profile

red_flags.label: Must verify via link to see results

explanation.short: The sender domain is a lookalike — legitimate job platforms send alerts from their own primary domain, not hyphenated notification subdomains.
tells: A sending domain that appends '-notifications' or similar words to a brand name is a common phishing pattern — real platforms send from their own primary domain
'Profile visibility expires in 12 hours' is fabricated urgency — legitimate job platforms do not delete or suspend profiles this way
The 'verify here' link is commonly designed to lead to a fake login page built to capture your credentials
Curiosity about who viewed your profile is the emotional hook intended to make you click before checking the URL
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to the job platform's official website by searching for it — never use any link in the email. Log in there to check profile views or recruiter activity.
explanation.consequence: This type of fake login page is designed to capture your username and password, which could allow an attacker to access your account and misuse your profile.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a job platform 'notification' asks you to verify, always go directly to the site — never through the email link.

message.subject: Earn $500–$800/week stuffing envelopes from home
message.body:
We are looking for motivated individuals to earn $500–$800 weekly stuffing envelopes at home. No experience needed. Start immediately. There is a small $25 registration/materials fee to receive your starter kit. Pay at the link below and we'll ship your kit within 3 days.

earnathhome-register.com/start

red_flags.label: Envelope stuffing is not a real paid job

explanation.short: Envelope stuffing jobs have been a well-known scam for decades — no one pays hundreds per week for this, and any job requiring a fee to start is a scam.
tells: Legitimate jobs never require you to pay a fee to receive work or materials
Envelope stuffing as a viable paid employment model does not exist at scale — it is a long-running scam format
A .biz domain combined with a generic 'HR Department' sender name indicates no real, verifiable company behind the message
The small upfront fee is the entire point — once paid, you receive nothing meaningful and the sender moves on
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and delete. If you received this through a job platform, report it as a job scam using that platform's reporting tools.
explanation.consequence: Victims typically pay the fee, receive worthless materials or nothing at all, and find the sender completely unreachable afterward.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any job requiring you to pay a fee to get started — however small — is a scam. Legitimate employers pay you, not the reverse.

framing: You recently applied to several jobs online.

message.body: Hey! I manage social media for several brands and I'm looking for brand ambassadors. You'd post 2–3 times a week about products and earn $300–$500/week. Just need to buy a starter product bundle at a discount ($129) to get familiar with the line. DM me 'INTERESTED' to start!

red_flags.label: Must buy products to become an ambassador
Offer comes from personal account, not brand

explanation.short: Real brand ambassadors are not required to purchase products upfront — any arrangement requiring payment to work is an MLM structure or outright scam.
tells: Legitimate brand partnerships provide products for free as part of a paid arrangement — requiring purchase is a red flag
Paying for a 'starter bundle' is typically the actual business model in these schemes — the promised weekly income is the fiction used to justify it
Real brand ambassador programs are run through official company channels, not unsolicited personal DMs
Unverifiable income promises are commonly used to make an upfront cost feel reasonable
explanation.safe_move: Do not pay anything. If you are interested in legitimate brand ambassador work, search for the company's official website and look for an ambassador program listed there.
explanation.consequence: Victims who pay may receive products they cannot sell, and the promised weekly income typically never materializes.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any job offer that requires you to buy something first is either a scam or an MLM — neither are reliable income.

message.subject: You are hired as a Mystery Shopper — check enclosed
message.body: Congratulations! You have been selected as a Mystery Shopper for our national retail audit program. Your assignment kit and $2,950 check will arrive by SwiftShip within 3 days. Deposit the check, keep $350 as your fee, and use the remainder to evaluate CashWire services at ValueMart. Send the evaluation via the included form.

red_flags.label: Deposit check and wire money elsewhere
Job involves wiring money via CashWire
Keep a cut from the check

explanation.short: Mystery shopper check scams are one of the most common job frauds — the check bounces after you have already wired the money.
tells: Real mystery shopping pays modest per-assignment fees — not hundreds of dollars plus access to thousands more
Legitimate mystery shoppers are never asked to evaluate wire transfer or money transfer services using funds from a mailed check
Checks from unknown senders may appear to clear initially but can bounce days or weeks later — leaving you liable for any wire already sent
Being selected without any application or interview process is a universal signal of a fraudulent job offer
explanation.safe_move: Do not deposit any unsolicited check. Report the incident to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov.
explanation.consequence: Victims who wire money before the check bounces are typically left responsible for the full amount sent — that money is generally unrecoverable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Mystery shopping jobs that mail you a check and ask you to wire money are always scams — 100% of the time.

framing: You've been job hunting for 3 months and are getting desperate.

message.subject: Employment Agreement — Please Review and Sign
message.body:
Please find attached your employment agreement for the Remote Project Coordinator role. Review the document, enable macros if prompted to view the full contract, and reply with your signed copy.

Start date: March 1. We look forward to welcoming you to the team!

red_flags.label: Contract sent without prior interview contact

explanation.short: Enabling macros in a Word document from an unknown sender is one of the most common ways malware is installed on a victim's computer.
tells: 'Enable macros to view the full document' is a standard malware delivery technique — legitimate employment contracts do not require macros to be enabled
Legitimate employment contracts are typically sent through established e-signature or HR portals, not macro-enabled Office files
.co domains are inexpensive to register and are commonly used in impersonation and phishing attacks
Receiving an employment contract with no prior verified relationship or interview process through official channels is a significant warning sign
explanation.safe_move: Do not open the attachment or enable macros. Search for the company independently and contact them through an officially verified channel to confirm whether the message is genuine.
explanation.consequence: Enabling macros in this type of document could run hidden code that installs a keylogger or remote access tool on your device.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never enable macros in an Office document you received unexpectedly — this is almost always a malware delivery attempt.

message.body: CareerLink: Your application has been reviewed and you've been shortlisted for a remote role paying $72/hr. Complete your profile at: careerlink-apply.net/shortlist?ref=7829

red_flags.label: May not have applied to this

explanation.short: A high-pay shortlisting text from a random 10-digit number pointing to a lookalike domain is credential harvesting, not a genuine job offer.
tells: Legitimate job platforms send SMS alerts from short codes or verified sender IDs — not random 10-digit numbers
A hyphenated domain that appends '-apply' or similar words to a brand name is a common phishing pattern, not the platform's real domain
No role title, company name, or reason for the high rate is provided — real shortlist communications include meaningful specifics
A tracking reference parameter in the link is commonly used to associate your click with your phone number for targeting purposes
explanation.safe_move: Do not click the link. Go directly to the job platform's official website by searching for it and log in there to check your applications and messages.
explanation.consequence: This type of link is commonly designed to lead to a fake login page where your credentials are captured, potentially resulting in account takeover.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Job platform notifications should always be verified by logging in to the platform directly, never through a link in a text.

framing: Your friend says they got the same message.

message.subject: Authorize access to complete your application
message.body:
To complete your application for the Data Analyst role, please authorize our HireNow portal to access your CloudSync account. This is required to verify your work history documents.

Authorize: hirenow-portal.co/oauth/cloudsync

This process takes under 2 minutes.

red_flags.label: Job application requires cloud account access

explanation.short: No legitimate job portal requires access to your personal cloud storage account — this is an OAuth token theft attempt designed to hijack your files.
tells: Employers verify work history via references and resume documents — never by requesting access to a candidate's personal cloud account
Authorizing an OAuth request from an unverified portal could grant it broad read and write access to your stored files
.co domains are budget registrations commonly used in phishing and impersonation attacks
Established applicant tracking systems are well-known, verifiable platforms — an unfamiliar portal demanding cloud access is a strong warning sign
explanation.safe_move: Do not authorize the request. Search for the company independently and, if the role is legitimate, submit any required documents as PDF attachments through a verified official channel.
explanation.consequence: Completing the OAuth grant could give attackers broad access to your entire cloud account, including synced files, documents, and potentially connected services.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never authorize third-party apps to access your accounts during a job application — no legitimate employer requires this.

message.body: Hey! I saw you're in finance too — small world! I've been doing freelance consulting while also investing. My mentor taught me a crypto strategy that's been life-changing. I was skeptical too but I've made $18k this month. Happy to show you how it works over a call sometime if you're curious? No pressure at all!

red_flags.label: Stranger offering investment advice via DM

explanation.short: This is the opening move of a pig-butchering scam — build trust over weeks, then introduce a 'mentor' and a fake crypto platform.
tells: The script follows pig-butchering exactly: shared interest → casual conversation → mention of crypto gains → offer to explain
The 'mentor' is the next step — eventually you'll be invited to invest on a platform the mentor 'recommends'
$18k/month claims are unverifiable and are used to establish credibility without evidence
'No pressure at all' is a disarming phrase designed to make you lower your skepticism
explanation.safe_move: Do not engage further. Block the account. Report it as a romance/investment scam on the platform.
explanation.consequence: Engaging could lead to a weeks-long relationship that ends with you investing on a fake exchange where your funds are unwithdrawable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Strangers who mention crypto gains and offer to teach you their 'strategy' are running pig-butchering scams — 100% of the time.

framing: You've been on a dating app for a few weeks.

message.subject: Your JobBoard review was flagged — verify account to avoid suspension
message.body:
A recent review you posted may violate our community guidelines. To prevent account suspension, please verify your identity at the link below within 48 hours.

Verify: glassdoor-jobs.net/verify-account

Failure to verify will result in removal of your profile and reviews.

red_flags.label: Threat to remove profile and reviews

explanation.short: The sender domain does not match the legitimate service's official domain — this is a credential phishing page designed to steal your login.
tells: Legitimate platform communications come from the platform's official domain — not lookalike domains with added words like '-jobs'
Legitimate platforms display account warnings directly in your account dashboard, not through suspension threats sent by email alone
The 48-hour urgency and 'verify identity' link pattern is classic phishing
As a job seeker with an active account, this type of threat is specifically designed to motivate immediate action
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to the platform's official website by searching for it, then log in to check your account status — do not use any link from the email.
explanation.consequence: This type of fake login page is designed to capture your credentials — and if you reuse passwords, many other accounts could be compromised too.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Account violation notices are always verifiable by logging into the platform directly — never act on email links alone.

message.subject: Confirm your phone number to receive direct deposit
message.body: Hi! To complete your payroll setup, we need to verify your phone number for direct deposit. We'll send a 6-digit code to your phone. Please reply to this email with the code once you receive it so we can finalize your account.

red_flags.label: Asked to email back a code sent to your phone
Payroll setup via email reply, not HR portal

explanation.short: Legitimate payroll departments use secure HR portals — asking you to reply to an email with an SMS code is account takeover.
tells: They triggered a verification code to be sent to your number, and now want you to hand it back via email
The code is not for 'direct deposit setup' — it is the key to whatever account they are targeting
Real payroll onboarding uses established HR platforms and secure portals — not email code exchanges
A domain inconsistent with a real corporation's infrastructure is a common signal of a fraudulent sender
explanation.safe_move: Do not reply with any code. Search for the company's official website independently and contact them directly to verify the request.
explanation.consequence: Sharing the code could hand attackers access to whatever account it was issued for — likely your bank or email.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Payroll setup never requires you to share a code that was texted to you — that is account takeover, not onboarding.

framing: You didn't try to log into anything just now.

message.subject: Your Zoomify Pro account for your interview is expiring
message.body:
Your free Zoomify Pro trial for job seekers expires in 24 hours. To continue using Zoomify for your upcoming interviews without interruption, renew at $14.99/month. Act now to avoid reverting to basic — which disables video in interviews.

Renew: zoomify-business.net/renew

red_flags.label: Lookalike of real video platform (not zoomify.com)
Targeted at job seekers — interview disruption fear

explanation.short: The sender domain does not match the legitimate service — this phishing email exploits job seekers' anxiety about interview tools to steal payment information.
tells: The sender domain does not match the official domain of the service it impersonates — always check the actual domain before acting
Threatening to disable video in upcoming interviews targets the specific fear of a job seeker
Renewing through an email link could take you to a fake payment page where your card details are captured
Legitimate software services manage subscriptions within their own app or account dashboard, not via emailed renewal links
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to the service's official website by searching for it to check your subscription status — do not use any link from the email.
explanation.consequence: Your payment card details could be captured by a fake renewal page — and no subscription would actually be renewed.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Manage all software subscriptions through the official app or website — never through a renewal link in an email.

message.subject: Update on your application to Meridian Analytics
message.body:
Hi Alex,

Thank you for applying to the Data Analyst position at Meridian Analytics. After careful review, we've decided to move forward with other candidates whose experience more closely matches the role.

We'll keep your profile on file and encourage you to apply to future openings.

Best,
TalentHub Recruiting

explanation.short: This is a legitimate rejection email — it comes from the platform's official domain, references a specific role, and asks for nothing.
tells: Sent from the platform's official domain — not a lookalike or third-party address
References a specific company and role consistent with a real application
No links to click, no requests for information, no urgency
Polite, specific, and asks nothing from you — scam emails always want something
explanation.safe_move: No action needed. Keep track of your applications to recognize legitimate follow-ups.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a routine rejection notice.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate rejection emails come from the platform's domain, reference your specific application, and ask for nothing.

green_flags.label: No login or personal info requested

message.subject: Interview Confirmation — Thursday Feb 27 at 2pm EST
message.body:
Hi Jordan,

This is a confirmation for your video interview on Thursday, February 27 at 2:00 PM EST for the Product Manager role at Veritas.

Interview link: cloudmeet.com/xyz-abc-def (CloudMeet)
Duration: 45 minutes
Interviewers: Tara Simmons (HR), Devon Park (Hiring Manager)

Please bring a copy of your resume. Let me know if you need to reschedule.

Tara Simmons | HR Business Partner | Veritas HR

explanation.short: This is a legitimate interview confirmation — it comes from the company's official domain and contains specific, consistent details.
tells: Sent from a corporate email address at the company's own domain
Contains specific interviewer names, a meeting link from a named conferencing platform, and matches a role you presumably applied for
No payment, no credential request, no urgency beyond normal scheduling
Provides a reschedule option — a normal professional courtesy
explanation.safe_move: Add it to your calendar and prepare for the interview. Verify by checking your original application confirmation.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a routine interview confirmation.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Interview confirmations from company domains with specific names and a rescheduling option are legitimate — scams don't offer to reschedule.

green_flags.label: Includes specific interviewers, time, and duration
Offers reschedule option — standard professional practice

message.subject: Background check requested by Apex Technologies
message.body:
Apex Technologies has requested a background check as part of your pre-employment process. Please complete the form at sterlingcheck.com/candidate to submit your information securely.

This authorization expires in 7 days. If you have not applied to Apex Technologies, disregard this email.

red_flags.label: Third party asking for personal info

explanation.short: This is a legitimate background check request — it comes from a well-known screening service's official domain and the link matches that domain.
tells: The sender and link both use the screening company's official domain — not a lookalike
Background checks from a third-party screening service are standard hiring practice before employment
The email acknowledges you may not have applied and advises you to disregard if so — phishing messages do not do this
No urgency tactics beyond a routine 7-day authorization window, which is standard for this type of request
explanation.safe_move: If you recently interviewed at the named employer and are expecting this step, proceed via the screener's official website. If unexpected, verify with your employer contact first.
explanation.consequence: No risk if you are expecting this step. If unexpected, verify with your hiring contact before submitting any information.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Background checks from known screening services are legitimate — verify that any link goes to the screener's official domain before submitting information.

green_flags.label: Background check is a standard pre-employment step
Advises disregarding if you did not apply

message.subject: Offer Letter — Product Manager, Veritas
message.body:
Hi Jordan,

Please find attached your offer letter for the Product Manager position at Veritas. Start date: March 10. Salary: $95,000/year plus benefits.

We use Workday for onboarding — you'll receive a separate invite from workday.com within 24 hours to complete your forms.

Please sign via SignVault (a separate email will follow from signvault.com) and return by February 28.

Congratulations!
Derek Walsh | VP of HR | Veritas

explanation.short: This is a legitimate offer letter — sent from a company domain, referencing established onboarding platforms, with specific details consistent with a real hiring process.
tells: Sent from the company's own domain, consistent with prior contact in the interview process
References established enterprise HR and e-signature platforms, each with their own named domains
Specific salary, start date, and role are consistent with a real offer following an interview
No payment requests, no unusual links — just instructions to use established tools
explanation.safe_move: Sign via the e-signature email when it arrives from the platform's official domain. Complete onboarding through the HR platform invite, verifying it also comes from that platform's official domain.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email. Verify that each follow-up invite arrives from the official domain of the platform it claims to be from.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A real offer letter from a company's domain referencing established HR platforms is legitimate — verify each platform invite separately when it arrives.

green_flags.label: References established enterprise onboarding and signing platforms
Includes specific salary, start date, and role
No payment or unusual action requested

message.body: Hi! A recruiter at Apex Technologies viewed your profile and saved it to their candidate list. Log in at talenthub.com to see your profile activity and message them directly if you're interested.

red_flags.label: Platform DM with link to site

explanation.short: This is a legitimate job platform notification — it comes from a verified platform account and directs you to the platform's own domain.
tells: Sent from @talenthub_official — a verified platform account, not a personal-style handle
Directs you to the platform's own domain — not a third-party or lookalike URL
No request for personal info, no urgency, no payment
Profile activity notifications are a standard job platform feature
explanation.safe_move: Log in to the platform directly by navigating to its official website — do not use any link from the message if you have any doubt about its authenticity.
explanation.consequence: No risk — routine job platform activity notification.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate job platform notifications come from verified accounts and direct you only to the platform's official domain.

green_flags.label: No login or personal info requested

message.subject: Unusual direct deposit detected on your account
message.body:
Hi Jordan,

We noticed a direct deposit of $3,800 from PINNACLE-STAFFING was posted to your account today — which is outside your usual deposit history.

If this was expected (e.g., a new employer or freelance payment), no action is needed.

If this is unexpected, please call the number on the back of your debit card immediately.

BluePeak Security Team

red_flags.label: Unexpected large deposit from unknown source

explanation.short: This is a legitimate bank security notice — it comes from the bank's official domain and directs you to your card's back number, not a link.
tells: Sent from the bank's official domain — not a lookalike or third-party address
Asks you to call the number on the back of your card — not a separate number embedded in the email
Acknowledges a legitimate scenario (new employer) to help you assess before acting
No embedded link to click, no urgency beyond 'if unexpected, call your bank'
explanation.safe_move: If the deposit is from a new employer or side work, no action is needed. If unexpected, call your bank using the number printed on the back of your debit card — never use a number from the email itself.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email. An unexpected deposit could indicate a check scam setup — if uncertain, contact your bank directly.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate bank security emails direct you to your card's back number — they do not include emergency call-now links.

green_flags.label: Directs you to number on your card, not an embedded link
No unusual action required if deposit is expected

message.subject: Password reset requested for your account
message.body:
We received a request to reset the password for your JobBridge account (jordan@email.com). If you requested this, click the link below within 1 hour:

jobbridge.com/reset?token=a9f2c...

If you did not request this, no action is needed — your current password remains unchanged.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate password reset email — it comes from the platform's official domain, only acts if you requested it, and reassures you no action is needed if you didn't.
tells: Sent from the platform's official security domain — not a lookalike or third-party address
The reset link points to the platform's own domain with a reset token — no redirects to external sites
Explicitly states 'if you did not request this, no action is needed' — phishing emails typically never say this
A short token expiry window is standard security practice for password resets
explanation.safe_move: If you initiated the reset, use the link. If not, ignore the email — your password remains unchanged.
explanation.consequence: No risk if you did not request the reset. If you didn't, someone may be attempting to access your account — consider enabling two-factor authentication.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate password reset emails offer a clear 'if you didn't do this, ignore it' option — phishing emails create panic instead.

green_flags.label: Reset link points to official domain only
Clearly states no action needed if unrequested
Short expiry window follows standard security practice

framing: You just signed up for a new streaming service.

message.subject: Your resume optimizer subscription renews today — $189/year
message.body: Your CareerBoost Pro annual subscription will renew for $189 today. If you did not authorize this charge, call our cancellation team at 1-866-555-0412 within 6 hours to receive a refund.

red_flags.label: Surprise charge — must call to cancel
Exploits job seeker fear of missing a paid tool

explanation.short: This fake billing notice targets job seekers who may have trialed resume tools — the phone number is the trap.
tells: The sending domain does not match a legitimate company's primary domain — real companies send billing emails from their own verified domain
Calling the 'cancellation team' could connect you to scammers who may request remote access to 'process the refund'
Job seekers commonly use resume tools, making this a targeted, plausible-sounding scam
Legitimate subscription renewal emails typically show the last 4 digits of the card being charged
explanation.safe_move: Do not call the number in the email. Check your bank or card statement for any actual charge. If you have an account with the service, manage billing by navigating directly to the company's official website — never through a link or number from the message.
explanation.consequence: Calling this type of number could connect you to scammers who may ask for remote computer access and attempt to steal your banking credentials.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never call a number in a billing email about an unexpected charge — verify all billing through the company's official website.

message.subject: Your I-9 Employment Eligibility Verification is pending
message.body: Your employer has initiated an I-9 verification with Immigration Services. You are required to complete your electronic I-9 form within 3 business days or your employment authorization will be placed on hold. Complete at: uscis-employment-verify.com/i9

explanation.short: U.S. government agencies always communicate from .gov domains — any .com claiming to be a government agency is a red flag.
tells: Legitimate U.S. government agencies always use .gov domains — a .com domain claiming to be a government entity is a strong indicator of fraud
Real I-9 forms are completed in person or through an employer's HR system, not via an unsolicited external website link
Threatening to put employment authorization 'on hold' creates panic targeting new job seekers
Government employment verification processes are managed by employers, not initiated by direct email to employees
explanation.safe_move: Contact your employer's HR department directly to verify the I-9 process. Complete any required forms through your employer's official channels — not through any link in an unsolicited email.
explanation.consequence: This type of fake government form is commonly designed to capture your Federal Benefits number, ID documents, and other sensitive identity information.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: All Immigration Services and government employment forms use .gov domains — any .com claiming to be a government agency should be treated as fraudulent.

framing: You recently moved and updated your address with the government.

message.subject: Your home office equipment has shipped
message.body:
Hi Jordan,

Your order from WorkGear Supply (Order #SW-98812) has shipped and is estimated to arrive Thursday, Feb 27.

Tracking: SW8819922 — Track at swiftship.com/track

If you did not place this order, contact WorkGear Supply at workgear.com/support.

SwiftShip

explanation.short: This is a legitimate shipping notification — it comes from the carrier's official domain, includes a specific order and tracking number, and redirects to the merchant for any disputes.
tells: Sent from the carrier's official sending domain — not a lookalike or third-party address
Contains a specific order number and tracking number consistent with a real shipment
Tracking link directs to the carrier's own domain — no third-party redirects
Directs you to the merchant's own domain for dispute resolution rather than a phone number or unfamiliar link
explanation.safe_move: Track the package by navigating directly to the carrier's official website. If you don't recognize the order, contact the merchant by searching for their official website independently — do not use contact details from the email.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate shipping emails come from the carrier's official domain and include specific order numbers — no fees or login required.

green_flags.label: Includes specific order and tracking numbers
Tracking link points to carrier's own domain

message.subject: Updated banking details for future payments
message.body:
Dear Valued Client,

Please be advised that SupplyCore has updated its banking details effective immediately. Kindly update your records and ensure all future payments are directed to:

Bank: PinnacleBank
Account: 8847221903
Routing: 021000021

Please confirm receipt of this email.

Kind regards,
Accounts Receivable — SupplyCore Ltd

red_flags.label: Bank account change requested via email only
supplycore-ltd.net vs your vendor's real domain
Asks you to confirm receipt — validates active email

explanation.short: Vendor bank account changes should always be verified by phone call to a known number — never actioned from an email alone.
tells: Business Email Compromise (BEC) works by intercepting or spoofing vendor email to redirect payments
The sending domain may differ from your actual vendor's known domain by a subtle variation — always compare carefully
Legitimate vendors establish banking change procedures in advance — not through a one-off unsolicited email
Calling your vendor's known number — not any number in this email — is the only safe verification method
explanation.safe_move: Do not update any banking details based on this email. Contact the vendor directly using the phone number from your existing contract or prior invoices to verify whether any banking change is legitimate.
explanation.consequence: Acting on this type of email could result in your next payment being redirected to a fraudster's account. BEC losses average $130,000 per incident for small businesses.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any vendor banking change must be verified by phone to a known contact — email alone is never sufficient authorization.

framing: You just started a new job and aren't sure what vendors your company uses.

message.subject: Urgent — wire needed today
message.body: Hi Sarah, I need you to process an urgent wire transfer of $22,500 to a new vendor before close of business today. This is time sensitive for a deal we're closing. I'm in back-to-back meetings — please handle and confirm when done. I'll explain later. Thanks.

red_flags.label: CEO requesting urgent wire via email
No invoice, PO, or vendor verification info
'In meetings' — unavailable to verify by phone

explanation.short: CEO fraud (whaling) follows this exact script: urgent wire, no documentation, unavailable for verification, deadline pressure.
tells: Legitimate wire transfers follow an approval process with invoices, purchase orders, and dual authorization
A domain that differs from the company's known address by a single character is a common indicator of a spoofed or lookalike sender
Claiming to be unavailable for calls is specifically designed to prevent you from verifying the request directly
No legitimate executive would authorize a wire transfer of this size with no supporting documentation via a single email
explanation.safe_move: Do not initiate any wire transfer. Contact the requestor using their known personal phone number — not any contact information from the email — to verify before taking any action.
explanation.consequence: Once wired, funds are typically moved internationally within hours and are generally unrecoverable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify wire requests by phone to a known number — no matter how senior the requestor appears to be.

message.subject: Invoice #INV-2024-8812 — Due in 3 days
message.body:
Please find attached Invoice #INV-2024-8812 for $1,247.00 for office supplies delivered February 10. Payment is due within 3 days to avoid a 5% late fee. Pay at: officepro-supplies-invoice.com/pay

Thank you for your business.

red_flags.label: Invoice with no reference to a purchase order
Direct payment link in invoice email

explanation.short: Fake invoice attacks work by sending plausible-looking invoices hoping someone in accounting pays without cross-checking a PO.
tells: The sending domain does not match a known, established vendor domain — mismatched or hyphenated billing domains are a common red flag
Match every invoice to a purchase order — no PO reference is a major red flag in AP processes
Legitimate invoices from established vendors don't include direct payment links with short deadlines
Sending to the accounting department hoping someone pays without verification is the core of this attack
explanation.safe_move: Match the invoice to a purchase order before paying. Contact the vendor directly using contact information from your existing records — never use contact details or links from the invoice itself.
explanation.consequence: Payment could go to the scammer. Without PO matching, businesses commonly pay fraudulent invoices.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Every invoice should match a purchase order before payment — no PO match, no payment.

framing: You're in a rush to catch a flight and this pops up.

message.subject: Mandatory Safety Compliance Filing — $1,200 Penalty Pending
message.body: Your business has been flagged for a missing annual Workplace Safety Board safety compliance filing. A $1,200 civil penalty is pending. To avoid this penalty and a possible audit, complete your compliance submission and pay the filing fee of $299 at osha-regulatory-notice.com/filing within 10 days.

red_flags.label: Pay a fee to avoid a penalty — classic scam
No specific filing or business identifier cited

explanation.short: Workplace Safety Board is a federal agency — all communications come from osha.gov, and federal penalties are never resolved by paying a fee to a .com website.
tells: All official Workplace Safety Board communications come from .gov domains — any .com domain claiming to be Workplace Safety Board is not legitimate
Real Workplace Safety Board violations include your business name, EIN, specific violations cited, and a formal citation
Paying a 'filing fee' to avoid a government penalty is a classic scam structure — no federal agency operates this way
The vague filing claim is designed to be plausible for any business without being verifiable
explanation.safe_move: Ignore the message. If you have genuine concerns about Workplace Safety Board compliance, visit the official Workplace Safety Board website directly by searching for it — never use any link or contact from the message.
explanation.consequence: You could pay the fee, receive no protection from any penalty (which likely didn't exist), and your payment details may be captured.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Federal agency enforcement always comes from .gov sources — never pay a government penalty through a .com website link.

message.subject: Request to update my direct deposit
message.body: Hi, I recently opened a new bank account and need to update my direct deposit information before the next payroll run. New account: BluePeak Bank, Account 9912004471, Routing 021000021. Please update before Friday. Thank you!

red_flags.label: Direct deposit change requested via email
No request through official HR system

explanation.short: Payroll diversion fraud works by impersonating an employee requesting a bank change just before payday.
tells: Real payroll updates are processed through HR systems — not email requests
A domain differing from the real company domain by only one character (e.g., .co instead of .com) is a common spoofing technique
Timing just before payroll is deliberate — it creates urgency and ensures a large payment goes to the scammer
Verify any direct deposit change by calling the employee at their known phone number
explanation.safe_move: Call the employee at their known, verified phone number to confirm before changing any payroll information. Do not use any contact details provided in the email.
explanation.consequence: An entire paycheck could be deposited to the scammer's account on the next payroll run.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: All payroll changes must be initiated through your HR portal and verified verbally — email alone is never sufficient.

framing: It's the end of the month and you're paying bills.

message.subject: Action Required: Verify your BizListings to prevent suspension
message.body:
Your BizListings profile for Acme Plumbing is at risk of suspension. Unverified listings are removed after 30 days. Re-verify your listing now to keep your reviews and ranking active.

Verify at: bizlistings-verify.com/listing?id=29847

red_flags.label: Threat to remove reviews and ranking
Uses your business name to appear legitimate
Verify link doesn't go to bizlistings.com

explanation.short: This message impersonates a business listing service to steal your account credentials through a fake verification page.
tells: The sending domain does not match the legitimate service's domain — adding words like 'verify' to a domain name is a common phishing pattern
Using your actual business name makes the email appear targeted and legitimate — scammers harvest these from public directories
A 'verify listing' link in an unsolicited email commonly leads to a fake login page designed to capture your credentials
Threats of suspension or removal are used to create urgency and bypass careful evaluation
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to the business listing service's official website by searching for it, and check your profile status from there. Do not use any link from the email.
explanation.consequence: A fake login page could capture your account credentials, potentially giving access to connected business systems.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always navigate to business service platforms directly through your browser — never use a verification link sent in an unsolicited email.

message.subject: Your BooksOnline account has been locked — action required
message.body:
We've detected unusual activity on your BooksOnline accounting account. To protect your financial data, we've temporarily locked access. Unlock your account at: booksonline-help.net/unlock

Failure to verify within 24 hours will result in account termination and possible data loss.

explanation.short: Accounting software phishing is high-value — your bookkeeping credentials can give access to your entire financial history and bank connections.
tells: The sending domain does not match the official software domain — legitimate accounting software notifies users through the app itself, not external domains
Threatening data loss tied to your entire accounting history is specifically calibrated to provoke immediate action
Real lockout notifications typically include details such as the last login IP and direct you to the official site — not an email link
Accounting credentials are among the highest-value targets for small business fraud
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to the accounting software's official website by searching for it and log in to check your account status. Do not use any link from the email.
explanation.consequence: Compromised bookkeeping credentials could expose bank feeds, vendor payment details, payroll data, and years of financial records.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Accounting software issues are always resolved through the official app or website — never through an email unlock link.

framing: You just woke up and this is the first thing you see.

message.subject: Delinquent Sales Tax Notice — Immediate Payment Required
message.body: Our records indicate your business owes $3,419.00 in unpaid sales tax for Q3. Failure to remit within 5 days will result in account levy, license suspension, and referral to our collections agency. Pay online: state-tax-compliance-dept.com/pay

red_flags.label: Not your state's actual tax authority domain
Threat of levy and license suspension

explanation.short: State tax authorities use .gov domains — a .com domain claiming to be your state's Department of Revenue is always fake.
tells: Every state's Department of Revenue uses a .gov domain — a .com domain claiming this authority is not legitimate
Real tax delinquency notices arrive by certified mail with specific filing periods and penalty breakdowns
Paying through a .com link sends money to scammers, not the actual state government
Threats of levy and license suspension are designed to panic business owners into immediate payment without verification
explanation.safe_move: Verify by visiting your state's official tax authority website directly — search for it rather than using any link in the message — or call their publicly listed number.
explanation.consequence: Payment could go to scammers while your real tax account remains unaffected — and may still carry a balance.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: State and federal tax notices arrive by certified mail — never pay a tax bill through a link in an email.

message.subject: Your NovaSoft 365 license is expiring — remote session required
message.body:
Your NovaSoft 365 for Business license expires in 48 hours due to a billing issue. To prevent service interruption, our technician needs to connect remotely to renew your license. Schedule your 15-minute session: novasoft-365-support.net/schedule

Note: This affects all users in your organization.

red_flags.label: Remote access required to renew license

explanation.short: No software vendor needs remote access to your computer to renew a license — this is a remote access scam targeting business admins.
tells: Software license renewals are handled through vendor portals, not remote support sessions
The sending domain does not match the legitimate software vendor's domain — appending words like 'support' to a domain is a common IT impersonation pattern
Claiming all users are affected multiplies urgency — a common pressure tactic in business-targeted scams
Granting remote access to an admin workstation could expose your entire network
explanation.safe_move: Manage your software subscription by navigating directly to the vendor's official website. Do not grant remote access in response to any unsolicited email.
explanation.consequence: Remote access to an admin account could enable credential theft, ransomware installation, and network-wide compromise.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Software license renewals never require remote access — manage all renewals through the vendor's official admin portal.

framing: You're in the middle of a work deadline.

message.subject: Notice of Contract Dispute — Action Required
message.body:
Our client has engaged us regarding an outstanding contract dispute with your business. Please review the attached notice of claim (DOC file) and respond within 10 days.

Failure to respond may result in legal action. Enable all document features to view the full claim.

Peterson & Associates LLP

red_flags.label: Law firm name unverifiable from .co domain
DOC attachment from unknown law firm

explanation.short: 'Enable all document features' in a Word file is malware delivery — real law firms send PDFs, not macro-enabled documents.
tells: Legitimate legal notices are sent as PDFs, not macro-enabled Word documents
'Enable all document features' is a standard malware trigger — it is designed to run embedded malicious code
Threatening legal action creates urgency that pressures recipients into opening attachments without thinking
Real law firms serve formal notice through registered mail, not via attachments sent from unverified email domains
explanation.safe_move: Do not open the attachment. If you believe there may be a real dispute, search for the law firm independently and contact them through verified contact information you find yourself.
explanation.consequence: Enabling macros could install ransomware or a remote access tool, potentially encrypting or compromising your entire business network.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never enable macros or document features in a file received by email — this is a common malware delivery method.

message.subject: FINAL NOTICE: yourbusiness.com domain expires in 5 days
message.body:
This is a final notice that yourbusiness.com expires in 5 days. Failure to renew will result in domain loss and potential acquisition by competitors. Renew now for $79/year: domainguard-register.com/renew

Act now to protect your online presence.

red_flags.label: Threat of domain loss and competitor acquisition

explanation.short: Domain renewal notices must come from your actual registrar — third-party domain 'expiry' notices are scams designed to transfer your domain and overcharge.
tells: Your domain registrar is already known to you — renewal notices from unrecognized companies are not legitimate
Paying this 'renewal' may transfer your domain to a different registrar at an inflated price
Domains typically cost $10–$15/year — $79 is a significant markup and a common warning sign
Scammers harvest public domain expiry data and mass-send these notices hoping business owners panic
explanation.safe_move: Log in to your actual domain registrar's website directly — search for it rather than using any link in the message — to check your domain's real expiry date. Renew only there.
explanation.consequence: You may pay an inflated fee and could inadvertently transfer your domain to a predatory registrar, losing control of your web presence.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Domain renewals come only from your registrar — ignore all domain notices from companies you don't recognize.

framing: You've been meaning to cancel some subscriptions.

message.subject: Authorize new compliance app for your organization
message.body:
Your NovaSoft 365 admin account requires authorization of our new Compliance Monitoring app to meet updated security standards. This app requires access to: all user emails, file storage, calendar events, and admin settings.

Authorize for your organization: novasoft365-admin.net/authorize

red_flags.label: Requests all email, files, calendar, and admin access
Org-wide OAuth consent via email link

explanation.short: An OAuth grant with access to all email, files, and admin settings from a lookalike domain is a complete organizational account takeover.
tells: Lookalike domains that differ slightly from a vendor's real domain are a hallmark of phishing — legitimate software vendors use their own verified domain for app authorizations
Legitimate compliance apps are deployed through an official admin portal, not via email links
The permission scope requested (all email + files + admin) represents essentially full organizational access
'Required for compliance' is a common invented pretext — real compliance changes are announced with formal documentation
explanation.safe_move: Do not authorize. Log in directly to your software platform's official admin portal by searching for it, and manage any app permissions from there.
explanation.consequence: Authorizing could give attackers persistent access to your entire organization's email, files, and admin controls — potentially surviving even password changes.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never authorize third-party app access through an email link — all app permissions are managed in the official admin portal.

message.subject: Consumer complaint filed against your business — response required
message.body:
A consumer complaint (Case #BBB-2024-04419) has been filed against your business. You have 7 days to respond or your BBB accreditation will be suspended. View and respond to the complaint: bbb-complaint-portal.org/case/04419

Immediate response is required to maintain your rating.

explanation.short: The BBB sends all communications from bbb.org — any BBB complaint notice from a different domain is fake.
tells: The BBB's official domain is bbb.org — all legitimate complaint management happens there, not through third-party domains
Links in messages from lookalike domains commonly lead to phishing pages or malware downloads
Real BBB complaints include your business's specific accreditation details — generic notices are a red flag
Threatening accreditation suspension is specifically designed to pressure businesses into clicking without thinking
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to bbb.org by typing it into your browser and search for any complaints against your business using your business name.
explanation.consequence: The link may be designed to harvest your credentials or install malware — and any business details you provide could make follow-up scams more convincing.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: The BBB only communicates through bbb.org — any other domain claiming to be the BBB is fake.

framing: Tax season just ended and you're worried about your filing.

message.subject: Purchase inquiry — I'll pay via certified check
message.body: Hi, I'm interested in the commercial equipment you listed for $3,200. I'll send a certified check for $5,500 — please keep your fee and use the remaining $2,300 to pay my shipping coordinator who will pick up the item. Let me know if this works!

red_flags.label: Certified checks can be faked and bounce
Buyer using personal QuickMail for business purchase

explanation.short: This is a check overpayment scam — the certified check will bounce after you've already sent money to the 'shipping coordinator.'
tells: Certified and cashier's checks can be professionally counterfeited — they may appear to clear for one to two weeks before bouncing
The so-called shipping coordinator is part of the scam — any money sent goes directly to the fraudster
No legitimate buyer needs to overpay and have you forward the difference to a third party
Business equipment buyers typically pay via ACH, wire, or escrow — not certified check with an overpayment arrangement
explanation.safe_move: Decline. For business equipment sales, use a secure payment method like a verified wire transfer where funds are confirmed before releasing the item.
explanation.consequence: The check may bounce after you've already sent funds. Your bank could hold you liable for the full check amount.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any buyer who overpays and asks you to forward the difference to a third party is running a check scam.

message.subject: RE: New vendor payment
message.body: Hi — I need you to process a payment to a new vendor for a project I'm handling. $14,800 wire to NexaTrade LLC, account 7712004591, routing 021000089. This needs to go out today and I need confirmation ASAP. Don't discuss with anyone — this is confidential until the deal closes.

red_flags.label: New vendor with no invoice or PO

explanation.short: The secrecy instruction ('don't discuss with anyone') is the clearest signal — it's designed to prevent verification of what is a CEO fraud attack.
tells: Legitimate wire transfers have invoices, purchase orders, and can withstand internal discussion and review
Confidentiality demands are specifically used to stop recipients from calling their actual executive to verify
A domain that differs by one character from a company's real domain is a classic business email compromise technique
Any wire over a few thousand dollars should require dual authorization and documented business justification
explanation.safe_move: Do not wire anything. Call your CEO directly on their known phone number to verify — regardless of the confidentiality request.
explanation.consequence: Funds wired via business email compromise are transferred quickly and internationally. Recovery rates are low even with rapid reporting to authorities.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Confidentiality requests in payment emails are red flags, not permissions — always verify large wire requests by phone.

framing: You just started a new job and aren't sure what vendors your company uses.

message.subject: Unusual login attempt on your PayStream account
message.body:
We detected a sign-in attempt to your PayStream merchant account from an unrecognized device. To secure your account and prevent unauthorized transactions, please verify your identity within 4 hours.

Verify: paystream-security.net/merchant-verify

Ignoring this notice may result in account suspension.

explanation.short: Payment processor credentials are high-value targets — a lookalike security domain is commonly used to steal your merchant account login.
tells: Legitimate payment processors send security notices from their own verified domain and direct you to their official site — not to a hyphenated lookalike domain
Merchant account phishing is highly targeted at small businesses, as it provides access to payment processing and business banking
Real payment processors typically alert users via in-app notifications and direct them to the official platform to review activity
Short urgency windows like '4 hours' are designed to prevent careful evaluation
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to your payment processor's official website by searching for it — do not use any link in the email — and log in to check account activity.
explanation.consequence: Compromised merchant account credentials could allow an attacker to redirect payouts, process fraudulent refunds, or access linked business banking.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Payment processor security alerts are always verified through the official site — never through an email link.

message.subject: Invoice #SC-2024-1109 — $892.40 due March 15
message.body:
Dear Acme Plumbing,

Please find attached Invoice #SC-2024-1109 for $892.40 for pipe fittings delivered February 8 (PO #ACM-2024-0091).

Payment is due March 15 to our account on file. No changes to our banking information.

Questions? Contact us at accounts@supplycore.com or 1-800-555-0210.

SupplyCore Accounts

explanation.short: This is a legitimate invoice — from the vendor's real domain, referencing a specific PO, and explicitly noting no banking changes.
tells: Sent from the vendor's actual domain, not a lookalike
References a specific PO number that should match your purchase records
Explicitly states 'no changes to our banking information' — a transparency signal consistent with fraud-aware vendor practices
Payment terms are standard, with a direct vendor contact provided for questions
explanation.safe_move: Match the invoice to the referenced PO number in your system and pay to the account already on file for this vendor.
explanation.consequence: No risk. Verify the PO number matches your records before approving payment.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate invoices reference specific PO numbers and come from the vendor's real domain — verify both before paying.

green_flags.label: Standard net-30 payment terms with no urgency

message.subject: Change Order #CO-14 — Additional Materials
message.body:
Hi,

This is a formal change order for the office renovation project. CO-14 adds $3,400 for structural reinforcement materials discovered during Phase 2 demo (see attached scope document).

Per our contract, change orders over $1,000 require your written approval. Please reply to approve or call us at 1-800-555-0318.

BuildRight Project Management

explanation.short: This is a legitimate change order — from the contractor's real domain, referencing your specific project and contract terms, and requiring your approval before proceeding.
tells: Sent from the contractor's own domain, consistent with prior project communications
References a specific change order number and project phase — consistent with real construction management documentation
Follows stated contract terms requiring written approval over a threshold amount — a scammer would not know or cite your contract
Provides a callback number for verification rather than demanding immediate action
explanation.safe_move: Verify by calling the contractor at their known number. Review the attached scope document before approving.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email. Review the scope carefully and approve only if the work is verified.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate change orders reference specific contract terms and project details — and require your approval before work proceeds.

green_flags.label: References specific change order and project phase
Requires your written approval before proceeding

message.body: PinnacleBank Business: Outgoing wire of $14,500 to NexaTrade LLC was submitted. If not authorized by you, call 1-800-555-0100 immediately — this is the number on the back of your business debit card.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate bank wire notification — short code sender, reference to the number on your card, and no links or login requests.
tells: Comes from a short code, which is consistent with how legitimate banks send business banking alerts
Provides the number from the back of your debit card as the verification path — not a separate unknown number
No link, no request to reply with anything, no ask for credentials
Wire confirmation texts are a genuine fraud prevention service offered by most business banks
explanation.safe_move: If you authorized the wire, no action needed. If not, call the number on the back of your business debit card immediately — time matters for wire fraud.
explanation.consequence: If unauthorized, calling your bank quickly can sometimes halt the wire. Act within minutes.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate bank wire alerts come from short codes and reference the number on your card — they never include links or ask you to log in.

green_flags.label: No login or personal info requested
Directs to number on your card, not an external number

framing: You got this at 11 PM while half asleep.

message.subject: Payroll processing confirmation — February 28
message.body:
Your payroll of $22,140.00 for 8 employees was processed on February 28 and will fund on March 1. ACH deposits are on their way.

View your payroll summary at payrollnow.com/reports.

If you did not authorize this payroll run, call 1-800-555-0191 immediately.

PayrollNow Team

red_flags.label: Large payroll amount leaving your account

explanation.short: This is a legitimate payroll confirmation — from the payroll provider's official domain, confirming a scheduled run, with a direct contact if something is wrong.
tells: Sent from the payroll provider's official domain
Confirms an amount and employee count that should match your authorized payroll run
Directs to the provider's own domain to verify the payroll summary
Includes an emergency contact number for unauthorized transactions — standard payroll security practice
explanation.safe_move: Log in directly to your payroll provider's official portal by navigating to it yourself — never via a link in the email — and verify the amount and employee count match your records.
explanation.consequence: No risk if the payroll matches your authorization.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Payroll confirmation emails from your provider's real domain with matching details are routine — verify any discrepancies through the official portal.

green_flags.label: No login or personal info requested

message.subject: Admin: A new app was granted access to your organization
message.body:
An admin in your organization granted the following app access on Feb 14:

App: VidLink Pro
Permissions: Calendar read/write, presence status
Granted by: admin@yourbusiness.com

If you authorized this, no action needed. If not, review app permissions at admin.novasoft.com.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate admin notification from the software provider — it comes from the provider's official domain and references a specific app grant with limited, named permissions.
tells: Sent from the software provider's official domain
Lists the specific app, specific permissions, and the admin account that granted access
Directs to the provider's official admin portal for review
Does not ask you to click anything in the email — just informs and tells you where to review
explanation.safe_move: Verify the grant matches an action taken by your admin. Navigate directly to your admin portal by typing the address yourself — do not use the link in the email.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email. If the grant was unexpected, revoke the app through your admin portal.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate admin notifications are informational — they come from the provider's domain and direct you to the official portal, not an email link.

green_flags.label: No login or personal info requested

framing: You haven't checked your email in 3 days.

message.subject: CP2000 Notice — Discrepancy in reported income
message.body:
This is a notification that the NRS has mailed a CP2000 notice to your registered business address regarding a discrepancy in your 2022 business tax return. The notice was mailed February 12.

You do not need to take action in response to this email. Your mailed notice contains all details and response instructions.

NRS.gov — Never share personal tax info by email.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate government tax notification email — it comes from an official .gov domain, asks for nothing, and says to wait for the mailed notice.
tells: Sent from an official .gov domain, consistent with genuine government agency email infrastructure
Explicitly says no action is needed in response to the email — no link, no payment, no call
Notifies you a physical letter was mailed — consistent with how tax authorities actually operate
Includes a reminder not to share personal tax info by email — a standard government safety message
explanation.safe_move: Watch for the mailed notice and consult a tax professional about the discrepancy.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email. A discrepancy notice is a common correspondence item, not an audit.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government tax agencies email you only to notify — never to collect. All action items come by mail, not by email.

green_flags.label: Defers all action to official mailed correspondence

message.subject: Request for Quotation — see attached PO
message.body:
Dear Supplier,

We are interested in placing a large order with your company. Please review the attached purchase order and provide pricing. The document requires macros to be enabled to display our full specifications.

We look forward to your response.

GlobalVendors Procurement

explanation.short: Fake RFQ (request for quotation) emails are a common malware delivery vector targeting small businesses — the attachment is the payload.
tells: No legitimate purchase order requires macros to be enabled — real POs are PDFs or plain spreadsheets
An RFQ with no product specifics is consistent with a mass-sent phishing attempt — real buyers specify what they need
A generic company name and mismatched domain are inconsistent with a real procurement department
Enabling macros in an unsolicited document can install malware immediately on your business network
explanation.safe_move: Delete the email. Do not open the attachment. If a legitimate buyer wants to work with you, they can submit inquiries through your website.
explanation.consequence: Enabling macros in this type of document could deliver ransomware or a remote access tool to your business network.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Unsolicited RFQs requiring macro-enabled documents are a known malware delivery method — real procurement teams use PDFs and don't require macros.

framing: You're expecting a document from a colleague.
