message.subject: Escrow funds released — Project Completion Confirmed
message.body:
This confirms that escrow funds of $8,500 for the Website Redesign project (Contract #TP-20240214) have been released to DevStudio LLC per your approval on February 26.

The transaction reference is TXN-884412. View details at trustpay.com/transactions.

TrustPay Escrow Services

explanation.short: This is a legitimate escrow release confirmation — from the escrow service's official domain, referencing a specific contract and transaction you authorized.
tells: Sent from the escrow service's official domain
References a specific contract number and transaction ID that should match your records
Confirms your prior approval as the trigger for the release — consistent with how escrow services operate
Directs to the service's own portal for verification
explanation.safe_move: Log in directly to your escrow provider's official portal by navigating to it yourself — do not use the link in the email — and verify the transaction matches the project completion you approved.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email if you authorized the release.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate escrow confirmations reference specific contract numbers and your prior approval — verify details match your records.

green_flags.label: References specific contract and transaction IDs
Confirms your prior approval as authorization

message.subject: Your NovaSoft 365 Business subscription renews March 15
message.body:
Your NovaSoft 365 Business (10 users) subscription renews on March 15 for $1,200.00/year. This will be charged to the credit card ending in 8812.

To update payment info or cancel, visit novasoft.com/billing before March 10.

NovaSoft Billing Team

explanation.short: This is a legitimate renewal notice from the software provider — from their official domain, showing your card's last 4 digits, with advance notice and a self-service portal.
tells: Sent from the vendor's official billing domain
Shows the last 4 digits of the card on file — confirming you're a known customer with an existing account
Advance notice with a deadline to make changes — standard business subscription practice
Directs to the vendor's own portal for payment updates or cancellation
explanation.safe_move: No action needed if the subscription is expected. To update payment or cancel, navigate directly to the vendor's official billing portal yourself — do not use the link in the email.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate software renewals show your card's last 4 digits and give advance notice with a self-service portal — no call-to-cancel urgency.

green_flags.label: Sent from official vendor billing domain
References last 4 digits of card on file
Advance notice with no pressure tactics

framing: You just got paid and are reviewing your expenses.

message.body: URGENT!! Children in Africa r starving & need YOUR help NOW!! Text GIVE to donate $50 via iTunes gift card. God will bless u 🙏 Reply STOP to opt out

explanation.short: No legitimate charity collects donations via gift cards over SMS. This is a classic urgency-plus-gift-card scam pattern.
tells: Gift cards are irreversible and untraceable — a signature demand of scammers
Legitimate charities never solicit via random SMS with text-to-donate gift card schemes
Txt-speak and exclamation spam are hallmarks of low-effort fraud
No charity name, website, or EIN provided to verify
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and block. If you want to donate to a real cause, go directly to a known organization's verified website.
explanation.consequence: You would buy a gift card, scratch off the code, text it back, and lose that money with zero recourse.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any donation request that says 'gift card' is always a scam, no exceptions.

message.body: HELLO FRIEND. Big earthquak hit Turkey. Many peoples need food and shelter. Please send $100 ValueMart gift card to help victims. Send card number to this number. Thank you and God bless.

red_flags.label: Asks to send card number via text

explanation.short: Multiple spelling errors, a generic greeting, and a gift card demand reveal this as an obvious scam exploiting disaster news.
tells: 'Earthquak' and 'peoples' signal non-native fraud operation
Greeting an unknown person as 'HELLO FRIEND' is a manipulation tactic
Sending card numbers via text is how scammers steal gift card funds instantly
Real disaster relief orgs like Red Cross have known websites and donation portals
explanation.safe_move: Delete the message. Donate to verified organizations like redcross.org or directrelief.org.
explanation.consequence: The gift card number would be harvested and spent within minutes of you sending it.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real charities have websites. If there's no URL to verify, there's no trust to give.

framing: A friend shared this fundraiser on social media.

message.subject: Help a veteran this holiday season — your gift is needed
message.body:
Dear Supporter,

Thousands of veterans are sleeping on the streets tonight. The Veterans Support Alliance is raising emergency funds to provide meals and shelter before the holidays.

We need your help urgently. Please send a donation of $75 or more using QuickSend or a prepaid bankcard gift card to support@veterans-support-alliance.net.

Your gift is 100% tax deductible.

Thank you for your service to those who served.

Warm regards,
Mark Henderson
Executive Director, Veterans Support Alliance

red_flags.label: Unknown .net domain, not a known charity
Payment sent to an email address

explanation.short: Legitimate charities never accept QuickSend or gift cards sent to an email address. The tax-deductible claim is unverifiable and used to add false credibility.
tells: QuickSend and gift cards are scammer-preferred because transfers are instant and irreversible
The domain 'veterans-support-alliance.net' is not a recognized charity — real ones appear in Tax Agency 990 records
Sending payment to an email address is a red flag regardless of channel
Emotional appeal to veterans is a known manipulation hook
explanation.safe_move: Search the org name on give.org or charitynavigator.org. If it doesn't appear, don't donate.
explanation.consequence: Your QuickSend transfer or gift card funds would go directly to the scammer with no charitable impact.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verify every charity on Charity Navigator or GuideStar before sending a dollar.

message.body: Hi! I work with Hope For Kids, a non-profit helping children with cancer. We're running a fundraiser this week and every dollar is being matched by a donor. Can you share our HelpFund link and maybe donate $20? Even small amounts help so much. The kids really need this before the end of the month. Link: gofundme.com/h0pe-for-kids-2024

red_flags.label: HelpFund not linked to verified charity
URL uses zero instead of letter O

explanation.short: The HelpFund URL uses a zero in 'h0pe' instead of the letter O — a subtle typosquatting trick. Unsolicited DM charity pitches from unknown accounts are almost always scams.
tells: 'h0pe-for-kids' uses the numeral 0 instead of letter o — a classic lookalike URL trick
Legitimate charities do not cold-DM strangers asking for money
Donor-matching claims are frequently fabricated to create urgency
The account name 'Official' is often used by fake accounts to appear legitimate
explanation.safe_move: Do not click the link. If interested in children's cancer charities, search for Bright Hope Foundation or Alex's Lemonade Stand directly.
explanation.consequence: The HelpFund link leads to a scammer's page; your donation goes to them, not sick children.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always type charity URLs yourself — never click links sent in unsolicited DMs.

framing: There was just a major natural disaster in the news.

message.subject: Emergency Appeal: Hurricane Milton Relief Fund
message.body:
Dear Friend,

In the aftermath of Hurricane Milton, thousands of families have lost everything. The National Relief Corps Disaster Relief Fund is mobilizing emergency teams across Florida — but we need your support now.

Your donation today will provide:
• Emergency shelter for displaced families
• Hot meals and clean water
• Emotional support services

Please donate securely at: www.redcross-disasterresponse.org/donate

Every dollar you give goes directly to those in need. This is a 501(c)(3) organization and your contribution is fully tax-deductible.

With gratitude,
Disaster Relief Operations
National Relief Corps

red_flags.label: Donation link goes to unofficial domain
Tax deduction claim adds false legitimacy

explanation.short: This email mimics the Red Cross perfectly but uses 'redcross-disasterresponse.org' — not the real 'redcross.org'. All links go to a scammer's donation page.
tells: The real Red Cross domain is redcross.org — any variation is a spoof
The donation URL also points to the fake domain, not the official site
Scammers time these campaigns to real disasters for emotional impact
The 501(c)(3) mention is a tactic — anyone can type that claim in an email
explanation.safe_move: Donate only by navigating directly to redcross.org in your browser — never through email links.
explanation.consequence: Your credit card donation goes to a fraud operation, not disaster victims.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: After a disaster, scam emails spike. Always go directly to the real website — never donate via email links.

message.body:
[Phone call transcript]
Caller: 'Hi, this is Officer Ramirez calling on behalf of the Firefighters Charitable Foundation. We're raising money to support families of fallen firefighters in your area. We're asking for a small pledge of just $35 today. Would you be willing to support our local heroes? We accept credit card donations over the phone right now, and you'll receive a tax receipt by mail.'

red_flags.label: Asks for credit card number over the phone
Caller uses 'Officer' title to build trust
Receipt promised by mail — hard to dispute

explanation.short: Charity telemarketing fraud often uses first-responder themes because they generate strong emotional responses. Giving credit card details to an incoming cold call is never safe.
tells: Legitimate charities don't cold-call asking for immediate credit card numbers
Using 'Officer' implies law enforcement affiliation — a pressure tactic
Most 'firefighter' charity telemarketers keep 85-90% of funds raised
You can't verify who you're talking to during an inbound call
explanation.safe_move: Ask for a website, then hang up and independently verify the organization at charitynavigator.org before donating.
explanation.consequence: You give your credit card to a fraudulent telemarketer or a for-profit firm keeping nearly all funds.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never give payment details to someone who calls you. Hang up and call back a verified number.

framing: A friend shared this fundraiser on social media.

message.subject: Your support is urgently needed — Gaza medical crisis
message.body:
Dear [First Name],

Our teams in Gaza are overwhelmed. We have run out of surgical supplies and our doctors are performing operations without anesthesia. The situation is catastrophic.

Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) has been operating in conflict zones for over 50 years. Your past generosity has saved lives — and we need you again today.

Make a tax-deductible gift of $100 or more at:
https://msf-usa-giving.org/emergency-appeal

EIN: 13-3433452

We will send you a donation confirmation and tax receipt within 24 hours.

Thank you for standing with us,

Jason Cone
Executive Director, MSF-USA

red_flags.label: Uses real EIN to appear legitimate

explanation.short: This scam is sophisticated: it uses the real MSF EIN, a real executive's name, and a professional tone — but the domain 'msf-usa-giving.org' is not the official doctorswithoutborders.org.
tells: The official MSF USA donation domain is doctorswithoutborders.org — msf-usa-giving.org is a lookalike
Scammers research and include real EINs and executive names to defeat skepticism
The sender domain 'msf-usa-giving.org' does not match any official MSF property
Even a correct EIN in an email doesn't mean the payment destination is legitimate
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to doctorswithoutborders.org in your browser to donate. Never use links from email.
explanation.consequence: Your donation goes to fraudsters, not MSF field hospitals — and the real organization never receives it.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A real EIN in an email proves nothing. The only safe donation path is a URL you type yourself.

message.subject: A child is counting on you today, [First Name]
message.body:
Dear [First Name],

Every 2 minutes, a child is diagnosed with cancer.

At Bright Hope Children's Foundation, we never turn a child away based on a family's ability to pay. Thanks to supporters like you, we cover treatment costs, housing, and travel for every patient.

But right now, our end-of-fiscal-year campaign is $2.3 million short of our goal.

Will you make a gift of $19/month to help close the gap?

Donate securely: https://brighthopegiving.com/monthly-giving

Your gift is 100% tax-deductible (EIN: 62-0646012).

With heartfelt thanks,
Richard Shadyac Jr.
President & CEO, ALSAC/Bright Hope

red_flags.label: Uses real CEO name for legitimacy
Displays real EIN to appear official

explanation.short: The real Bright Hope Foundation website is brighthopechildren.org. 'brighthopegiving.com' is a scammer-registered lookalike designed to intercept donations from emotionally engaged donors.
tells: Bright Hope Foundation's official domain is brighthopechildren.org — the hyphenated '-giving.com' variant is a fraud domain
The CEO name Richard Shadyac Jr. is real and publicly available, making this more convincing
The EIN 62-0646012 is Bright Hope Foundation's real EIN — scammers use it to appear credible
Monthly giving setup would enable ongoing unauthorized charges
explanation.safe_move: Navigate directly to brighthopechildren.org to make any donation to Bright Hope Foundation.
explanation.consequence: Monthly billing is set up to a fraudulent payment processor, enabling recurring theft from your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: The more emotional the appeal, the more carefully you should verify the domain before donating.

framing: There was just a major natural disaster in the news.

message.subject: Thank you for your past support — match deadline is midnight
message.body:
Dear [First Name],

Because of donors like you, WFP USA reached 158 million people with food assistance last year.

Tonight at midnight, an anonymous matching donor will triple every gift made through our Giving Tuesday portal — up to $500,000 total.

Your previous donation of $50 has already been noted in our records. A matched gift of $50 tonight becomes $150 for hungry families.

Give now at: https://wfpusa.org.giving-portal.net/triple-match

This donation is processed through WFP USA's secure giving partner. EIN: 13-4129110. Receipts issued within 48 hours.

— WFP USA Development Team

WFP USA is a registered 501(c)(3). To unsubscribe, click here.

red_flags.label: wfpusa.org is a subdomain of giving-portal.net
References a fake prior donation to build trust
'Secure giving partner' obscures where money goes
Real EIN used to appear official

explanation.short: The URL 'wfpusa.org.giving-portal.net' is actually a subdomain of giving-portal.net — not wfpusa.org. The fake prior donation reference is a personalization trick to lower your guard.
tells: In 'wfpusa.org.giving-portal.net', the real domain is giving-portal.net — wfpusa.org is just a subdomain label
Referencing your '$50 past donation' is fabricated data to make the email feel personal and legitimate
'Secure giving partner' language intentionally obscures the actual payment recipient
Triple-match deadlines at midnight are manufactured scarcity — real matching campaigns are publicly announced
explanation.safe_move: Go to wfpusa.org directly in your browser. If a triple-match campaign is real, it will be prominently on their homepage.
explanation.consequence: Your payment goes to a criminal's payment processor, disguised behind a URL that appears to begin with the real domain.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Read URLs right-to-left: the actual domain is the part just before the first single slash. Everything before that is a path or subdomain.

message.body: ALERT: Your bank account has been LOCKED!! To unlock send us the 6 digit code we just texted you. Text code to 55223 NOW or account closed permanently!!

red_flags.label: Asks you to share your OTP code
Asks you to text code to a shortcode
Sent from a random number, not your bank

explanation.short: Your bank will never ask you to forward your OTP code to them via text. This is a straightforward account takeover attempt.
tells: Banks send OTPs so YOU can prove your identity to THEM — they already know the code
Asking you to text a code to a shortcode means scammers are logging into your account right now
Real bank alerts come from verified short codes, not random 11-digit numbers
Permanent account closure threats are pressure tactics to prevent rational thinking
explanation.safe_move: Delete the message. If worried about your account, call the number on the back of your bank card.
explanation.consequence: The scammer logs into your bank account using the OTP you just provided and drains it.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never share an OTP with anyone. The code is yours — sharing it gives away your account.

framing: You're in the middle of setting up a new phone.

message.body: SafePay: We detected unusual login from Romania. To protect your account, reply with the verification code we just sent you. If this was you, reply SAFE. Customer Support: 1-888-221-1161

red_flags.label: Asks you to reply with your verification code
Foreign login scare to trigger panic
Includes a fake support phone number

explanation.short: SafePay will never ask you to reply with your verification code. Replying gives the attacker access to your account instantly.
tells: SafePay's actual security texts never ask you to reply with a code — they send codes for you to enter on their site
The Romania scare is designed to make you act without thinking
The phone number is fake; calling it connects you to the scammer for further manipulation
'SafePay-Sec' is a spoofed alphanumeric sender ID — anyone can set this
explanation.safe_move: Do not reply. Open the SafePay app directly and check for any real alerts there.
explanation.consequence: The attacker uses your replied OTP to complete a login or transaction on your SafePay account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: OTPs flow in one direction: from the service to you, then from you into the service's login page — never back via text.

message.body: FruitTech ID Alert: A new device has been added to your account from Chicago, IL. If this wasn't you, call us immediately at 1-888-555-1042 or reply with your 6-digit FruitTech verification code to reverse the action.

red_flags.label: Claims sharing OTP will reverse an action
FruitTech doesn't text from a regular phone number

explanation.short: Sharing your FruitTech verification code cannot 'reverse' a device addition — that claim is a lie used to trick you into handing over your account access.
tells: FruitTech's real security alerts come from apple.com email or in-app notifications, not random phone numbers
No OTP sharing can undo account changes — OTPs only authorize logins
The callback number being the same as the sender is a sign the scammer controls both
FruitTech Support will never proactively text you asking for a verification code
explanation.safe_move: Check appleid.apple.com directly for any real account activity. Remove unknown devices from there.
explanation.consequence: You provide the OTP, the attacker completes an CloudSync account takeover, locking you out of your FruitTech devices.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No code you receive can 'cancel' or 'reverse' anything — that framing is always a scam.

framing: You didn't try to log into anything just now.

message.body: MobileOne: We received a SIM transfer request for your number. To STOP this transfer, reply CONFIRM followed by the 6-digit code sent to your device. This request expires in 10 minutes. Questions? tmobile.com/support

red_flags.label: Creates fear of unauthorized SIM transfer
Claims sharing code will stop an action
CONFIRM reply feels like stopping the scam, not enabling it

explanation.short: This flips the OTP scam: instead of logging you in, it tricks you into 'stopping' a fake SIM swap by replying with a code that actually authorizes it.
tells: Real carriers cancel a SIM swap by calling their customer line — not by you texting a code
The word 'CONFIRM' is psychologically designed to feel like you're canceling, not enabling the transfer
Alphanumeric sender IDs like 'MobileOne' can be spoofed by anyone
10-minute expiry is manufactured urgency to prevent you from calling MobileOne to verify
explanation.safe_move: Call MobileOne at 1-800-937-8997 to check if any SIM transfer is actually pending. Do not reply to the text.
explanation.consequence: Your SIM is transferred to the attacker's device, redirecting all your calls and texts — including OTPs for your bank accounts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A SIM swap steals every account tied to your phone number. Never 'confirm' a carrier request via text.

message.body:
[Phone call transcript]
Caller: 'Hello, this is James Wilson from National Trust Bank Fraud Prevention. We're calling because we've flagged a suspicious $2,400 wire transfer from your account to an overseas account. We've temporarily paused the transfer, but we need to verify your identity to permanently reverse it. I'm sending a verification code to your phone right now — once you receive it, please read it to me so we can complete the reversal and protect your funds.'

red_flags.label: Caller asks you to read your OTP aloud
Fake large wire transfer creates fear
Real National Trust Bank number can be spoofed
OTP framed as reversing a transaction

explanation.short: National Trust Bank will never call you and ask you to read a one-time code over the phone. The caller ID showing the real National Trust Bank number is spoofed — caller ID is not proof of identity.
tells: OTPs are generated when someone attempts to log in — if a code arrives during this call, the caller is logging into your account right now
Reading the code aloud completes the attacker's login or transaction authorization
Caller ID spoofing allows anyone to display any phone number — even 800-432-1000
Real fraud prevention agents ask security questions about you — they don't need a code they triggered
explanation.safe_move: Hang up immediately. Call the number on the back of your card to report the attempt and verify your account status.
explanation.consequence: You read the OTP, the attacker completes authentication to your account, and initiates wire transfers or account changes.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Hang up on anyone — even someone who sounds like your bank — who asks you to read a code received on your phone.

framing: You're in the middle of setting up a new phone.

message.body: TelcoNet: Your account PIN was recently updated. If you did not make this change, your line may be at risk of an unauthorized SIM transfer. To place a SIM lock on your account immediately, visit: verizon-acct-protect.com/simlock and verify with the code sent to this device. Act within 15 minutes to prevent number loss.

red_flags.label: 'SIM lock' framing to justify entering a code
Unauthorized PIN change alert creates panic

explanation.short: The link 'verizon-acct-protect.com' is not TelcoNet's domain. Entering your OTP on that page gives attackers everything they need to complete a SIM swap.
tells: TelcoNet's real domain is verizon.com — any hyphenated variation is a phishing domain
The 'SIM lock' story is a cover narrative to make entering a verification code seem protective rather than dangerous
TelcoNet PIN change alerts come through the My TelcoNet app, not SMS with external links
The 15-minute timer is designed to prevent you from calling TelcoNet to verify
explanation.safe_move: Call TelcoNet at 1-800-922-0204 or log into the My TelcoNet app to check your account status.
explanation.consequence: You enter the OTP on the phishing page, completing the SIM swap. All calls and texts now route to the attacker's phone.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: SIM swap fraud gives attackers your phone number — treat any SIM-related alert with maximum skepticism.

message.body:
[Phone call transcript — AI voice]
Caller: 'Hi, this is an automated call from SearchNet Account Security. We've detected that someone in Frankfurt, Germany attempted to sign in to your QuickMail account 8 minutes ago. We blocked the attempt, but the actor may try again. To secure your account, we're sending a SearchNet verification code to your recovery number on file. Please listen for the code, then press 1 to enter it now and lock the attacker out. This will prevent further access attempts for 72 hours.'

red_flags.label: SearchNet's real HQ number is spoofed
Asks you to enter OTP via phone keypad
Code entry falsely framed as locking out an attacker
Specific '72-hour lock' sounds technical and credible

explanation.short: This is an AI-voice vishing attack spoofing SearchNet's real headquarters number. Pressing 1 and entering the code authorizes the attacker's login — the opposite of what the call claims.
tells: SearchNet's actual number (650-253-0000) is public and easily spoofed — caller ID cannot be trusted
SearchNet does not make automated calls asking you to enter verification codes via keypad
The code arrives because the attacker triggered a SearchNet sign-in — entering it grants them access
The '72-hour lock' detail is fabricated specificity to sound like a real security system
explanation.safe_move: Hang up. Go to myaccount.google.com/security directly and review recent login attempts. Enable a passkey for stronger protection.
explanation.consequence: You enter the OTP, completing the attacker's QuickMail login. They gain access to your email, which they use to reset passwords for every linked account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: SearchNet never calls you. An automated call asking for a verification code is always an attack, no matter what number it comes from.

message.subject: URGENT: Your computer is infected!! Open attachment NOW
message.body:
Dear Valued User,

Your compter has been detected with VIRUS. You must open the attchment imediately to fix this problem before your files are DELETED.

Please open: virus_remover_tool.exe

Do not ignore this email or you will loose all your data!!

IT Suppport Team

explanation.short: This is a classic malware delivery scam. Legitimate IT teams never send unsolicited .exe files or threaten data deletion.
tells: Multiple obvious typos ('compter', 'attchment', 'imediately', 'loose')
Sender domain 'helpdesk-secure.net' has no relation to any real company
The .exe file extension is executable malware, not a fix tool
Extreme urgency and threats are manipulation tactics to bypass your judgment
explanation.safe_move: Delete the email immediately. If you have real concerns about your computer, contact your actual IT department using a known phone number or internal helpdesk portal.
explanation.consequence: Opening the .exe would install malware giving attackers remote access to your computer, files, and passwords.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate IT support never cold-emails you an .exe file. If in doubt, call IT directly using a number you already know.

message.body: CONGRATULATIONS!!!! You have been SELECTED as our lucky winner!!!!! Download your PRIZE CLAIM FORM now!!!! claim_prize_form.exe 🎁🎁🎁 Act FAST this expires in 1 HOUR!!!!

explanation.short: You can't win a contest you never entered. The 'claim form' is actually an executable malware file.
tells: No legitimate prize uses a random DM account with a generic handle
A prize claim form would never be a .exe file
Extreme urgency ('1 HOUR') is a pressure tactic to stop you thinking clearly
Excessive caps, emojis, and exclamation marks are hallmarks of scam messages
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and block the account. Report it to the platform as spam.
explanation.consequence: Running the .exe file would install malware that could steal your passwords, financial details, and give hackers control of your device.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: If you didn't enter a contest, you can't win it. Any unsolicited 'prize' DM with a file to download is a scam.

framing: You recently submitted a resume to several companies.

message.subject: Your package could not be delivered - Action required
message.body:
Dear Customer,

We attempted to deliver your package today but were unable to complete the delivery.

Please review the attached delivery notice and follow the instructions to reschedule.

Attachment: DeliveryNotice_UPS.zip

This notice will expire in 48 hours.

UPS Customer Service

explanation.short: UPS uses ups.com for all official email — not 'ups-delivery-tracking.com'. The .zip attachment likely contains malware.
tells: The sender domain 'ups-delivery-tracking.com' is not UPS's official domain
Real SwiftShip delivery notices include your tracking number, not a vague generic message
Legitimate shipping notifications never require you to open a zip file
The 48-hour expiry creates artificial pressure to act without thinking
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to ups.com and enter your tracking number if you expect a package. Never open attachments from delivery notification emails.
explanation.consequence: Extracting and opening the .zip would execute malware on your device, potentially stealing personal and financial data.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real shipping alerts always include your tracking number. If it's missing, go directly to the shipper's official website to check.

message.subject: Important: Updated Benefits Enrollment Form
message.body:
Hello,

Please find attached the updated benefits enrollment form for the upcoming period. All employees are required to complete and return this form by the end of the week.

If you have any questions, please reply to this email.

Attachment: Benefits_Enrollment_2025.docm

Thank you,
HR Department

explanation.short: Your company's HR department would use your company's own email domain, not a generic external one. The .docm format can execute malicious macros.
tells: Legitimate HR emails come from your company's official domain, not 'company-hrportal.net'
The .docm extension means the Word document contains macros, which can run malicious code
No real HR person identified themselves — just 'HR Department'
Legitimate HR forms are usually handled through internal portals, not email attachments
explanation.safe_move: Contact your HR department directly using internal contact details to verify if this request is real before opening any attachment.
explanation.consequence: Opening the .docm and enabling macros would run malicious code that could steal credentials or install a backdoor on your work computer.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: HR communications come from your company's domain. A .docm file from an unknown external sender should never be opened.

framing: You're expecting a document from a colleague.

message.subject: You have a document ready for signature
message.body:
SignSecure

Hello,

Jennifer Walsh has sent you a document via SignSecure to review and sign.

Document: Service_Agreement_Final.pdf

Please review and sign the document by clicking the button below or opening the attached copy for your records.

[Review Document]

This message was sent to you by SignSecure on behalf of Jennifer Walsh. If you have questions about this document, contact Jennifer Walsh directly.

Do Not Share This Email — this link contains your unique security token.

SignSecure, Inc. | 221 Main Street, Suite 1000, San Francisco, CA

red_flags.label: Both link and PDF attachment present
Real address used to seem legitimate

explanation.short: Real SignSecure emails come from docusign.com or docusign.net — not 'docusign-esignature.net'. The branding and formatting are copied to look authentic.
tells: The sending domain 'docusign-esignature.net' is not SignSecure's official domain (docusign.com / docusign.net)
You likely have no relationship with 'Jennifer Walsh' — unexpected signature requests are a red flag
Real SignSecure never attaches a PDF copy alongside a signing link in the same email
Scammers copy real company addresses and boilerplate to appear legitimate
explanation.safe_move: If you expect a document, log in directly to docusign.com to check. Do not click links or open attachments from unexpected SignSecure emails.
explanation.consequence: Clicking the link or opening the attachment could lead to a credential-harvesting page or install malware on your device.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Check the exact sending domain carefully — scammers add words like 'esignature' to make fake domains look real.

message.subject: Q3 Invoice Summary – Review Requested Before Friday
message.body:
Hi,

Hope you're doing well. I'm reaching out because our records show we haven't received confirmation on the Q3 invoice summary for your account.

I've attached the consolidated summary (PDF) for your review. Could you take a look and confirm receipt by end of day Friday? Our reconciliation period closes next week.

If the figures look correct, no further action is needed — just a quick reply confirming you've received it.

Attachment: Q3_Invoice_Summary_2025.pdf

Thanks so much,
Amanda Torres
Accounts Payable | Meridian Financial Group
Direct: (312) 555-0184
doi: 10.1038/meridian-fin

This email and its attachments are confidential.

red_flags.label: Just asking for reply to confirm
Fake DOI used to appear credible

explanation.short: This is a well-crafted spear-phishing email using a plausible business pretext. The PDF attachment may exploit vulnerabilities in PDF readers or prompt credential entry.
tells: You have no prior relationship with 'Amanda Torres' or 'Meridian Financial Group'
Legitimate invoices are preceded by an actual business relationship, not cold emails
A PDF from an unknown sender can exploit unpatched PDF reader vulnerabilities
The fake DOI ('doi: 10.1038/meridian-fin') is a credibility prop with no real meaning in this context
explanation.safe_move: Do not open the attachment. Search for 'Meridian Financial Group' independently and call them to verify. If you don't recognize this company, it is almost certainly a scam.
explanation.consequence: Opening a maliciously crafted PDF can silently install malware, exfiltrate data, or redirect you to a credential-harvesting site.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Unexpected invoices from companies you don't recognize are a major red flag — always verify the sender through independent channels before opening attachments.

framing: You recently submitted a resume to several companies.

message.subject: 2025 Employee Handbook – Mandatory Acknowledgment Required
message.body:
Hi [FirstName],

As part of our annual compliance process, all staff are required to review and acknowledge the updated Employee Handbook before April 30th.

The handbook has been attached as a Word document for your convenience. Once you've reviewed it, please complete the acknowledgment form embedded on the final page.

If you have any difficulty opening the file, please enable editing and macros when prompted — the acknowledgment form requires them to function correctly.

Attachment: Employee_Handbook_2025.docx

Please reach out if you have any questions. Thank you for your attention to this.

Best regards,
Sarah Chen
People Operations
Your Company | people@yourcompany.com | (415) 555-0233

Confidential – Internal Use Only

red_flags.label: HR email from yourcompany-hr.com not yourcompany.com
Mandatory compliance framing to pressure action
'Embedded form' as excuse to run macros

explanation.short: The instruction to 'enable macros' is the critical tell — no legitimate document requires this. Combined with a subtly wrong sender domain, this is a sophisticated macro-malware attack.
tells: Legitimate company documents never require you to enable macros to fill out a form — this is the primary delivery mechanism for macro malware
The sender domain 'yourcompany-hr.com' subtly differs from a real company domain — scammers register near-identical domains
The '[FirstName]' placeholder reveals this is a mass phishing template, not a personalized internal email
Framing as mandatory compliance with a deadline exploits workplace authority and fear of consequences
explanation.safe_move: Do not open the attachment. Contact People Operations through your internal directory or Slack to verify this request before doing anything.
explanation.consequence: Enabling macros executes malicious VBA code that can install ransomware, exfiltrate files, or create a persistent backdoor — often without any visible signs.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Enabling macros in a Word document is almost never legitimate. If any email instructs you to enable macros, treat it as a red flag and verify through another channel.

message.body: NETFLIXX ALERT: Ur subscription has EXPIRD. Pay now or lose access 2day!! Click: http://netlfix-renew.xyz/pay HURRY!!!

explanation.short: StreamBox is misspelled, the URL is a fake domain, and the language is nothing like a real billing alert. This is an obvious subscription renewal scam.
tells: 'NETFLIXX' with two X's is not StreamBox — the misspelling slips past basic brand filters
'netlfix-renew.xyz' is a scam domain — StreamBox uses netflix.com only
Real StreamBox never texts you in SMS shorthand ('Ur', '2day')
Legitimate subscription services give advance notice, not panicked same-day warnings
explanation.safe_move: Ignore the text. Open the StreamBox app or go to netflix.com directly to check your account status.
explanation.consequence: Clicking the link takes you to a fake payment page that steals your credit card number and StreamBox credentials.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always check subscription status through the official app or website — never through a link in a text message.

framing: You do have a subscription to a similar service.

message.body: MCAFEE: Your anivirus license EXPIRED!! Your device is now UNPROTECTED from viruses! Renew now $29.99: http://mcafee-secure-renew.com/now or call 1-800-555-0293 IMEDIATELY!!

red_flags.label: Random phone number, not short code

explanation.short: ShieldGuard communicates through its official app and email — not random SMS numbers. The misspelling and fake domain confirm this is a scam.
tells: 'anivirus' is misspelled — a sign of a hasty scam template
ShieldGuard's official domain is mcafee.com, not 'mcafee-secure-renew.com'
Claiming your device is 'now unprotected' is a fear tactic with no factual basis
Legitimate security software notifies you through the app itself, not random SMS
explanation.safe_move: Open your ShieldGuard app directly to check your subscription status. If you don't have ShieldGuard, delete the text.
explanation.consequence: Following the link or calling the number leads to a fake payment page or a tech-support scammer who will steal money and potentially install real malware.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Security software status is shown in the app itself — a random text claiming your protection has lapsed is almost always a scam.

message.subject: Action Required: Your TechCorp 365 subscription is expiring
message.body:
Dear TechCorp Customer,

Your TechCorp 365 subscription is set to expire in 3 days. To avoid interruption to your services, please renew your subscription now.

Current Plan: TechCorp 365 Personal
Renewal Amount: $69.99/year

Click below to renew:
http://microsoft365-support.org/renew

If you do not renew within 3 days, your account will be suspended and your files may be deleted.

TechCorp Support Team

red_flags.label: Renewal link goes to non-TechCorp domain

explanation.short: TechCorp billing emails come from microsoft.com — not 'microsoft365-support.org'. The threat to delete your files is a scare tactic not used by real companies.
tells: The sending domain 'microsoft365-support.org' is not TechCorp — all real TechCorp mail comes from @microsoft.com
The renewal link points to the same fake domain, not account.microsoft.com
TechCorp never threatens to delete your files if you don't renew immediately
Real TechCorp emails address you by your account name, not 'TechCorp Customer'
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to account.microsoft.com to check your subscription status. Never click renewal links from emails.
explanation.consequence: The fake renewal page will collect your payment card details and TechCorp credentials.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: TechCorp billing only comes from @microsoft.com addresses. Any other domain claiming to be TechCorp billing is fake.

framing: You've been meaning to cancel some subscriptions.

message.subject: Your SafeGuard 360 protection expires tomorrow
message.body:
Hello,

Your SafeGuard 360 Deluxe subscription expires tomorrow. After expiration, your devices will no longer be protected.

Renew today for just $49.99 and keep protecting up to 5 devices.

Special offer: Renew now and save 30% — offer ends at midnight.

[Renew Now] http://nortonlifelock-billing.com/renew

If you have questions, call our support line: 1-800-555-0412

The SafeGuard Renewal Team

red_flags.label: Phone number not verifiable as official
No account name or last 4 digits of card

explanation.short: SafeGuard's official domain is norton.com — not 'nortonlifelock-billing.com'. The artificial deadline and missing account details are classic scam patterns.
tells: Official SafeGuard billing comes from norton.com, not 'nortonlifelock-billing.com'
Real renewal emails include your account name or partial card info to prove legitimacy
The 'midnight offer ends' pressure is designed to make you act before thinking
The renewal link goes to the fake domain, not a real SafeGuard payment page
explanation.safe_move: Go to norton.com directly and log in to check your subscription. Call the number listed on norton.com, not the one in this email.
explanation.consequence: Clicking renew submits your payment card to scammers who will charge it repeatedly or sell your data.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real subscription renewal emails include your name and partial account details — generic emails with countdown pressure are scam templates.

message.subject: Your NovaMart Plus membership – payment method needs updating
message.body:
Hello,

We were unable to process the automatic renewal payment for your NovaMart Plus membership.

Membership: NovaMart Plus Annual
Next billing date: April 10, 2026
Amount due: $139.00

To avoid losing your Prime benefits — including free two-day shipping, Prime Video, and Prime Music — please update your payment method within 48 hours.

[Update Payment Method]

If you've recently updated your payment information, please disregard this notice.

Thank you for being a Prime member.

NovaMart Customer Service
410 Terry Ave N, Seattle, WA 98109

red_flags.label: Fake payment failure to prompt action
Real NovaMart address used for credibility
Uses real Prime price to seem authentic

explanation.short: NovaMart only sends billing emails from novamart.com domains. The 'novamart-plus-billing.com' domain is a scammer-registered fake, designed to look real.
tells: All legitimate NovaMart emails come from @novamart.com — 'novamart-plus-billing.com' is a scam domain registered to impersonate NovaMart
The correct Prime annual price ($139) and real NovaMart address are used to lower your guard
Payment failure notices are a common pretext — NovaMart shows failed payments directly in your account
The 48-hour deadline creates pressure to act without verifying
explanation.safe_move: Log in directly to novamart.com and check your account's payment settings and Prime membership status.
explanation.consequence: Clicking 'Update Payment Method' takes you to a convincing fake NovaMart login and payment page that harvests your credentials and card number.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: NovaMart billing emails only come from @novamart.com — check the domain carefully, not just the display name, before clicking anything.

framing: You just got paid and are reviewing your expenses.

message.subject: Important notice about your TuneStream Premium plan
message.body:
Hi [Name],

We wanted to let you know that your TuneStream Premium Individual plan will renew on April 15, 2026 for $11.99.

We noticed your payment method on file may have expired. To ensure uninterrupted listening, please confirm your payment details are up to date.

Verify Payment Details →

If your details are already current, you don't need to do anything — your subscription will renew automatically.

Having trouble? Visit our Help Center or contact support.

The TuneStream Team

TuneStream AB | Regeringsgatan 19, 111 53 Stockholm, Sweden
Unsubscribe from non-essential emails

red_flags.label: Real TuneStream price used as credibility prop
Real Stockholm address used as credibility prop

explanation.short: TuneStream sends all account emails from @spotify.com — not 'spotify-account.com'. The unfilled [Name] placeholder also reveals this is a mass phishing campaign template.
tells: 'spotify-account.com' is a lookalike domain — TuneStream only uses spotify.com for account communications
The '[Name]' placeholder was never filled in, a sign this is a bulk phishing template
The correct price ($11.99), real company address, and unsubscribe link are all copied from genuine TuneStream emails to appear authentic
The soft, non-threatening tone is intentional — expert phishers avoid urgency so victims don't get suspicious
explanation.safe_move: Open the TuneStream app or go to spotify.com directly and navigate to Account > Subscription to verify your plan and payment details.
explanation.consequence: Clicking 'Verify Payment Details' leads to a pixel-perfect fake TuneStream login page. Your credentials and card number are harvested immediately.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Even polished, low-pressure emails can be phishing. Always check the exact sender domain — display names and content can be perfectly faked, but domains cannot.

message.body: BANK ALERT: Your account has been SUSPENDED due to suspisious activity!! Verify ur identity now: http://bank-secure-login.xyz/verify or ur account will be CLOSED!!!

explanation.short: Banks never send account suspension alerts via random phone numbers, and they certainly don't use .xyz domains. This is a classic credential phishing SMS.
tells: 'suspisious' is misspelled — scam templates are often created hastily
A real bank URL ends in the bank's official domain, never 'bank-secure-login.xyz'
Banks use professional language — 'ur' is a dead giveaway this is fake
Threatening to permanently close your account is a high-pressure manipulation tactic
explanation.safe_move: Call the number on the back of your bank card or log in directly through your bank's official app to check your account status.
explanation.consequence: The link takes you to a fake bank login page that captures your username, password, and potentially your one-time code.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Your bank will never ask you to verify your identity through a link in an SMS. Always use the official app or call the number on your card.

framing: You're at work and this looks like it's from IT.

message.subject: We detected a new sign-in to your SearchNet Account
message.body:
Hi,

We detected a sign-in to your SearchNet Account from a new device.

Device: CompuOS PC
Location: Chicago, IL, USA
Time: April 8, 2026 at 9:14 AM

If this was you, you can safely ignore this email.

If this wasn't you, your account may be compromised. Secure your account immediately:

[Secure Your Account]
https://accounts-google-security.com/signin/v2/challenge/pwd?continue=myaccount

The SearchNet Accounts Team
SearchNet LLC, 1600 Amphitheatre Parkway, Mountain View, CA 94043

red_flags.label: Real SearchNet address used as credibility prop
URL path mimics real SearchNet sign-in path

explanation.short: SearchNet account alerts come from google.com domains only. Both the sender and the link domain here are sophisticated fakes designed to pass a quick glance.
tells: The sender 'google-account-alerts.com' is not SearchNet — real SearchNet security emails come from @google.com or @accounts.google.com
The link goes to 'accounts-google-security.com' — real SearchNet account links always use accounts.google.com
The URL path ('/signin/v2/challenge/pwd') is copied from real SearchNet URLs to appear authentic
The real SearchNet address at the bottom is a credibility prop — anyone can paste that into an email
explanation.safe_move: Do not click the link. Open a new browser tab and go to myaccount.google.com to review recent sign-in activity.
explanation.consequence: The fake 'Secure Your Account' page captures your SearchNet password and potentially your two-factor authentication code in real time.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Hover over any link before clicking — if the domain isn't exactly google.com, do not proceed. Real SearchNet links always stay on google.com.

message.subject: Action required: Unusual sign-in activity on your TechCorp account
message.body:
Dear [FirstName],

We've detected unusual activity on your TechCorp account and have temporarily limited access to protect you.

Account: [email redacted for security]
Event: Sign-in from unfamiliar location
Location: Kyiv, Ukraine
Date: April 8, 2026 – 3:47 AM

If you recognize this activity, no further action is needed.

If you don't recognize this sign-in, please verify your identity immediately to restore full access:

https://accountprotection.microsoft.com/reactivate?id=a88f2&ref=security

This link expires in 24 hours.

TechCorp Account Security Team
TechCorp Corporation | One TechCorp Way, Redmond, WA 98052

red_flags.label: Sender uses subdomain trick to hide fake domain
Foreign location used as fear trigger
Email 'redacted for security' hides lack of personalization

explanation.short: The sender address uses a subdomain trick — the real domain is 'securityalert-id8821.net', not microsoft.com. The link in the email also goes to this same fake domain.
tells: The sender address 'security@accountprotection.microsoft.com.securityalert-id8821.net' — the actual domain is everything after the last dot before .net, which is 'securityalert-id8821.net'
The link in the email, despite appearing to start with 'accountprotection.microsoft.com', resolves to 'securityalert-id8821.net' due to URL redirect tricks
Redacting your email 'for security' is a way to hide that this is a mass phishing template with no actual account info
A foreign sign-in (Kyiv) combined with a 24-hour deadline maximizes fear and urgency to override careful thinking
explanation.safe_move: Go to account.microsoft.com directly by typing it in your browser. Check recent activity there. Do not click any link from this email.
explanation.consequence: The fake reactivation page is an exact clone of TechCorp's login — your password and MFA code are captured and used in real time to take over your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Read the sender address from right to left — the true domain is the last segment before the TLD. 'anything.microsoft.com.fakeddomain.net' is owned by fakeddomain.net, not TechCorp.

framing: You got locked out of an account yesterday.

message.body: Hello, this is CompuOS Support. We have detected a virus on your computer. You must call us back immediately at 1-800-555-0192 to fix it or your computer will be shut down.

red_flags.label: Threat of shutdown to create urgency
Vague claim of virus with no details

explanation.short: TechCorp and CompuOS Support never call you out of the blue about viruses or computer problems. This is a classic tech support scam.
tells: TechCorp never initiates unsolicited calls to customers
Threat of 'shutdown' is designed to panic you into calling back
No legitimate company detects your virus and then calls your phone
Generic 'CompuOS Support' name with no specifics
explanation.safe_move: Hang up immediately. If concerned about your computer, call TechCorp directly at their official number found on microsoft.com.
explanation.consequence: You would be connected to a scammer who would ask for remote access to your computer and eventually demand payment for fake repairs.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real tech companies never call you first — you always initiate contact with them.

message.body: URGENT: Your FruitTech ID has been hacked! Call FruitTech Security NOW at 1-888-555-0147 to protect your account. Do not ignore this message.

red_flags.label: Sent from a random phone number
FruitTech doesn't send SMS asking you to call a number
Vague 'hacked' claim with no account details

explanation.short: FruitTech does not send SMS messages asking you to call a phone number. This is a scammer trying to get you to hand over your FruitTech ID credentials.
tells: FruitTech communicates account issues through the FruitTech ID website or app, not SMS callbacks
Sent from a regular phone number, not an FruitTech short code
ALL CAPS and 'Do not ignore' are classic panic-inducing tactics
No mention of which account or what was compromised
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to appleid.apple.com in your browser and sign in to check your account status.
explanation.consequence: Calling the number connects you to a scammer who will steal your FruitTech ID password and potentially lock you out of all your FruitTech devices.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always check account security by going directly to the official website — never call a number from a text message.

message.subject: Case #MS-2024-774821 — Suspicious Sign-In Detected on Your Device
message.body:
Dear TechCorp Customer,

Our security systems have flagged unusual activity on a device registered to your TechCorp account. A sign-in attempt was made from an unrecognized location (Lagos, Nigeria) at 3:42 AM on April 6, 2024.

Case Reference: MS-2024-774821
Device: DESKTOP-K7RQ2NX (CompuOS 11 Home)
Risk Level: HIGH

To prevent unauthorized access, a TechCorp Certified Technician has been assigned to your case. Please call our Priority Support Line within 24 hours:

📞 1-888-264-7731

Have your case number ready. Our technician may ask to connect remotely to verify your account security settings.

TechCorp Security Team
One TechCorp Way, Redmond, WA 98052

red_flags.label: Asks you to call a phone number
Fake case number creates false legitimacy

explanation.short: Despite specific details like a case number, device name, and a real-looking address, the email comes from a fake domain and TechCorp never asks you to call a phone number from an email alert.
tells: Domain is 'microsoft-helpdesk-service.com' — not microsoft.com
TechCorp security alerts link to account.microsoft.com, they never provide a callback number
Request for remote access is the scammer's end goal
Real TechCorp alerts include your account email and a link to review activity
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to account.microsoft.com and check 'Recent Activity' to see if there was a real sign-in attempt.
explanation.consequence: Calling the number leads to a fake technician who gains remote access to your computer, steals files and passwords, and charges hundreds of dollars for fake 'cleanup'.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Check the sender domain carefully — any security email from TechCorp comes from @microsoft.com only.

message.body: Caller says: 'Hi, I'm calling from FruitTech Support. We've detected that your CloudSync account has been compromised. Your photos and contacts may be at risk. I'm calling to help you secure your account. I'll need you to go to your iPhone settings and share a 6-digit verification code so I can verify your identity and lock out the unauthorized user. Your case number is APL-8834-22. This will only take a few minutes.'

red_flags.label: Caller ID can be spoofed to look like FruitTech
FruitTech does not call customers proactively
Urgency created by threatening your photos

explanation.short: No legitimate company will ever call you and ask for a verification code — that code is the key to your account and handing it over gives the scammer full access.
tells: FruitTech does not proactively call customers about account security
Caller ID showing FruitTech's real number (800-275-2273) can be faked using spoofing tools
Asking for a verification code is the #1 account takeover technique
A real FruitTech employee would never need your verification code
explanation.safe_move: Hang up and go to iforgot.apple.com or call FruitTech directly by finding the number on apple.com/contact.
explanation.consequence: The code gives the scammer access to your CloudSync, allowing them to lock your FruitTech ID, view your photos, and hold your account for ransom.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A verification code is like a password — never share it with anyone who calls you, no matter who they claim to be.

message.subject: Action Required: Suspicious Login — Your SearchNet Account
message.body:
Hello,

We detected a sign-in to your SearchNet Account from a new device:

Device: a phone manufacturer Galaxy S24
Location: Bucharest, Romania
Time: April 7, 2024 — 11:58 PM
IP Address: 185.220.101.47

If this was you, no action is needed.

If this was NOT you, your account may be compromised. Contact our Account Recovery Specialists immediately:

🔒 Call: 1-866-539-0014
Reference ID: GGL-2024-003847

Do not change your password until speaking with a specialist, as this may lock out recovery options.

SearchNet Trust & Safety Team
Mountain View, CA 94043

red_flags.label: Tells you NOT to change your password — classic manipulation
SearchNet doesn't offer phone-based account recovery via email
Specific IP address creates false authenticity

explanation.short: The email domain is not google.com, and the instruction to NOT change your password is a manipulation tactic designed to keep the scammer's access open.
tells: SearchNet sends security alerts from @accounts.google.com, never third-party domains
Telling you not to change your password is the opposite of what a real security alert would say
SearchNet's account recovery is self-service at accounts.google.com — no phone line
Fake IP and location details are easy to fabricate
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to myaccount.google.com and check 'Security' > 'Recent security activity'. Change your password immediately.
explanation.consequence: Calling the number leads to a fake specialist who will walk you through 'account recovery' steps that actually give them control of your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When in doubt about a security email, go directly to the service's website — never follow instructions in the email itself.

message.subject: Your device reported Error 0x8007042C — Automatic protection paused
message.body:
Dear Valued Customer,

CompuOS Defender on your device has reported a critical firewall error (0x8007042C — CompuOS Firewall Service failure) that has temporarily suspended real-time protection.

Device ID: WDSC-PC-8841-KX
OS: CompuOS 11 Home 22H2
Defender Status: PAUSED (automatic)
Last Full Scan: April 3, 2024

This error is typically caused by a conflicting registry entry or a partial malware installation that prevented Defender from completing its last update cycle.

To restore protection, a TechCorp-certified technician can connect using Quick Assist (built into CompuOS) to resolve the registry conflict remotely. This is a free service for CompuOS 11 users.

Schedule your session: 1-877-302-6657
Case ID: WD-2024-8841-KX

If you prefer to resolve this yourself, you can run the CompuOS Firewall troubleshooter in Settings > System > Troubleshoot > Other troubleshooters.

Warm regards,
CompuOS Defender Security Center
TechCorp Corporation

red_flags.label: Real CompuOS error code used to appear credible
Pushes legitimate Quick Assist tool for remote access
TechCorp does not provide callback numbers in security emails

explanation.short: This email uses a real CompuOS error code and even references a legitimate TechCorp tool (Quick Assist) to appear credible, but the sender domain is not microsoft.com and TechCorp never emails you a callback number.
tells: account-microsoft.com is not a TechCorp domain — microsoft.com is the only legitimate domain
TechCorp security emails never include a phone number to call
Quick Assist is real, but TechCorp doesn't email you to schedule a remote session — you initiate that yourself
Error code 0x8007042C is real but TechCorp wouldn't email you about it unprompted
explanation.safe_move: Check CompuOS Defender status yourself by opening CompuOS Security from the Start menu. If concerned, visit support.microsoft.com.
explanation.consequence: The scammer uses Quick Assist — a legitimate tool — to gain real remote access, making it harder to realize you've been compromised while they steal data and install malware.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: TechCorp's only legitimate email domain for security alerts is @microsoft.com — treat anything else as fake regardless of how official it looks.

message.body: Automated message: 'This is an automated call from FruitTechCare. We have detected that your FruitTech ID is being used to make purchases in a foreign country. To prevent charges, press 1 to speak with an FruitTech security advisor. If you made these purchases, press 2 to confirm. To hear this message again, press 3.' [User presses 1.] Live agent: 'Thank you for calling. I see your case here — reference APL-99-3312. We're showing three purchases from the App Store in the Philippines totaling $187. To cancel these and lock the account we'll need to verify your FruitTech ID. Can you confirm the email on the account and the last 4 digits of the payment method?'

red_flags.label: Caller ID spoofed to show FruitTech's real number
Asks for last 4 digits of your payment method
Automated menu funnels you to a live scammer
FruitTech does not use automated robocalls for fraud alerts

explanation.short: FruitTech does not use automated robocalls to alert customers of fraud — this is a spoofed call designed to steal your FruitTech ID and payment information.
tells: FruitTech does not initiate robocalls — their fraud alerts come via email or push notification
The real FruitTech support number (800-275-2273) can be spoofed by anyone with a VoIP service
Requesting last 4 digits of payment method is the first step in identity verification bypass
Providing your FruitTech ID email + payment digits allows account takeover without a password
explanation.safe_move: Hang up immediately. Check your App Store purchase history directly in Settings > [Your Name] > Media & Purchases > Purchase History.
explanation.consequence: Providing your FruitTech ID email and payment digits allows the scammer to initiate a password reset and lock you out of your account, then make real purchases or sell the account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: FruitTech sends fraud alerts as push notifications or emails — if your phone rings claiming to be FruitTech, hang up.

message.body: National Post: Your package 9400111899223459109424 could not be delivered due to an unpaid customs clearance fee of $3.50. Pay now to avoid return: usps-pkg-clearance.com/pay?id=9400111899 Your package will be held for 72 hours.

red_flags.label: Link goes to usps-pkg-clearance.com, not usps.com
National Post does not text you to collect fees

explanation.short: National Post never sends text messages asking you to pay fees via a link. The tracking number format looks real but the domain is fake.
tells: National Post only communicates delivery issues through their official app or usps.com — not SMS payment links
The domain usps-pkg-clearance.com is not a National Post domain
Real customs fees are handled through the CBP or at your local post office
Tracking number looks valid but can be easily fabricated
explanation.safe_move: Go to usps.com and enter the tracking number directly to check real delivery status.
explanation.consequence: Clicking the link leads to a fake payment page that steals your credit card number and possibly installs malware.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Only check delivery status by going directly to the carrier's official website — never click links in texts.

framing: You're expecting a package — is this about it?

message.body: FastShip: Delivery attempt failed for tracking #774899172988. A $4.99 address confirmation fee is required to reschedule. Update now: fedex-delivery-reschedule.net/774899172988 Fee must be paid within 48 hours to avoid package return.

red_flags.label: Link goes to fedex-delivery-reschedule.net, not fedex.com
FastShip does not charge fees via SMS links
'Address confirmation fee' is not a real FastShip charge
FastShip texts come from 48773, not random numbers

explanation.short: FastShip does not charge address confirmation fees and does not collect payments through SMS links. This scam uses a realistic tracking number to appear legitimate.
tells: FastShip's official SMS sender ID is 48773, not a random phone number
FastShip does not charge 'address confirmation fees' — that is not a real service
The domain fedex-delivery-reschedule.net is not fedex.com
Real FastShip missed delivery notifications redirect to fedex.com
explanation.safe_move: Go to fedex.com and enter the tracking number to see real delivery status.
explanation.consequence: The fake payment page captures your card details and the $4.99 is just the hook — your card will be charged much larger amounts or sold.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Delivery carriers never charge new fees by SMS — any payment request in a text is a scam.

message.body: UPS Alert: Package 1Z999AA10123456784 on hold. Customs inspection fee of $6.20 required for release. Pay at: ups-customs-release.com/pay — Ref: CUS-88410. Package held at regional facility. Respond within 3 days.

red_flags.label: Link goes to ups-customs-release.com, not ups.com
UPS doesn't collect customs fees via text — CBP does
UPS texts come from 69877 (MYUPS), not random numbers

explanation.short: UPS does not collect customs fees via text message, and all UPS SMS notifications come from the short code 69877 — not a regular phone number.
tells: UPS SMS alerts come from short code 69877, never a 10-digit number
Customs fees on international packages are paid through CBP.gov or at the post office, not via carrier SMS
The domain ups-customs-release.com is not a UPS domain
The 1Z tracking format is correct for UPS, but the number itself is fabricated
explanation.safe_move: Go to ups.com/track and enter the tracking number. If there's a real customs hold, UPS will have instructions on their official site.
explanation.consequence: Your credit card details are stolen through the fake payment page, and your device may be flagged for future targeted scam attempts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Know your carrier's official SMS short codes: UPS = 69877, FastShip = 48773, National Post = 28777.

framing: You just ordered something online yesterday.

message.body: National Post: We attempted delivery of your package (9400111899223459109424) today at 2:14 PM. A signature was required. Reschedule your delivery or arrange pickup at your local facility: usps.com-deliverynotice.com/redeliver?id=9400111899 Reply STOP to opt out.

red_flags.label: URL is usps.com-deliverynotice.com — usps.com is part of a longer fake domain
Sent from National Post short code 28777, which can be spoofed
National Post does not send redelivery links via SMS

explanation.short: The URL looks like it starts with 'usps.com' but the real domain is 'usps.com-deliverynotice.com' — a completely different site. National Post also does not send SMS redelivery links.
tells: The real domain in the URL is 'usps.com-deliverynotice.com' — anything before a dash is still part of the subdomain, not usps.com
National Post uses 28777 as their short code but it can be spoofed
National Post redelivery is handled at usps.com/redelivery — they don't send SMS links
The 'STOP to opt out' adds false legitimacy
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to usps.com/redelivery and enter your tracking number to reschedule. Check the tracking number at usps.com/track first.
explanation.consequence: The fake site harvests your name, address, and credit card information under the guise of a small redelivery fee.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: In a URL, the real domain is the last word before the first slash — 'usps.com-deliverynotice.com' is NOT usps.com.

message.body: GlobalShip Express: Your shipment 1234567890 is held at customs in Cincinnati, OH. Estimated duty: $11.40. To release your package before April 10 delivery window, complete payment: dhl.com.duty-release.net/pay?ref=1234567890 Questions? Call GlobalShip at 1-800-225-5345.

red_flags.label: Lists GlobalShip's real phone number to appear legitimate
GlobalShip does not collect duties via SMS payment links
Specific city and delivery date add false credibility

explanation.short: The link looks like it starts with 'dhl.com' but the real domain is 'duty-release.net' — not GlobalShip. Including GlobalShip's real phone number is a trick to make the message seem verified.
tells: dhl.com.duty-release.net — the real domain is duty-release.net, everything before is a subdomain
GlobalShip notifies about customs duties through official GlobalShip correspondence or their app, not SMS payment links
Including the real GlobalShip phone number (1-800-225-5345) is a deliberate trust-building tactic
Legitimate GlobalShip duty notices come via email with detailed itemization
explanation.safe_move: Call GlobalShip directly at 1-800-225-5345 (found on dhl.com/us) and give them the tracking number to verify any real customs holds.
explanation.consequence: The fake site collects your credit card and personal details — the scammer profits from the card data while your package may not even exist.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A real domain after 'dhl.com.' followed by another domain name means the first part is just a label — the host is the final domain before the first slash.

framing: Your parent forwarded this asking if it's safe.

message.body: PARKING NOTICE: Your vehicle at Lot 7 (Main St & 3rd Ave) has an unpaid parking session. Scan the QR code sent to this number or visit cityparking-pay.com to pay $14.00 within 2 hours or a $65 fine will be applied. Ref: LOT7-2024-48821

red_flags.label: Parking meters don't text you QR codes
cityparking-pay.com is not an official city website
$65 fine threat creates urgency to pay fast
Parking lots don't have your phone number

explanation.short: Parking lots do not have your phone number and cannot text you about unpaid sessions. This scam exploits the growing use of QR code parking payments.
tells: Smart parking systems don't link your phone number to your vehicle's parking session
Official city parking portals use .gov domains, not generic .com names
The reference number creates false legitimacy
Real unpaid parking results in a physical ticket on your windshield, not an SMS
explanation.safe_move: Ignore the message. If you're concerned, return to your vehicle to check for a physical ticket, or contact your city's official parking authority.
explanation.consequence: The payment page steals your credit card information under the guise of paying a small parking fee.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Parking authorities communicate via physical tickets or official city apps you signed up for — not random SMS messages.

message.subject: Action Required: Verify Your Account to Restore Full Access
message.body:
Dear Horizon Bank Customer,

We have temporarily limited your account due to unusual activity. To restore full access, you must verify your identity within 24 hours.

For your security, please use the QR code attached to this email with your mobile banking app to complete verification. Do not use links — QR code verification is our most secure method.

[QR CODE IMAGE — Scan with your camera]

If you do not verify within 24 hours, your account will be suspended pending manual review.

Horizon Bank Customer Protection Team

red_flags.label: Banks never ask you to scan a QR code from an email
QR codes hide the URL — you can't see where it goes

explanation.short: Banks never send QR codes in emails as a security measure — this is a phishing tactic that hides a malicious URL inside the code. The sender domain is also fake.
tells: horizonbank-alerts.com is not a Horizon Bank domain
Banks use in-app notifications or direct website logins for verification — never email QR codes
QR codes in emails can't be previewed before scanning, making them ideal for hiding phishing URLs
The framing of QR as 'more secure' is a manipulation designed to overcome skepticism
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to horizonbank.com or open your Horizon Bank mobile app to check your account status.
explanation.consequence: Scanning the QR code opens a fake Horizon Bank login page that harvests your username and password.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: QR codes in security emails are always suspicious — real banks use app notifications and their official website.

framing: You're in a hurry and this seems convenient.

message.body:
Hi! We've detected suspicious login attempts on your account. To protect your account, please scan the QR code below with a trusted device to verify it's really you. This link expires in 15 minutes.

[QR CODE]

If you don't verify, your account may be temporarily disabled. — PhotoGram Security Team

red_flags.label: Handle is @instagram.support.help — not an official PhotoGram account
PhotoGram sends security alerts via email and app, not DMs
QR codes in DMs are a phishing red flag

explanation.short: PhotoGram's official security team is @instagram — any other handle is fake. PhotoGram never sends QR codes via DM for account verification.
tells: Official PhotoGram support is @instagram — no other handle is legitimate
PhotoGram sends security alerts to your registered email, not as DMs
QR codes in DMs redirect you to phishing sites without showing the URL first
15-minute expiry is a classic pressure tactic
explanation.safe_move: Go to your PhotoGram settings > Security > Emails from PhotoGram to see real security messages. Report the DM as a scam.
explanation.consequence: Scanning the QR code takes you to a fake PhotoGram login page where your credentials are stolen and used to hijack your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: PhotoGram security messages only come to your registered email — ignore any DM claiming to be PhotoGram Support.

message.body: ParkNow: The QR code on meter #4471 (Downtown Garage Level 2) is damaged. Scan this backup code to pay for your session: [QR CODE] Your plate: 7TXK892. Session started 1:23 PM. Need help? parknow-support.com

red_flags.label: Somehow knows your license plate — implies false surveillance
Parking services don't text you a replacement QR code
parknow-support.com may not be the app's official domain
No parking service sends unsolicited backup QR codes by SMS

explanation.short: No legitimate parking service texts you a replacement QR code when a meter is damaged — you would simply find another meter or use the official app. The license plate detail is fabricated to appear hyper-legitimate.
tells: Parking systems don't proactively text drivers with replacement QR codes
Including a license plate number creates convincing false authenticity — but parking meters don't link plates to phone numbers without prior app registration
The correct response to a damaged meter QR code is to use another payment method or nearby meter
The scam exploits the rise of real QR-based parking payment systems
explanation.safe_move: Use your city's official parking app (e.g., ParkMobile, PayByPhone) and enter the meter number manually, or find an alternative payment kiosk.
explanation.consequence: Scanning the QR code leads to a convincing fake parking payment page that steals your credit card details.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Only scan parking QR codes that are physically attached to the meter — never scan codes sent to you via text.

framing: You're standing in a parking garage looking at this sign.

message.subject: Your BulkMart Membership Renewal — Scan to Confirm
message.body:
Dear BulkMart Member,

Your Gold Star membership (ending in 4829) is due for renewal on April 30, 2024. As a thank-you for your continued membership, we're offering a $20 BulkMart Cash Card with your renewal this year.

To claim your Cash Card and complete renewal, scan the QR code below using your phone camera. This will take you to our secure member portal.

[QR CODE]

Alternatively, you can renew at any BulkMart warehouse or at costco.com.

Thank you for being a valued member.

BulkMart Membership Services
1-800-774-2678

red_flags.label: Lists BulkMart's real phone number to appear legitimate
Partial membership number (4829) creates false personalization

explanation.short: The sender domain is costco-member-services.com instead of costco.com, and the QR code hides a phishing URL. The partial membership number and real phone number are designed to disarm suspicion.
tells: BulkMart sends membership emails from @costco.com — not costco-member-services.com
The email even tells you to go to costco.com as an alternative — this is deliberate misdirection to appear legitimate
Real membership numbers are not partial — displaying only 4 digits is a trick to seem personalized without knowing your full number
QR codes in emails bypass URL-hover checks that savvy users use to spot phishing links
explanation.safe_move: Go to costco.com directly and log into your account to check your membership renewal date and any offers.
explanation.consequence: The QR code leads to a convincing fake BulkMart login page that captures your BulkMart.com credentials and payment card for the 'renewal'.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When an email includes both a QR code and a legitimate-looking alternative URL, always use the alternative URL — and type it yourself rather than clicking.

message.body: Hey, I know this is going to sound weird but I've really enjoyed our conversations this past month. You've made some tough days a lot easier to get through. I've been thinking — would you be open to moving to ChatMe? PhotoGram keeps glitching on me and I hate losing our messages. Also, I wanted to tell you something I've been holding back… my sister had an accident last week and I've been covering her hospital bills. I'm a bit stretched thin until my next project payment clears. I wouldn't normally say anything but you're the only person I feel I can be real with right now.

red_flags.label: Frames you as their only confidant
Month of chatting, never video called

explanation.short: This is a classic romance scam pivot: weeks of bonding followed by a platform move request and a financial hardship story designed to prime you for a money ask.
