tells: Asking to move to ChatMe removes platform moderation and reporting tools
The family medical emergency is a standard pre-ask softener in romance scams
Positioning you as 'the only person I can be real with' is emotional isolation
Real financial hardship from someone you've never met in person is a major red flag
explanation.safe_move: Do not move to another platform and do not send money. Reverse-image-search their profile photo. Report the account.
explanation.consequence: Moving to ChatMe cuts you off from platform protections. The next message would be a direct request to PeerSend or wire money for the 'hospital bill.'
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Anyone who moves fast emotionally and slow on video calls is not who they claim to be.

framing: You just moved to a new city and don't know many people.

message.body: I feel like I can finally breathe talking to you — most people here just want surface stuff. I'm actually stuck in Dubai right now, the project wrapped up early and my return flight got canceled because of a billing issue with my card (it's a long story involving a bank freeze back home). I'm so embarrassed to even say this. I have the money, I just can't access it until Monday. The hotel is asking me to settle the bill by tonight or they'll hold my passport. Would you be able to help bridge just $400? I'll pay you back the moment I land, I promise. I have your address, I'll even mail you cash.

red_flags.label: Frames ask as embarrassing to lower guard

explanation.short: The 'stranded abroad' emergency is one of the most common romance scam scripts; the time pressure and passport detail are added to bypass your critical thinking.
tells: Hotels cannot legally hold passports in most countries — this is fabricated pressure
Tight tonight deadline is designed to stop you from thinking clearly
The embarrassment framing is a manipulation to make you feel like helping is kind
'I have the money, I just can't access it' is a textbook line from this scam
explanation.safe_move: Never wire or send money to someone you haven't met in person. Suggest they contact their embassy or a local assistance service.
explanation.consequence: You would send $400 and never hear from them again, or be asked for more money with another emergency story.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Urgency plus overseas location plus money is always a scam triangle.

message.body: Hey it's David — the guy from the hiking app 😊 I know we've only been talking a few weeks but honestly you feel like someone I've known forever. Quick heads up: my PeerSend got flagged for 'unusual activity' and they froze it while I'm traveling for work. My cousin's wedding is Saturday and I need to send him a gift before then. Is there any chance you could send $150 to his PeerSend (@chen_tommy_k) and I'll square you up the moment I'm back Tuesday? I feel terrible asking but I literally have no other option right now.

red_flags.label: Asks you to pay a third party
Only weeks of knowing each other

explanation.short: Directing you to pay a third-party PeerSend is a money-mule tactic; the account freeze story is an excuse invented to make you the only solution.
tells: Paying a stranger's 'cousin' you've never heard of via PeerSend is a money-mule pattern
Account freeze is a fabricated story with no way for you to verify
'No other option' is a manipulation phrase to block you from suggesting alternatives
Repayment promised after a specific date gives a false sense of security
explanation.safe_move: Do not send money to unknown third-party accounts. If this were real, the person could call their bank or use a credit card.
explanation.consequence: Your $150 goes to a scammer. You may also be unknowingly participating in a money-mule network.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: If someone you met online asks you to pay anyone — including a third party — stop and do not send.

framing: Your teenager got this and is asking you about it.

message.body: I've been staring at this message for twenty minutes trying to figure out how to say this. We talked about your mom's surgery in March and I thought about you every day until you said she was recovering. I don't share things like that with people. So I trust you, and that's the only reason I'm even typing this. I did something impulsive — I accepted a short-term contract in Singapore and my wire transfer from my firm is delayed because of a compliance hold on international transfers (new EU banking rules, completely out of my hands). My lease payment is due Thursday back in Basel and if I miss it I lose the flat. It's €600. I have the funds sitting in my account, I just can't move them for another 5 business days. I've never asked anyone for money in my life and I hate myself for asking now. Please say no if it's too much — I mean that.

red_flags.label: References your personal detail to build trust
Overseas work travel conveniently isolates them

explanation.short: This is a highly sophisticated romance scam: the scammer has been collecting personal details from your prior conversations and is now using them to manufacture deep trust before making a financial ask.
tells: Using your personal disclosures (mom's surgery) to create false intimacy is a calculated manipulation
EU compliance hold on international transfers is a plausible-sounding fabrication that's hard to disprove
The 'please say no' line is reverse psychology designed to make you want to say yes
The ask is calibrated to feel small (€600) relative to the emotional bond built
explanation.safe_move: Do not send money. Gently ask to video call 'before anything' — scammers will always have an excuse to avoid live video.
explanation.consequence: After the first transfer, another emergency follows. Victims of this script lose thousands over months before realizing the relationship was entirely fabricated.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: The more emotionally convincing the ask feels, the more carefully you should slow down.

message.body: Hey, I feel awkward bringing this up and honestly considered not saying anything. I know we were going to meet next weekend and I'm still planning on it — this has nothing to do with that. I got blindsided today: my accountant made an error on my quarterly taxes and the tax agency has issued a short-pay notice. The amount is $1,100 and they want resolution by COB Friday or it triggers a lien process that affects my business license. I have the cash in my savings, I just can't liquidate fast enough because my financial advisor has a 3-day hold on transfers over $1,000 (something about fraud protection on their end, ironic I know). I wouldn't breathe a word of this to anyone else. Could you front it and I'll do a same-day transfer to you Monday? I'll even send you a screenshot of the tax agency notice so you can see this is real.

red_flags.label: References future in-person meeting to seem real
Fake Tax Agency lien pressure with Friday deadline
Offers to send fake document as proof

explanation.short: This scam uses a scheduled in-person meeting as a trust anchor and a fake Tax Agency notice (with an offered 'screenshot' that will be forged) to extract $1,100.
tells: The tax agency does not issue same-day COB deadlines via informal processes — this is fabricated urgency
Offering to send a screenshot is a classic move; forged Tax Agency documents are easy to create
Financial advisors do not block personal savings transfers — this detail is invented
Mentioning the upcoming meeting is a calculated anchor to make the relationship feel real and the ask feel safer
explanation.safe_move: Do not send money. Any real Tax Agency issue has a formal process — call the tax agency directly at 1-800-829-1040 to verify. Never pay based on a screenshot.
explanation.consequence: You send $1,100 and the 'meeting next weekend' never happens. The scammer disappears or fabricates another emergency.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Screenshots prove nothing — scammers forge documents routinely. Always verify tax issues directly with the tax agency.

message.subject: Tax Agency Notice CP2000 — Underreported Income Requires Response by April 14
message.body:
Dear Taxpayer,

Our records indicate that income reported on your 2023 Form 1040 does not match third-party information returns filed with the tax agency (Form 1099-NEC, Payer EIN: 47-3821900). A discrepancy of $3,847.00 has been identified.

Case Reference: Tax Agency-2024-CP2000-77412

You must resolve this balance to avoid additional penalties under IRC §6662. Payment options:
— Pay online via secure Tax Agency payment portal (link below)
— Purchase Tax Agency-approved payment vouchers at any major retailer

Failure to respond by April 14, 2024 may result in a federal tax lien filed against your property.

Click here to access your secure taxpayer account and resolve this matter.

Internal Revenue Service
1111 Constitution Ave NW, Washington, DC 20224
Taxpayer Advocate: 1-877-777-4778

red_flags.label: Clickable link in tax notice email
Federal lien threat to create fear

explanation.short: The real Tax Agency sends letters via postal mail, never emails with payment links, and absolutely never asks for 'payment vouchers' available at retailers — that is gift card fraud.
tells: Tax Agency emails come from @irs.gov only — irs-secure-notice.com is a scam domain
'Tax Agency-approved payment vouchers at any major retailer' means gift cards, which the tax agency never accepts
The tax agency sends CP2000 notices by National Post mail, not email
Clickable payment links in emails always lead to phishing or fake payment pages
explanation.safe_move: Go to Tax Agency.gov directly in your browser and log into your account, or call 1-800-829-1040. Never click email links.
explanation.consequence: Clicking the link leads to a fake Tax Agency portal that steals your SSN and financial info, or you're directed to buy gift cards.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: The tax agency contacts you by letter. Any tax email with a payment link is a scam.

message.from_name: Federal Benefits Office of Inspector General
message.body: [Robocall transcript] This is a call from the Federal Benefits Administration Office of Inspector General. Your Federal Benefits number ending in the digits on file has been suspended due to suspicious activity linked to a vehicle found in Texas containing narcotics and cash in your name. A federal arrest warrant has been issued. To speak with an officer before law enforcement is dispatched to your address, press 1 now. If you do not respond, a warrant will be executed within the hour. This is your final notification.

red_flags.label: Press 1 to avoid arrest — high pressure

explanation.short: The Federal Benefits Agency does not suspend Federal Benefits numbers, issue arrest warrants, or use robocalls with press-1 prompts — this is a well-known grandparent/senior scam script.
tells: Federal Benefits numbers cannot be 'suspended' — this concept does not exist
Federal law enforcement does not call to warn you before executing a warrant
The Texas drug bust narrative is a scripted template used by Federal Benefits Agency impersonation scammers
Legitimate government agencies never use robocalls with press-1 urgency for legal matters
explanation.safe_move: Hang up immediately. If concerned, call the real Federal Benefits Agency at 1-800-772-1213 or OIG hotline at 1-800-269-0271 independently.
explanation.consequence: Pressing 1 connects you to a scammer who will demand gift cards, wire transfers, or personal info to 'clear your name.'
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government agencies send letters — they do not threaten arrest by robocall. Hang up first, verify second.

framing: Your elderly parent got this and is scared.

message.subject: FTC File No. 2024-CPB-00341 — Required Identity Verification for Refund Disbursement
message.body:
Dear Consumer,

The Consumer Protection Bureau has concluded enforcement action in the matter of In re: National Data Solutions, LLC (Docket No. C-4782). As part of the settlement, eligible consumers who were affected by unauthorized data sharing practices between January 2019 and March 2023 are entitled to compensation.

Our records indicate you may be eligible for a refund of up to $847.00.

To receive your disbursement, you must verify your identity and confirm your mailing address through our secure consumer portal by May 3, 2024. Verification requires:
— Last four digits of your SSN
— Date of birth
— Current mailing address

FTC settlements are administered under 15 U.S.C. § 57b and refunds are processed via the FTC's Redress Program. For questions, contact the Redress Administrator at 1-844-830-2501.

Do not reply to this email. Visit the secure portal:
[Verify My Eligibility →]

Consumer Protection Bureau
600 Pennsylvania Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20580

red_flags.label: Asks for SSN digits via email portal

explanation.short: This is an extremely convincing FTC impersonation: real statute citations, a real address, and a real-sounding docket — but the domain is not ftc.gov and it asks for your SSN digits through an email link.
tells: The sender domain ftc-notifications.gov.report-center.com is a subdomain of report-center.com, not a government domain
The FTC never collects SSN digits via email portals — real redress programs send checks by mail
Real FTC refund programs are listed at ftc.gov/refunds and never require unsolicited email action
The phone number 1-844-830-2501 is not an official FTC line
explanation.safe_move: Go to ftc.gov/refunds directly in your browser to check for real refund programs. Never submit SSN info from an email link.
explanation.consequence: Entering your SSN digits and DOB gives scammers enough to commit identity theft or open accounts in your name.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real government refunds never require you to verify via an emailed link — always go to the agency's official .gov domain directly.

message.body: CBP ALERT: A package addressed to you (Tracking: CBP-2024-US-8841203) has been intercepted at Chicago O'Hare International Mail Facility. Contents flagged under 19 U.S.C. § 1595a for suspected prohibited items. You are required to verify your identity and provide a processing clearance bond of $199 within 48 hours to release the package or contest the seizure. Failure to respond initiates formal forfeiture proceedings. Contact your assigned CBP Officer: cbp-release.us/case/8841203

red_flags.label: Cites real statute to appear legitimate
CBP does not initiate enforcement via SMS

explanation.short: Customs and Import Services does not contact individuals by SMS to demand clearance bonds — this scam uses a real statute citation and realistic tracking format to appear credible, but the domain and payment demand reveal it.
tells: CBP's official domain is cbp.dhs.gov — cbp-release.us is a scammer-registered domain
CBP does not request payments via text message links; formal seizures use written notices
19 U.S.C. § 1595a is a real law but CBP does not cite it in SMS alerts to the public
The $199 'clearance bond' is a fabricated fee designed to sound bureaucratic but small enough to pay
explanation.safe_move: Do not click the link or pay anything. Contact CBP directly at 1-877-227-5511 or visit cbp.dhs.gov to report.
explanation.consequence: Clicking the link leads to a phishing page that steals payment information; the $199 is just the entry point for larger demands.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government enforcement agencies use written correspondence, not SMS payment links. Always verify at the official .gov domain.

framing: You recently moved and updated your address with the government.

message.subject: New sign-in from CompuOS 11 device in Kyiv, Ukraine — Action Required
message.body:
Hi,

We detected a sign-in to your SearchNet Account from a device we don't recognize.

Device: CompuOS 11 PC
Location: Kyiv, Ukraine
Time: Today at 3:42 AM (your local time)
IP Address: 194.33.40.117

If this was you, no action is needed. If this wasn't you, your account may be compromised. Secure your account immediately:

[Review Activity and Secure Account →]

If you don't act within 24 hours, we may temporarily suspend your account to protect you.

Thanks,
The SearchNet Team

This email cannot receive replies. To manage your account security settings, visit myaccount.google.com.

red_flags.label: Alarming foreign location to trigger panic
Link to 'secure' account goes to phishing page

explanation.short: This is a credential phishing email using a lookalike SearchNet domain and a frightening foreign login alert to make you click quickly without checking the sender address.
tells: SearchNet security emails come from @google.com or @accounts.google.com — not google-security-center.com
A foreign login alert is chosen specifically to trigger fear and fast clicking
The 24-hour suspension threat is false urgency; SearchNet does not suspend accounts on this timeline for login alerts
The link leads to a fake SearchNet login page that harvests your credentials
explanation.safe_move: Go to myaccount.google.com directly in your browser and check Recent Security Activity. Do not click email links.
explanation.consequence: Clicking and logging in gives scammers your SearchNet credentials, granting access to QuickMail, Drive, and all connected accounts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always check the full sender domain before clicking any security alert. SearchNet.com is the only real SearchNet domain.

message.subject: Unusual Sign-In Activity: Your TechCorp Account Requires Verification
message.body:
Dear TechCorp Account User,

We have detected sign-in activity that doesn't match your usual patterns:

Sign-in attempt:
App: MailHub Mobile
Location: Lagos, Nigeria
Date/Time: April 6, 2024 — 11:17 PM UTC
Status: Blocked (suspicious activity)

To confirm your identity and restore full account access, please verify your account within the next 12 hours. After this period, your account will be placed in restricted mode and your MailHub, CloudDrive, and Teams access will be suspended.

[Verify Your Identity Now →]

If you initiated this sign-in, you may disregard this message.

TechCorp Support
One TechCorp Way, Redmond, WA 98052

red_flags.label: Threatens to cut off MailHub, CloudDrive, Teams
Lagos location used to trigger fear

explanation.short: TechCorp account emails come from microsoft.com — microsoftonline-support.net is a phishing domain, and the 12-hour deadline with multi-service suspension threat is designed to panic you into clicking.
tells: microsoftonline-support.net is not a TechCorp domain — legitimate emails come from @microsoft.com
The threat to suspend MailHub, CloudDrive, AND Teams simultaneously is disproportionate and fake
Real TechCorp security alerts don't use 12-hour deadlines for account verification
Lagos, Nigeria is chosen as a maximally alarming location to override careful thinking
explanation.safe_move: Go to account.microsoft.com directly and check your sign-in activity. Do not click the link in the email.
explanation.consequence: The verify link leads to a fake TechCorp login that steals your credentials and potentially your 2FA codes.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Hover over or inspect any sender address before acting on security alerts — the domain must end in @microsoft.com.

framing: You just woke up and this is the first thing you see.

message.subject: Your FruitTech ID has been locked — Verification required
message.body:
Dear [First Name],

Your FruitTech ID (•••••@gmail.com) has been locked because we noticed unusual activity on your account. For your protection, we've temporarily limited access to FruitTech Pay, CloudSync, and the App Store.

To unlock your account, verify your information:

[Unlock FruitTech ID →]

If you don't verify by April 10, 2024, your account will remain locked and you may lose access to purchased content.

If you believe you received this email in error, visit apple.com/support.

FruitTech Support
FruitTech Inc., One FruitTech Park Way, Cupertino, CA 95014
© 2024 FruitTech Inc. All rights reserved.

red_flags.label: Claims FruitTech Pay and CloudSync are restricted
Your email shown masked to seem personalized

explanation.short: This is a near-perfect FruitTech phishing email; the only clear tell is that the sender domain is apple.com.id-verify.net — apple.com is a subdomain of id-verify.net, not the real FruitTech domain.
tells: The real FruitTech sends email from @apple.com — in apple.com.id-verify.net, the actual domain is id-verify.net
FruitTech does not threaten to remove purchased content for account verification delays
The masked email (•••••@gmail.com) is a social engineering trick to make the email feel targeted
The formatting, address, and copyright are copied from real FruitTech emails to maximize deception
explanation.safe_move: Go to appleid.apple.com directly in your browser. FruitTech account lockouts are always manageable there without clicking email links.
explanation.consequence: Clicking leads to a pixel-perfect fake FruitTech ID login that captures your FruitTech ID credentials and security questions.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Check the domain after the last dot before .com, .net, etc. — apple.com.id-verify.net is owned by id-verify.net, not FruitTech.

message.subject: Critical security alert for your linked SearchNet Account
message.body:
Hi,

A critical security alert was triggered for your SearchNet Account.

Someone has your password.

SearchNet detected that your password was found in a data breach from a third-party site. Your account may be at risk if you use the same password elsewhere.

What you should do:
Change your password immediately.

[Go to SearchNet Account →]

You're receiving this email because automatic security alerts are enabled for your account.

The SearchNet Accounts Team

© 2024 SearchNet LLC, 1600 Amphitheatre Parkway, Mountain View, CA 94043

red_flags.label: Sender domain is accounts-activity.net, not google.com
Claims your password was found in a breach
Sparse authoritative text mimics real SearchNet alerts

explanation.short: This drill has only one tell: the sender address google.com.accounts-activity.net is a subdomain of accounts-activity.net — the email is otherwise indistinguishable from a real SearchNet security alert.
tells: google.com.accounts-activity.net: the real domain here is accounts-activity.net, not google.com
Real SearchNet password breach alerts come from no-reply@accounts.google.com
The sparse, authoritative styling is deliberately copied from real SearchNet security emails
The 'someone has your password' line is calibrated to be scary enough to click without thinking
explanation.safe_move: Go to myaccount.google.com > Security > Password checkup to check for real breach alerts. Never follow email links for password changes.
explanation.consequence: Clicking leads to a flawless fake SearchNet login that steals your credentials; if you use QuickMail for 2FA, everything linked to it is then compromised.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Parse the sender domain left to right past the last slash. If it doesn't end in @google.com, it's not SearchNet — no matter how real it looks.

framing: You're at work and this looks like it's from IT.

message.body: Meridian Bank Fraud Alert: A charge of $349.99 at TechMart (Merchant ID: 8841) was attempted on your account ending in 4471. If you did NOT authorize this, reply STOP to block your card immediately or call 1-888-232-1911 to speak with fraud prevention.

red_flags.label: Reply STOP to block card — banks don't work this way
Phone number 1-888-232-1911 is not Chase's real number
Asks you to take action via SMS reply

explanation.short: Real bank fraud alerts ask you to reply Y or N to confirm a transaction — they never ask you to reply STOP to block your card, and 1-888-232-1911 is not Chase's fraud line.
tells: Chase's real fraud line is 1-800-935-9935 — 1-888-232-1911 is a scammer number
Replying STOP to a scammer SMS connects you to a social engineer or confirms your number is active
Real fraud alerts only ask you to confirm or deny a charge (Y/N), never to block a card by text
The merchant detail (TechMart, Merchant ID) is added to make the message look legitimate
explanation.safe_move: Call the number on the back of your Meridian Bank card or go to chase.com directly. Never call numbers from SMS alerts.
explanation.consequence: Calling 1-888-232-1911 connects you to a scammer posing as Chase fraud prevention who will try to get your full card number, PIN, or OTP.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: The phone number on the back of your card is always the right number — never call a number from a text message.

message.subject: Important: Unusual Activity Detected on Your Horizon Bank Account
message.body:
Dear Horizon Bank Customer,

We have detected unusual activity on your Horizon Bank checking account. For your security, online access to your account has been temporarily limited.

Transaction flagged:
Date: April 5, 2024
Merchant: NovaMart.com
Amount: $1,204.88
Status: Pending — Awaiting Verification

To restore full account access and confirm or deny this transaction, please verify your identity:

[Confirm or Deny This Transaction →]

If you do not verify within 24 hours, the transaction will be processed and your account may remain limited.

Horizon Bank Customer Service
155 5th St, San Francisco, CA 94103
1-800-555-4732

red_flags.label: Large alarming NovaMart charge to trigger action
Transaction processes in 24 hours if ignored

explanation.short: Horizon Bank emails come from @horizonbank.com — wellsfargo-secure-alerts.com is a phishing domain, and the email link leads to a fake login page.
tells: wellsfargo-secure-alerts.com is not a Horizon Bank domain — official emails come from @horizonbank.com
Banks do not process pending fraud-flagged transactions if you ignore a 24-hour email — this is false urgency
The link to 'confirm or deny' leads to a credential-harvesting fake banking page
The real Horizon Bank number (1-800-555-4732) is included to add legitimacy, but the domain gives it away
explanation.safe_move: Log into your Horizon Bank account at horizonbank.com directly or call 1-800-555-4732. Do not click email links.
explanation.consequence: Entering your credentials on the fake page gives scammers full access to your Horizon Bank account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any bank email asking you to click a link to verify a transaction is suspicious — always go to the bank's website directly.

framing: You just used your card at a gas station an hour ago.

message.body: National Trust Bank: Did you attempt a $2,847.00 transfer to PeerSend on 04/07? Reply YES to approve or NO to cancel and freeze your account. If NO, you'll receive a call from our Fraud Team at 1-800-732-9194 within 5 minutes.

red_flags.label: 1-800-732-9194 is not National Trust Bank's fraud line
Asks you to approve a nearly $3K transfer by SMS reply
Promises incoming call — scammer will spoof National Trust Bank

explanation.short: This drill is designed to be nearly indistinguishable from a real National Trust Bank fraud alert; the tells are the non-National Trust Bank phone number and the incoming call promise, which sets up a spoofed callback scam.
tells: National Trust Bank's real fraud line is 1-800-432-1000 — 1-800-732-9194 is a scammer number
Real bank fraud SMS alerts confirm or deny small suspicious transactions — $2,847 transfers are handled by phone calls initiated by you
Promising 'you'll receive a call in 5 minutes' sets up a spoofed-caller-ID call where the scammer poses as National Trust Bank and asks for your OTP
The PeerSend detail is chosen because peer-to-peer transfers are hard to reverse, creating real urgency
explanation.safe_move: Do not reply and do not wait for the call. Call National Trust Bank at 1-800-432-1000 (the number on your card) to verify.
explanation.consequence: Replying NO triggers the scammer's callback. They spoof National Trust Bank's number, pose as fraud agents, and ask for your one-time passcode to 'secure your account' — giving them full access.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a bank text promises an incoming call, hang up and call the bank yourself using the number on the back of your card.

message.body: Chase: Fraud alert on acct ending 7823. $1,150.00 charge at ValueMart.com on 04/07 flagged. Reply 1 to approve, 2 to decline. Declined? We'll text a secure link to verify your identity: chase-secure-verify.com/v/7823

red_flags.label: Link goes to chase-secure-verify.com, not chase.com
Declining triggers a link — classic phishing pivot
Large ValueMart.com charge used to trigger action

explanation.short: This scam mimics Chase's real fraud alert SMS format almost perfectly; the only tell is that declining sends you a link to chase-secure-verify.com instead of chase.com.
tells: chase-secure-verify.com is not a Chase domain — Chase links always go to chase.com
Real Chase fraud alerts that require further verification direct you to the Chase app or chase.com, never a separate domain
The reply-1-or-2 format is copied from real Chase SMS alerts to maximize credibility
The last 4 digits (7823) may be correct if this SMS is part of a targeted attack using leaked card data
explanation.safe_move: Do not click the link. Open the Chase app directly or call the number on the back of your card to address the flagged charge.
explanation.consequence: Clicking chase-secure-verify.com leads to a pixel-perfect Chase login page that steals your credentials; combined with real last-4 digits, this is a highly targeted attack.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any link in a bank text that doesn't go directly to the bank's main domain (chase.com, horizonbank.com, bankofamerica.com) is a scam link.

framing: Your elderly neighbor showed you this on their phone.

message.from_name: Sarah Mitchell – Talent Acquisition, Pinnacle Advisory
message.subject: Offer Letter – Senior Financial Analyst | Pinnacle Advisory Advisory (Remote)
message.body:
Dear [Candidate],

Congratulations! Following your application and our internal review, we are pleased to extend a conditional offer for the role of Senior Financial Analyst within Pinnacle Advisory Advisory Services. Compensation is $94,500/year plus benefits.

To initiate payroll setup before your start date of May 5th, HR requires your Federal Benefits Number, bank routing number, and account number via our secure onboarding portal linked below. This step must be completed within 48 hours to hold your start date.

→ Complete Onboarding: https://deloitte-hr-onboard.com/setup

Welcome to the team!

Sarah Mitchell
Talent Acquisition Partner
Pinnacle Advisory Advisory Services

red_flags.label: Requests SSN and bank account before starting
Offer made without a formal interview process
Link goes to a third-party site, not deloitte.com

explanation.short: Legitimate employers never ask for your SSN or bank account details before your first day via email. The sender domain 'deloitte-careers-hr.com' is not Pinnacle Advisory's official domain.
tells: Sender domain is 'deloitte-careers-hr.com' — not 'deloitte.com'
Requests SSN and full bank account info via a web link before employment starts
Onboarding link goes to a non-Pinnacle Advisory domain
Pressure to respond within 48 hours to protect the offer
explanation.safe_move: Look up Pinnacle Advisory's official careers site and call their HR line directly to verify. Never submit financial info via email links.
explanation.consequence: Your SSN and bank account would be used for identity theft and your direct-deposit account drained.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real employers use verified HR systems (Workday, ADP) after you start — they never collect bank info via a random link.

message.subject: Next Steps – Remote Operations Coordinator Role (NovaMart Logistics)
message.body:
Hi there,

Thank you for your interest in the Remote Operations Coordinator position with NovaMart Logistics (WAR5 fulfillment division). We reviewed your resume on JobBoard and believe you are a strong fit.

Your starting pay is $27.50/hr with full benefits from day one. Before we can send your official offer letter, our HR system requires a background check authorization. Please provide the following to proceed:

• Full legal name and date of birth
• Current address
• Last 4 digits of your SSN (for identity match)
• A $35 background check processing fee via QuickSend to: payroll.novamart.checks@gmail.com

Once received, your offer letter will be emailed within 1 business day.

Best,
Marcus Webb
Talent Acquisition | NovaMart Logistics

red_flags.label: Charges a fee to process your application
Asks for payment via QuickSend to a QuickMail address
Requests partial SSN before an offer is made
Claims to have found you on JobBoard without prior contact

explanation.short: No legitimate employer charges you a fee to run a background check. NovaMart would never send recruiting emails from 'novamart-talent-hub.net' or collect payments via QuickSend to a QuickMail account.
tells: Domain is 'novamart-talent-hub.net', not 'novamart.com'
$35 fee sent via QuickSend to a personal QuickMail address is a major scam signal
Real background checks are handled by third-party firms after you are hired — never paid by the candidate
Requests PII (DOB, address, partial SSN) before any formal offer
explanation.safe_move: Report the email to NovaMart's abuse team and never pay fees to get a job. Search NovaMart Jobs directly at novamart.com/careers.
explanation.consequence: You would lose $35 and hand over enough personal data for identity theft.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Paying to get hired is always a scam — legitimate employers absorb all hiring costs.

message.from_name: Jennifer Okafor | Talent Partner, Summit Consulting Group
message.subject: Confidential Offer – Associate Consultant | Summit Consulting Digital (Chicago)
message.body:
Dear Priya,

Following your final-round interviews with the Chicago office, I'm delighted to confirm that the team has decided to move forward with an offer for the Associate Consultant role within our Digital & Analytics practice.

Details:
