Jennifer Okafor
Talent Acquisition Partner | Summit Consulting Group
Direct: +1 (312) 555-0174

red_flags.label: Requests direct deposit info before official offer letter
Portal URL is personalized with candidate name to seem legitimate
Asks you to keep offer secret, limiting outside verification
Onboarding done through a non-mckinsey.com domain

explanation.short: The sender domain 'mckinsey-recruiting.com' is not Summit Consulting's domain (mckinsey.com), and no employer collects direct deposit information before sending a formal offer letter.
tells: Summit Consulting's official domain is mckinsey.com — 'mckinsey-recruiting.com' is a lookalike
Direct deposit details are requested before the offer letter is even sent
The onboarding portal URL does not resolve to mckinsey.com
Confidentiality request discourages the candidate from verifying with others
explanation.safe_move: Go to mckinsey.com and use their official contact page to verify the offer. Do not submit banking details through any unsolicited link.
explanation.consequence: Bank account credentials would be captured and accounts drained; identity theft via I-9 documents is also likely.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify an offer by calling the company's main switchboard number found on their official website — not a number in the email.

message.subject: ⚠️ Action Required: Suspicious Login Detected on Your CryptoVault Wallet
message.body:
Dear CryptoVault User,

We detected a login attempt to your wallet from an unrecognized device in Kyiv, Ukraine at 3:14 AM EDT.

To protect your assets, your wallet has been temporarily restricted. To restore full access and confirm ownership, you must verify your Secret Recovery Phrase through our secure verification portal within 2 hours or your wallet will remain locked.

🔐 Verify My Wallet Now: https://metamask-wallet.io/verify

Do NOT share this link with anyone. This process is required to confirm you are the authorized owner.

If you did not attempt this login, please verify immediately.

CryptoVault Security Team

red_flags.label: Asks for your Secret Recovery Phrase (seed phrase)
2-hour deadline to act or lose access
Creates fear with a foreign login location
Security alert from an unofficial domain

explanation.short: CryptoVault is a self-custodial wallet — it has no servers that can detect logins or lock your wallet. Any request for your seed phrase is always a scam, regardless of how official it looks.
tells: CryptoVault (metamask.io) never sends security emails — it's non-custodial software
Sender domain 'metamask-wallet.io' is not metamask.io
Requesting your Secret Recovery Phrase is the #1 crypto scam signal
Artificial 2-hour timer creates panic to override rational thinking
explanation.safe_move: Delete the email. Your CryptoVault wallet cannot be locked remotely. Go to metamask.io directly if you have concerns.
explanation.consequence: Entering your seed phrase gives the scammer complete control of your wallet and all assets inside — permanently.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Your seed phrase is like your wallet's master key. No legitimate service will ever ask for it.

message.body:
Hello! We noticed unusual activity on your CoinVault account. Your account has been flagged for a security review due to multiple failed login attempts.

To avoid suspension, please verify your identity by providing your 2FA backup code and confirming your wallet address through our support form here: https://coinvault-support-verify.com/account-check

Our team is online now and can resolve this in under 5 minutes. If you do not respond within 30 minutes, your account may be suspended and funds held pending investigation.

— CoinVault Security Team

red_flags.label: Unsolicited DM from a fake support account
Threatens account suspension to pressure action
Handle mimics official name but is not verified

explanation.short: CoinVault support never reaches out via DM, and no legitimate service will ever ask for your 2FA backup code — that code is the key to bypassing all your account security.
tells: Real CoinVault support contacts you via verified email, not social DMs
Asking for a 2FA backup code would let attackers fully bypass your account protection
Link goes to 'coinvault-support-verify.com', not coinvault.io
Unverified lookalike handle impersonating official support
explanation.safe_move: Go to coinvault.io directly and log in to check your account status. Open a support ticket through the official site only.
explanation.consequence: Sharing your 2FA backup code hands over full account access, enabling the attacker to withdraw all funds.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate support never DMs you first. Always go to the company's site directly — never via links sent to you.

message.subject: Important: CryptoVault Extension Security Patch Required (v11.9.2)
message.body:
Dear CryptoVault User,

A critical security vulnerability (CVE-2024-38812) has been identified in CryptoVault Extension versions 11.8.x and below. This vulnerability allows malicious websites to read wallet state under specific conditions.

The patched version (v11.9.2) is now available. Because browser auto-updates can take up to 72 hours, we recommend manually updating now to protect your assets.

To apply the security patch immediately:
1. Click the update link below
2. Remove your current extension when prompted
3. Install the patched version
4. Re-enter your Secret Recovery Phrase to restore your wallet

Download CryptoVault v11.9.2 (Verified): https://metamask.support-update.com/extension/v11.9.2

If you do not update, your wallet may be vulnerable until the browser auto-update completes.

CryptoVault Security
ConsenSys

red_flags.label: Email domain is not metamask.io or consensys.io
Update process requires re-entering seed phrase
References a CVE to appear technically credible
Extension download is from a non-official domain
Instructs you to remove your current extension

explanation.short: CryptoVault updates through the Chrome Web Store automatically — they never email you to manually install an extension. Any update process that asks for your seed phrase is stealing it.
tells: Sender domain 'metamask.support-update.com' is not metamask.io or consensys.io
No legitimate wallet update requires re-entering your Secret Recovery Phrase
Extensions should only be installed from the Chrome Web Store or Firefox Add-ons, never a direct download link
The CVE reference is fabricated to add technical credibility
explanation.safe_move: Update CryptoVault only through your browser's official extension store. Never download extensions from links in emails.
explanation.consequence: Installing the fake extension and entering your seed phrase gives the attacker permanent access to all your wallets.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Browser extensions update through the browser's own store — no legitimate extension developer will email you a direct download link.

message.body:
Hey, hope I'm not bothering you — I saw you're active in the Aave governance forum. I'm part of a small group running a delta-neutral USDC strategy on a new L2 (Arbitrum-based). We've been averaging 34% APY for 6 months, fully on-chain and audited by Certik.

We're opening 3 spots to external LPs for the next cycle (starts Monday). Min. entry is 2 ETH. Funds go into a verified multisig — I can share the contract address and the audit report if you want to verify.

Not trying to hard-sell, just figured someone active in governance would appreciate a legit alpha opportunity. Interested in the details?

red_flags.label: 34% APY claim far exceeds sustainable DeFi yields
Unsolicited DM pitching an investment opportunity
Only 3 spots available to create urgency
Claims CertiK audit but offers no way to verify upfront
References your real activity to build false trust

explanation.short: This is a 'pig butchering' crypto scam — the attacker references your real on-chain activity to seem credible, then lures you into a fake high-yield fund where your deposited ETH disappears.
tells: 34% sustained APY is not achievable through any legitimate delta-neutral strategy
Genuine high-yield opportunities are never pitched via unsolicited DMs
Scarcity of '3 spots' is a classic pressure tactic
The offer to 'share the contract address' after you engage is designed to string you along
explanation.safe_move: Do not engage. Block the account. Real DeFi opportunities are public and don't require a cold DM invitation.
explanation.consequence: Your deposited ETH would be transferred to the scammer's wallet the moment it enters the 'multisig' — unrecoverable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: In DeFi, if someone DMs you an investment opportunity, it is a scam — no exceptions.

message.subject: Re: Your LocalList Listing – Yamaha RX-V685 Receiver ($350)
message.body:
Hi,

I saw your listing for the Yamaha receiver on LocalList and I'm very interested. I'm willing to pay your asking price of $350 — no haggling.

I'm currently deployed overseas (Navy) so I can't pick it up in person, but I'll arrange a courier to come collect it. I'll send a cashier's check for $600 to cover the item plus the courier's fee. Once you receive the check, please just wire the $250 difference to my courier's payment account before they pick up.

The check will clear quickly. I just need your mailing address to send it. This is completely safe — cashier's checks are guaranteed funds.

Thanks,
Tyler

red_flags.label: Sends a cashier's check for more than the asking price
Asks you to wire money back from the overpayment
Buyer cannot meet in person due to a convenient excuse
Insists on sending their own courier
Claims cashier's checks are guaranteed funds

explanation.short: This is the classic cashier's check overpayment scam. The check is fake — it will bounce after you have already wired the 'courier fee' out of your own pocket.
tells: Overpayment with a cashier's check and a request to wire the difference is a textbook scam pattern
Cashier's checks can appear to clear and then bounce days later — they are NOT guaranteed
Military deployment excuse is one of the most common scam pretexts
Buyer never wants to meet locally or use a local courier
explanation.safe_move: Only accept cash for local LocalList sales. Never accept overpayment or wire money to strangers.
explanation.consequence: The check bounces days later and you are liable for the full $250 you wired plus bank fees, while the item is gone.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: For local marketplace sales, cash in person only. Any buyer who can't meet locally is a red flag.

message.body:
Hi! I'm interested in the sectional sofa you posted on FriendNet Marketplace. I'd like to buy it for the full $800 asking price.

I'm out of state visiting family right now and can't come pick it up until next weekend. Would you be willing to accept QuickSend payment today to hold it for me? Once I send you the money, can you just mark it as sold so no one else grabs it?

Also, I have a friend with a truck in your area who can pick it up Saturday — I'll send you their contact info. You'd just need to give them the address. Does that work?

Thanks so much — this is exactly what I've been looking for!

red_flags.label: Buyer is conveniently unavailable for local pickup
Sends a third party to collect without meeting themselves
Pushes to pay now to 'hold' the item remotely
Overly enthusiastic tone to lower guard
Uses QuickSend which has no buyer/seller protection

explanation.short: This setup allows a scammer to either send a fraudulent QuickSend payment that gets reversed, or use a 'friend' to pick up the item while the payment is fake or disputed.
tells: Remote buyers who can't meet in person are extremely common in marketplace scams
Sending a third-party pickup person without the buyer present is a setup to retrieve goods without valid payment
QuickSend payments can be reversed if the scammer uses a compromised account or files a false dispute
Urgency to pay now and mark sold prevents you from finding a safer buyer
explanation.safe_move: Only sell to buyers who can meet you in person and pay cash. Never release an item to someone you haven't met.
explanation.consequence: You hand the sofa to a stranger and the QuickSend payment is later reversed, leaving you with nothing.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: FriendNet Marketplace is for local, in-person transactions. Treat any remote buyer as a scam risk.

message.subject: Payment Received – $1,250.00 for 'Vintage Leica M6 Camera'
message.body:
Hello,

This is a confirmation that $1,250.00 USD has been sent to your SafePay account by David Lerner (d.lerner.photo@gmail.com) for the item 'Vintage Leica M6 Camera.'

IMPORTANT: To comply with SafePay's updated Goods & Services policy for high-value items, your funds are temporarily on hold. To release the payment to your account balance, you must provide a National Post tracking number showing the item has been shipped.

Once tracking is entered, the $1,250.00 will be released within 2 hours.

Enter Tracking Number: https://paypal-seller-confirm.com/release-funds

Do not delay shipment as this may result in payment cancellation.

SafePay Seller Protection Team

red_flags.label: Instructs you to ship before funds are in your account
Invents a SafePay policy to justify why funds aren't available yet
Demands tracking number before you've confirmed receiving money
Threatens payment cancellation to pressure fast shipping

explanation.short: This is a fake SafePay confirmation email. No money was sent — the scammer wants you to ship the camera first, after which you discover the payment never existed.
tells: Sender domain 'paypal-seller-confirm.com' is not paypal.com
SafePay does not hold funds and require tracking numbers before releasing — check your actual SafePay account balance
Pressure to ship immediately before verifying funds is the core mechanic of this scam
The 'release funds' link goes to a non-SafePay domain
explanation.safe_move: Always log in to paypal.com directly to verify your balance before shipping anything. Ignore any email claiming funds are 'on hold.'
explanation.consequence: You ship a $1,250 camera and receive nothing — no payment was ever made.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Only ship after you see the money in your actual SafePay balance — emails proving payment are easily faked.

message.subject: Escrow Payment Initiated – Buyer Protected Transaction #OFU-8841923
message.body:
Hi Sandra,

Great news! A buyer has initiated an escrow-protected transaction for your 2021 Trek Marlin 7 Mountain Bike listed at $750.

Transaction #OFU-8841923
Item: 2021 Trek Marlin 7
Amount: $750.00
Buyer: Michael Torres
Escrow Status: Funds Held Pending Delivery Confirmation

To complete the transaction:
1. Ship the item using any trackable courier within 48 hours
2. Send the tracking number to this email
3. Buyer has 48 hours to confirm receipt, then funds are released to you

TradeLocal Buyer & Seller Guarantee ensures you are protected throughout this process.

Ship to:
Michael Torres
3847 Clover Hill Rd
Austin, TX 78741

Transaction Services | TradeLocal

red_flags.label: Email is from a fake TradeLocal escrow domain
Instructs you to ship before any real payment is verified
Fake transaction number to appear legitimate
TradeLocal does not offer an escrow shipping service
48-hour deadline pressures fast action without verification

explanation.short: TradeLocal does not have an escrow service. This fake email tricks you into shipping your bike with a promise of payment that will never come.
tells: TradeLocal does not offer escrow — any email claiming to be 'TradeLocal Escrow' is fake
Sender domain 'offerup-escrow-secure.com' is not offerup.com
No real funds are held anywhere — this is a paper transaction designed to get you to ship first
Transaction ID and buyer details are fabricated to add authenticity
explanation.safe_move: Sell the bike locally for cash in person. Verify any marketplace transaction by logging into the app directly — never via email.
explanation.consequence: You ship a $750 bike and receive no payment — the escrow was fictional and the buyer disappears.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Marketplace escrow scams are widespread. If a payment requires you to ship first, it's not real.

message.subject: Updated Banking Details for ACH Payments – Action Required
message.body:
Dear Accounts Payable Team,

Please be advised that OfficePro Solutions has recently transitioned to a new banking partner for ACH and wire transfer payments. Effective immediately, all outstanding and future invoices should be directed to our new account:

Bank Name: Pinnacle Financial Partners
Account Name: OfficePro Solutions LLC
Routing Number: 064102108
Account Number: 7739201045
Reference: Your company account number

Our previous account will remain active for 30 days but we encourage immediate updates to avoid processing delays. Please update your vendor records accordingly and confirm receipt of this notice.

Thank you for your continued partnership.

Regina Holloway
Accounts Receivable Manager
OfficePro Solutions

red_flags.label: Banking details changed via unsolicited email
New bank is an obscure institution, not a major bank
No callback number provided to verify the change

explanation.short: This is a Business Email Compromise (BEC) scam. The email spoofs a vendor to redirect your payments to a criminal's account. OfficePro' official domain is staples.com, not 'staples-businesssolutions.net'.
tells: Legitimate vendors do not change banking details via a single unsolicited email
Sender domain 'staples-businesssolutions.net' is not staples.com
Any bank change request must be verified by calling your vendor contact at a number from your records — not from the email
No callback verification process is offered
explanation.safe_move: Call your OfficePro account rep using the number on previous invoices or their official website to verify any banking changes before updating records.
explanation.consequence: Your next ACH payment goes directly to the scammer's account — often $10,000s — with little chance of recovery.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Vendor bank account changes must always be verified by phone using a number you already have on file.

message.subject: Invoice #LZ-2024-09341 – Overdue Notice ($2,870.00)
message.body:
Dear Client,

Our records indicate that Invoice #LZ-2024-09341 in the amount of $2,870.00 is now 47 days past due. This invoice relates to your business formation and registered agent services for the period of January–March 2024.

To avoid late fees and suspension of your registered agent service (which could affect your business's good standing), please remit payment immediately via ACH or wire:

Bank: Chase Business Banking
Account Name: DocuLaw Services LLC
Routing: 021000021
Account: 9901234887

Alternatively, pay via our billing portal: https://legalzoom-billing.com/pay

If you believe this is an error, reply to this email.

Kevin Marsh
Accounts Receivable
DocuLaw Business Services

red_flags.label: Invoice for services you may not recognize
Threatens harm to your business standing to pressure payment
Requests wire transfer to an account in the email
Payment portal is not on legalzoom.com

explanation.short: The sender domain 'legalzoom-billing.com' is not DocuLaw's official domain (legalzoom.com). This is a fake overdue invoice designed to frighten business owners into wiring money.
tells: Official DocuLaw emails come from legalzoom.com, not 'legalzoom-billing.com'
Threatening loss of business good standing is a fear tactic
Wire transfer instructions in an email are a BEC hallmark
Log into your actual DocuLaw account at legalzoom.com to check for any real invoices
explanation.safe_move: Log in to your DocuLaw account directly at legalzoom.com to check for legitimate invoices. Do not pay based on an unsolicited email.
explanation.consequence: You wire $2,870 to a criminal's Meridian Bank account — wire transfers are nearly impossible to recover.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Before paying any invoice received by email, log into the vendor's actual website to confirm the amount and invoice number.

message.subject: RE: Invoice #GRN-448821 & #GRN-448956 – Updated Remittance Instructions
message.body:
Hi Tom,

Following up on our call last week — as I mentioned, we've completed our banking migration to First Horizon Bank. For the two open invoices below, please use the updated wire instructions going forward:

Invoice #GRN-448821 – $14,220.00 (net-30, due 4/18)
Invoice #GRN-448956 – $8,750.00 (net-30, due 4/25)

Updated Wire Info:
Bank: First Horizon Bank
ACA Routing: 084000026
Account: 5541029837
Account Name: W.W. Hartfield Inc.

Our old Horizon Bank account (#ending 4412) will close April 12th. Please update your records before then to avoid returned payments.

Let me know if you have any questions — happy to hop on a call.

Rachel Voss
Accounts Receivable | W.W. Hartfield, Inc.
Direct: (847) 555-0293

red_flags.label: Bank account change delivered via email only
Fabricates a prior call to build false familiarity
Uses specific invoice numbers to appear legitimate
Old account closing soon creates pressure to act fast

explanation.short: This is a sophisticated BEC attack. The domain 'grainger-invoicing.com' is not grainger.com, and the reference to a 'call last week' is fabricated to bypass skepticism. Real invoice numbers may have been obtained from previous data exposure.
tells: Hartfield's official domain is grainger.com — 'grainger-invoicing.com' is a lookalike
Banking changes are never legitimately communicated by email alone
The reference to a prior call is designed to make recipients assume the change was already discussed
Specific invoice amounts and a closing deadline create a sense of routine urgency
explanation.safe_move: Call Hartfield's official AR line (found on grainger.com) to verify the banking change before processing any wire transfer.
explanation.consequence: Two wire transfers totaling $22,970 go to criminal accounts — recoverable only if reported within hours.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any vendor banking change must be verified via a phone call to a number from your own records — never trust the number in the email.

message.subject: Annual Contract Renewal – Invoice SF-2024-ANN-00431 ($48,000)
message.body:
Hi Lisa,

Hope you're well. As your renewal date approaches (May 1st), I'm sending over Invoice SF-2024-ANN-00431 for your Enterprise license renewal — $48,000 for the 12-month term, consistent with last year.

One note: our finance team has migrated to a new banking system (KeyBank, effective Q2). Please use the wire details below for this renewal payment:

Bank: KeyBank National Association
Routing: 041001039
Account: 2209981234
Account Name: CloudForce.com, Inc.

Your renewal confirmation and updated contract will follow once payment is received. The prior net-30 terms apply.

Let me know if anything looks off — happy to get on a call.

Daniel Park
Enterprise Account Finance | CloudForce
CloudForce Tower, San Francisco, CA

red_flags.label: New wire instructions delivered in the same email as the invoice
Contract/confirmation promised only after payment, not before
Renewal framing matches real enterprise software cycles

explanation.short: CloudForce communicates through salesforce.com, not 'salesforce-finance.com.' This highly targeted BEC email matches real enterprise renewal cadences to slip through approvals without scrutiny.
tells: 'salesforce-finance.com' is not a CloudForce domain — CloudForce uses salesforce.com
New banking details should never be accepted from a single email — always call to verify
Promising the contract 'after' payment is backwards — real contracts precede payment
The $48,000 amount matches plausible enterprise SaaS pricing to avoid triggering suspicion
explanation.safe_move: Log into your CloudForce account or call your dedicated Account Executive at a number from CloudForce.com to verify the invoice and wire instructions.
explanation.consequence: A $48,000 wire transfer to a criminal account — enterprise BEC losses are rarely recovered in full.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: For invoices over $10,000, always verify wire instructions by calling your vendor contact using a number from your own records.

message.subject: Your StreamBox Account Has Been Suspended – Action Required
message.body:
Dear StreamBox Member,

Your account has been suspended due to a billing issue with your payment method on file. To restore access to your account and avoid losing your viewing history and profile settings, please update your billing information within 24 hours.

Update Your Billing Now: https://netflix-account-update.com/billing/verify

If payment is not updated by [DATE], your account will be permanently closed.

For security, you will be asked to verify your current card details and confirm your password.

StreamBox Member Services
Helping you enjoy movies and TV, wherever you are.

red_flags.label: Threatens permanent account closure to pressure action
Billing update link goes to a non-StreamBox domain
Uses 'Dear StreamBox Member' instead of your name
Asks for both card details and password

explanation.short: StreamBox emails come from netflix.com, not 'netflix-account-update.com.' This phishing email harvests your credit card and StreamBox password through a fake billing page.
tells: Sender domain is 'netflix-account-update.com' — a clear lookalike domain
StreamBox always addresses you by your name on file, not 'Dear StreamBox Member'
The billing update link resolves to a non-StreamBox domain
Asking for your password during billing verification is a phishing tactic
explanation.safe_move: Go to netflix.com directly and check your account settings. StreamBox will show any real billing issues after you log in.
explanation.consequence: Your credit card details and StreamBox password are captured — card charged fraudulently and account taken over.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Go directly to the service's website to handle billing issues — never use links from emails claiming account problems.

framing: You recently changed your password on a few accounts.

message.body:
NovaMart Alert: Unusual sign-in detected on your account from Lagos, Nigeria. If this wasn't you, secure your account immediately: https://novamart-account-secure.com/verify-login

If this was you, reply SAFE to dismiss this alert. Failure to respond may result in account restriction. -NovaMart Security

red_flags.label: Link goes to a non-NovaMart domain
Uses a foreign location to maximize alarm
Account security alerts from SMS with clickable links
Asking you to reply SAFE confirms your number is active
Threatens account restriction to force a response

explanation.short: NovaMart's legitimate security texts do not include clickable links to non-NovaMart domains. The link 'novamart-account-secure.com' is a phishing site designed to steal your NovaMart credentials.
tells: Real NovaMart security URLs always resolve to novamart.com subdomains
'novamart-account-secure.com' is a lookalike, not NovaMart
SMS security alerts with clickable links are a phishing delivery method
The REPLY SAFE option harvests confirmation that your number is active
explanation.safe_move: Go to novamart.com directly and log in to check for any real security alerts under your account settings.
explanation.consequence: You enter your NovaMart credentials on a phishing page — account taken over, stored payment methods used for fraudulent purchases.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real security alerts show in your account dashboard. Never click security links in SMS messages.

message.subject: Your SafePay account access has been limited
message.body:
Dear [First Name],

We've noticed some unusual activity associated with your SafePay account and have temporarily limited your access as a precaution.

Account: [your registered email]
Limitation Type: Outgoing transfers restricted
Date Detected: April 7, 2026

To restore full access, please confirm your identity by clicking the button below. This process takes less than 2 minutes and is required to comply with our User Agreement (Section 10.2).

Restore My Account Access →

If you don't complete verification within 3 days, your account may remain limited indefinitely.

Thank you for helping us keep SafePay safe.

SafePay Account Services
SafePay, Inc. | 2211 North First Street, San Jose, CA 95131

red_flags.label: Email is from paypal.com.account-verify.net, not paypal.com
Threatens indefinite limitation to pressure action
Cites a real-sounding policy section to seem official
Uses SafePay's real San Jose address to appear legitimate
Contains unfilled template placeholders like [First Name]

explanation.short: The email address 'service@paypal.com.account-verify.net' looks like it ends in paypal.com but the actual domain is 'account-verify.net' — paypal.com is just a subdomain of the attacker's site. The unfilled '[First Name]' placeholder is a production error that exposes the mass phishing campaign.
tells: The sender domain reads 'paypal.com.account-verify.net' — the real domain is 'account-verify.net', not paypal.com
Unfilled template placeholder '[First Name]' indicates bulk phishing, not a message from SafePay
SafePay always uses your registered name and email in communications
Real SafePay limitations appear in your account dashboard under Resolution Center
explanation.safe_move: Go to paypal.com directly and check the Resolution Center. Real account limitations are shown there with clear instructions.
explanation.consequence: You log in through the phishing page and your SafePay credentials are stolen — full account access given to attackers.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Check the actual domain in the sender's email address carefully — 'paypal.com.evil.net' is NOT a SafePay domain.

message.subject: Your FruitTech ID was used to sign in on a new iPhone 15 Pro
message.body:
Your FruitTech ID was used to sign in to CloudSync on an iPhone 15 Pro.

Date and Time: April 7, 2026 at 11:43 PM
Device: iPhone 15 Pro
Location: Dallas, TX
Operating System: iOS 17.4

If you recently signed in to a new device, you can disregard this email.

If you did not sign in, your FruitTech ID may be compromised. Secure your account:

→ Review Activity and Secure Account

https://appleid.apple.com.id-session-verify.com/signin

If you need additional help, visit FruitTech Support.

FruitTech Inc. | One FruitTech Park Way, Cupertino, CA 95014

red_flags.label: Sender domain is apple-id.com, not apple.com
Link looks like apple.com but resolves to id-session-verify.com
Specific device/OS details make it look genuine
Uses FruitTech's real Cupertino address for legitimacy
Implies account compromise to provoke immediate click

explanation.short: The sender is 'apple-id.com' (not apple.com) and the verification link resolves to 'id-session-verify.com' — not FruitTech's servers. The specific iPhone model and iOS version are generic details used to make this mass phishing email feel personal.
tells: FruitTech sends account emails from apple.com — 'apple-id.com' is a registered lookalike domain
The link 'appleid.apple.com.id-session-verify.com' — the real domain is 'id-session-verify.com', not apple.com
Real FruitTech sign-in notifications arrive as push notifications on your trusted devices first
Hover over any link to check the actual URL before clicking
explanation.safe_move: Go to appleid.apple.com directly in your browser to review recent sign-in activity. Enable two-factor authentication if not already enabled.
explanation.consequence: Entering your FruitTech ID and password on the phishing page gives attackers full CloudSync access — photos, contacts, Find My, and any stored payment methods.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always hover over or long-press links to see the real destination before clicking — the visible URL text can be completely different from the actual link.

message.body: URGENT ALERT from National Trust Bank: Your acount has been FROZEN due to suspecious activity!! Click here to unlock your acount NOW or it will be closed in 24 hours: http://bankamerica-unlock.xyz/verify — If you do not act, your funds will be HELD. Call us at 555-203-8847. Gift cards accepted for verification fee.

red_flags.label: Asks for a gift card as a 'verification fee'
Link is a .xyz domain, not bankofamerica.com

explanation.short: This is an extremely obvious bank fraud scam — the bank name is misspelled, the link goes to a .xyz domain, and no real bank ever asks for gift cards as a verification fee.
tells: 'National Trust Bank' has a double-r typo — a basic error no real bank would make
No legitimate bank accepts gift cards for any purpose, ever
The link goes to 'bankamerica-unlock.xyz' — real National Trust Bank uses bankofamerica.com
Multiple typos, ALL CAPS urgency, and double exclamation marks are hallmarks of scam texts
explanation.safe_move: Delete the message. If you are concerned about your account, call the number on the back of your debit card.
explanation.consequence: You click the link, enter your banking credentials, and your account is taken over — or you buy gift cards and lose that money immediately.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real banks never ask for gift cards. Ever. Full stop.

message.body:
Dear Valued Custumer, Horizon Bank here. We have detected FRAUDULENT CHARGES on you're account ending in 0000. To stop these charges you must verify you're identity IMEDIATELY. Click link: http://wellsfrago-secure.com/stop-fraud

Do NOT ignore this message or you're account will be DEBITED $499. Respond YES to confirm you received this alert.

red_flags.label: Account 'ending in 0000' is not your real account
Threatens to charge your account $499 if you ignore
Multiple grammar errors (you're instead of your, custumer)

explanation.short: The bank name is misspelled as 'Wells Frago,' the account number is a generic '0000,' and the link goes to 'wellsfrago-secure.com' — all clear signs of a scam text.
tells: 'Wells Frago' is a misspelling of Horizon Bank — real banks spell their own names correctly
Your bank knows your real account number — 'ending in 0000' is a giveaway it's fake
'wellsfrago-secure.com' is a misspelled lookalike domain, not horizonbank.com
Threatening a $499 charge if you don't respond is a fear tactic with no legal basis
explanation.safe_move: Delete the text. Call Horizon Bank at 1-800-555-4732 (the number on the back of your card) if you have concerns.
explanation.consequence: Clicking the link leads to a credential-harvesting page — your online banking login is stolen and account drained.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Your real bank knows your name and the last 4 digits of your actual account. Generic details like '0000' mean it's fake.

message.subject: UNPAID INVOCE #00291 – PAY IMEDIATELY TO AVOID LEGAL ACTION!!!
message.body:
DEAR SIR/MADAM,

YOU HAVE A UNPAID INVOCE OF $3,200 THAT IS NOW SERIOUSLY OVERDUE!!!!

IF YOU DO NOT PAY WITHIN 24 HOUR YOUR COMPANY WILL FACE LEGAL ACTION AND YOUR CREDIT WILL BE EFFECTED.

Send payment via WESTERN UNION or GIFT CARD (iTunes or SearchNet Play) to avoid lawsuit.

INVOCE NUMBER: #00291
AMOUNT DUE: $3,200.00

PLEASE CLICK LINK TO PAY: http://free-billing-notify.com/pay-now

DO NOT IGORE THIS NOTISE.

Billing Dept.

red_flags.label: Entire message in ALL CAPS with excessive exclamation marks
Requests payment via gift cards or GlobalWire
Sender uses a free, suspicious billing domain
Threatens lawsuits and credit damage to cause panic

explanation.short: This is an obvious fake invoice scam. No legitimate business sends invoices in all caps with multiple misspellings, and no real creditor accepts gift cards as payment.
tells: All-caps writing with excessive exclamation points is a classic scare tactic
'INVOCE,' 'IMEDIATELY,' and 'IGORE' are basic misspellings no real billing department would make
Gift cards and GlobalWire are untraceable — only scammers ask for these
Sender domain 'free-billing-notify.com' is not a real company's domain
explanation.safe_move: Delete the email. If you think there might be a real outstanding invoice, contact your vendor directly using contact info from your own records.
explanation.consequence: You buy gift cards and hand over the codes — money is instantly gone with no way to recover it.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Gift cards are for gifts. Any 'invoice' or 'debt collector' demanding gift cards is always a scam.

message.body: Hi, I just sent you $500 on QuikPay by mistake. Can you please send it back? I need it for rent this week.

explanation.short: Classic overpayment scam — the $500 was sent from a stolen account or will be reversed, leaving you out real money.
tells: Legitimate accidental payments can be reversed by the sender's bank
The emotional story about rent creates urgency
You have no way to verify this person's identity
The 'payment' may be clawed back after you send your own money
explanation.safe_move: Do not send money. Check your QuikPay account — if you received a payment, contact QuikPay support to let them handle the reversal.
explanation.consequence: You send $500 'back' and the original fraudulent payment gets reversed, leaving you out $500.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never send money to someone who claims they paid you by accident. Let the bank or payment app handle reversals.

message.body: Hey! I was trying to pay my babysitter and accidentally sent $250 to your number on ZapSend. I'm so sorry! Could you send it back to me at this number? I can send you $10 extra for the trouble.

red_flags.label: No way to verify the payment exists

explanation.short: The 'bonus for your trouble' is a manipulation tactic. No legitimate accidental sender would offer you extra money.
tells: Offering $10 extra is designed to make you feel obligated
If someone really sent money by mistake, they'd contact ZapSend support
You never received any payment from this person
The casual tone is crafted to seem trustworthy
explanation.safe_move: Ignore the message. Check your ZapSend account directly — if there's no payment, there's nothing to return.
explanation.consequence: You send $250 to a scammer. Any 'payment' you received was from a stolen account and gets reversed.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Payment apps have built-in dispute and reversal processes. You should never manually send money back to a stranger.

message.body: This is Marcus from CashDash support. A payment of $1,200 was flagged on your account. To reverse the hold, please send a confirmation payment of $50 to verification account @CashDash-Verify. Reference code: CD-88412.

red_flags.label: Claims to be from CashDash support
Asks you to send money to 'verify'
Fake reference number adds false legitimacy

explanation.short: No payment app will ever ask you to send money to 'verify' or 'reverse' a transaction. This is an impersonation scam.
tells: Support contacts you through the app, not via random text messages
Legitimate companies never ask for 'confirmation payments'
The reference code is fake — designed to look official
The @CashDash-Verify account is controlled by the scammer
explanation.safe_move: Open the CashDash app directly and check your account. Contact support through the app if you're concerned.
explanation.consequence: You send $50 to a scammer's account. They may follow up asking for more 'verification' payments.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate support will never ask you to send money. Always contact support through the official app.

message.body: Hey, we matched on LinkMeet last week! I just tried to send you $75 on QuikPay for that concert ticket but I think I sent it twice by accident 😅 Can you check and send one back? My QuikPay is @jesstorres. I really appreciate it!

red_flags.label: Claims prior relationship to build trust
No way to verify double payment
Vague reference to a prior interaction

explanation.short: This scam uses a fake social connection to make the request feel natural. You never sold a concert ticket to this person.
tells: The reference to LinkMeet is vague enough to seem plausible but can't be verified
If they really double-paid, they can dispute it through QuikPay
The friendly tone and emoji are designed to disarm you
They're directing you to send money to a specific account they control
explanation.safe_move: Check your QuikPay transaction history. If you don't see any payments from this person, it's a scam.
explanation.consequence: You send $75 to a stranger. There was no double payment.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Even if someone claims to know you, verify any payment claims directly through the app before sending money.

message.body: Hi, this is really embarrassing but I'm your neighbor in 4B. I was sending rent to our landlord through ZapSend and accidentally used your phone number instead. It was $1,450. I can show you the screenshot if you want. Could you please send it to our landlord's ZapSend? His handle is @mgrprop_mgmt. I'm going to be late on rent if this doesn't go through today. 🙏

red_flags.label: Asks you to forward a large sum
Asks you to send to a third party

explanation.short: This sophisticated scam uses hyperlocal details (apartment number) and redirects the payment to a 'landlord' — actually another scammer account. Screenshots prove nothing.
tells: A real neighbor would knock on your door, not text a stranger's number
Being asked to forward money to a third party is a major red flag
Payment screenshots are trivially easy to fake
The same-day deadline pressures you to skip verification
The large amount ($1,450) means significant financial loss
explanation.safe_move: If you actually have a neighbor in 4B, go knock on their door. Otherwise ignore this completely.
explanation.consequence: You send $1,450 to a scammer's account. The 'payment' you received (if any) gets reversed as fraudulent.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When someone asks you to forward money to a third party, it's almost always a scam. Legitimate mistakes are handled by the payment platform.

message.body: NOTICE: A refund of $89.99 has been issued to your CashDash account. Due to a processing error, $189.99 was deposited instead. Please return the $100 overage to @cashdash_adjustments to avoid account suspension. Ref #RF-20260409.

red_flags.label: Asks you to send money back

explanation.short: Fake 'official' account running an overpayment scam. Real companies handle refund errors internally — they never ask you to send money back via DM.
tells: The handle @cashdash_refunds_official is not a verified CashDash account
Companies fix overpayment errors by debiting your account, not asking you to manually send money
The threat of suspension creates pressure
The reference number is meaningless
explanation.safe_move: Log into CashDash directly and check your balance. Contact real support if anything looks off.
explanation.consequence: You send $100 to a scammer's account. There was no overpayment.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Companies never ask you to manually return money via DM. Overpayment corrections happen automatically through the platform.

message.body: Wrong number! I just sent $200 to you on QuikPay that was meant for my mom. Please send it back when you get a chance. Thanks!

explanation.short: Simple overpayment scam. The casual, friendly tone makes it seem harmless, but you should never send money based on a stranger's text.
tells: Check your QuikPay — you likely received nothing
If they really sent it by mistake, they can contact QuikPay to reverse it
The mention of 'my mom' is designed to make you feel bad
Casual tone ('when you get a chance') is intentionally disarming
explanation.safe_move: Check your QuikPay balance. If you didn't receive anything, ignore the message entirely.
explanation.consequence: You send $200 to a scammer. There was no accidental payment.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify in the payment app itself before taking any action based on a text from an unknown number.

message.body: ZapSend Alert: A payment of $3,000.00 was sent to your account. The sender has filed a dispute. To avoid a freeze on your account, please transfer $3,000.00 to @ZS-Disputes within 2 hours. Case #ZS-44019.

red_flags.label: Sent from regular phone number, not official

explanation.short: This combines impersonation with urgency. ZapSend would never text you from a regular phone number or ask you to transfer money to resolve a dispute.
tells: Official alerts come from short codes or the app, not random numbers
Disputes are handled by the platform — they debit your account directly if needed
The 2-hour timer is a high-pressure tactic
The @ZS-Disputes account is controlled by the scammer
explanation.safe_move: Open ZapSend directly and check for any alerts. Contact their official support if concerned.
explanation.consequence: You send $3,000 to a scammer. The 'incoming payment' was fake or from a stolen account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Payment platforms resolve disputes through their own systems. They never ask you to send money to a separate account.

message.body: You received $32.00 from Sarah M. via QuikPay. No action needed. View in app: quikpay.com/activity

explanation.short: Standard payment notification from QuikPay's official short code. No money or action is requested.
tells: Sent from a short code (87225), not a random phone number
States 'No action needed'
Links to the service's own domain
Reasonable amount matching a real transaction
explanation.safe_move: Open QuikPay directly (not from the link) to verify the payment if you want confirmation.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring a real payment notification just means you won't see the funds until you check the app.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate payment apps notify you of received funds without asking you to do anything.

message.body: Hey, just sent you $45 on ZapSend for my half of dinner last night. Let me know if you got it! — Dave

green_flags.label: No money or action requested from you
Amount matches a typical dinner split

explanation.short: A friend confirming they sent you money for a shared expense. No action is requested from you.
tells: References a specific event (dinner last night) you can verify
Does not ask you to send any money
The amount is reasonable for a dinner split
Casual, natural language from someone you know
explanation.safe_move: Check your ZapSend app to confirm the payment arrived. Reply to your friend if it did.
explanation.consequence: If you ignore this, you might not realize a friend paid you back. No financial risk.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate payment messages reference specific shared expenses and don't ask you to send money back.

message.body: CashDash: Your $150.00 payment to Mike R. was completed. Ref: #CD-77291. If you didn't authorize this, open the app and tap 'Dispute' or call 1-800-555-0199.

green_flags.label: Directs you to the app, not a link
Confirming a transaction, not requesting action

explanation.short: A standard payment confirmation from CashDash's official short code. It confirms what you did and tells you how to dispute if needed.
tells: Official short code (22888) rather than a phone number
Directs you to the app or an official phone number — not a suspicious link
Confirms a completed transaction rather than asking for action
Provides a real support path through the app
explanation.safe_move: If you recognize the payment, no action needed. If you don't, open the CashDash app and use the dispute feature.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring a legitimate confirmation has no risk. If you didn't make the payment, you should dispute it through official channels.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Official payment confirmations come from short codes, reference the app for disputes, and never ask you to send money.

message.body: Hey! I just QuikPay'd you the $120 for the concert tickets. Should show up any minute. Thanks for grabbing those!

red_flags.label: No transaction receipt or confirmation number

explanation.short: A friend letting you know they sent payment for something specific. No action is requested from you.
tells: References a specific purchase you'd remember
Does not ask you to send any money
Friendly, natural language
No links, no urgency, no threats
explanation.safe_move: Check your QuikPay to confirm receipt. If the payment shows up, you're all set.
explanation.consequence: No risk. This is a normal friend-to-friend payment confirmation.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real payment messages from friends reference specific shared purchases and never ask you to send money back.

message.subject: Re: 2BR Apartment on Oak Street - $650/mo
message.body:
Hi,

Thank you for your interest in the apartment. The rent is $650/month which includes all utilities. I'm currently overseas on a missionary trip so I can't show the place in person, but I can send you photos and the keys via FastShip once you wire the first month's rent and security deposit ($1,300 total) via GlobalWire. The place is available immediately.

God bless,
David

red_flags.label: Rent seems too low for a 2BR
Cannot view the property before paying
Offers to mail keys — not how rentals work

explanation.short: Classic rental scam — overseas landlord, too-good pricing, and wire transfer demand. You'll never get keys or an apartment.
tells: $650 for a 2BR with utilities included is suspiciously cheap
Legitimate landlords don't mail keys via FastShip
Wire transfers (GlobalWire) are irreversible — scammers' preferred method
Being 'overseas' is an excuse to avoid showing the property
Religious references are used to build false trust
explanation.safe_move: Never wire money for a rental you haven't seen in person. Search for the listing on multiple sites — it may be copied from a real listing.
explanation.consequence: You wire $1,300 and never receive keys. The apartment may not even exist or belong to this person.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never pay for a rental without viewing it in person and verifying the landlord's identity and ownership.

message.subject: Application Approved! Secure Your Unit - 1BR Downtown $750/mo
message.body:
Congratulations! Your application for the 1BR at 455 Pine Ave has been pre-approved.

To secure this unit before other applicants, please submit your holding deposit of $900 within 24 hours via the link below:

https://homescout-deals.net/secure-deposit/unit-455

This deposit is fully refundable if you don't move forward.

HomeScout Listings Team

red_flags.label: Domain is homescout-deals.net, not the real site

explanation.short: Fake pre-approval from an impersonation of HomeScout. The lookalike domain and 24-hour pressure are designed to steal your deposit.
tells: The domain 'homescout-deals.net' is not the real HomeScout website
You never submitted an application
Legitimate landlords don't demand deposits within 24 hours sight-unseen
The 'fully refundable' promise is meaningless from a scammer
explanation.safe_move: Go to the real HomeScout website directly (not through this link) and search for the listing. If it exists, contact the actual landlord.
explanation.consequence: You pay $900 to a fake site. The listing may not exist, and you'll never get the 'refundable' deposit back.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify rental listings on the official platform. Never pay deposits through links in unsolicited emails.

message.body: Hi! We saw you were looking for apartments on RentEasy. We manage several buildings in your area and have a stunning 2BR available for $800/mo — way below market because the owner wants it rented ASAP. We can hold it for you with a $500 deposit via CashLink. Want photos?

red_flags.label: Deposit via CashLink (not standard for rentals)
No specific address or building name

explanation.short: Unsolicited DM offering a too-good-to-be-true rental with a CashLink deposit. Legitimate property management companies don't solicit renters via DM or accept deposits through payment apps.
tells: Legitimate property managers don't cold-DM people
No specific address given — just 'in your area'
CashLink is not a standard method for rental deposits
Below-market pricing is a lure to get you to pay quickly
explanation.safe_move: Search for 'Property Solutions LLC' independently. Ask for the property address and verify ownership through public records before paying anything.
explanation.consequence: You send $500 via CashLink and the 'property manager' disappears. CashLink payments are hard to reverse.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate property managers use business accounts and standard payment methods. They don't cold-message renters on social media.

message.subject: Your RentEasy Application - Next Steps
message.body:
Dear Applicant,

Thank you for your application through RentEasy for the unit at 1220 Maple Drive, Unit 3A.

Due to high demand, we require a refundable application processing fee of $150 and a good-faith deposit of $500 before we can schedule your in-person tour. This ensures serious applicants only.

Please complete payment here: thomaswright-properties.com/apply/payment

Once received, we'll schedule your tour within 48 hours.

Best regards,
Thomas & Wright Property Group
Licensed Property Management

red_flags.label: Company name sounds professional but may be fake
Must pay before you can even tour

explanation.short: Professional-looking email, but requiring payment before a tour is a major red flag. Legitimate property managers let you view units before collecting deposits.
tells: $150 'processing fee' plus $500 deposit before you even see the unit is not standard
Legitimate property managers show you the unit first
The professional formatting is designed to build false trust
Claiming 'high demand' creates artificial urgency
explanation.safe_move: Search for Thomas & Wright Property Group independently. Verify the listing on RentEasy directly. Never pay before touring a property.
explanation.consequence: You pay $650 and either never get a tour scheduled, or you tour a property this company doesn't actually manage.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate property managers show properties before collecting deposits. Application fees should be modest ($25-75) and never bundled with large deposits upfront.

message.subject: Re: 3BR House on Elm Street - Lease Available
message.body:
Hi,

Great news — I spoke with the homeowner and they've agreed to your requested move-in date.

Here's the summary:
- 3BR/2BA, 1,400 sq ft at 782 Elm Street
- $1,650/mo (first + last + $1,650 security = $4,950 due at signing)
- Pet-friendly, washer/dryer in-unit
- Lease starts May 1st

I've attached the lease agreement for your review. To hold the unit, we need the security deposit ($1,650) wired to the homeowner's escrow account by Friday. I've included the wire instructions in the attached PDF.

Let me know if you have questions!

Sarah Chen
Premier Realty Group
License #RE-20241887

red_flags.label: PDF attachment could contain malware or fake wire details

explanation.short: Highly polished scam impersonating a realtor. The specific details build trust, but the wire transfer to a 'homeowner's escrow account' and PDF attachment are the giveaways.
tells: Legitimate rental deposits go through the property management company, not wired to a 'homeowner's escrow'
The license number can be verified with your state's real estate commission
Wire transfers are irreversible — legitimate agents accept checks or secure online payments
The PDF may contain fake wire instructions routing money to a scammer
Professional details (sq ft, license number) are easy to fabricate
explanation.safe_move: Verify the agent's license number with your state's real estate commission. Visit the property in person. Never wire deposit money.
explanation.consequence: You wire $1,650 to a scammer's account. The 'realtor' may be impersonating a real person whose identity was stolen.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify real estate licenses independently. Legitimate deposits use traceable payment methods, never wire transfers to personal accounts.

message.body: Hey, saw your post looking for a roommate. I actually have a spare room in my place — $400/mo utilities included. It's furnished too. Just need first month upfront via ZapSend to hold it. Can send pics if you're interested.

red_flags.label: Very cheap furnished room with utilities
Wants payment before you see the room
ZapSend is not standard for rental deposits

explanation.short: Furnished room at $400 with utilities included is a lure. Asking for payment via ZapSend before viewing is the classic sign.
tells: The price is unrealistically low for a furnished room with utilities
Legitimate roommates meet you in person first
Payment app deposits offer no renter protection
Responding to your post makes it feel natural, but it's a common tactic
explanation.safe_move: Insist on seeing the room in person before paying anything. Meet the person in the shared space, not a public location.
explanation.consequence: You send $400 and either the room doesn't exist or it's not this person's to rent.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always visit a rental in person and verify the person actually lives there before sending any money.

red_flags.label: renteasy-secure.co is not the real RentEasy domain
Claims other applicants to create urgency

explanation.short: Fake RentEasy email using a lookalike domain. The 'verified listing' and competing applicants are social engineering to rush your payment.
tells: The domain 'renteasy-secure.co' is not RentEasy's real domain
Legitimate platforms show listings in the app, not via unsolicited email links
Competition claims ('4 other applicants') create artificial urgency
Application fees should be paid through the official platform, not a link in an email
explanation.safe_move: Go to the real RentEasy website and search for this listing directly. Never click links in unsolicited listing emails.
explanation.consequence: You submit $75 and personal information (for the 'background check') to a phishing site. Your data and money are both stolen.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always access rental platforms directly through their official website or app. Check that the domain matches exactly.

message.subject: Re: Apartment at 510 River Road - Available for Immediate Move-in
message.body:
Hello,

Thank you for your interest. I own the property at 510 River Road but I'm currently working in London for 6 months. My previous tenant just moved out.

I've listed it at $950/month because I'd rather have a reliable tenant quickly than leave it empty. I can offer a virtual tour via video call and will send the keys by express courier once the lease is signed and first month + deposit ($1,900) is received.

I accept payment via bank transfer or cryptocurrency for convenience. Please send a copy of your ID so I can run a background check on my end.

Best,
James Okafor

red_flags.label: Asks for copy of your ID

explanation.short: Multiple red flags: overseas landlord, couriered keys, crypto payment, and requesting your ID. This person likely doesn't own the property.
tells: Overseas landlord who can't show the property is the #1 rental scam pattern
Bank transfers and cryptocurrency are irreversible
Sending your ID enables identity theft
Couriering keys is not how legitimate rentals work
The 'virtual tour' may use photos stolen from a real listing
explanation.safe_move: Look up 510 River Road in public property records to find the real owner. Never send ID or money to an unverified landlord.
explanation.consequence: You lose $1,900 and your personal ID is used for identity theft. The apartment is not this person's to rent.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verify property ownership through public records. Never send money or ID to someone you haven't met who can't show you the property in person.

message.subject: Your Showing Confirmation - 220 Birch Lane, Unit 5B
message.body:
Hi,

This confirms your in-person showing for the apartment at 220 Birch Lane, Unit 5B:

Our leasing agent Maria will meet you there. If you need to reschedule, reply to this email or call (555) 734-0088.

Greenfield Property Management
1450 Commerce Blvd, Suite 200

Gives a specific address to visit

green_flags.label: Provides phone number and physical address

No money requested at this stage
Named leasing agent and company with a physical address
Photo ID for building access is a normal security requirement

explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate property managers invite you to see the property in person, provide their contact info, and don't ask for money before a showing.

message.body: Hi! Thanks for reaching out about the 1BR at our Riverside location. We have openings for tours this Thursday at 10 AM or Friday at 3 PM. Would either work for you? Our leasing office is in the building lobby. No fees or deposits needed for the tour — just come check it out! Feel free to call us at (555) 291-4400 if you prefer to schedule by phone.

red_flags.label: Received via DM rather than email

green_flags.label: Inviting you to tour in person

explanation.short: This is a response to your inquiry, offering an in-person tour with no payment required. This is exactly how legitimate leasing offices operate.
tells: They're responding to your outreach, not cold-messaging you
Explicitly says no fees or deposits for the tour
Provides a phone number as an alternative contact method
Invites you to visit the physical leasing office
explanation.safe_move: Schedule the tour. You can verify the property by searching for Apex Living Communities and their Riverside location.

explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate leasing offices respond to your inquiries, invite you to tour for free, and provide multiple ways to contact them.

message.body: SwiftShip: Your package could not be delivered. Update your address to reschedule: swiftship-redeliver.com/update

red_flags.label: Sent from a regular phone number
No tracking number or package details
You may not be expecting a package

explanation.short: Fake delivery notification using a lookalike domain. Real carriers use their official domain and include tracking numbers.
tells: The domain 'swiftship-redeliver.com' is not SwiftShip's real website
No tracking number or package description provided
Legitimate carriers text from short codes, not random phone numbers
The link likely leads to a phishing site to steal your personal info
explanation.safe_move: Go to SwiftShip's official website and enter any tracking numbers you have. Never click links in delivery texts.
explanation.consequence: The link leads to a fake site that harvests your address, name, and possibly payment information.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate delivery notifications include tracking numbers and come from official short codes. Always check the carrier's real website.

message.body: ParcelDash Notice: A $2.99 redelivery fee is required for your package. Pay now to avoid return to sender: parceldash-fee.com/pay

explanation.short: The small fee is the bait — the real goal is to capture your credit card information on the fake payment site.
tells: Delivery companies don't charge redelivery fees via text message
The domain is not ParcelDash's official site
The $2.99 fee seems small but the phishing site will steal your full card details
No tracking number or delivery details provided
explanation.safe_move: Check ParcelDash's real website with your tracking number. Legitimate redelivery can be scheduled for free.
explanation.consequence: You enter your credit card on a phishing site. The scammers now have your full payment information.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Delivery services don't charge redelivery fees via text links. The small amount is designed to make you think 'it's only $3, why not?'

message.subject: ACTION REQUIRED: Customs Hold on Your International Shipment
message.body:
Dear Customer,

Your international shipment (AWB #SW-7729041) is being held at customs and requires a clearance fee of $18.50 before it can be released for delivery.

Failure to pay within 48 hours will result in the package being returned to the sender or destroyed per customs regulations.

Pay customs fee: https://swiftship-intl.net/customs/clearance

SwiftShip International Logistics
Customs & Import Division

explanation.short: Fake customs hold notice. While customs fees are real, this email uses a fake domain and urgency to phish your payment details.
tells: The domain 'swiftship-intl.net' is not SwiftShip's real domain
Real customs fees are paid through the carrier's official website or at delivery
The threat of destruction adds false urgency
The AWB number looks real but is fabricated
explanation.safe_move: Go to SwiftShip's official website and look up the tracking number. If there's a real customs hold, you'll see it there.
explanation.consequence: You enter payment information on a phishing site, giving scammers your credit card or bank details.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Customs fees do exist, but they're paid through official carrier channels. Always verify on the carrier's real website.

message.body: QuikPost driver here - I'm at your address but nobody's home. I have a package that requires a signature. Reply YES to authorize release without signature, or click here to reschedule: qpost-schedule.com/delivery

red_flags.label: Claims to be a delivery driver
Asks you to reply YES (confirms your number is active)
Driver wouldn't text from personal number

explanation.short: Pretending to be a delivery driver adds believability, but drivers don't text from personal numbers or offer to waive signatures via text.
tells: Delivery drivers use the carrier's app notification system, not personal texts
Replying YES confirms your number is active and leads to more scam texts
The rescheduling link goes to a phishing domain
Signature-required packages have specific procedures that don't involve texting
explanation.safe_move: Check QuikPost's official tracking. If you're expecting a signature-required package, call QuikPost customer service.
explanation.consequence: Replying YES flags your number as active for more scams. The link steals personal or payment info.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Delivery drivers communicate through their carrier's official app. They don't text you from personal numbers or ask you to click links.

message.subject: Delivery Exception: Package Rerouted - Confirmation Needed
message.body:
Dear Valued Customer,

Your package (Tracking: PD-48820176-US) has been rerouted due to an address discrepancy flagged by our automated system.

Current status: Held at local distribution center
Original address: [partially redacted for security]
Issue: ZIP code mismatch

To confirm your correct delivery address and release the package, please verify your identity:
https://parceldash-tracking.com/verify/PD-48820176

Note: You may be asked to confirm the last 4 digits of the payment method used for the original purchase as an additional security measure.

This must be resolved within 3 business days or the package will be returned.

ParcelDash Customer Experience Team
Ref: #PD-CE-20260409-7741

red_flags.label: Asks for identity and payment verification
Technical language (ZIP code mismatch, automated system) adds false legitimacy

explanation.short: Sophisticated phishing that mimics a real delivery exception workflow. The partial address redaction and technical language are designed to make it feel like a genuine security process, but it's harvesting your identity and payment info.
tells: The domain 'parceldash-tracking.com' is not ParcelDash's official domain
Asking for 'last 4 digits of payment method' is a pretext to capture financial data
Partially redacting your address is a trick — they don't actually have it
Real delivery exceptions are handled by updating your address on the official site
Multiple reference numbers add false legitimacy
explanation.safe_move: Go to ParcelDash's official website and enter the tracking number. If there's a real issue, you'll see it there.
explanation.consequence: You provide your full address, identity details, and partial payment information to a phishing site. This enables identity theft and financial fraud.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: The more 'official' and 'secure' an email looks, the more carefully you should verify the sender's domain. Real companies never ask for payment details to deliver a package.

message.body: SwiftShip: Delivery attempted 4/9. Package being held for 5 days then returned. Schedule free redelivery: swftshp-redeliver.com/pkg/88291

red_flags.label: Regular phone number, not short code

explanation.short: The misspelled domain (swftshp) is subtle but reveals this as phishing. Real SwiftShip texts come from short codes and link to swiftship.com.
tells: 'swftshp-redeliver.com' drops vowels to look like an abbreviation but is a fake domain
Sent from a 10-digit number instead of an official short code
No tracking number or package description included
The 5-day hold creates just enough urgency without seeming extreme
explanation.safe_move: Go to swiftship.com directly and check any pending deliveries. Don't click the link.
explanation.consequence: The link leads to a phishing site designed to harvest your address, identity, or payment information.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always look carefully at domains in delivery texts. Misspelled or abbreviated domains are a telltale sign of phishing.

message.body: Your QuikPost package is waiting for pickup at a nearby access point. A $4.95 storage fee applies after 24hrs. Pay and schedule pickup: quikpost-pickup.net/fees

explanation.short: Fake storage fee scam. The small charge gets you to enter payment details on a phishing site.
tells: Delivery access points don't typically charge storage fees within 24 hours
The domain is not QuikPost's official website
The small fee makes the payment seem low-risk, but the real target is your card details
Sent from a regular number, not QuikPost's official messaging
explanation.safe_move: Check QuikPost's official site or app for any packages awaiting pickup.
explanation.consequence: Your credit card details are captured by the phishing site.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Carrier fees are handled through official channels, not via text links. Small fees are bait for stealing payment info.

message.subject: Import Duty Payment Required - Package #SW-INT-60291

The duty math is specific to seem legitimate, but anyone can calculate 12% of $285
Real carriers notify you through their official tracking system
Storage fees threat adds pressure to pay quickly
explanation.safe_move: Check SwiftShip's official website with the tracking number. Real import duties are handled through the carrier's official customs clearance process.
explanation.consequence: You submit payment details to a phishing site. Your financial information is stolen.

message.body: SwiftShip: Your package was delivered at 2:14 PM to your front door. Tracking #SW-4492017. View photo: swiftship.com/track/SW-4492017

explanation.short: Standard delivery confirmation from SwiftShip's official short code with a real tracking number and official domain link.
tells: Sent from short code 56789, not a random phone number
Includes a specific tracking number
Links to swiftship.com — the official domain
No payment, personal info, or action requested
explanation.safe_move: Check your front door for the package. You can verify on swiftship.com if you'd like to see the delivery photo.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring this just means you might not retrieve your package promptly. No risk.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate delivery notifications come from short codes, include tracking numbers, and link to official domains.

message.subject: Your Package is Ready for Pickup
message.body:
Hi,

Your ParcelDash package is ready for pickup at:

ParcelDash Access Point
Corner Market, 450 Main Street
Pickup hours: Mon-Sat 9 AM - 8 PM

Tracking: PD-33810294
Pickup deadline: April 16, 2026

Bring a photo ID to collect your package. No fees apply.

Track your package: parceldash.com/track

ParcelDash Customer Service

green_flags.label: Email from parceldash.com and links to parceldash.com
Provides specific pickup location and hours

explanation.short: Legitimate pickup notification from ParcelDash. It provides specific details, uses the official domain, and explicitly says no fees.
tells: Email is from parceldash.com — the official domain
Specific tracking number you can verify
Explicitly states 'No fees apply'
Provides a real pickup location with hours
Photo ID requirement is standard for package pickup
explanation.safe_move: Verify the tracking number on parceldash.com, then pick up your package at the listed location.
explanation.consequence: If you don't pick it up by April 16, it may be returned to the sender.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate pickup notices come from official domains, include tracking numbers, provide specific locations, and don't charge unexpected fees.

message.body: QuikPost: Your package #QP-8817204 is out for delivery today. Estimated arrival: 1:00 PM - 5:00 PM. Track: quikpost.com/track

explanation.short: Standard out-for-delivery notification from QuikPost's official short code. Informational only, with no payment or action requested.
tells: Official short code (44567)
Specific tracking number included
Links to quikpost.com — the real domain
A delivery window of 1-5 PM is normal
No money, links to phishing sites, or personal info requested
explanation.safe_move: You can track your package at quikpost.com if you want a more precise delivery time.
explanation.consequence: No risk. This is a helpful notification about an incoming delivery.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real delivery updates come from short codes, include tracking numbers, and never ask for payment or personal information.

message.subject: Voicemail — 11:47 PM
message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"Hey honey, it's Mom. I'm in trouble — I got into a fender bender and they're saying I need to pay the other driver $2,000 right now or they're calling the police. I'm so scared. Can you please send it to this CashDash? I'll explain when I get home. Please hurry."

[End of voicemail — 38 seconds]

red_flags.label: Unusual demand (pay at scene via app)

explanation.short: AI-cloned voice impersonating a family member. Real accidents are never settled via payment apps at the scene.
tells: No legitimate accident requires immediate payment app transfer
Late-night timing exploits panic
No verifiable details (location, other driver)
AI can clone anyone's voice from social media clips
explanation.safe_move: Hang up and call your mom on her known number. Never send money based on a voicemail alone.
explanation.consequence: You send $2,000 to a scammer's account. The accident never happened.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any urgent call asking for money — even from someone who sounds like family — deserves a callback on their real number.

message.subject: Voicemail — 6:12 PM
message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"Hey, it's David. I need you to handle something urgently before end of day. We have a vendor payment that got stuck and I need you to wire $4,500 to the account I'm going to text you. I'm stuck in a board meeting and can't do it myself. Don't email me about it — just get it done and I'll approve the paperwork tomorrow. Thanks."

[End of voicemail — 42 seconds]

red_flags.label: Says not to email or verify

explanation.short: Classic CEO fraud using AI-cloned voice. Legitimate bosses don't ask employees to wire money outside normal processes.
tells: Asks you to skip verification channels (don't email me)
Wire transfer request outside normal procedures
Artificial urgency — 'before end of day'
Promise to handle paperwork later is a stalling tactic
explanation.safe_move: Call your boss directly on a number you already have. Verify with your finance team using your standard process.
explanation.consequence: You wire $4,500 to a fraudulent account. Your boss never made the request.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any request to send money that specifically tells you NOT to verify through other channels is a major red flag.

message.subject: Voicemail — 2:33 AM
message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"Grandma, it's Tyler. Please don't tell Mom and Dad. I got arrested — I was at a party and they're saying I had something I shouldn't have. The lawyer says I can get out tonight if I post $3,000 bail. Can you go to a CashWire and send it? I'll pay you back, I promise. Please, Grandma, I'm really scared."

[End of voicemail — 45 seconds]

red_flags.label: Asks to keep it secret from family

explanation.short: The 'grandparent scam' uses AI voice cloning to impersonate a grandchild in trouble. Real bail is never paid via CashWire.
tells: Bail is posted through the court, not via wire transfer services
Asking you to keep it secret prevents you from verifying the story
2 AM timing exploits confusion and panic
Scammers harvest grandchildren's names and voices from social media
explanation.safe_move: Hang up. Call your grandchild or their parents on a known number. If someone is truly arrested, you can verify with the local police department directly.
explanation.consequence: You send $3,000 via CashWire to a scammer. The money is unrecoverable once picked up.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Anyone who asks you to send money AND keep it secret is trying to prevent you from discovering the truth.

message.subject: Voicemail — 9:18 AM
message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"This is an urgent message from the CrestBank fraud prevention department. We've detected unauthorized transactions on your account ending in 4821. To secure your account immediately, please call us back at 800-555-0412 and have your full account number, Federal Benefits number, and online banking PIN ready for verification. Do not use your account until this is resolved."

[End of voicemail — 34 seconds]

red_flags.label: Asks to call back a provided number

explanation.short: Banks never ask for your full SSN, PIN, or account number over the phone. This voicemail routes you to a scammer's call center.
tells: Banks already have your account details — they never ask you to recite them
Requesting your SSN and PIN together is a massive red flag
The callback number routes to a scam operation, not the real bank
Real fraud alerts give you a reference number to use when YOU call the bank's known number
explanation.safe_move: Ignore the callback number. Call the number printed on the back of your bank card to check for real fraud alerts.
explanation.consequence: Calling back gives scammers your SSN, account number, and PIN — enough for full account takeover and identity theft.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Your bank already knows your account details. Any caller asking YOU to provide them is not your bank.

message.subject: Voicemail — 4:45 PM
message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"Dad! Oh my god, Dad, my car broke down on the highway and the tow truck driver says it's $800 cash only. I left my wallet at home. Can you ZapPay me $800 right now? I'm stranded and it's getting dark. The driver says he's leaving in 10 minutes if I can't pay. Please hurry!"

[End of voicemail — 29 seconds]
