explanation.short: Scammers clone a family member's voice and create a fake emergency. Legitimate tow services accept credit cards and don't impose 10-minute ultimatums.
tells: Tow companies accept credit cards and send invoices
The 10-minute deadline is designed to prevent you from thinking
No details about location or tow company name
Payment app request for a supposed in-person service is suspicious
explanation.safe_move: Call your daughter on her saved number. If she's truly stranded, you can call a tow service directly.
explanation.consequence: You send $800 to a scammer's payment app account. Your daughter was never stranded.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Tight deadlines in emergency requests exist to stop you from verifying. Always call back on a known number first.

message.subject: Voicemail — 3:20 PM
message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"Hello, this is Officer Daniels with the county sheriff's office. We have a warrant for your arrest due to unpaid federal taxes. You must call us back immediately at this number to arrange payment and avoid being taken into custody today. This is your final notice."

[End of voicemail — 22 seconds]

explanation.short: Law enforcement and the tax agency never call to demand immediate payment or threaten arrest by voicemail.
tells: Real arrest warrants are served in person, not by voicemail
Tax agencies send official letters by mail first
Police don't accept payments to cancel warrants
'Final notice' by phone is always a scam tactic
explanation.safe_move: Delete the voicemail. If concerned, call your local sheriff's office using the number from their official website.
explanation.consequence: Calling back leads to a fake officer who demands gift cards or wire transfers to 'clear the warrant.'
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government agencies communicate through official mail. Anyone threatening arrest over the phone and demanding payment is a scammer.

message.subject: Voicemail — 1:05 PM
message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"Hey babe, I'm at the hospital — I slipped at work and they think my wrist is broken. They're saying my insurance card is expired and I need to pay $1,500 upfront before they'll do the X-ray. Can you QuickSend me the money? My phone is almost dead so just send it, don't try to call back. Love you."

[End of voicemail — 31 seconds]

explanation.short: Hospitals treat emergencies regardless of insurance status and never demand payment app transfers before providing care.
tells: Emergency rooms are legally required to treat patients (EMTALA law)
Hospitals bill afterwards — they don't demand payment apps
Telling you not to call back prevents verification
'Phone almost dead' is a convenient excuse to block follow-up
explanation.safe_move: Call your husband on his known number. If no answer, call his workplace or the hospital directly.
explanation.consequence: You send $1,500 to a scammer. Your husband is fine and never called.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Anyone who asks for money AND tells you not to call back is trying to prevent you from discovering the lie.

message.subject: Voicemail — 10:30 PM
message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"Hey Dad, it's Jake. Listen, I know this is weird but I got into a situation. I was rear-ended and the other driver got hurt. My lawyer — his name is Mr. Corbin — is going to call you in a few minutes. He'll explain everything. I just need you to work with him on the bail thing. Please don't post about this or tell anyone yet, it could hurt my case. I love you, Dad."

[End of voicemail — 48 seconds]

red_flags.label: Hands off to a 'lawyer' who will call
Asks you to keep it secret
Primes you for a follow-up scam call

explanation.short: This is a sophisticated two-stage scam. The first call impersonates your son to prime you, then an accomplice calls as the 'lawyer' to extract money.
tells: The handoff to a 'lawyer' is a classic escalation tactic — it adds fake authority
Real lawyers don't cold-call family members to arrange bail payments
Asking you not to tell anyone prevents verification
The setup is designed so you're expecting and trusting the second call
explanation.safe_move: Call your son immediately on his known number. Do not engage with anyone who calls claiming to be his lawyer.
explanation.consequence: The 'lawyer' calls and convinces you to wire thousands for bail, legal fees, or bond. None of it is real.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Two-stage scams are especially dangerous because the first call makes the second seem expected and legitimate. Always verify independently.

message.subject: Voicemail — 10:15 AM
message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"Hi, this is Kelly calling from Maplewood Family Medicine to confirm your appointment with Dr. Patel on Thursday, April 10th at 2:30 PM. If you need to reschedule, please call us back at 847-555-0190 during office hours. Thank you!"

[End of voicemail — 18 seconds]

explanation.short: This is a standard medical appointment confirmation. The office provides their name, doctor, date, and a callback number during business hours.
tells: Specific appointment details (doctor name, date, time) match a real appointment
No request for personal information or payment
Callback number matches the office's known number
No urgency or pressure — just asks you to call during office hours if needed
explanation.safe_move: If you recognize the appointment, no action needed. If unsure, call the office back using the number from your own records.
explanation.consequence: Missing this confirmation might result in a missed appointment.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate appointment reminders give specific details, don't ask for personal info, and are happy for you to call back at your convenience.

message.subject: Voicemail — 11:02 AM
message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"This is an automated message from GreenLeaf Pharmacy. Your prescription is ready for pickup. If you have questions, press 0 to speak with the pharmacist or call us at 630-555-0244. Goodbye."

[End of voicemail — 14 seconds]

explanation.short: A standard automated pharmacy notification. It gives no personal details over the phone (for privacy) and simply says the prescription is ready.
tells: Automated system — consistent with pharmacy notification systems
No request for payment, personal info, or login credentials
Short, professional message with no pressure
Callback number provided for questions
explanation.safe_move: Pick up your prescription at the pharmacy. If you weren't expecting a refill, call the pharmacy to confirm.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring this means your prescription sits at the pharmacy uncollected.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate automated pharmacy messages are brief, don't ask for information, and match a prescription you're expecting.

green_flags.label: No personal info or payment requested

message.subject: Voicemail — 3:48 PM
message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"Hey, it's Priya. Just wanted to follow up on the marketing sync from this morning — I sent you the updated slide deck on Slack but wanted to make sure you saw it before tomorrow's presentation. No rush, just give me a shout when you get a chance. Talk later!"

[End of voicemail — 21 seconds]

explanation.short: A normal coworker follow-up about a meeting and shared work documents. No requests for money, info, or urgent action.
tells: References a specific, verifiable meeting that occurred
Points you to a legitimate work channel (Slack) you already use
No urgency — says 'no rush'
No request for money, credentials, or personal information
explanation.safe_move: Check Slack for the slide deck and respond to your coworker as normal.
explanation.consequence: Missing this means you might not review the slides before the presentation.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate work voicemails reference specific events you can verify and direct you to channels you already use.

green_flags.label: No requests for money or info

message.subject: Voicemail — 7:55 PM
message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"Hey, it's Emma. I'm at the airport and they won't let me board — there's some kind of issue with my ticket and they're saying I owe $650 for the rebooking fee. My cards aren't working for some reason. Can you QuickSend me $650? My flight leaves in 20 minutes and I'll miss my connecting flight if I don't get on. I'll pay you back tonight, I promise."

[End of voicemail — 33 seconds]

red_flags.label: Airlines don't accept payment app transfers

explanation.short: Airlines accept credit cards, debit cards, and their own payment systems — never payment app transfers from third parties.
tells: Airlines have their own payment systems at the gate
A 20-minute deadline prevents you from thinking clearly
If her cards aren't working, a payment app wouldn't solve the airline's payment issue
Voice cloning makes this sound like your real sister
explanation.safe_move: Call your sister on her saved number. If she's really stuck, she can use the airline's customer service desk.
explanation.consequence: You send $650 to a scammer's account. Your sister is not at any airport.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Airlines never accept peer-to-peer payment apps. Any scenario where someone at an airport needs you to send money via app is fabricated.

message.body:
🎉 CONGRATULATIONS! You've been randomly selected as our weekly winner! You've won a $10,000 GramRewards cash prize! 💰

To claim your prize, click the link below and enter your shipping address and payment details for the $4.99 processing fee:

👉 gramrewards-claims.net/winner

Hurry — you have 24 hours to claim or the prize goes to the next person!

red_flags.label: Prize you never entered to win

explanation.short: You can't win a contest you never entered. Legitimate prizes never require a 'processing fee' to claim.
tells: You never entered any GramRewards giveaway
Paying money to receive money is the hallmark of a scam
The 24-hour deadline is artificial pressure
The link goes to an unofficial domain designed to harvest your payment info
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and block the account. You cannot win a contest you never entered.
explanation.consequence: Clicking the link and entering payment details gives scammers your credit card number. There is no $10,000 prize.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: If you didn't enter a contest, you didn't win a contest. Legitimate prizes never charge fees to collect.

message.body: SweepWin Alert: Your number was selected in our monthly drawing! You've won a FREE iPhone 16 Pro! Claim now at sweepwin-prizes.com/claim before midnight tonight. Reply STOP to opt out.

explanation.short: Nobody gives away $1,000+ phones via random text messages. This link leads to a data-harvesting page.
tells: You never entered a SweepWin drawing
Free flagship phones don't get distributed by text
The midnight deadline is pressure to act without thinking
The claim URL is designed to steal personal information
explanation.safe_move: Delete the text. Block the number.
explanation.consequence: The claim page asks for your address, SSN, and credit card for 'shipping.' Your identity and card get stolen.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No legitimate company texts random people to give away expensive devices. If it sounds too good to be true, it is.

message.subject: Official Notification: You Have Won €75,000!
message.body:
Dear Winner,

Your email address was selected in the LuckyDraw International Sweepstakes (Ref: LD-2026-44819). You have won €75,000.00!

To process your claim, please reply with:
- Full name
- Home address
- Phone number
- Date of birth
- Bank account details for direct deposit

A processing fee of €150 is required before disbursement. Payment instructions will follow.

Congratulations!
Dr. Hans Mueller
Claims Director, LuckyDraw International

explanation.short: Classic advance-fee lottery scam. You never entered this lottery, they want your identity and bank details, and you'd need to pay to 'claim' money.
tells: You never entered any LuckyDraw International Sweepstakes
Asking for DOB, address, and bank details is identity theft
Legitimate lotteries never charge processing fees
The generic 'Dear Winner' greeting means this was sent to thousands of people
explanation.safe_move: Delete the email. Never reply with personal information to unsolicited prize notifications.
explanation.consequence: Replying gives scammers everything they need for identity theft. The €150 fee is the first of many they'll request.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: You cannot win a lottery you never entered. Any 'prize' that asks for your bank details and a fee is a scam.

message.body:
Hi there! 👋 We noticed you've been a StreamBox subscriber for over a year — thank you for your loyalty!

As a thank-you, you've been selected for a $500 StreamBox credit. To claim it, just verify your account at:

streambox-loyalty.com/verify

You'll need to log in with your StreamBox credentials to apply the credit. This offer expires in 48 hours!

red_flags.label: Asks you to log in on external site
URL is not the real StreamBox domain

explanation.short: This DM sends you to a fake login page to steal your StreamBox credentials. Real loyalty rewards appear in your account, not via DMs.
tells: The URL is not streambox.com — it's a lookalike domain
Legitimate services apply credits automatically or notify you in-app
Logging in on a fake site gives scammers your username and password
The 48-hour timer creates artificial urgency
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to streambox.com or the StreamBox app. If there's a real credit, it will show in your account.
explanation.consequence: Entering your credentials on the fake site gives scammers access to your account, payment methods, and personal data.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate companies apply rewards to your account directly. Any DM asking you to 'verify' on an external link is phishing.

message.body: Congrats! Your mobile # has been selected for a $5,000 MegaMart gift card! You're 1 of 5 winners today. Claim here: megamart-giftcards.info/claim. Msg&data rates may apply.

explanation.short: Stores don't give away $5,000 gift cards via random text messages from unknown numbers.
tells: You never entered any MegaMart contest
$5,000 gift cards aren't distributed by SMS to random numbers
The .info domain is not affiliated with any real store
The 'Msg&data rates' disclaimer is added to look official
explanation.safe_move: Delete and block. If you shop at a real store, check their official app for any actual promotions.
explanation.consequence: The link leads to a form that harvests your personal and financial information.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Large-value prizes delivered by random texts from unknown numbers are always scams.

message.subject: You've been nominated for a Creator Bonus! 🎁
message.body:
Hi there,

Great news! Based on your engagement this quarter, you've been nominated for a $250 Creator Bonus from GramRewards.

To accept the bonus, we just need to verify your identity and set up your payout method:

1. Click here to verify: gramrewards-creators.com/verify
2. Upload a photo of your government ID
3. Enter your bank routing and account number

Bonuses not claimed within 7 days will be reassigned.

Best,
The GramRewards Creator Team

red_flags.label: Asks for photo of government ID

explanation.short: No legitimate creator program asks you to upload government ID and bank details via an email link. This is identity theft.
tells: Uploading government ID to an unverified site enables identity fraud
Bank routing and account numbers allow unauthorized withdrawals
The domain gramrewards-creators.com is not an official platform
Real creator programs handle payouts through their existing platform
explanation.safe_move: If you're in a real creator program, log in to the platform directly. Payouts are managed through the platform's settings, never via email links.
explanation.consequence: Uploading your ID and bank details gives scammers everything for identity theft and bank fraud.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never upload government ID or bank details through a link in an unsolicited email. Legitimate platforms manage payments in-app.

message.body:
Hey! I'm doing a giveaway with GramRewards to celebrate hitting 50K followers 🎉 I already picked you as one of my 10 winners! You get a $200 gift card 💳

Just DM @GramRewards_Official to claim yours — they'll walk you through it. Congrats!! 🥳

red_flags.label: Won a giveaway you never entered
Sends you to a separate account to 'claim'
Uses follower milestone to seem legitimate

explanation.short: This influencer giveaway scam sends you to a fake 'brand' account that will ask for personal info or a 'shipping fee' to claim your prize.
tells: You never entered this giveaway
Legitimate giveaways don't DM winners asking them to contact a separate account
The @GramRewards_Official account is a scam account that will request payment or info
The friendly tone and emojis are designed to lower your guard
explanation.safe_move: Ignore the DM. Legitimate giveaways announce winners publicly and ship prizes without additional steps.
explanation.consequence: The 'GramRewards_Official' account asks for a shipping fee, your address, and credit card number. There is no gift card.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real giveaways don't require you to DM a separate account. If you didn't enter, you didn't win.

message.body: LuckyDraw: Your $25 BeanBrew Coffee gift card is ready! You earned this through your recent purchase at participating stores. Redeem at luckydraw-rewards.com/redeem. Code: LD8847. Standard msg rates apply.

explanation.short: This looks more polished than typical scams — it uses a short code and small dollar amount to seem credible. But the redemption domain isn't BeanBrew's, and the 'recent purchase' is deliberately vague.
tells: The redemption URL is luckydraw-rewards.com, not beanbrew.com
'Recent purchase at participating stores' is intentionally vague so anyone might think it applies to them
Legitimate store rewards come from the store itself, not a third-party 'LuckyDraw'
The small $25 amount is designed to seem believable
explanation.safe_move: If you shop at BeanBrew, check their app or website directly. Don't click the link in the text.
explanation.consequence: The redemption page asks for your email login or credit card to 'verify' your identity, stealing your credentials.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Small-value rewards can be just as dangerous as big ones. Always verify rewards through the brand's official channels.

message.subject: You earned a $5 reward!
message.body:
Hi Jamie,

Thanks for being a BrightMart Rewards member! You've earned enough points for a $5 reward.

Your reward has been automatically applied to your account and will be deducted from your next in-store or online purchase.

No action needed — just shop and save!

View your rewards balance: brightmart.com/rewards

Happy shopping,
BrightMart Rewards Team

You're receiving this because you enrolled in BrightMart Rewards. Manage preferences: brightmart.com/email-preferences

explanation.short: This is a legitimate loyalty reward notification. The reward is auto-applied, no action or payment is needed, and all links go to the company's official domain.
tells: Uses your name, not 'Dear Customer' or 'Dear Winner'
Reward is automatically applied — no clicking, no claiming, no fee
All links go to brightmart.com, the official domain
Includes an unsubscribe/preferences link (required by law for marketing emails)
Small, reasonable reward amount consistent with loyalty programs
explanation.safe_move: No action needed. The reward will apply to your next purchase. You can verify by logging in to brightmart.com directly.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring this just means you might forget about your $5 reward.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate rewards are auto-applied, don't require fees, and come from the company's real domain. No action needed means no risk.

green_flags.label: Reward applied automatically — no action needed

message.body: PayNow: You received $12.50 cashback from your purchase at FreshGrocer on 4/7. It's been added to your PayNow balance. No action needed.

red_flags.label: Asks you to click or act

explanation.short: A legitimate cashback notification from a payment service. It references a specific purchase, the amount is reasonable, and no action or link is provided.
tells: References a specific merchant and date you can verify
Sent from a short code consistent with the service
No link, no request for action, no fee
Amount is consistent with typical cashback (small percentage of a grocery purchase)
explanation.safe_move: No action needed. You can verify the cashback by opening the PayNow app.
explanation.consequence: This is just a notification. Ignoring it has no negative effect.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate financial notifications reference specific transactions, come from official short codes, and don't ask you to do anything.

message.subject: Tax Document Required — Sweepstakes Winnings
message.body:
Dear Valued Winner,

Congratulations on your recent SweepWin prize of $2,500!

Per federal regulations, prizes over $600 require a completed W-9 form before disbursement. Please download and complete the attached W-9 form and return it to this email address along with:

- A clear photo of your driver's license (both sides)
- Your Federal Benefits Number (for tax reporting)

Tax Agency regulations require us to collect this information. Failure to respond within 10 business days will result in forfeiture of your winnings.

Compliance Department
SweepWin Promotions

explanation.short: This scam weaponizes real tax knowledge to seem credible. While prizes over $600 do require tax forms, this email is designed to steal your identity using the W-9 as a pretext.
tells: You never entered a SweepWin contest
While W-9 requirements are real, scammers exploit this knowledge to seem legitimate
Sending driver's license photos and SSN via email is never safe
The attachment may contain malware
Legitimate prize notifications come through the platform where you entered
explanation.safe_move: Delete the email. Never email photos of your ID or SSN. If you actually entered a sweepstakes, contact the organizer through their official website.
explanation.consequence: Sending your SSN, license photos, and completed W-9 gives scammers everything for complete identity theft — loans, tax fraud, and more.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Scammers use real regulatory knowledge to seem credible. Even if a tax requirement sounds legitimate, verify through official channels — never send sensitive documents via email.

message.body:
Thanks for your order today! ☕ Here's a code for $2 off your next drink: BREW2OFF. Just show it at the register next time you visit. Valid for 30 days at any BeanBrew location.

No purchase minimum. One per customer.

red_flags.label: Asks you to click a link

explanation.short: A standard promotional code from a coffee shop you just visited. The coupon is a simple in-store code — no links, no personal info requested.
tells: References a specific purchase you made today
It's just a coupon code to show at the register — nothing to click
Small, reasonable discount typical of customer retention
30-day validity is generous, not pressuring
No personal information or payment details requested
explanation.safe_move: Use the code on your next visit if you want. No risk involved in a simple coupon code shown at the register.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring it just means missing a $2 discount.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate promotional codes are simple, don't require clicking links or providing info, and come from businesses you actually patronize.

green_flags.label: Simple code, no link to click

message.subject: Great meeting you at the FinanceForward Summit!
message.body:
Hi Jamie,

It was great chatting with you at the FinanceForward Summit last week — your talk on compliance automation was really insightful. I mentioned I'd send over that case study on AI-driven risk assessment we discussed.

Here it is: nexwave-consulting.com/resources/risk-assessment-2026.pdf

I'd also love to set up a follow-up call to discuss how NexWave could help with the challenges you mentioned around vendor onboarding. Would sometime next week work?

Best regards,
Marcus Chen
Senior Consultant, NexWave Consulting
marcus.chen@nexwave-consulting.com | +1 (415) 555-0294

red_flags.label: References public event details you attended
You don't actually remember this person
Pushes for a follow-up call quickly

explanation.short: AI-crafted spear phishing that scraped public conference details to create a convincing backstory. The PDF link likely delivers malware.
tells: Conference speakers and attendees are listed publicly — scammers scrape this data
You don't actually remember meeting 'Marcus Chen'
The PDF link is hosted on their domain, not a neutral platform — it could deliver malware
The email is perfectly crafted with no typos, which is characteristic of AI-generated phishing
explanation.safe_move: Do not click the PDF link. If the conference was real, check the official attendee list or reach out to organizers to verify this person.
explanation.consequence: The PDF installs malware that can steal credentials, encrypt files, or give attackers persistent access to your system.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When someone references a public event to establish trust, verify their identity independently before clicking any links or attachments they send.

message.subject: Senior Product Manager — $185K-$210K, Remote
message.body:
Hi Jamie,

I'm a recruiter at BrightPath Talent and came across your profile on CareerLink. Your background in product management at mid-size SaaS companies caught my eye — particularly your experience with cross-functional team leadership.

I'm working with a well-funded Series C startup that's looking for a Senior PM to lead their platform team. The role is fully remote with a $185K-$210K base + equity.

I've put together a detailed role brief with the full job description, benefits, and company info:

👉 brightpath-talent.io/roles/sr-pm-2026-brief.pdf

Would love to hop on a quick call if this looks interesting. What does your availability look like this week?

Best,
Rachel Torres
Senior Recruiter, BrightPath Talent
brightpath-talent.io

red_flags.label: References details from your public profile
Job seems perfectly tailored to you
Recruiter and firm you've never heard of

explanation.short: AI-generated recruitment phishing that scraped your public CareerLink profile to craft a personalized pitch. The PDF likely contains malware.
tells: Your job title, company size, and skills are publicly available on career platforms
AI can generate perfectly tailored job descriptions in seconds
The salary range is intentionally attractive to prevent critical thinking
The PDF on an unknown domain is the attack vector
explanation.safe_move: Don't download the PDF. Look up BrightPath Talent independently. If the firm exists, contact them through their verified website to confirm Rachel works there.
explanation.consequence: The PDF installs an info-stealer that harvests passwords, financial data, and personal files from your computer.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a job opportunity seems perfectly tailored to you and comes from an unknown recruiter, verify the firm and person independently before downloading anything.

message.subject: Re: Q2 vendor review — follow-up
message.body:
Hi Jamie,

Just following up on our discussion from the vendor review meeting last Tuesday. I've compiled the comparative analysis you requested, including the pricing breakdowns for the three finalists.

I've shared the document here since the file was too large for our email system:

📎 stratton-docs.com/shared/vendor-analysis-q2-2026.xlsx

Let me know if the access link works. I set it to expire Friday so we can finalize before the board presentation.

Thanks,
David Park
Director of Procurement
Stratton Advisory Group

red_flags.label: 'Re:' subject implies prior conversation that didn't happen
File shared on external domain instead of company tools
Expiring link creates urgency to click

explanation.short: This sophisticated attack fakes an ongoing email thread with a 'colleague' to make the external file link seem expected. The xlsx file likely contains malware macros.
tells: The 'Re:' prefix makes it look like a reply to an existing conversation — but you never had this conversation
Real colleagues share files through your company's file-sharing system, not external domains
stratton-docs.com is not your company's domain
The Friday deadline creates subtle urgency to click before verifying
explanation.safe_move: Search your email for any prior thread with this person. Check with your actual procurement team. Never open xlsx files from unverified external links.
explanation.consequence: The Excel file contains malicious macros that install a backdoor on your system, potentially compromising your entire company network.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Emails that seem to continue a conversation you don't remember are a major red flag. Always verify with the supposed sender through a separate channel.

message.subject: Jamie, 3 executives from your industry viewed your profile
message.body:
Hi Jamie,

Your profile has been getting attention! This week, 3 executives from fintech companies viewed your profile, including a VP of Product at a Series D startup.

See who viewed your profile and their companies:

👉 linkedln-mail.com/profile-views/weekly-report

Profile views from senior leaders often lead to opportunities. Don't miss out on making a connection.

Best,
The ProConnect Team

You are receiving this email because you have a ProConnect account. Unsubscribe: linkedln-mail.com/unsubscribe

red_flags.label: Link leads to fake login page

explanation.short: The domain 'linkedln-mail.com' uses a lowercase L to mimic ProConnect. This phishing email steals your login credentials through a fake profile-views page.
tells: Look closely: 'linkedln' uses an L and N, not 'proconnect' — it's a lookalike domain
Real ProConnect profile-view emails come from @proconnect.com
The link leads to a fake login page that captures your password
The curiosity about who viewed your profile is designed to bypass caution
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to proconnect.com and check your profile views there. Never click links in emails that look like ProConnect.
explanation.consequence: Entering your credentials on the fake page gives attackers access to your ProConnect, connected email, and any accounts using the same password.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always check the sender domain character by character. Lookalike domains swap similar-looking letters (l/i, rn/m, 0/O) to trick you.

message.subject: Investment opportunity — loved your product demo
message.body:
Hi Jamie,

I caught your product demo at the TechBridge Showcase last month and was really impressed by your approach to compliance workflow automation. We've been tracking this space at Meridian Ventures and think there's strong product-market fit.

I'd love to explore whether there's a fit for our Series A program. We typically invest $2-5M with hands-on operational support.

I've put together a preliminary term sheet framework based on what I saw — obviously just a starting point for discussion:

📎 meridian-vc.co/deals/preliminary-terms-scamgym.pdf

Would you be free for a 30-minute Zoom this week? I can work around your schedule.

Best,
Anika Sharma
Partner, Meridian Ventures
meridian-vc.co

red_flags.label: References a public event you participated in
Unsolicited VC interest with perfect context
File named after your product — extra manipulation

explanation.short: Highly targeted AI-generated spear phishing using scraped event data. The personalized PDF filename is designed to make you feel the offer is real and specific to you.
tells: Event participation, demo details, and your product name are all publicly available
AI can generate convincing VC outreach in seconds
The personalized filename ('scamgym') is crafted to eliminate doubt
Real VCs use SignSecure, Notion, or SearchNet Docs for term sheets — not direct PDF downloads
explanation.safe_move: Do not download the PDF. Look up Meridian Ventures independently. Verify Anika Sharma exists on ProConnect. Reach out through verified channels.
explanation.consequence: The PDF exploits a vulnerability in your PDF reader or contains embedded malware that compromises your system.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Unsolicited investment interest that seems too perfectly aligned with your work deserves extra scrutiny. Verify the person and firm through independent channels before engaging.

message.subject: Urgent: Data breach notification — your records may be affected
message.body:
Dear Jamie,

I'm reaching out from ClearView Legal regarding the recent data breach at PulseHealth, where your account data may have been compromised. As outside counsel representing affected users, we're conducting outreach to potentially impacted individuals.

Based on our records, your email and associated personal information were included in the breach dataset. You may be entitled to compensation as part of the class action settlement.

To check your eligibility and file a claim, please complete the secure intake form:

🔒 clearview-legal.net/breach-claims/pulsehealth/intake

The claims window closes May 15, 2026.

Regards,
Tom Reynolds, Esq.
ClearView Legal Group
clearview-legal.net

red_flags.label: Intake form on unknown legal domain
Unsolicited contact from unknown law firm

explanation.short: This exploits real data breach news to phish victims. AI generates convincing legal language referencing actual incidents to harvest personal data through fake 'claim forms.'
tells: Legitimate breach notifications come from the breached company, not unknown law firms
Real class action settlements are filed through court-approved websites
The intake form will ask for SSN, address, and financial details
Scammers monitor breach news and target affected users within days
explanation.safe_move: Check if PulseHealth actually had a breach through their official website or news sources. Legitimate settlements are announced through court-approved channels.
explanation.consequence: The intake form harvests your SSN, date of birth, and financial details for identity theft — ironically using a data breach as the pretext.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When you hear about a data breach, get information from the affected company directly. Never provide personal information through unsolicited emails claiming to represent breach victims.

message.subject: Invitation to speak at AI & Society Symposium 2026
message.body:
Dear Jamie,

On behalf of the Westlake University Department of Computer Science, I'm writing to invite you to be a panelist at our upcoming AI & Society Symposium on May 22-23, 2026.

Your work on scam detection training tools has been referenced in several papers from our research group, and we believe your perspective would add tremendous value to our panel on "AI Safety in Consumer Applications."

We cover travel and accommodation. Please review the speaker packet with logistics and honorarium details:

📎 westlake-symposium.edu.co/speakers/packet-2026.pdf

We'd need your confirmation by April 20.

Warm regards,
Dr. Lisa Nakamura
Associate Professor, Computer Science
Westlake University

red_flags.label: Flattery about your work being 'referenced in papers'

explanation.short: The domain .edu.co is designed to look like a .edu university domain but is actually a commercial Colombian domain. The speaker packet PDF is the attack vector.
tells: '.edu.co' is not an American university domain — real ones end in '.edu'
Flattery about your work being cited in papers is designed to lower your guard
The PDF download is hosted on the suspicious domain
Legitimate academic invitations can be verified through the university's official website
explanation.safe_move: Search for 'Westlake University AI & Society Symposium' independently. Check the official university website (ending in .edu) to verify the event and speaker.
explanation.consequence: The PDF installs malware on your system. The symposium and professor may not exist.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Academic domains matter. A .edu.co domain is not the same as .edu. Always verify academic invitations through the institution's official website.

message.subject: Thanks for presenting at TechBridge — recording link
message.body:
Hi Jamie,

Thanks again for presenting at TechBridge Showcase last month! Your session on scam detection training had a great turnout.

As promised, here's the link to your session recording on our official VidShare channel:

https://youtube.com/watch?v=dQw4w9WgXcQ

We've also posted the slides you shared on our events page at techbridge.org/events/showcase-2026.

We'd love to have you back next year. Stay in touch!

Best,
Sarah Kim
Events Coordinator, TechBridge
techbridge.org

explanation.short: A legitimate follow-up from a conference organizer. The links go to VidShare and the organization's official domain, and the email references a real event you participated in.
tells: The email comes from the organization's official domain (techbridge.org)
Links point to VidShare (trusted platform) and the org's own events page
References specific details about your presentation that match reality
No request for personal information, payment, or downloads
Casual, appropriate tone consistent with event follow-ups
explanation.safe_move: Safe to click the VidShare link and check the events page. You can also verify by going to techbridge.org directly.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring this means missing the recording of your own presentation.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate conference follow-ups come from official domains, link to trusted platforms, and don't ask for anything from you.

green_flags.label: Links to VidShare and official site
References real event you participated in
No requests for info, payment, or downloads

message.subject: Senior PM role — would love to connect
message.body:
Hi Jamie,

I'm a recruiter at Pinnacle Search Partners and found your profile on ProConnect. I'm working with a client in the fintech space looking for a Senior PM.

Before sending any details, I wanted to check if you're open to new opportunities. If so, I'm happy to share more about the role on a quick call.

No attachments, no links — just wanted to gauge your interest first.

Best,
Jennifer Walsh
Senior Recruiter
Pinnacle Search Partners
jennifer.walsh@pinnacle-search.com
+1 (646) 555-0183
pinnacle-search.com

explanation.short: A professional recruiter outreach. Notably, the email contains no links or attachments — just an offer to have a conversation. This is how legitimate recruiters typically operate.
tells: No links, no attachments, no PDF downloads — just a conversation request
Doesn't oversell or provide salary ranges upfront to bait you
Professional signature with verifiable firm name and phone number
Asks about your interest before sharing details — respectful of your time
You can verify the firm and recruiter on ProConnect independently
explanation.safe_move: If interested, look up Pinnacle Search Partners and Jennifer Walsh on ProConnect to verify. Then respond or call the number provided.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring this means potentially missing a real job opportunity.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate recruiters lead with conversation, not attachments. No links and no urgency is a good sign. Always verify the person and firm independently.

green_flags.label: Asks about interest before sharing details
Full name, firm, phone number provided

message.subject: Your SwiftLedger invoice #SL-29441 is past due
message.body:
Hi Jamie,

I hope this finds you well. I'm following up on invoice #SL-29441 ($3,200) for the Q1 consulting services we provided. It was due March 31 and appears to still be outstanding.

I understand things slip through the cracks — just wanted to flag it before it goes to our collections team at the end of the week.

Here's the invoice for your reference: swiftledger-finance.com/invoices/SL-29441.pdf

If you've already processed payment, please disregard. Otherwise, can you let me know the expected payment date?

Thanks,
Kevin Okafor
Accounts Receivable
SwiftLedger Finance
swiftledger-finance.com

red_flags.label: Invoice for services you never used

explanation.short: AI-generated fake invoice scam with a polite, professional tone. The collections threat creates pressure to open the PDF without verifying whether you actually owe anything.
tells: You never used SwiftLedger Finance consulting services
The polite, professional tone is designed to make it seem like an honest mistake worth investigating
The 'end of week' collections deadline subtly pressures you to act quickly
The PDF invoice is the malware delivery mechanism
'If you've already paid, disregard' gives it plausible deniability
explanation.safe_move: Do not open the PDF. Check your actual accounts payable records. If you don't recognize the vendor, it's fake.
explanation.consequence: Opening the PDF installs malware. Some victims also pay the fake invoice, losing $3,200.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Unexpected invoices deserve verification through your own records, not by clicking the sender's links. Legitimate vendors are in your system already.

message.subject: TechBridge Monthly Newsletter — April 2026
message.body:
Hi Jamie,

Here's what's happening in the TechBridge community this month:

📅 Upcoming Events
- April 15: AI Ethics Roundtable (Virtual) — Register at techbridge.org/events
- April 22: Startup Office Hours with Meridian Partners

📝 From the Blog
- "Building Trust in AI Products" by Dr. Maya Patel
- "Lessons from Our 2025 Showcase Speakers"

Read more at techbridge.org/blog

👋 You're receiving this because you spoke at TechBridge Showcase 2026.
Unsubscribe: techbridge.org/unsubscribe

— The TechBridge Team

red_flags.label: Email you didn't explicitly subscribe to

explanation.short: A standard community newsletter from an organization you participated in. All links go to their official domain, it explains why you're receiving it, and includes an unsubscribe option.
tells: All links point to techbridge.org, the organization's official domain
Explains why you're receiving the email (you spoke at their event)
Includes a proper unsubscribe link (legally required for marketing emails)
Content is informational — no requests for money, info, or urgent action
Consistent with how community organizations maintain engagement
explanation.safe_move: Safe to read and click links to techbridge.org. You can unsubscribe if you don't want future emails.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring it means missing community events that might be relevant to your work.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate newsletters come from official domains, explain why you're subscribed, offer unsubscribe options, and don't ask for anything.

green_flags.label: All links to official techbridge.org domain
Explains why you received this email
No requests for info or payment

message.body: TrustBank Alert: Unusual activity on your account. Verify now at trustbank-secure-verify.com to avoid suspension.

explanation.short: The domain 'trustbank-secure-verify.com' is not the bank's real website.
tells: The URL doesn't match any real bank domain
Banks don't threaten immediate suspension via text
No account details or personalization
explanation.safe_move: Log into your bank's official app or website directly to check for alerts.
explanation.consequence: The fake site harvests your banking credentials.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always check the domain carefully — banks use their own domain, never hyphenated lookalikes.

spot_flag_options.label: Asks you to call an unverified number

message.body: PinnacleBank Fraud Dept: A $742.00 charge was attempted on your debit card. If this wasn't you, call immediately: 1-888-555-0277 to block your card.

explanation.short: The message wants you to call a number controlled by scammers, not your real bank.
tells: Banks send fraud alerts but direct you to the number on your card, not a new number
The phone number in the text may route to a scam call center
Legitimate alerts usually ask you to confirm via the bank app
explanation.safe_move: Call the number on the back of your bank card or log into your bank app to check transactions.
explanation.consequence: Calling the fake number connects you to scammers who will ask for your card number, PIN, and personal details.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never call a phone number from an unsolicited text — always use the number on your card or your bank's official website.

spot_flag_options.label: Asks you to share a verification code
Sent from a short code number
Uses a bank name you recognize

message.body: HarborCredit: We detected a suspicious login. For security, reply with the 6-digit code we just sent to verify your identity.

red_flags.label: Asks you to share a verification code
Asks you to reply directly to the text

explanation.short: No legitimate bank will ever ask you to share a verification code via text reply.
tells: Verification codes are meant for you to enter on official sites, never to share
Scammers are trying to log into your account and need the code you received
Banks handle suspicious logins through their own app or call center
explanation.safe_move: Never share verification codes with anyone. Log into your bank account directly to check for alerts.
explanation.consequence: Sharing the code gives scammers full access to your bank account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verification codes are like keys — they're only for you to use directly on the official website or app.

spot_flag_options.label: Sender email domain doesn't match the company
The email has a company logo
Has an unsubscribe link in the footer

message.subject: Action Required: Your storage is full
message.body:
Hi,

Your CloudDrive account has reached its storage limit. Files will stop syncing in 24 hours.

Upgrade your plan now to avoid losing access:
https://clouddrive-notifications.net/upgrade

CloudDrive Team

red_flags.label: Sender domain doesn't match the real company
Link goes to the fake domain, not the real service

explanation.short: The sender domain 'clouddrive-notifications.net' is not the company's real domain.
tells: Real companies send from their own domain, not a lookalike
The link domain matches the fake sender, not a real service
Legitimate storage warnings don't threaten to stop syncing in 24 hours
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to your cloud storage provider's website or app to check your storage status.
explanation.consequence: Clicking the link leads to a fake login page that steals your email and password.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always check the sender's email domain — it's the fastest way to spot phishing.

spot_flag_options.label: Includes a company address in the footer
Link text says one URL but goes to another

message.subject: Password Expiration Notice
message.body:
Dear Employee,

Your email password will expire in 3 days. Please update it to maintain access to your account.

Click here to update your password: https://workmail.com/password-reset
[Actual link: https://workmail-corp.com/reset-pw]

IT Department
WorkMail Corporate
100 Business Ave, Suite 200

red_flags.label: Displayed URL differs from actual destination

explanation.short: The link text shows one URL but actually leads to a different, fake domain.
tells: The visible URL and the actual link destination don't match
The sender domain 'workmail-corp.com' is not the real company domain
Legitimate IT departments address you by name, not 'Dear Employee'
explanation.safe_move: Hover over links before clicking. Contact your IT department directly through known channels.
explanation.consequence: Entering your password on the fake page gives attackers access to your email and all connected accounts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always hover over links to check where they really go — if the displayed text and actual URL differ, it's a red flag.

spot_flag_options.label: Asks for a small redelivery fee
Doesn't mention a carrier you use

message.body: ExpressParcel: Delivery failed — address incomplete. Pay $1.99 redelivery fee to update and reschedule: expressparcel-redeliver.com/pay

red_flags.label: Requests a small payment for redelivery
Suspicious domain not matching a real carrier

explanation.short: Legitimate delivery services never charge a fee via text to redeliver a package.
tells: Real carriers reschedule for free through their official site
The domain is not a recognized delivery service
The small dollar amount is designed to seem harmless
explanation.safe_move: Check the carrier's official website or app using a tracking number from your order confirmation.
explanation.consequence: The payment page captures your credit card information for fraud.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: If a delivery text asks for money, it's a scam — real carriers don't charge redelivery fees via text.

spot_flag_options.label: Claims customs fees on a domestic package

message.body: 📦 Your domestic package is held at customs. Pay $3.50 clearance fee to release: quickclearance-pkg.com/fee

red_flags.label: Domestic packages don't go through customs

explanation.short: Domestic packages never go through customs — this makes no sense.
tells: Customs only applies to international shipments
The fee request via a random link is a credit card harvesting tactic
No legitimate carrier uses this domain
explanation.safe_move: Ignore the text. If you're expecting a package, check the tracking on the retailer's website.
explanation.consequence: Entering payment info on the fake site gives scammers your credit card details.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Think about whether the claim even makes sense — domestic packages and customs don't go together.

spot_flag_options.label: Asks you to install remote access software
Uses a company name you recognize
Has an employee name and title

message.subject: Critical: Virus detected on your device
message.body:
Dear Customer,

Our security scan detected malware on your device linked to your NexTech account. To remove it, please install our Remote Assist tool:

https://nextech-helpdesk.com/remote-assist

One of our technicians will guide you through the removal process.

Best regards,
Daniel Torres
Senior Support Engineer
NexTech

red_flags.label: Claims to have scanned your device without your knowledge
Sender domain is not the real company

explanation.short: No legitimate company will email you to install remote access software because of a 'detected virus.'
tells: Companies can't remotely scan your personal device without your permission
Remote access tools give scammers full control of your computer
The sender domain doesn't match a real tech company
explanation.safe_move: Run your own antivirus scan. Never install remote access software at someone else's request.
explanation.consequence: Installing the software gives scammers full access to your computer, files, and accounts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No company can detect viruses on your device remotely — if someone claims they did, it's always a scam.

spot_flag_options.label: Asks for payment via gift cards

message.body: ALERT: Your NetShield antivirus subscription ($349.99) renews today. To cancel, call 1-877-555-0412. Refunds issued via gift card for fastest processing.

red_flags.label: References a subscription you didn't sign up for
Directs you to call a scam number

explanation.short: No legitimate company processes refunds via gift cards — that's a classic scam indicator.
tells: Gift card refunds are untraceable and only used by scammers
The fake subscription is designed to create panic
The callback number connects to a scam call center
explanation.safe_move: Check your bank statement for actual charges. If you don't recognize a subscription, dispute it through your bank.
explanation.consequence: Calling the number leads to scammers who will ask for gift card codes, draining the cards immediately.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Gift cards are for gifts, not payments or refunds — any mention of gift cards in a business transaction is a scam.

spot_flag_options.label: Asks for your Federal Benefits Number
Email is well-formatted with a logo

message.subject: Identity Verification Required by April 15
message.body:
Dear User,

As part of our new security requirements, all accounts must complete identity verification by April 15.

Please confirm your identity by providing:
- Full name
- Date of birth
- Last 4 digits of your SSN

Verify here: https://verifyhub-accounts.com/verify

Failure to verify will result in account suspension.

VerifyHub Security Team

red_flags.label: Asks for Federal Benefits Number digits
Sender domain is not a real company

explanation.short: Legitimate services never ask for your SSN via email — this is identity theft.
tells: No real company asks for SSN digits via email
The domain is a fake lookalike
Threatening suspension creates panic to rush you into sharing sensitive info
explanation.safe_move: Never provide personal identification numbers via email. Contact the company through their official website if concerned.
explanation.consequence: Providing SSN digits allows scammers to commit identity theft and open fraudulent accounts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Your SSN should never be shared via email or text links — legitimate verification happens through secure, official channels.

spot_flag_options.label: Contains a QR code to scan

message.subject: Verify Your PayStream Account
message.body:
Dear PayStream User,

We're updating our security protocols. Please verify your account by scanning the QR code below with your phone camera.

[QR Code Image]

This will direct you to our secure verification portal. Verification must be completed within 48 hours.

Thank you,
PayStream Trust & Safety

red_flags.label: QR code hides the actual destination URL
Sender domain doesn't match the real company

explanation.short: QR codes in emails are a newer scam tactic — they hide where the link actually goes.
tells: QR codes can't be hovered over to preview the URL like regular links
Legitimate companies rarely use QR codes in verification emails
The sender domain is a fake
explanation.safe_move: Never scan QR codes from unexpected emails. Go directly to the company's website or app.
explanation.consequence: The QR code leads to a phishing page that captures your login credentials.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: QR codes in emails are suspicious because they prevent you from seeing the destination — always go to the official site directly.

spot_flag_options.label: Claims to be a 2FA code you didn't request

message.body: Your MegaMart verification code is 847291. If you didn't request this, someone may be trying to access your account. Secure it now: megamart-secure.net/protect

red_flags.label: Link goes to a fake domain

explanation.short: If you didn't request a code, the 'secure your account' link is the real attack.
tells: You didn't initiate any login, so the code is bait
The link domain is fake — real companies use their main domain
The fear of compromise is designed to make you click without thinking
explanation.safe_move: If you didn't request a code, ignore the message. If concerned, log into your account directly through the official app.
explanation.consequence: The 'protection' link is a phishing page that steals your login credentials.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Unrequested verification codes paired with 'protect your account' links are a two-step phishing trick — the code is bait to make the link seem urgent.

spot_flag_options.label: Unexpected shared document from a stranger

message.subject: Q3 Budget Report — shared with you
message.body:
Hi,

I've shared the Q3 budget report with you. Please review and leave your comments by end of week.

Open document: https://docsync-share.com/doc/q3-budget-review

Thanks,
Jennifer Walsh
Finance Director

red_flags.label: Unexpected document share from unknown sender
Domain is not a real document service
No prior relationship or context established

explanation.short: Fake shared document notifications trick you into entering credentials on a phishing page.
tells: You have no relationship with this sender or their company
The domain is not a recognized document platform
Legitimate shared docs come through platforms you already use
explanation.safe_move: Don't click links from unknown senders sharing unexpected documents. Verify with the sender through a separate channel if needed.
explanation.consequence: The link leads to a fake login page that steals your email credentials.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: If you don't know the sender and weren't expecting a shared document, it's almost certainly phishing.

spot_flag_options.label: Asks you to reply YES or NO
Says someone will call you after you reply

message.body: SummitBank Fraud Alert: Did you authorize a $289.00 purchase at ElectroMart? Reply YES or NO. If NO, a fraud specialist will call you shortly to secure your account.

red_flags.label: Promises a follow-up call from a 'specialist'
Gets you to engage by replying
Sets up a social engineering phone call

explanation.short: Replying 'NO' triggers a scam call where fake fraud specialists extract your banking details.
tells: The reply confirms your number is active and you're engaged
The follow-up call is from scammers, not your bank
Real bank fraud alerts use their app or ask you to call the number on your card
explanation.safe_move: Don't reply. Call your bank directly using the number on your card to check for fraud.
explanation.consequence: The follow-up call from the 'fraud specialist' is a social engineering attack to get your account details.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Be wary of any text that sets up a phone call — scammers use texts to warm you up for the real attack on the phone.

spot_flag_options.label: Invoice for a product you never bought

message.subject: Invoice #CG-20260409 — $399.99 Charged
message.body:
Thank you for your purchase!

Product: CyberGuard Premium Protection (1-year)
Amount: $399.99
Tax: $32.00
Total: $431.99

If you did not authorize this purchase, call our billing department immediately at 1-866-555-0388 to request a full refund.

CyberGuard Inc.

red_flags.label: Invoice for a product you never purchased
Directs you to call a scam refund line

explanation.short: Fake invoices for products you never bought are designed to scare you into calling the scam 'refund' line.
tells: You never purchased this product
The phone number connects to scammers who will request remote access or payment info
The sender domain is not a real company
explanation.safe_move: Check your actual bank/credit card statements. If there's no charge, ignore the email.
explanation.consequence: Calling the number leads to scammers who will ask for remote access to your computer or your bank details for a fake 'refund.'
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: If you get an invoice for something you didn't buy, check your bank statement first — if there's no charge, the invoice itself is the scam.

message.body: SwiftShip: Your package couldn't be delivered. Update your address at swiftship-redelivery.com/update to reschedule. Ref #SW-48291

red_flags.label: Asks you to submit your address on a random site

explanation.short: Real carriers don't ask you to update your address through a random website link.
tells: The domain is not a recognized shipping company
Legitimate carriers redirect to their official site or leave a door tag
Collecting your address is the first step in identity fraud
explanation.safe_move: Check the tracking number on the retailer's website or the carrier's official app.
explanation.consequence: The site collects your personal address and then asks for payment info for a fake redelivery fee.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Delivery problems are handled through official carrier websites, not random links in texts.

message.body: CrestBank: Your debit card has been locked due to suspicious activity. Unlock here: crestbank-unlock.com/verify

red_flags.label: No specific transaction or account details

explanation.short: Banks don't send unlock links via text — you'd need to call or visit a branch.
tells: The domain is not a real bank website
Real card locks require calling the number on your card
No specific account details are mentioned
explanation.safe_move: Call the number on the back of your debit card to check if it's actually locked.
explanation.consequence: The unlock page is a phishing site that steals your banking credentials.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Banks never send 'unlock' links via text — always use the number on your card.

message.body: Hi! I saw your listing for the vintage lamp. Is it still available? I'm in the area and could pick up this weekend if that works for you.

red_flags.label: Tries to move conversation off the platform

explanation.short: This is a normal marketplace inquiry — they're asking about availability and suggesting a local pickup.
tells: The buyer is asking a straightforward availability question
They're offering to pick up locally, which is standard for marketplace sales
No suspicious links, payment demands, or off-platform requests
explanation.safe_move: Respond normally and arrange a safe public meeting spot for the exchange.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is normal marketplace communication.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Not every message is a scam — simple availability questions with local pickup suggestions are normal buyer behavior.

message.body: FreePay: You sent $45.00 to Mike R. If you didn't make this payment, call 1-800-555-0100 (on the back of your card).

explanation.short: This is a standard payment confirmation from a short code, referencing the number on your card.
tells: Sent from a short code (5 digits), which is standard for financial notifications
Directs you to the number on your card, not a random number
Simple confirmation of a transaction you likely made
explanation.safe_move: If you made the payment, no action needed. If not, call the number on your card as instructed.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a standard transaction notification.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate banking texts come from short codes and tell you to call the number on your card — not a number in the text.

message.body: EZToll: You have an unpaid toll of $4.75. Pay now to avoid a $50 late fee: eztoll-payments.com/pay

red_flags.label: Small initial amount to seem harmless

explanation.short: Toll agencies mail paper invoices — they don't text you with threats of $50 late fees.
tells: The domain is not an official toll agency
A $50 late fee on a $4.75 toll is designed to scare you into paying
Toll agencies send bills by mail, not SMS
explanation.safe_move: Visit your state's official toll website to check for unpaid tolls.
explanation.consequence: The payment site captures your credit card information.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Toll agencies send paper bills — any text demanding toll payment is a scam.

message.body: ⚠️ Your CoinVault wallet has been flagged for unusual activity. Verify your wallet immediately: coinvault-secure.io/verify

red_flags.label: Security alerts don't come via DMs

explanation.short: Crypto platforms don't send security alerts through social media DMs.
tells: The domain is not a real crypto platform
Security alerts come via email or in-app notifications, not DMs
The urgency is designed to bypass careful thinking
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to your crypto wallet app or official website to check your account status.
explanation.consequence: The verification page steals your wallet credentials or seed phrase, draining your funds.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Crypto security alerts never come via social media DMs — always check through the official app.

message.body: BrightPost: Your package 1Z999AA10123456784 was delivered at 2:14 PM. Left at front door. Track: brightpost.com/track

explanation.short: This is a standard delivery confirmation from a short code with a proper tracking number and specific delivery details.
tells: Sent from a 5-digit short code, typical for legitimate carrier notifications
Includes a specific tracking number and delivery time
The domain matches the sender name — no lookalike tricks
Delivery confirmations don't ask for any action or payment
explanation.safe_move: No action needed. You can verify the tracking number on the carrier's website if you like.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a normal delivery notification.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate delivery texts include specific tracking numbers, delivery times, and don't ask for payment or personal info.

message.body: Hey! We were in the same photography workshop last Saturday. Loved your portfolio — especially the street photography series. Want to grab coffee sometime and talk shop?

red_flags.label: Message from someone you don't know well

explanation.short: This is a normal social connection from someone referencing a specific shared experience.
tells: References a specific event you both attended
Shows genuine knowledge of your work (street photography)
No financial requests, suspicious links, or pressure
Suggests a casual public meetup
explanation.safe_move: If you remember them from the workshop, feel free to connect. Meet in a public place.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is normal social networking.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real connections reference specific shared experiences — scammers use vague, generic flattery.

message.body: StreamPlus: Your account will be suspended due to a billing issue. Update payment at streamplus-billing.com/update to keep your subscription active.

red_flags.label: Asks you to enter payment info via link

explanation.short: Streaming services handle billing through their app — they don't text you links to update payment.
tells: The domain doesn't match any real streaming service
Legitimate services notify you in-app or via email from their real domain
The suspension threat creates urgency to enter payment details
explanation.safe_move: Open the streaming service's official app to check your billing status.
explanation.consequence: The fake billing page captures your credit card information.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Subscription services handle billing in their own app — a text link asking for payment info is always a scam.

thread.body: Hey! I saw your profile and thought we have a lot in common. I'm a marine engineer working offshore right now.
Oh interesting! How long have you been doing that?

About 8 years now. I'm actually heading home next month — would love to meet up! But I'm having a small issue... my wallet got frozen because of international regulations. Could you help me with a small transfer? I'll pay you back double when I'm home.

message.body: Hey! I saw your profile... [conversation where they ask for a money transfer after building rapport]

red_flags.label: Asks for money from someone you just met
Elaborate backstory to justify the request
Claims to be overseas and unable to handle finances

explanation.short: Asking a stranger for a money transfer after brief online interaction is a classic romance scam pattern.
tells: Moved very quickly from introduction to asking for money
The 'frozen wallet' story is a common excuse used in romance scams
Promising to 'pay back double' is unrealistic and manipulative
Working offshore/overseas is a common cover story to avoid meeting
explanation.safe_move: Never send money to someone you've only met online. Cut contact immediately.
explanation.consequence: The money is gone permanently — the person will keep asking for more or disappear.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Anyone who asks for money before meeting you in person is a scammer — no matter how genuine the conversation feels.

thread.body: I know this sounds random, but your comment on that travel post really stood out. Where was that photo taken?
That was in Portugal! Lisbon is amazing. Have you been?

Not yet, but it's on my list! I actually run a small travel investment fund. We've been getting 15-20% monthly returns. I don't usually share this, but since we seem to click... want me to show you how it works?
That sounds too good to be true honestly.
I totally get the skepticism! Here, I'll send you screenshots of my returns. You can start with just $200 to test it out — no pressure. I just hate seeing good people miss opportunities.

message.body: I know this sounds random... [conversation that starts with shared interests and pivots to an investment pitch with unrealistic returns]

red_flags.label: Quickly pivots from personal chat to money

explanation.short: This is a 'pig butchering' scam — building rapport before pitching a fake investment with impossible returns.
tells: 15-20% monthly returns don't exist in legitimate investments
The pivot from friendly chat to investment pitch is a calculated pattern
Screenshots of returns are easily faked
Starting with a small amount builds trust before larger requests
explanation.safe_move: Block and report the account. Never invest based on a DM from a stranger.
explanation.consequence: Early 'profits' are fake — once you invest more, the platform and your money disappear.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: If someone you met online steers conversation toward investments, it's a scam — no matter how natural the conversation felt.

thread.body: Hi, I'm interested in the couch you listed for $300. Is it still available?
Yes it's still available! When would you like to see it?

Great! I'm buying it for my son who just moved. I'll send a cashier's check for $800 — the extra $500 is to cover the movers I've arranged. Just send the $500 to the mover via CashDirect after you deposit the check.

message.body: Hi, I'm interested in the couch... [conversation where buyer sends overpayment and asks you to forward the excess]

red_flags.label: Sends more money than the asking price
Asks you to forward money to a third party
Uses a cashier's check which can be forged
Doesn't want to see the item in person

explanation.short: The overpayment check is fake — it will bounce after you've already sent real money to the 'mover.'
tells: No legitimate buyer overpays and asks you to forward the difference
Cashier's checks can be forged and take days to bounce
The 'mover' is the scammer collecting your real money
They never want to see the item because the purchase is fake
explanation.safe_move: Only accept the exact asking price. Meet in person for local sales. Never forward money to third parties.
explanation.consequence: You send $500 of your own money to the 'mover.' The $800 check bounces days later, and your bank holds you responsible.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: If a buyer offers more than your asking price and wants you to forward the difference, it's always a scam.

thread.body: Hey, is the bike still available? What size is the frame?
Yes! It's a 56cm frame. Great condition.
Perfect, that's my size. Would you take $180 instead of $200? I can pick it up today after work, around 5:30. I have cash.

message.body: Hey, is the bike still available? [Normal negotiation with reasonable offer and local cash pickup]

explanation.short: This is a normal marketplace negotiation — reasonable price offer with local cash pickup.
tells: Asks relevant questions about the item (frame size)
Offers a reasonable price (10% below asking)
Wants to meet locally and pay cash
No suspicious links, overpayments, or third-party payment requests
explanation.safe_move: Accept or counter the offer. Meet in a public place for the exchange.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is standard marketplace behavior.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Normal buyers ask product-specific questions, negotiate reasonably, and offer to meet locally with cash.

thread.body: Hi! We reviewed your resume on JobConnect and think you'd be a great fit for our Remote Data Entry Coordinator role. Pay is $45/hr, fully remote, flexible hours. Interested?
That sounds interesting! Can you tell me more about the company and the role?

We're GlobalTask Solutions, a growing BPO firm. Before we proceed with the interview, we need to set up your work equipment. We'll send you a check for $3,500 to purchase the required software and a laptop from our approved vendor. Just deposit the check and order from the link we'll provide.

message.subject: Job Opportunity: Remote Data Entry Coordinator
message.body: Hi! We reviewed your resume... [job offer that requires depositing a check to buy equipment from their vendor]

red_flags.label: Sends a check for you to buy equipment
$45/hr for data entry is unrealistically high
Must buy from their specific vendor

explanation.short: Legitimate employers provide equipment directly — they never send you a check to buy your own from 'their vendor.'
tells: The check is fake and will bounce after you've spent real money
The 'approved vendor' is controlled by the scammers
$45/hr for basic data entry is unrealistically high
Real jobs require interviews, not just a resume review
explanation.safe_move: Research the company independently. Legitimate employers never ask you to deposit checks for equipment.
explanation.consequence: The check bounces, and you're out thousands of dollars spent at the fake vendor.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any job that sends you money before you start working is a scam — real employers provide equipment directly.

thread.body: Hi Jordan, thanks for applying to the Marketing Associate position at BrightLeaf Media. We'd love to schedule a first-round interview. Are you available this Thursday or Friday between 10am-2pm ET?
Hi! Thursday at 11am works great for me.

Perfect. I've sent a calendar invite for Thursday at 11am ET via Zoom. The meeting link is in the invite. You'll be speaking with our Marketing Director, Lisa Chen. Let me know if you have any questions beforehand!

message.subject: Re: Marketing Associate Application - Interview Scheduling
message.body: Hi Jordan, thanks for applying... [standard interview scheduling exchange with calendar invite]

red_flags.label: You're not familiar with this company

explanation.short: This is a normal interview scheduling process — you applied, they responded with availability and a structured interview setup.
tells: References a position you applied for
Asks for your availability instead of demanding immediate action
Sends a calendar invite through a standard platform
Names a specific interviewer and their role
explanation.safe_move: Confirm the interview. Research the company and interviewer on their official website before the call.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a standard hiring process.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate recruiters reference your specific application, offer scheduling flexibility, and use standard tools like calendar invites.

thread.body: Hey, I'm from CoinVault support. We noticed your account was flagged for a potential security breach. Are you the owner of wallet ending in ...7x3F?
I do have a CoinVault account, what's going on?

We're seeing unauthorized access attempts. To secure your funds, I need to verify your identity. Can you share your 12-word recovery phrase so I can confirm you're the real owner? This is time-sensitive — the attacker could drain your wallet.

message.body: Hey, I'm from CoinVault support... [conversation where fake support asks for your recovery phrase]

red_flags.label: Real support doesn't contact you via DM
Uses partial wallet info to seem legitimate

explanation.short: No legitimate crypto company will ever ask for your recovery phrase — sharing it gives them complete access to your funds.
tells: Recovery phrases should NEVER be shared with anyone, including support
Real support teams communicate through official ticketing systems, not DMs
The partial wallet address is a guess or publicly visible info
The urgency prevents you from thinking clearly
explanation.safe_move: Never share your seed phrase. Contact CoinVault through their official website's support portal.
explanation.consequence: Sharing your recovery phrase gives the scammer complete control of your wallet — all funds are stolen instantly.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Your recovery phrase is the master key to your crypto — no one legitimate will ever ask for it.

thread.body:
Hi, this is accounting at SupplyNet. We're updating our banking details. Please use the new account for future payments:

Bank: First National
Routing: 021000021
Account: 483920175

Please update your records and send the outstanding invoice #SN-4872 ($12,400) to this account.
I'll need to verify this change. Can you confirm the contact name on our account?

Of course — your account manager is Rebecca Torres. This change is urgent as our old account is being closed by end of week. We'd appreciate payment to the new account by Friday to avoid service interruption.

message.subject: Updated Banking Information — Action Required
message.body: Hi, this is accounting at SupplyNet... [vendor impersonation asking to redirect payment to new bank details]

red_flags.label: Requests a change to payment bank details
Email domain doesn't match vendor's known domain

explanation.short: This is a business email compromise (BEC) scam — attackers impersonate a vendor to redirect payments to their own account.
tells: Changing bank details via email is a common BEC tactic
The urgency about the old account 'closing' prevents proper verification
The email domain is a lookalike, not the vendor's real domain
Knowing the account manager's name doesn't prove legitimacy — that info may be public
explanation.safe_move: Call your vendor contact at their known phone number to verify any banking changes. Never update payment details based on email alone.
explanation.consequence: Sending $12,400 to the new account sends it directly to the scammer — the money is unrecoverable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any request to change payment details must be verified by phone using a number you already have on file — never trust the email.

thread.body:
Hi, attached is Invoice #PM-2026-0328 for March web hosting and maintenance services as per our contract. Total: $450.00. Payment terms: Net 30.

Let me know if you have any questions.
Got it, thanks. Quick question — did you complete the SSL renewal that was on the task list?

Yes! SSL was renewed on March 22nd, valid through March 2027. I included a note about it in the itemized breakdown on page 2 of the invoice. Let me know when payment is processed — no rush, Net 30 is fine.

message.subject: Invoice #PM-2026-0328 — March Services
message.body: Hi, attached is Invoice #PM-2026-0328... [normal vendor invoice exchange with specific service details and standard payment terms]

explanation.short: This is a normal vendor invoice — consistent with an existing contract, standard payment terms, and the vendor answers specific questions accurately.
