tells: References a specific contract and ongoing service relationship
Standard Net 30 payment terms with no urgency pressure
The vendor answers a specific technical question accurately
No bank detail changes or unusual payment methods requested
explanation.safe_move: Process the invoice through your normal payment workflow. Verify the payment details match your records.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a standard business transaction.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate vendor invoices reference specific contracts, use standard terms, and don't pressure you to change payment methods.

thread.body: I love the dining table you listed! Is it solid wood?
Yes, it's solid oak. 6-seater. Are you interested?
Definitely! I want to buy it but I'm out of state. Can I pay through QuickSend? I'll also include extra for shipping — I'll arrange my own shipper.
I'd prefer local pickup. It's heavy furniture.
No worries, my shipper handles heavy items. Just share your QuickSend email and I'll send $650 — $400 for the table and $250 for the shipper. You just pay the shipper when they arrive.

message.body: I love the dining table... [out-of-state buyer wants to overpay and have you forward money to their shipper]

red_flags.label: Sends more than the asking price
Asks you to pay their shipper from the overpayment
Claims to be out of state to avoid meeting
Wants to use a different payment method

explanation.short: This is the classic overpayment/fake shipper scam — you'll forward real money while their payment bounces.
tells: A buyer who overpays and asks you to forward the difference is always a scam
The 'shipper' is the scammer collecting your real money
They avoid meeting in person by claiming to be out of state
They push to use a specific payment platform they can exploit
explanation.safe_move: Only accept exact payment for the item. For large furniture, insist on local pickup and cash.
explanation.consequence: Their payment reverses or bounces, but you've already sent $250 of your own money to the fake shipper.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never forward any portion of a payment to a third party — legitimate buyers arrange and pay for their own shipping.

thread.body: Congratulations! After reviewing your profile, you've been selected for a Product Tester position. Earn $500-$800/week reviewing products from home. No experience needed!
What does the role involve exactly?

Simple! We send you products to review. But first, we need to set up your tester account. There's a one-time $49.99 activation fee for the starter kit (testing forms, product catalog, shipping labels). You'll earn it back with your first assignment!

message.subject: You've Been Selected — Product Tester Position
message.body: Congratulations! After reviewing your profile... [fake job that requires an upfront activation fee]

red_flags.label: Requires payment to start a job
Job offer you didn't apply for

explanation.short: Legitimate employers never charge you to start working — the activation fee is the scam itself.
tells: Real jobs never require upfront payment from employees
You didn't apply for this job — they contacted you unsolicited
$500-800/week for no experience is unrealistically high for product testing
The 'starter kit' is worthless or doesn't exist
explanation.safe_move: Ignore the offer. Legitimate product testing jobs are posted on real job boards and never charge fees.
explanation.consequence: You pay $49.99 and either receive nothing or get directed to more fake 'upgrades' requiring additional payments.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: If a job requires you to pay them, it's not a job — it's a scam. Real employers pay you.

thread.body: Yo, I accidentally sent 0.5 ETH to your wallet address instead of my friend's. Our addresses are really similar. Can you check? Here's the transaction hash: 0x7f3a...d829
I don't see anything in my wallet.
It might be pending. Try connecting your wallet to ethcheck-verify.io to see pending transactions — it shows stuff that hasn't confirmed yet. I'm seriously stressed, that's like $900.

message.body: Yo, I accidentally sent 0.5 ETH to your wallet... [claims accidental transfer and directs you to a phishing site]

red_flags.label: Directs you to connect your wallet to a fake site
Uses distress to make you want to help
Connecting your wallet to an unknown site is dangerous

explanation.short: There is no accidental transfer — the goal is to get you to connect your wallet to a malicious site that drains it.
tells: The transaction hash is fake — you can verify on a real block explorer
Connecting your wallet to an unknown site can grant it permission to drain your funds
The emotional distress story is designed to lower your guard
Legitimate blockchain transactions are visible on official explorers, not random websites
explanation.safe_move: Check any transaction on official block explorers only. Never connect your wallet to sites sent by strangers.
explanation.consequence: Connecting your wallet to the site triggers a malicious smart contract that drains all your crypto.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never connect your wallet to any site shared by a stranger — use only official block explorers to verify transactions.

thread.body: Hey! I think we matched on that hiking group last week. Were you on the Mt. Wilson trail Saturday?
Yes I was! Were you in the group with the blue backpack?
Ha, that was me! The group's doing Griffith Park next weekend if you want to join. Same meetup page, Saturday 8am. It'd be fun to chat more on the trail!

message.body: Hey! I think we matched on that hiking group... [follow-up from a shared real-world activity with a group invitation]

red_flags.label: Invites you to meet in person

explanation.short: This is a normal follow-up from a shared activity — they reference specific details and invite you to a group event.
tells: References a specific shared experience with verifiable details
Invites you to a group activity, not a private meeting
Points you to the same platform you already use
No financial requests, suspicious links, or personal info requests
explanation.safe_move: If you remember them from the hike, feel free to join the group event.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is normal social connection building.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: People who reference specific shared experiences and invite you to group activities are usually genuine — scammers use vague flattery and isolate you.

thread.body:
Hi team, this is Michael from CleanPro Services. Just a heads up — we're switching payment processors and our old system is going offline Friday. Can you process this month's cleaning invoice through our new portal?

New payment link: https://cleanpro-payments.com/invoice/oct-2026
Hi Michael, I don't see this change mentioned in our contract. Can you provide documentation?

Apologies for the confusion! The transition happened quickly. I've attached the official notice from our finance team. The new portal uses the same credentials — just pay through the new link. We need to close out October billing by Friday.

message.subject: Payment Portal Update — CleanPro Services
message.body: Hi team, this is Michael from CleanPro... [vendor impersonation redirecting payment through a new fake portal]

red_flags.label: Redirects payment to a new, unknown portal
Claims documentation but it could be forged

explanation.short: Scammers impersonate a known vendor and redirect payments to a fake portal they control.
tells: Payment portal changes should be verified through established contacts, not email links
The Friday deadline prevents proper verification
The email domain is not the vendor's real domain
Attached 'official notices' are easily forged
explanation.safe_move: Call your vendor contact at their known phone number to verify any payment changes. Never click payment links from emails.
explanation.consequence: Paying through the fake portal sends money directly to the scammer's account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify payment changes by calling the vendor at a number you already have — never trust email-based payment redirects.

thread.body: Hi! I'm selling a PS5 Pro for $250 — barely used, still has warranty. Interested?
That's a great price. Can I see it in person?
I'm moving tomorrow so I can't do in-person. But I can ship it through SafeTrade — they hold the money in escrow until you confirm delivery. Here's the listing: safetrade-deals.com/listing/ps5pro

message.body: Hi! I'm selling a PS5 Pro for $250... [seller uses fake escrow site to take payment without delivering]

red_flags.label: Price is suspiciously low for the item
Directs you to an unknown escrow service
Uses moving as an excuse for urgency

explanation.short: The 'escrow' site is fake — your payment goes straight to the scammer, and no item is shipped.
tells: A PS5 Pro for $250 is well below market value — classic bait pricing
Refusing to meet in person prevents you from verifying the item exists
The escrow site is controlled by the scammer, not a real service
'Moving tomorrow' creates urgency and an excuse to avoid meeting
explanation.safe_move: Only buy in person for high-value items. If buying online, use the marketplace's built-in payment protection.
explanation.consequence: You pay $250 to a fake escrow site and receive nothing — the seller disappears.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: If a deal seems too good to be true and the seller can't meet in person, walk away. Use only established payment platforms.

message.body: ALERT: Your device has been compromised. Call NetSecure support at 1-866-555-0499 immediately to prevent data loss. Case #NS-77201

red_flags.label: Unexpected security alert from unknown sender
Directs you to call a scam support number
Uses fear of data loss to create panic

explanation.short: No company can detect that your device is compromised via a random text message.
tells: Unsolicited security alerts from unknown numbers are always fake
The callback number connects to a scam call center that will request remote access
A case number makes it look official but is meaningless
Real security software alerts you through the app, not via SMS
explanation.safe_move: Run your own antivirus scan. Never call phone numbers from unsolicited security alerts.
explanation.consequence: Calling the number leads to scammers who will request remote access to your device and steal your data or demand payment.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Security alerts don't come via random text messages — your actual security software notifies you through its own app.

message.body: RIDGELINE BANK ALERT: Unauthorized purchase of $1,249.00 flagged on your account. If this was NOT you, secure your account now: ridgelinebank-verify.com/secure

red_flags.label: Sent from full phone number, not short code

explanation.short: The link goes to a fake domain designed to steal your login credentials.
tells: ridgelinebank-verify.com is not the real bank domain
Sent from a regular phone number, not a bank short code
No last 4 digits of your card referenced
Pushes you to click a link instead of calling
explanation.safe_move: Call the number on the back of your debit card.
explanation.consequence: Clicking the link leads to a credential-harvesting page that captures your banking login.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real bank fraud alerts come from short codes and reference your specific card digits.

message.body: Ridgeline Bank Fraud Alert: Did you authorize $1,249.00 at Vertex Electronics? Reply 1 for YES or 2 for NO. Card ending 7734.

explanation.short: Legitimate fraud alert from official short code with specific card and merchant details.
tells: Sent from a 5-digit short code
References your card ending 7734
No links — just reply 1 or 2
Names the specific merchant
explanation.safe_move: Reply 1 or 2. Or call your bank directly to confirm.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring a real fraud alert may allow unauthorized charges to continue.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real bank alerts use short codes, reference your card digits, and never include links.

message.body: QuikToll: You have an unpaid toll of $4.75. To avoid a $50 late fee, pay now at ezpass-tollpay.com/settle. Due today.

explanation.short: Fake toll notice using a lookalike domain to harvest payment info.
tells: ezpass-tollpay.com is not a real toll authority site
Creates urgency with a same-day deadline and late fee threat
Toll agencies mail notices, they don't text from random numbers
Small dollar amount designed to make you pay without thinking
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to your toll agency's official website or call them.
explanation.consequence: The site collects your credit card number for fraudulent charges.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Toll agencies send paper notices by mail, not text messages with payment links.

message.body: QuickShip: Your package is out for delivery. Estimated arrival by 4pm. Track at quickship.com or in the QuickShip app. No action needed.

explanation.short: Standard delivery notification from a legitimate short code with no payment request.
tells: Sent from a 5-digit short code
Links to the official quickship.com domain
No payment or personal info requested
Says 'No action needed'
explanation.safe_move: Track your package through the app or official website if you're expecting a delivery.
explanation.consequence: Missing a real delivery update means you might not be home to receive your package.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real delivery updates come from short codes, link to official domains, and never ask for payment.

message.subject: CRITICAL: Your Pinnacle license expires in 2 hours
message.body:
Dear Customer,

Your Pinnacle Pro license has been flagged for immediate deactivation due to a billing error. All your files and settings will be permanently deleted in 2 hours.

To prevent data loss, call our priority support line immediately: +1 (833) 901-4455

Our technician will need remote access to resolve this issue.

Pinnacle Software Support Team

explanation.short: Fake support email designed to get you to grant remote access to your computer.
tells: pinnacle-software-help.net is not an official domain
Legitimate companies don't threaten to delete your files in 2 hours
Asks for remote access — a major red flag
No account details or personalization
Uses fear of data loss to bypass your judgment
explanation.safe_move: Go to the official Pinnacle Software website and contact support through their verified channels.
explanation.consequence: Granting remote access lets scammers install malware, steal files, and drain accounts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real software companies never cold-email you demanding remote access to fix billing issues.

message.subject: Your Pinnacle Pro subscription renews in 7 days
message.body:
Hi Jordan,

This is a reminder that your Pinnacle Pro annual subscription (account #PS-44821) renews on April 16 for $79.99.

If you'd like to make changes, log in to your account at pinnaclesoftware.com/account.

No action needed if you want to continue your subscription.

Thanks,
Pinnacle Software Billing

explanation.short: Legitimate renewal reminder from the official domain with account details and advance notice.
tells: Sent from pinnaclesoftware.com — the official domain
Uses your first name and account number
Gives 7 days notice, not a 2-hour countdown
Links to the official site
Says no action needed to continue
explanation.safe_move: Log in directly at pinnaclesoftware.com to review your subscription if needed.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring this means your subscription renews automatically — no harm done.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real renewal notices come from official domains, include account details, and give you time to decide.

message.subject: Action Required: Verify your identity to avoid account suspension
message.body:
We detected unusual sign-in activity on your CloudVault account. Your account will be suspended within 24 hours unless you verify your identity.

Click here to verify: cloudvault-verify.org/confirm-identity

You will need to provide your password and answer security questions.

CloudVault Security Team

red_flags.label: No account name or email referenced

explanation.short: Phishing email using a fake domain to collect your password and security answers.
tells: cloudvault-verify.org is not the official CloudVault domain
Legitimate services never ask you to enter your password via email link
No username or account email referenced
Arbitrary 24-hour deadline creates panic
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to cloudvault.com and check your account security settings.
explanation.consequence: Entering your password gives attackers full access to your account and stored files.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real security alerts never ask you to re-enter your password through an email link.

message.subject: New sign-in from Chrome on CompuOS
message.body:
Hi Alex,

We noticed a new sign-in to your CloudVault account (alex.m@email.com).

Device: Chrome on CompuOS
Location: Denver, CO
Time: April 8, 2026 at 3:42 PM MDT

If this was you, no action is needed.

If this wasn't you, go to cloudvault.com/security to review your account.

CloudVault Security

explanation.short: Standard new sign-in notification from the real CloudVault domain with specific device details.
tells: Sent from cloudvault.com — the official domain
Addresses you by name and shows your email
Includes specific device, location, and time
Does not ask for your password
Says no action needed if it was you
explanation.safe_move: If you don't recognize the sign-in, go directly to cloudvault.com/security to change your password.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring a real new sign-in alert could mean someone else has access to your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real sign-in alerts include specific device details and never ask for your password.

message.subject: Invoice #NS-88471 — Your NovaSuite plan renewed for $349.99
message.body:
Your NovaSuite Premium plan has been auto-renewed.

Amount charged: $349.99
Transaction ID: TXN-8847102

If you did not authorize this charge, call our cancellation line immediately at +1 (866) 440-2918 to receive a full refund within 24 hours.

NovaSuite Billing Department

red_flags.label: Wants you to call a number
No customer name or account info

explanation.short: Fake renewal invoice designed to scare you into calling a scam refund line.
tells: novasuite-renewal.com is not the real NovaSuite domain
Inflated price to create panic
The phone number connects to scammers who will request remote access
No customer name or account email
Real companies process refunds through their website, not a special phone line
explanation.safe_move: Check your bank statement. If no charge exists, delete the email.
explanation.consequence: Calling the number leads to a refund scam where they request remote access and steal money.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always check your bank statement first — if no charge exists, the invoice is fake.

message.subject: Your NovaSuite receipt — $14.99/month
message.body:
Hi Taylor,

Thanks for being a NovaSuite subscriber! Here's your receipt:

Plan: NovaSuite Standard
Amount: $14.99
Card ending: 5512
Next billing date: May 8, 2026

Manage your subscription at novasuite.com/account.

NovaSuite Team

explanation.short: Standard monthly receipt from the official NovaSuite domain with card and account details.
tells: Sent from novasuite.com — the official domain
Uses your name and card ending digits
Reasonable monthly amount
Links to official account management page
No urgency or threats
explanation.safe_move: Keep for your records. Manage your subscription at novasuite.com/account if needed.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a standard billing receipt.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real receipts come from official domains, show card digits, and use reasonable amounts.

message.subject: You've been selected — Remote Data Entry Position ($45/hr)
message.body:
Congratulations!

After reviewing your resume on JobNexus, we'd like to offer you a Remote Data Entry Specialist position at Helix Dynamics.

Pay: $45/hour, flexible schedule
Start: Immediately

To proceed, please complete our onboarding form which requires your full SSN, bank routing number for direct deposit, and a copy of your ID.

Reply to this email to get started.

Best,
Sarah Mitchell
HR Director, Helix Dynamics

red_flags.label: Unusually high pay for simple work

explanation.short: Fake job offer designed to steal your identity and banking information.
tells: $45/hr for data entry with no experience required is unrealistic
No interview — just immediate 'offer'
Asks for SSN, bank info, and ID before any hiring process
helix-dynamics-hiring.com is not a legitimate company domain
Real employers don't request sensitive info via email before hiring
explanation.safe_move: Research the company independently. Never provide SSN or bank info before a verified hiring process.
explanation.consequence: Providing this info leads to identity theft and fraudulent bank transactions.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate employers interview you first and collect tax/banking info through secure HR portals after hiring.

message.subject: Interview invitation — Junior Data Analyst at Helix Dynamics
message.body:
Hi Morgan,

Thank you for applying to the Junior Data Analyst role at Helix Dynamics through JobNexus.

We'd like to invite you to a 30-minute video interview. Please use the link below to select a time:

helixdynamics.com/interviews/schedule?ref=JN-4471

You'll meet with our hiring manager, David Chen. Please have your portfolio ready.

Looking forward to speaking with you,
Recruiting Team
Helix Dynamics

green_flags.label: References specific role you applied for
Does not ask for sensitive info

explanation.short: Legitimate interview invitation with proper hiring process and no sensitive data requests.
tells: Sent from helixdynamics.com — the company's official domain
References a specific role you applied for
Invites you to an interview, not an immediate 'offer'
Names the interviewer
No requests for SSN, bank info, or ID
explanation.safe_move: Verify the company exists, then schedule the interview through the provided link.
explanation.consequence: Missing a real interview invitation means losing a job opportunity.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real recruiters invite you to interviews first and never ask for sensitive info before hiring.

message.subject: OVERDUE: Invoice #ORN-2247 — Payment due immediately
message.body:
FINAL NOTICE

Invoice #ORN-2247 for $2,875.00 is now overdue. To avoid service interruption and collection action, please wire payment immediately to:

Bank: First Continental
Routing: 071923844
Account: 884712093

Or pay via QuickSend to payments@orion-officesolutions.net

This matter will be referred to collections if not resolved by end of business today.

Orion Office Solutions — Accounts Receivable

explanation.short: Fake overdue invoice using urgency and threats to trick you into wiring money.
tells: No description of what the invoice is for
Demands wire transfer or QuickSend — both are irreversible
orion-officesolutions.net is a suspicious domain
Threatens same-day collections to create panic
You likely never ordered from this vendor
explanation.safe_move: Check your purchasing records. If you have no relationship with this vendor, delete it.
explanation.consequence: Wire transfers are irreversible — you cannot recover money sent to scammers.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate invoices describe specific services, reference PO numbers, and don't demand same-day wire transfers.

message.subject: Invoice #ORN-2247 for March office supplies
message.body:
Hi Jamie,

Please find attached your invoice for March office supply orders:

Invoice #ORN-2247
PO Reference: PO-00891
Items: Printer toner (x4), Copy paper (x10 reams), Desk organizers (x3)
Total: $287.50
Payment terms: Net 30 (due April 30, 2026)

Pay online at orionoffice.com/pay or mail a check to our office.

Questions? Contact your account rep, Lisa Huang, at lisa@orionoffice.com.

Thanks,
Orion Office Solutions

explanation.short: Legitimate vendor invoice with itemized charges, PO reference, and standard payment terms.
tells: Sent from orionoffice.com — the real company domain
References your PO number
Itemizes specific products ordered
Net 30 payment terms — no urgency
Names your account representative
Offers standard payment options
explanation.safe_move: Cross-reference the PO number with your purchasing records and process normally.
explanation.consequence: Not paying a legitimate invoice could damage your vendor relationship.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real vendor invoices include PO references, itemized charges, and standard payment terms.

message.subject: Mandatory: Reset your WorkStream password now
message.body:
Your WorkStream password expires today. To maintain access to company systems, you must reset it immediately.

Reset Password: workstream-portal.co/reset-password

Failure to reset within 4 hours will result in account lockout and you will need to contact IT for manual recovery.

WorkStream IT Administration

explanation.short: Phishing email impersonating IT to harvest your work credentials via a fake reset page.
tells: workstream-portal.co is a lookalike domain, not the real company portal
4-hour deadline creates artificial urgency
No employee name or employee ID
Real IT departments don't send password resets via external domains
explanation.safe_move: Contact your IT department directly through your company directory.
explanation.consequence: Entering your credentials gives attackers access to all company systems you can reach.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Company IT password resets come from internal domains and your IT team, not external emails.

message.subject: Password expiration reminder — 14 days remaining
message.body:
Hi Casey (Employee ID: WS-7823),

Your WorkStream password will expire in 14 days (April 23, 2026).

To update it, use the password change tool inside WorkStream:
1. Log in to workstream.com
2. Go to Settings > Security > Change Password
3. Follow the prompts

Need help? Reach IT Support at ext. 4400 or visit us in Room 202.

IT Administration
WorkStream, Inc.

green_flags.label: Directs to change password within app

explanation.short: Legitimate IT password reminder from official domain with employee details and plenty of time.
tells: Sent from workstream.com — the company domain
Includes employee name and ID
14-day notice — no artificial urgency
Tells you to change password inside the app, not via email link
Provides physical office location and extension number
explanation.safe_move: Follow the steps to change your password inside WorkStream when convenient.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring this means your password will expire and you'll need to contact IT.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real IT reminders come from company domains, include your employee details, and give advance notice.

message.body: SwiftPost: Your package could not be delivered due to incomplete address. Update your delivery info to avoid return: swiftpost-redelivery.com/update

red_flags.label: Regular phone number, not short code

explanation.short: Fake delivery notice using a lookalike domain to steal your personal information.
tells: swiftpost-redelivery.com is not an official carrier domain
Sent from a regular phone number, not a carrier short code
No tracking number or package details
Real carriers leave a physical notice or reattempt delivery
explanation.safe_move: Check your tracking on the official SwiftPost website or app.
explanation.consequence: The site collects your address, phone, and payment details.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real delivery carriers include tracking numbers and don't text from regular phone numbers.

message.body: SwiftPost: Delivery attempted for tracking #SP8841207. No one was home. We'll try again tomorrow. Track at swiftpost.com/track or reschedule in the app.

explanation.short: Legitimate missed delivery notification with tracking number and official domain.
tells: Sent from a 5-digit short code
Includes a specific tracking number
Links to official swiftpost.com domain
No payment or personal info requested
Says they'll try again tomorrow
explanation.safe_move: Track your package on the official site or reschedule through the app.
explanation.consequence: Not rescheduling means they'll try again anyway — no urgent risk.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real carriers text from short codes, include tracking numbers, and link to their official domain.

message.subject: Refund of $299.00 processed to your account — action required
message.body:
A refund of $299.00 has been processed to your PayCircle account. However, due to a system error, $2,990.00 was refunded instead.

To avoid account suspension, please return the excess $2,691.00 via bank transfer to:

Account: 7790234811
Routing: 021000089

Please complete this within 48 hours.

PayCircle Support Team

explanation.short: Classic overpayment scam — they claim they refunded too much and want you to wire back the difference.
tells: The 'overpayment' never happened — check your account balance
Asks for irreversible bank transfer
paycircle-help.com is not PayCircle's official domain
Real payment platforms handle refund errors internally
No account name or email referenced
explanation.safe_move: Log in to your real PayCircle account to check your balance. You'll see no extra money.
explanation.consequence: You wire $2,691 of your own money to scammers — the 'refund' was never real.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Payment platforms never ask customers to wire back overpayments — they reverse transactions internally.

message.subject: Refund issued — $299.00 to your bankcard ending 8834
message.body:
Hi Dana,

Your refund of $299.00 from Lumina Electronics has been processed.

Refund amount: $299.00
To: bankcard ending 8834
Expected arrival: 5-10 business days
Reference: REF-44102

No action needed. The refund will appear on your statement.

PayCircle

explanation.short: Legitimate refund confirmation from official PayCircle domain with specific transaction details.
tells: Sent from paycircle.com — the official domain
Uses your name and card ending digits
Names the merchant the refund is from
Includes a reference number
Says no action needed — no requests for payment
explanation.safe_move: Wait for the refund to appear on your statement. Contact PayCircle if it doesn't arrive in 10 days.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a standard refund confirmation.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real refund notices come from official domains, name the merchant, and never ask you to send money back.

message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"Hey, it's me — your grandson. I'm in real trouble. I got into a car accident and I've been arrested. I need you to send $3,000 for bail right away. Please don't tell mom and dad — I'm so embarrassed. The bail bondsman needs the money via gift cards. I'm calling from someone else's phone so don't call back. Please, grandma, I really need your help."

red_flags.label: Asks you not to tell anyone
Claims to be calling from another phone

explanation.short: Grandparent scam using a cloned or imitated voice to extract emergency money via gift cards.
tells: Doesn't identify themselves by name — waits for you to say a name
Asks for gift cards — no legitimate bail system accepts gift cards
Demands secrecy so you can't verify the story
Claims a different phone so you can't call back
AI can now clone voices from social media clips
explanation.safe_move: Hang up and call your grandson directly on their real number. Verify with family.
explanation.consequence: You lose $3,000 in gift cards that cannot be recovered once the codes are shared.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: If a family member calls in crisis, hang up and call them back on their known number.

message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"Hey Grandma, it's Marcus. Just calling to let you know I got a flat tire on the highway so I'm going to be about an hour late for dinner. AAA is on the way. Call me back on this number when you get this — it's my regular phone. See you soon! Love you."

explanation.short: Normal voicemail from a family member calling from their own phone with no money request.
tells: States their name upfront — Marcus
Calling from their regular phone number
No money requested
No secrecy demanded
Casual tone — just running late
Invites you to call back
explanation.safe_move: Call Marcus back to acknowledge and adjust dinner plans.
explanation.consequence: Not calling back just means Marcus might wonder if you got the message.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real family calls use their own name, their own phone, and never ask for emergency money via gift cards.

message.body: Congratulations! You've been selected as a $10,000 MegaRewards winner! To claim, pay a $49.99 processing fee: megarewards-claim.com/winner. Offer expires in 2 hours!

red_flags.label: Must pay fee to collect prize

explanation.short: Classic advance-fee scam — you pay $49.99 and the $10,000 prize doesn't exist.
tells: You can't win a contest you didn't enter
Legitimate prizes never require upfront fees
2-hour deadline prevents you from thinking clearly
megarewards-claim.com is not a real company
Sent from a regular phone number
explanation.safe_move: Delete the message. You cannot win a contest you never entered.
explanation.consequence: You lose $49.99, and they now have your credit card number for future charges.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real prizes never require you to pay a fee to collect your winnings.

message.body: BrightMart: You earned a $5 reward from your March purchases! Redeem at checkout in-store or online at brightmart.com/rewards. Expires Apr 30. Reply STOP to unsubscribe.

explanation.short: Standard store rewards notification for a modest earned discount with no fees.
tells: Sent from a short code
Based on your actual purchases
$5 is a realistic store reward
No fee required — just use it at checkout
Links to official domain
Includes STOP to unsubscribe
explanation.safe_move: Use the reward at BrightMart next time you shop, or ignore it.
explanation.consequence: Not using it just means you miss a small discount.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real rewards are modest, based on your activity, and never require fees to claim.

message.body: Hey! I'm interested in the couch you listed. I'll pay full price + $50 extra for holding it. I'll send payment through QuickSend but my app is glitching — can you click this link to accept the payment? sellersafe-pay.com/accept/txn8847. I'm out of town so my movers will pick it up.

red_flags.label: Asks you to click external link
Sending movers instead of coming themselves

explanation.short: Marketplace scam using a fake payment link to steal your credentials or financial info.
tells: Offering extra money is a red flag — nobody overpays willingly
sellersafe-pay.com is a phishing site, not a real payment platform
Avoids in-person meeting
QuickSend doesn't require external links to receive payments
The 'movers' story is a common excuse to avoid face-to-face transactions
explanation.safe_move: Only accept payments through the marketplace platform or in-person cash.
explanation.consequence: The fake link steals your login credentials or payment information.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real buyers don't overpay, don't send external payment links, and are willing to meet locally.

message.body: Hi! Is the couch still available? I'm in the Maplewood area and could come look at it this weekend. Would you take $180 instead of $200? I can do cash or PeerSend at pickup. Thanks!

green_flags.label: No links or weird payment methods

explanation.short: Normal marketplace inquiry from a local buyer who wants to see the item in person.
tells: Mentions being in the local area
Wants to see it before buying
Reasonable price negotiation
Offers cash at pickup — standard for marketplace sales
No links, no external payment sites
explanation.safe_move: Arrange a meetup in a public place during daylight hours.
explanation.consequence: Not responding means you might miss a legitimate sale.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real buyers negotiate reasonably, want to inspect items, and pay with cash or standard apps at pickup.

message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"This is Officer Daniel Wright, badge number TF-4412, calling from the Federal Tax Bureau. Your Federal Benefits number has been flagged for suspicious activity and a warrant has been issued for your arrest. You must call us back immediately at this number to resolve this matter. Failure to respond within 24 hours will result in arrest and seizure of your assets. Do not ignore this call."

red_flags.label: Provides fake badge number for legitimacy

explanation.short: Government impersonation scam using arrest threats to extract money or personal information.
tells: Tax agencies contact you by mail first, never by threatening phone calls
Government agencies don't leave voicemails threatening arrest
The 'badge number' is fake — designed to sound official
Real warrants are served in person, not announced via voicemail
The callback number goes to scammers who will demand payment
explanation.safe_move: Hang up. If concerned, contact the tax agency directly through their official website.
explanation.consequence: Calling back leads to demands for payment via gift cards or wire transfer.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government agencies always contact you by mail first and never threaten arrest by phone.

message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"Hi, this is Maria Torres from the City of Westfield Parks Department. I'm calling about the community garden plot you applied for — plot number 14B. Good news, it's available! Please call us back at 555-432-7100, Monday through Friday, 8 AM to 5 PM, to finalize your reservation. You can also visit us at City Hall, Room 104. Thanks!"

green_flags.label: Caller identifies with full name and department

explanation.short: Routine government call about a specific application with business hours and physical location.
tells: References your specific application and plot number
Provides normal business hours
Offers in-person option at a known location
No threats, urgency, or payment demands
Caller gives full name and department
explanation.safe_move: Call back during business hours or visit City Hall to confirm.
explanation.consequence: Not responding means you may lose your garden plot reservation.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real government contacts reference specific applications, offer business hours, and never threaten you.

message.body: URGENT: ChainVault has detected unauthorized access to your wallet. To secure your funds immediately, connect your wallet at chainvault-security.io/verify and enter your recovery phrase to re-authenticate. Failure to act within 1 hour may result in permanent loss of your assets.

explanation.short: Wallet-draining scam designed to steal your recovery phrase and all cryptocurrency.
tells: No legitimate service EVER asks for your recovery phrase
chainvault-security.io is a phishing domain
Real support never contacts you via DM first
1-hour deadline designed to prevent you from verifying
Entering your recovery phrase gives complete wallet access to scammers
explanation.safe_move: Never enter your recovery phrase anywhere online. Contact support through the official app only.
explanation.consequence: Sharing your recovery phrase means instant, irreversible loss of all crypto in your wallet.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Your recovery phrase is like your master key — no legitimate service will ever ask you to type it in.

message.body: ChainVault v3.2 is now available. This update includes improved transaction speeds and a redesigned portfolio view. Update through your device's app store. Full changelog at chainvault.com/releases. Questions? Visit our help center at chainvault.com/support.

green_flags.label: Does not ask for any credentials

explanation.short: Routine app update announcement from the official account with no credential requests.
tells: From the verified @chainvault account
Does not ask for recovery phrase or any credentials
Directs you to update through the app store — the correct channel
Links to official chainvault.com domain
No urgency or threats
explanation.safe_move: Update through your app store when convenient.
explanation.consequence: Not updating means you miss new features but your funds are safe.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate crypto services announce updates from official accounts and never ask for your recovery phrase.

message.body: Your one-time code is 847291. A VerifyNow agent is on the line to assist you. Please read this code to them to complete your identity verification.

red_flags.label: Asks you to share OTP with someone

explanation.short: Scammers are trying to get your OTP to take over your account.
tells: No legitimate service asks you to read a code to an agent
OTPs are for YOU to enter, never to share verbally
Sent from a regular phone number, not a service short code
explanation.safe_move: Never share OTP codes with anyone, even someone claiming to be support.
explanation.consequence: Sharing the code gives attackers access to your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: One-time codes are for you to enter — never read them aloud or share them.

message.body: NetMobile: We're processing your SIM replacement request. If you did NOT request this, reply CANCEL immediately with your account PIN to stop it.

explanation.short: SIM swap scam trying to trick you into revealing your account PIN.
tells: Carriers never ask for your PIN via text
Sent from a regular number, not a carrier short code
Creating urgency so you reply without thinking
explanation.safe_move: Call your carrier directly using the number on their website.
explanation.consequence: Sharing your PIN lets scammers complete a SIM swap and intercept your calls and texts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Your carrier will never text you asking for your account PIN.

message.body: SecureAuth: Unusual login from Moscow, Russia detected on your email. We've sent a verification code to your phone. Our security team will call you in 2 minutes to walk you through the recovery process.

red_flags.label: Primes you for incoming scam call

explanation.short: Sets up a scam call where they'll ask you to share the OTP they just triggered.
tells: This primes you to trust an incoming call and share your code
Real security services don't call you to 'walk through recovery'
Doesn't name the specific email provider
The code they'll ask for is the one they triggered by trying to log in as you
explanation.safe_move: Ignore the text and the call. Log in to your email directly to check for alerts.
explanation.consequence: Sharing the OTP when they call gives them full access to your email.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Security services send alerts — they don't call you to collect your verification codes.

message.body: Hi sorry I sent a verification code to your number by mistake. Can you please forward it to me? It's for my own account. Thanks so much!

red_flags.label: Asks you to share a code

explanation.short: Classic OTP interception — someone is trying to log into YOUR account and needs the code sent to your phone.
tells: Verification codes are tied to YOUR number, not theirs
You can't 'accidentally' send a code to the wrong number
The code was triggered by someone trying to access your account
explanation.safe_move: Ignore the message. Never forward verification codes.
explanation.consequence: Forwarding the code gives them access to whichever account they're trying to break into.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verification codes sent to your phone are always for your accounts — never share them.

message.body: BreezeBank: Your verification code is 539201. Enter this code in the app to complete your login. Do not share this code with anyone. Code expires in 10 minutes.

green_flags.label: Warns not to share the code
Says to enter in the app

explanation.short: Standard two-factor authentication code from your bank — sent because you just tried to log in.
tells: Sent from a bank short code
Explicitly says 'do not share this code'
Tells you to enter it in the app yourself
Has a reasonable 10-minute expiration
explanation.safe_move: Enter the code in the app if you initiated the login. If you didn't, change your password immediately.
explanation.consequence: If you requested this login, not entering the code just means you can't log in.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate OTP messages tell you to enter the code yourself and warn you not to share it.

message.body: CrestLine Wireless: Your account is being migrated to our upgraded network. To avoid service interruption, confirm your identity by replying with your account PIN and the last 4 of your SSN. Migration completes tonight.

explanation.short: SIM swap setup scam collecting your PIN and SSN to take over your phone account.
tells: Carriers never ask for PIN and SSN via text
Network migrations don't require customer action via SMS
Sent from a regular phone number
Same-night deadline to prevent verification
explanation.safe_move: Call your carrier directly from the number on your bill.
explanation.consequence: With your PIN and SSN, scammers can port your number to their device and intercept all your calls and texts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Carriers never ask for PINs or SSN digits by text — always call them directly to verify.

message.body: CrestLine: You've used 90% of your data for this billing cycle. To add more data or manage your plan, visit crestlinewireless.com/myaccount or call 611.

green_flags.label: Does not ask for any personal info

explanation.short: Standard carrier data usage alert from a short code with no personal info requests.
tells: Sent from a carrier short code
Links to official domain
Suggests 611 — the standard carrier support number
Just informational — no credentials or payment demanded
explanation.safe_move: Log in to your account to manage your data plan if needed.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring it may mean you run out of data and get slower speeds.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real carrier messages come from short codes, link to official sites, and don't ask for personal info.

message.body: NexGen Mobile fraud team: Someone attempted to port your number to another carrier. If this wasn't you, we need to verify your identity to block it. Reply with your NexGen PIN and the last 4 of your SSN. If no response in 30 min, the port will proceed.

red_flags.label: Uses port protection as pretext to enable porting

explanation.short: Sophisticated SIM swap scam using the fear of number porting to extract the exact info needed to port your number.
tells: Ironically, they're asking for the exact info they need to do a port
Carriers don't ask for PIN + SSN via text to stop ports
30-minute deadline prevents verification
Sent from a regular phone number
Real carriers have automated port protection that doesn't work this way
explanation.safe_move: Call your carrier immediately at the number on your bill to verify.
explanation.consequence: Providing PIN + SSN gives scammers exactly what they need to complete the port they claim to be stopping.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: If your carrier alerts you about porting, they'll never ask for credentials via text — call them directly.

message.subject: You have a new shared document

DocuVault File Sharing

explanation.short: Malware disguised as a PDF — the .exe extension means it's a program, not a document.

explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never open files with double extensions — .pdf.exe, .doc.scr, etc. are always malware.

message.subject: Security bulletin: Update your Keystroke app
message.body:
Hi Jordan,

We discovered and patched a vulnerability in Keystroke v4.1. We recommend updating to v4.2 through your device's app store.

What happened: A bug in the autofill feature could expose form data on certain websites.
What we did: Released a patch within 6 hours of discovery.
What you should do: Update through your app store.

No passwords were exposed. Your vault remains encrypted.

Full details: keystroke.io/blog/security-bulletin-april-2026

Keystroke Security Team

green_flags.label: Directs to app store, not a link
Links to public blog post for details

explanation.short: Transparent security bulletin from official Keystroke domain with clear instructions to update via app store.
tells: Sent from keystroke.io — the official domain
Explains the vulnerability clearly and honestly
Directs you to update through the app store, not a download link
Reassures you that passwords are safe
Links to a public blog post for full transparency
explanation.safe_move: Update through your app store and read the blog post if curious.
explanation.consequence: Not updating leaves a known vulnerability on your device.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Trustworthy companies are transparent about vulnerabilities and direct you to official update channels.

message.subject: Payment of $500 received — confirm or dispute
message.body:
You received a payment of $500.00 from Michael Torres.

This payment is pending your confirmation. If you did NOT expect this, the sender may have sent it to the wrong person.

To return the payment, click: zappay-center.com/return-payment

If not returned within 24 hours, the sender will file a dispute and your account may be restricted.

ZapPay Support

red_flags.label: Pressures you to send money back

explanation.short: Fake payment notification designed to trick you into 'returning' money you never received.
tells: Check your ZapPay account — no $500 was received
zappay-center.com is not ZapPay's real domain
The 'return payment' link is a payment page where YOU send money
Real payment disputes are handled by the platform, not by you manually
explanation.safe_move: Log in to your real ZapPay account. If no $500 is there, delete the email.
explanation.consequence: You send $500 of your own money to scammers — the original 'payment' never existed.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always check your actual account balance — if the money isn't there, the notification is fake.

message.body: CashWave: $1,200 was sent from your account to user @unknown_buyer. If unauthorized, reply STOP and call (844) 509-2213 within 1 hour to reverse.

red_flags.label: Wants you to call a scam number

explanation.short: Fake unauthorized transaction alert to lure you into calling a scam support line.
tells: Check your CashWave app — no $1,200 was sent
The phone number connects to scammers who will request remote access
@unknown_buyer is deliberately vague to prevent verification
Real payment apps notify you inside the app with transaction details
1-hour deadline to prevent you from verifying
explanation.safe_move: Open your CashWave app and check your recent transactions.
explanation.consequence: Calling the number leads to scammers who will steal your account credentials or request remote access.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always check the app itself for transaction history — don't trust SMS alerts from phone numbers.

message.subject: You sent $45.00 to @jennpark
message.body:

tells: Sent from cashwave.com — the official domain

Reasonable amount

explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a standard transaction receipt.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real payment confirmations come from official domains and include specific transaction details you recognize.

message.subject: Duplicate payment detected — refund initiated
message.body:
Dear Merchant,

Our system detected a duplicate payment of $3,200.00 from a customer. A refund has been automatically initiated to your account.

However, the refund exceeded the original amount by $3,200.00 due to a processing error. To avoid account suspension, please wire the excess back to:

Recipient: QuickSend Reconciliation
Bank: Pacific National
Routing: 121000248
Account: 9920471833

Please resolve within 48 hours.

QuickSend Business Payments

explanation.short: Advanced overpayment scam targeting businesses — the duplicate payment and refund never happened.
tells: Check your account — no extra money was deposited
Payment processors handle refund errors internally
quicksend-payments.com is a fake domain
Wire transfers are irreversible
The 'reconciliation account' belongs to scammers
explanation.safe_move: Log in to your real payment dashboard to verify. Contact your payment processor directly.
explanation.consequence: You wire $3,200 of your own money to scammers — the 'excess refund' was never real.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Payment processors never ask merchants to wire money back — they handle reconciliation automatically.

message.body: GlobalPost: Your package is on hold due to unpaid customs fee of $3.50. Pay now to release: globalpost-customs.com/pay. Package will be returned in 48 hours.

explanation.short: Fake customs fee scam using a small amount to steal your credit card details.
tells: globalpost-customs.com is a phishing domain

No tracking number provided
Real customs charges are handled at delivery or through official portals
Sent from a regular phone number
explanation.safe_move: Check tracking on the real carrier's website if you're expecting a package.
explanation.consequence: The site captures your credit card for fraudulent charges far exceeding $3.50.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate customs fees are collected at delivery or through official carrier portals, not via text links.

nationexpress-redeliver.com is a phishing domain

Real carriers contact the sender about label issues, not the recipient
Sent from a regular phone number

message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"Mom, it's me. I'm in the hospital — there was an accident at work and I need surgery. The hospital says they need a $5,000 deposit before they can operate. Can you wire it to this account they gave me? I'll text you the details. Please hurry, I'm in a lot of pain. Don't call Dad — I don't want him to worry."

red_flags.label: Uses fear for loved one's safety
Requests wire transfer for medical care
Hospitals don't require deposits via wire

explanation.short: Emergency scam using a cloned or impersonated voice to exploit parental fear.
tells: Doesn't say their own name
Hospitals treat emergencies first — they don't require wire deposits
Demands secrecy to prevent verification
Wire transfers are irreversible
Calling from an unknown number
AI voice cloning can mimic family members from social media audio
explanation.safe_move: Hang up. Call your child directly on their known phone number.
explanation.consequence: You lose $5,000 via an irreversible wire transfer.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: In any family emergency call, hang up and call the person back on their real number.

message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"Hey, it's David. I'm in back-to-back meetings all day and can't take calls. I need you to handle something urgent and confidential. We need to wire $12,000 to a vendor for a rush order before 3 PM or we lose the contract. I'll send you the wire details by email. Don't loop anyone else in — the deal is confidential until it's finalized. Thanks, I'm counting on you."

red_flags.label: Wire details coming by email — likely spoofed

explanation.short: CEO fraud using AI-cloned voice of your boss to authorize a fraudulent wire transfer.
tells: Combines urgency + secrecy + unreachability — classic BEC pattern
Real bosses don't ask employees to secretly wire money
Voice could be cloned from public talks or videos
The follow-up email will be spoofed too
Wire transfers are irreversible
explanation.safe_move: Walk to your boss's office or message them through internal chat to verify — never rely on voicemail alone.
explanation.consequence: $12,000 wired to scammers with no way to recover it.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any urgent, secret payment request — even from someone who sounds like your boss — must be verified through a separate channel.

message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"Hi Jordan, this is Priya from Dr. Patel's office at Riverside Medical. I'm calling to confirm your appointment on Thursday, April 11 at 2:30 PM. If you need to reschedule, please call us back at 555-772-4100 during office hours, Monday through Friday, 8 AM to 5 PM. See you Thursday!"

explanation.short: Standard appointment confirmation from a medical office with specific details.
tells: Names the doctor and office
Specific appointment date and time
Caller identifies herself by name
Provides business hours for callbacks
No money, no personal info requested
No urgency or threats
explanation.safe_move: Call back to confirm or reschedule during business hours.
explanation.consequence: Not confirming might mean your appointment gets released.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate medical calls reference specific appointments, identify the caller, and never ask for payment by phone.

message.body:
[Voicemail transcript]

"Hi, this is Lieutenant Adams from the County Sheriff's office. We have a warrant for your arrest due to failure to appear for jury duty. This is a serious federal offense. However, you can resolve this right now by paying the $1,500 fine over the phone. Call me back immediately at this number. If I don't hear from you by 5 PM today, deputies will be dispatched to your home."

red_flags.label: Asks for phone payment to avoid arrest
Sheriff + federal offense — doesn't make sense
Jury duty fines aren't paid by phone

explanation.short: Law enforcement impersonation scam using arrest threats to extract payment over the phone.
tells: Law enforcement doesn't call to collect fines by phone
Sheriff's office handling 'federal offense' — jurisdictions don't work that way
Missing jury duty doesn't result in immediate arrest
The callback number goes to scammers
Real warrants are served in person, never resolved by phone payment
explanation.safe_move: Hang up. If concerned, call your county clerk's office directly to verify jury duty status.
explanation.consequence: Calling back leads to demands for gift cards or wire transfers to 'pay the fine.'
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Law enforcement never calls to demand phone payment — fines go through the court system.

Payment via QuickSend to a random email

tells: No legitimate company charges an equipment deposit before you start
Recruiter email is QuickMail, not a company domain
Job offered with no interview — real hiring takes time
QuickSend payments are irreversible and a favorite of scammers

explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any job that requires upfront payment is a scam — no exceptions.

spot_flag_options.label: Salary seems very high for the role

Collecting SSN, DOB, and address together enables identity theft
Artificial deadline is designed to prevent you from thinking clearly

explanation.consequence: Your SSN, DOB, and address are enough to open fraudulent credit accounts in your name.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: SSN requests before a job offer are always a red flag — real background checks happen post-offer through vetted services.

red_flags.label: Sends a check, asks you to wire money back

Check amount exceeds what you need to spend

tells: Sending a check and asking you to forward money is the textbook fake check scam
The check will appear to clear initially but bounce days later
Money orders are irreversible — you lose the cash permanently
Legitimate employers do not ask employees to handle cash transfers
explanation.safe_move: Never deposit a check from a stranger and send money elsewhere. Report and ignore.
explanation.consequence: The check bounces after several days. You have already sent $950 in money orders — that money is gone.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any job that sends you a check and asks you to forward money is always a scam.

The domain uses a hyphenated variation, not the real company site

explanation.safe_move: Search the company directly and contact their real HR department to verify the opportunity.

tells: Legitimate employers pay in fiat currency — crypto salaries are a major red flag
Asking for a government ID photo before any interview is identity theft setup
No interview process means there is no real job
Crypto payments are irreversible and untraceable
explanation.safe_move: Never send a photo of your ID to a stranger. Verify any employer through official channels.
explanation.consequence: Your government ID is used to open fraudulent financial accounts or sold on dark web markets.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Crypto salaries and upfront ID requests are always scam signals — real jobs do not work this way.

framing: You listed a couch for sale online. This DM just came in.

spot_flag_options.label: Offers to pay more than asking price
Says they are too busy to pick up in person
Wants to close the deal today

message.body: Hi! Is the couch still available? I want it for my son who just moved. I can send $350 right now — I know you said $280, but I want to make sure you hold it. My mover will come pick it up tomorrow. Can you send me your PeerSend?

red_flags.label: Sends someone else to pick it up
Asks for your payment handle immediately
Never wants to meet you directly

explanation.short: Overpaying is a classic setup — they send a fake payment, then ask you to refund the difference.
tells: Offering over the asking price is the hallmark of an overpayment scam
Sending a third party to pick up keeps them anonymous
Requesting your PeerSend before any agreement is made is a data collection tactic
Real buyers negotiate down, not up
explanation.safe_move: Only accept cash in person or use a verified payment platform with buyer/seller protection. Never refund a difference.
explanation.consequence: They send a fake PeerSend screenshot or reversible payment, then ask you to send back the overage. You ship the item or hand it off and the payment reverses.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any buyer who offers more than you asked is running a scam — the overpayment is fake.

framing: You are selling a used laptop online. This message came in.

spot_flag_options.label: Sends a link to collect your payment info

message.body: Hey, I am really interested in the laptop! I am out of state but can pay right now. Click this to receive payment: pay-direct-transfer.com/receive/rt8821. Takes 2 minutes. Then I will arrange shipping at my cost!

red_flags.label: Sends a suspicious link to receive payment
pay-direct-transfer.com is not a real payment service
Buyer conveniently cannot meet in person

explanation.short: Legitimate payment apps do not send links to sellers — that link is a phishing page to steal your banking info.
tells: No real payment platform sends a link for the seller to click to receive money
pay-direct-transfer.com is not affiliated with any legitimate service
Out-of-state buyers who cannot meet are trying to force remote payment methods
Real CashLink or PeerSend transactions are initiated by the buyer in their own app — never via a link
explanation.safe_move: Only accept payment through official apps you open yourself — never click a link someone else sends to receive money.
explanation.consequence: The link collects your banking credentials or installs malware. You lose the laptop and your account access.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: To receive money, you open the app yourself — you never click a link the buyer sends.

framing: You are buying concert tickets from someone online.

spot_flag_options.label: Cannot transfer tickets through official platform
Tickets are priced well below market
Seller is in a hurry to sell

message.body: Hey! Still have 2 floor tickets for Saturday. $85 each (face value). My app is glitching so I cannot transfer on the platform. Send via QuickSend and I will screenshot you the barcodes. Fast sale — got another person interested.

red_flags.label: Only accepts QuickSend — no buyer protection
Convenient excuse for not using official transfer
Offers screenshots of barcodes instead of real transfer

explanation.short: QuickSend has no buyer protection — once sent, money cannot be recovered. The "barcodes" will be fake or already used.
tells: QuickSend is irreversible — scammers insist on it because you cannot dispute the charge
The "app glitch" excuse is standard to avoid traceable official transfers
Ticket barcodes can be reused, screenshot to multiple buyers, or simply fake
Urgency with a competing buyer is a pressure tactic to prevent you from thinking
explanation.safe_move: Only buy tickets through official platforms with buyer protection. Never pay via QuickSend for tickets.
explanation.consequence: You send the money and receive fake or already-used barcodes. QuickSend will not refund you.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Ticket sellers who cannot use the official platform transfer are always suspicious — QuickSend is not a safe way to buy tickets from strangers.

framing: You posted a rental listing. This message came in.

spot_flag_options.label: Asks you to wire the deposit
Wants to move in very quickly

message.body: Hello, I am relocating from abroad for work and your property looks perfect. I am ready to pay 3 months upfront. My employer will send a cashiers check for the full amount. Please wire back the extra once received. Can we proceed?

red_flags.label: Asks you to wire back money after receiving a check
Employer sends a cashiers check — classic fake check setup
Cannot view the property in person
Paying more than needed, expecting wire back

tells: Wiring money back after receiving a check is the fake check playbook
Cashier checks can be forged and take days to bounce after appearing cleared
Overseas applicants who cannot view property are a signature rental scam setup
No legitimate tenant has their employer overpay and ask for a refund
explanation.safe_move: Only accept verified electronic bank transfers. Wait 10+ business days for any check to fully clear before releasing any funds.
explanation.consequence: The check bounces a week later. You have already wired thousands of dollars that cannot be recovered.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never wire money back after receiving a check from a stranger — the check will always eventually bounce.

framing: You are selling a phone on a resale app.

spot_flag_options.label: Buyer sends a QR code to complete payment
Asks for more photos of the device
Tries to negotiate the price down
