VaultPay is not a recognized payment platform
Requires you to enter account info to receive money
Pays full price immediately without any questions

explanation.short: QR codes that require you to enter banking info to receive money are always phishing — you should never need to enter account info to get paid.
tells: Scanning a QR code to receive money never requires entering your account information
VaultPay does not exist as a legitimate payment service
Phishing QR codes redirect you to fake pages that steal credentials
Paying full price with no questions is a sign the buyer does not care about the item — only your info
explanation.safe_move: Use only known payment apps opened directly by you — never scan a QR code from a buyer to receive payment.
explanation.consequence: The QR code leads to a credential-harvesting page. Your banking login is captured and your account is drained.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: You never need to enter your account info to receive money — if a payment requires that, it is a scam.

framing: Someone is responding to your garage sale post.

spot_flag_options.label: Sends a prepaid label and asks you to cover a fee
Wants to buy several items at once
Sends a friend to pick up
Mentions using an online payment service

message.body: Interested in the table, chairs, and the lamp — take all three for $200? I can send a prepaid shipping label. I just need you to pay the $15 label activation fee to ShipNow first, then I will PeerSend you $200 right after.

red_flags.label: Asks you to pay a fee to activate a prepaid label
Your money goes out before their payment comes in
No real carrier charges a label activation fee
Payment promised only after you spend money first

explanation.short: Prepaid labels do not have activation fees — this fee goes straight to the scammer, and the PeerSend never arrives.
tells: No legitimate shipping carrier charges a fee to activate a prepaid label
Requiring you to pay first before receiving payment is always a red flag
Garage sale items are usually picked up in person — remote buyers add friction for a reason
Once you pay the $15 fee, the buyer disappears
explanation.safe_move: For large items, insist on cash at pickup. Never pay any fee before receiving payment.
explanation.consequence: You pay $15 and the buyer goes silent. No PeerSend arrives. The $15 is gone.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Prepaid labels never require fees from the recipient — any label fee request is a scam.

framing: You listed a bicycle for $180. This came in.

spot_flag_options.label: Wants to pay with gift cards
Wants to complete the transaction quickly

message.body: Hey, I will take the bike for $180. I only have NovaMart gift cards right now — can I pay you with those? I have two $100 cards. You can use them anywhere.

red_flags.label: Wants to pay with gift cards
Two $100 cards for a $180 item creates change scam risk
Gift cards may be stolen, drained, or fraudulently obtained
Cash or verified app is always possible for local pickup

explanation.short: Gift cards are untraceable and frequently stolen or fraudulent — they are not a legitimate payment method between individuals.
tells: No legitimate buyer pays for marketplace items with gift cards
Gift cards may have already been drained or obtained through fraud
If the cards are declined or invalid, you have given away your bike for nothing
Cash or an app like PeerSend or CashLink are always available for real local buyers
explanation.safe_move: Only accept cash or verified app payments for in-person sales. Never accept gift cards.
explanation.consequence: You hand over the bike and the gift cards turn out to be empty or invalid.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Gift cards are for gifts — never for paying strangers for marketplace items.

framing: You are selling furniture online and got this message.

spot_flag_options.label: Asks you to verify your identity to receive payment

message.body: Hi, I love your dining set and want to purchase it immediately. I use SecurePay escrow for large purchases — just verify your identity at securepay-seller-verify.com and they will release the $650 to you within the hour.

red_flags.label: Asks you to verify identity on a third-party site
securepay-seller-verify.com is not a real escrow service
Domain designed to look official but is fake
Payment released within the hour — pressure to act fast

explanation.short: Fake escrow services ask for your personal info and banking details under the guise of verifying you to receive payment.
tells: Real escrow services are used for business transactions, not resale marketplace furniture
securepay-seller-verify.com is a phishing site, not a real payment service
Clicking to verify your identity hands over your personal and banking information
Urgency pressure is designed to prevent you from researching the site first
explanation.safe_move: Insist on cash or verified payment apps for local sales. Never click a link to receive payment.
explanation.consequence: The site collects your personal and banking info. No $650 is ever released.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: You never need to verify your identity on a website to receive marketplace payment — that is always a scam.

framing: You work in accounting. This invoice just hit your inbox.

spot_flag_options.label: Vendor requests payment to a new bank account

message.subject: UPDATED: Invoice #GS-4491 - New Payment Instructions
message.body:
Hi,

Please note we have updated our banking details effective immediately. All outstanding invoices should be paid to our new account:

Bank: Apex Federal Credit Union
Routing: 021000089
Account: 7734901122

Invoice #GS-4491 for $14,300 is due by end of week. Please confirm receipt of these new instructions.

Rachel Voss
Accounts Receivable, Hartfield Supply Co.

red_flags.label: Vendor requests payment to a new bank account
grainger-supply.co is not the real vendor domain
End of week deadline creates pressure
Banking change sent via email with no prior notice

explanation.short: Legitimate vendors change banking details through verified channels — never via a single unsolicited email.
tells: Fraudsters intercept vendor email threads and swap in their own account details
The domain grainger-supply.co differs from the real vendor domain
Real banking changes require phone verification with a known contact
Any urgent payment change request via email should be treated as suspicious
explanation.safe_move: Call the vendor using a phone number from your existing records — not one in this email — to verify the change.
explanation.consequence: $14,300 is wired to the scammer. By the time the real vendor reports non-payment, the money is gone.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify payment detail changes by phone using a number you already have on file — never trust an emailed banking change.

framing: An invoice arrived for software your company uses.

spot_flag_options.label: Your company never ordered this service
Invoice amount is higher than usual

message.subject: Annual Renewal Invoice - DataVault Pro Business - $4,800
message.body:
Dear Valued Customer,

This is your annual renewal notice for DataVault Pro Business (5 seats, Enterprise tier).

Amount due: $4,800.00
Due date: Within 10 business days

Per your service agreement, failure to pay by the due date will result in data deletion. To pay or dispute, visit: datavault-pro-invoicing.com/renew

DataVault Pro Billing

red_flags.label: No record of subscribing to this service
Threatens data deletion to create fear
datavault-pro-invoicing.com is not a real vendor site
No account details or named contact

explanation.short: Phantom invoice scams bill for services you never ordered, counting on finance teams to pay without checking.
tells: If you have no record of the service, the invoice is fraudulent
Threatening data deletion is a fear tactic to pressure immediate payment
The billing domain is not affiliated with any real software company
Legitimate SaaS renewals include account IDs and named contacts
explanation.safe_move: Search your company records for any contract with this vendor. If none exists, do not pay and report the email.
explanation.consequence: $4,800 paid to a company you never contracted with — recovery is unlikely.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify invoices against purchase orders or signed contracts before authorizing payment.

framing: Your CEO just forwarded you this with "Please process ASAP."

spot_flag_options.label: CEO is pressuring an urgent wire transfer
Request comes on a Friday afternoon

message.subject: Fwd: Wire Needed Today - Confidential
message.body:
Please handle this wire today. New strategic partner — keeping quiet until announcement next week. Wire $38,500 to:

Beneficiary: NexCoin Holdings Ltd
Bank: Offshore Trust Bank, Cayman Islands
Account: 0049112233

Do not discuss with anyone until I give the go-ahead. I am in meetings all day.

- Marcus

red_flags.label: CEO demands urgent wire with secrecy instruction
Wire destination is an offshore Cayman Islands account
Told not to discuss with anyone
CEO email domain does not match company domain

explanation.short: This is a Business Email Compromise (BEC) scam — the CEO email is spoofed and the secrecy demand prevents verification.
tells: Real executives do not demand secrecy around legitimate wire transfers
Offshore Cayman accounts are a major red flag for fraudulent wires
The CEO email domain is vertex-electronics-corp.net, not the real company domain
BEC scams frequently use Friday timing when staff are rushed and less likely to verify
explanation.safe_move: Call the CEO directly on a known phone number to verify. Never wire money based solely on an email instruction.
explanation.consequence: $38,500 is wired offshore and is unrecoverable. BEC scams cost businesses billions annually.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any wire transfer request with secrecy instructions is a red flag — always verify by phone with a number from your own records.

framing: You manage vendor payments. This came in this morning.

spot_flag_options.label: No purchase order or contract number referenced
Invoice is attached as a file
Email is from someone new at the vendor

message.subject: Invoice #SA-7742 - Q1 Consulting Services
message.body:
Hi,

Please find attached Invoice #SA-7742 for Q1 consulting services totaling $9,200.

Kindly process payment at your earliest convenience. If you have any questions, just reply to this email.

Thank you,
Sandra Osei
Summit Analytics Accounts

red_flags.label: No purchase order or contract number referenced
Sandra Osei is not a known contact at this vendor
Q1 consulting is vague with no detail
Pushes for quick payment without detail

explanation.short: Legitimate vendor invoices always reference a purchase order or signed contract. A vague invoice with no PO is a classic BEC invoice-fraud setup.
tells: No purchase order or contract number tied to the invoice
Sandra Osei is not a named contact you recognize at this vendor
"Q1 consulting services" is vague — no line items, no delivery dates
Soft pressure to pay "at your earliest convenience" with no detail to verify against
explanation.safe_move: Don't pay until you match the invoice to an open PO. Call the vendor on a known main line to verify Sandra Osei and the invoice.
explanation.consequence: Payment is wired to the scammer. By the time the real vendor asks about the outstanding invoice, the money is gone.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Every invoice should reference a PO or contract. No PO, no payment — verify by phone first.

framing: A subscription renewal notice for office software just arrived.

spot_flag_options.label: Asks you to call a phone number to cancel
Renewal amount is higher than expected
Says your card will be auto-charged
No account number or username referenced

message.subject: Your NexCloud Business Plan Renews in 24 Hours - $899.00
message.body:
Dear Customer,

Your NexCloud Business Plan will automatically renew tomorrow for $899.00.

If you did not authorize this renewal or wish to cancel, please call our billing team immediately:

1-888-555-0147

Failure to call within 24 hours will result in the charge being processed.

NexCloud Billing Department

red_flags.label: Asks you to call a number to cancel — callback trap
No account number or login details referenced

explanation.short: The callback trap gets you on the phone with a scammer posing as billing support who then asks for payment or remote access.
tells: Real software companies let you cancel through your account dashboard — not a phone call
The domain nexcloud-renewal-center.com is not affiliated with the real service
24-hour urgency is designed to prevent you from checking your actual account
No legitimate billing notice omits your account number or username
explanation.safe_move: Log into your actual account at the real domain and check your subscription status there.
explanation.consequence: Calling the number connects you with a scammer who will ask for payment info or remote access to process your cancellation.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Manage subscriptions through your account dashboard — never by calling a number in an unsolicited email.

framing: Your company uses a cleaning service. This invoice arrived.

spot_flag_options.label: Amount is double what was agreed
Email is from a new contact
Requests wire transfer instead of usual check
Claims the invoice is past due

message.subject: Invoice #CP-1183 - March Services - PAST DUE
message.body:
Hello,

Our records show Invoice #CP-1183 for March cleaning services ($1,450) remains unpaid and is now 14 days past due.

To avoid a service interruption, please remit payment via wire transfer to our new banking account:

Bank: Granite Credit Union
Routing: 031100209
Account: 5529003341

We have updated our payment method and no longer accept checks.

Thank you,
Maria Santos
CleanPro Services

red_flags.label: Suddenly requires wire transfer instead of check
New banking account provided via email
Past due notice creates urgency to pay quickly
cleanpro-svcs.net may differ from real vendor domain

explanation.short: Switching to wire-only payment with new banking details via email is a hallmark of vendor impersonation fraud.
tells: Legitimate vendors do not suddenly stop accepting checks by email notice
New banking details sent via email without phone verification are always suspicious
Past due notices create pressure to pay quickly without double-checking
Wire transfers are irreversible and preferred by fraudsters for this reason
explanation.safe_move: Call your regular contact at the cleaning service using the number you have on file to verify the payment change.
explanation.consequence: $1,450 is wired to a fraudulent account. The real vendor calls weeks later asking about non-payment.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verify any payment method change by phone before processing — never trust a banking change sent only by email.

framing: Your small business got this from a legal services firm.

spot_flag_options.label: You never hired this law firm
Amount is small enough to pay without scrutiny

message.subject: Outstanding Balance - Legal Services - $340.00
message.body:
Dear Business Owner,

Our records indicate an outstanding balance of $340.00 for legal consultation services rendered in Q4.

This balance must be paid within 5 business days to avoid referral to collections and potential impact to your business credit.

Pay online: whitmorelegal-billing.com/pay

Whitmore Legal Group
Billing Department

red_flags.label: No record of hiring this firm
whitmorelegal-billing.com is a separate payment site
Addressed to Dear Business Owner, not by name

explanation.short: Phantom legal bills exploit fear of collections and business credit damage to get small payments without scrutiny.
tells: You would know if you hired a law firm — if you did not, the invoice is fraudulent
Collections threats for a $340 bill from a firm you never hired is a pressure tactic
The payment site is a separate domain from any real law firm
Real legal invoices include matter numbers, attorney names, and itemized services
explanation.safe_move: Search your records for any engagement with this firm. If none exists, do not pay and report to the FTC.
explanation.consequence: $340 paid to fraudsters — small enough that most businesses pay without investigating.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always match invoices to signed contracts or purchase orders — if there is no record, do not pay.

framing: You handle accounts payable. This came from a known vendor.

spot_flag_options.label: Sender domain ends in .co instead of .com
Pushes you to open the file before any other check

message.subject: Invoice Q1-2026 - Please Review and Approve
message.body:
Hi,

Attached is our Q1 invoice for your review and approval. Please open the file to verify line items before processing.

Total: $7,600

Let me know if you have any questions.

Tom Reeves
Vertex Electronics

red_flags.label: vertex-electronics.co instead of .com — a lookalike domain
Specifically asks you to open the file before processing
No purchase order or contract number in the invoice

explanation.short: The sender's domain ends in .co — one letter off from the real .com domain. Lookalike domains are how scammers impersonate known vendors.
tells: vertex-electronics.co is NOT the same as vertex-electronics.com — attackers register near-identical domains
The email pushes you to open the attachment before doing any verification
No purchase order or contract number is referenced for the $7,600 invoice
"Hi" with no name — a real vendor contact would address you directly
explanation.safe_move: Compare the sender's domain character-by-character to the real vendor's. Call Tom on a number you already have — not one from this email — before opening anything.
explanation.consequence: Opening the attachment installs malware. Even without opening it, replying confirms the account is active and invites more targeted attacks.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: For any invoice, read the sender's domain letter by letter. .co vs .com, -electronics vs .electronics, rn vs m — one character is enough.

framing: Someone new matched with you on a dating app.

spot_flag_options.label: Quickly pivots to discussing crypto investments

message.body: Hi! I love your profile. I am actually a financial analyst based in Singapore right now. The markets here are incredible — I have been making great returns on a crypto platform my mentor showed me. I think you would do well too. Want me to show you how it works? I made $4,200 last week alone.

red_flags.label: Quickly pivots to a crypto investment opportunity
Conveniently located overseas and unavailable in person
Quotes specific large earnings to build credibility
Mentions a mysterious mentor who taught them

explanation.short: This is a pig butchering scam — build trust first, then lure into a fake investment platform.
tells: Pivoting from romance to investment talk within the first few messages is the pig butchering playbook
The platform will show fake gains to encourage larger deposits
When you try to withdraw, fees and taxes appear that must be paid first
No one in a genuine relationship opens with investment advice
explanation.safe_move: Do not engage with investment suggestions from people you have only met online. Block and report.
explanation.consequence: You deposit money into a fake platform that shows fake gains. When you try to withdraw, the platform disappears.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any new online contact who pivots to investment opportunities is running a pig butchering scam.

framing: You have been chatting with someone online for two weeks.

spot_flag_options.label: Making lots of future plans together

message.body: I am so sorry I keep missing our video call times — the connection on the rig is terrible. I have shown you my photos, you know my face! I feel so close to you already. I cannot wait to meet when I get back. Speaking of which — I need a small favor. My bank account is frozen internationally. Could you send $300 to help me get my documents processed? I will pay it back double.

red_flags.label: Consistently avoids video calls with excuses
Asks for money after building emotional connection
Claims bank account is frozen internationally

explanation.short: Someone who consistently avoids video after weeks of contact is almost certainly using fake photos.
tells: Real people can manage at least one video call in two weeks
Offshore/rig job is a classic cover to explain no in-person meeting
The double repayment promise is a manipulation tactic
Once you send money the requests escalate — this is never a one-time ask
explanation.safe_move: Insist on a live video call. If they refuse with any excuse, stop contact immediately.
explanation.consequence: The $300 is gone. Follow-up requests will escalate to thousands of dollars over time.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Anyone who cannot do a single live video call after weeks of contact is using a fake identity.

framing: Someone reached out on a social platform after seeing your post.

spot_flag_options.label: Immediately wants to move to a different app
Shares a lot of personal info immediately
Claims to share all your interests

message.body: Hey! I saw your post and you seem like an amazing person. I do not use this app much — can we move to ChatMe? My username is james_nyc_real. I prefer it for private conversations.

red_flags.label: Immediately wants to move to ChatMe or another app
Frames the move as wanting privacy
New account with no post history

explanation.short: Moving off platform immediately removes the scammer from any reporting or accountability mechanisms.
tells: Scammers move to chat apps because they cannot be reported there as easily
A real person interested in you would engage on the platform where they found you
New accounts with no history are often freshly created for scamming
Privacy framing makes the request seem reasonable when it is not
explanation.safe_move: Stay on the original platform. If they insist on moving, disengage.
explanation.consequence: On ChatMe, they build rapport and eventually request money, gift cards, or compromising photos.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Anyone who immediately asks to move off-platform is trying to escape accountability — do not follow.

framing: You have been talking with someone online for a month.

spot_flag_options.label: Asks you to pay customs fees for a package they sent

message.body: I sent you a surprise gift — jewelry and some chocolates. The courier just called me saying it is held at customs and needs a $180 release fee to be delivered. I am so embarrassed to ask but could you pay it? I will send you the money back the moment I can access my international account.

red_flags.label: Asks you to pay a customs fee for their gift to you
Cannot pay themselves due to international account issues
Uses embarrassment framing to lower your guard
Promises to repay once account is accessible

explanation.short: The customs fee scam creates a gift as a pretext to get money from you — there is no package.
tells: No real gift-sender asks the recipient to pay customs fees
The international account excuse is a recurring theme in romance scams
Customs fees are never collected by random couriers calling your contact
The gift creates emotional obligation that makes the fee request feel reasonable
explanation.safe_move: Do not pay any fee. Real packages held in customs notify the recipient directly via official channels.
explanation.consequence: You pay $180 and no package ever arrives. The scammer may repeat this with new gift stories.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: You are never responsible for paying customs fees on a gift someone else sent you — that request is always a scam.

framing: You matched with someone and have been texting for a week.

spot_flag_options.label: Quickly escalates to exchanging intimate photos
Shares their own photos first unprompted

message.body: I feel so comfortable with you already. I am not usually this forward but I want to share something personal with you. I sent you a photo — I hope that is ok. Would you want to share one back? I promise it stays between us.

red_flags.label: Pushes for intimate photo exchange after one week
Promises it stays private — a false guarantee
Sends photo first to lower your inhibitions

explanation.short: This is a sextortion setup — the photo they sent is fake, and yours will be used as leverage to extort money.
tells: Escalating to intimate content within a week of meeting online is a major red flag
The promise of privacy is meaningless — you have no way to enforce it
Sending a photo first lowers your guard and creates a sense of reciprocity
After you send, they reveal they have screenshotted and threaten to share unless you pay
explanation.safe_move: Never share intimate photos with someone you have not met in person and established real trust with.
explanation.consequence: Your photo is used to extort money. Threats to send it to your contacts, employer, or family follow.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Once an intimate photo leaves your device, you lose all control over it — never send one to an online stranger.

framing: You have been chatting with someone for three weeks.

spot_flag_options.label: Asks you to pay for their bankcard to visit
Has been saying they will visit soon

message.body: I have been saving to come see you. I finally booked the flight! But the visa office here is asking for a $420 processing deposit that I need to pay by tomorrow or lose my slot. My savings account takes 5 days to clear. Could you send it via QuickSend? I will pay you back as soon as I land.

red_flags.label: Asks for money to pay a bankcard fee
Artificial tomorrow deadline to prevent scrutiny
Savings account clearing time is a convenient excuse

explanation.short: The visit fee scam creates anticipation of finally meeting, then monetizes your excitement.
tells: Governments collect bankcard fees directly — not through personal transfers from third parties
The deadline is artificial and designed to prevent you from researching
QuickSend is irreversible and preferred by scammers for that reason
The visit never happens — the scammer will have a new emergency after you pay
explanation.safe_move: Never send money to fund a stranger's travel to visit you. If the relationship is real, the visit will happen without your financial help.
explanation.consequence: You send $420 and the visit is cancelled with a new excuse. More fee requests follow.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: bankcard fees are paid directly to governments — anyone asking you to fund their travel expenses is running a scam.

framing: You are on a dating app and received this opening message.

spot_flag_options.label: Profile story is suspiciously perfect and detailed
Opening message could apply to anyone

message.body: Hi! I came across your profile and you really caught my attention. You seem like such an interesting and genuine person. I am a widower with one daughter, working as a petroleum engineer. I believe in real connection. Would love to get to know you better.

red_flags.label: Opening message is generic and could apply to anyone
Widower petroleum engineer is a common romance scam profile type
Emphasis on genuine connection is a manipulation setup
Nothing in the message references your actual profile

explanation.short: Generic openers sent to many targets, combined with a widower engineer story, are classic romance scam profile patterns.
tells: The message contains nothing specific to your profile — it is copy-pasted to many people
Widower petroleum engineer is one of the most common romance scam personas
Emphasizing genuine connection early is emotional priming before the eventual ask
A real interested person would mention something specific they noticed about you
explanation.safe_move: Ask something specific that requires knowledge of your profile. Scammers cannot answer naturally.
explanation.consequence: Extended emotional grooming leads to a money request weeks or months later.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A message that could have been sent to anyone was sent to everyone — it is a scam template.

framing: You have been in an online relationship for two months.

spot_flag_options.label: Insurance does not cover the treatment

message.body: I am so scared. I collapsed on my way to work and I am in the hospital here in Dubai. They are saying I need emergency surgery but require a $2,500 deposit before they will operate. I have no family here. You are the only one I trust. Please help me — I do not know what to do.

red_flags.label: Sudden medical emergency requiring urgent payment
Stranded abroad with no family or support
Hospital demanding a deposit before surgery
You are the only person they can turn to

explanation.short: The medical emergency abroad is one of the most emotionally powerful romance scam tactics — hospitals do not withhold emergency surgery for deposits.
tells: Hospitals in most countries cannot legally withhold emergency surgery for payment
The isolation framing makes you feel uniquely responsible
The large specific amount creates urgency and legitimacy
After you pay, recovery leads to another emergency or the contact disappears
explanation.safe_move: Contact the hospital directly using a number you find independently to verify. Do not send money based on this message alone.
explanation.consequence: $2,500 sent and the emergency resolves — until the next one, or the contact vanishes entirely.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No hospital withholds emergency surgery for an upfront deposit — that claim is always false.

framing: You donated to a disaster relief fund last year. This arrived.

spot_flag_options.label: Charity name is vague and generic

message.subject: Urgent: Help Flood Victims Today
message.body:
Dear Friend,

Floods in the Southeast have left thousands homeless. Your gift of $50 can provide emergency shelter for a family tonight.

For fastest processing, please donate using a NovaMart or retail gift card — just scratch the back and email us the code. 100% goes directly to victims.

American Relief Fund

red_flags.label: Requests donation via gift card codes
Vague charity name with no verifiable history
Asks you to email gift card codes

explanation.short: No legitimate charity accepts gift card codes as donations — this is a gift card scam using a disaster as cover.
tells: Legitimate charities accept credit cards or checks — never gift card codes
Gift card codes emailed to a stranger are instantly redeemable and unrecoverable
The charity name is generic enough to seem real but has no public record
Real charities are listed on Charity Navigator or GuideStar
explanation.safe_move: Donate only through verified charities found on Charity Navigator or GuideStar. Never use gift cards.
explanation.consequence: The gift card codes are redeemed immediately. Your money is gone and no donation reaches any victim.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Gift card donation requests are always scams — legitimate charities never ask for them.

framing: You received this around the holidays.

spot_flag_options.label: Charity name closely mimics a well-known organization

message.subject: Give Hope This Holiday Season
message.body:
This holiday season, 7-year-old Maya is fighting leukemia without health insurance. A gift of just $25 provides a week of treatment supplies.

Your donation is tax-deductible before December 31st. Give at: childrens-cancer-care-fund.com/donate

Childrens Cancer Care Fund

red_flags.label: Name mimics real cancer charities but is unverifiable
Donation site has no connection to a known charity
Uses a named child story that cannot be verified

explanation.short: Fake charities mimic real ones close enough that donors do not check — always verify before giving.
tells: Legitimate charities always include their EIN on donation requests
The organization name is close to real charities but has no public Tax Agency registration
Real charities have years of public financial records on GuideStar
Emotional child stories are the most effective lever for bypassing scrutiny
explanation.safe_move: Search the charity name on Charity Navigator or the tax agency Tax Exempt Organization Search before donating.
explanation.consequence: Your donation goes to scammers, not sick children. The story and the child are fabricated.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always look up a charity on Charity Navigator before giving — a 30-second check protects your donation.

framing: A stranger messaged you on social media.

spot_flag_options.label: Person collecting donations personally, not through a charity
Shares photos of people in need
Claims to be helping a local community
Responds very quickly to your questions

message.body: Hi, I am organizing food boxes for families in our neighborhood who lost jobs. We have 40 families this week and I am $200 short of our goal. If you can send anything to my PeerSend @marcus_helps it would mean the world. Every dollar counts!

red_flags.label: Collecting donations into a personal payment account
No registered charity or organization backing the effort
Cannot verify the 40 families or the need
Sends money to a personal handle, not an org

explanation.short: Collecting charity donations into a personal payment account is unaccountable — there is no way to verify the money reaches anyone in need.
tells: Legitimate community drives collect through verified platforms like HelpFund or 501c3 accounts
Sending to a personal PeerSend handle gives you no receipt and no accountability
The specific $200 shortfall creates a feeling that your gift will complete the goal
Real community organizations have names, websites, and transparent records
explanation.safe_move: If you want to help, find a verified local food bank and donate through their official channel.
explanation.consequence: Your money goes to the individual with no evidence it ever reaches any families.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Charitable giving through personal payment accounts has no accountability — always use verified organizations.

framing: You got this after a major earthquake was in the news.

A generous donor is MATCHING all gifts in the next 48 hours. Send your donation now to maximize impact:

cryptocurrency: bc1q9k4m7x2p...
CoinVault: cvault_relief_fund

Global Earthquake Relief

red_flags.label: Accepts crypto donations — untraceable and irrecoverable

No EIN, address, or leadership listed

tells: Crypto donations to charities are irrecoverable if the org is fake
Real matching drives are run by established foundations with public records
Disaster-named charities are often registered hours after news breaks

explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Stick to established disaster relief organizations — new ones that appear immediately after disasters are usually scams.

framing: This came from a veterans organization.

Uses military imagery and patriotic language
Asks for a monthly recurring gift

message.subject: 100% of Your Gift Goes Directly to Veterans
message.body:
Unlike other charities, we have ZERO overhead. Every penny you give goes straight to veterans in need — no admin fees, no fundraising costs.

Give $30 a month and provide a homeless veteran with meals for an entire month.

Donate: avsa-donate.org

American Veterans Support Alliance

No EIN or public Tax Agency registration
Name designed to sound official and established
Disparages other charities to seem more legitimate

explanation.short: 100% overhead-free claims are mathematically impossible — all organizations have operational costs. This claim signals a scam.
tells: No legitimate charity has zero overhead — staff, banking, and compliance all cost money
The 100% claim is designed to make you feel other charities are wasteful by comparison
The name sounds official but has no history in Tax Agency nonprofit records
Real veterans charities like DAV and VFW have decades of public financial disclosures
explanation.safe_move: Search the organization on Charity Navigator. Reputable veterans charities have ratings and financial disclosures.
explanation.consequence: Your recurring donation goes to scammers indefinitely until you cancel.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any charity claiming 100% goes to the cause is lying — real orgs have overhead, and transparency about it is the sign of legitimacy.

framing: A charity you donated to last year sent this update.

message.body:
Dear Supporter,

Thanks to donors like you, we served 12,000 children this year. As we close the books on 2025, a board member has offered to match large gifts of $500 or more — wired directly to our new account for tax efficiency.

red_flags.label: Requests wire transfer directly to a bank account

tells: Charitable donations go through payment processors, not direct wires

explanation.safe_move: Donate only through the charity's official website, which you find by searching directly — not through email links.
explanation.consequence: Your wire transfer reaches a fraudulent account. No matching gift, no tax receipt, no donation to the cause.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Charitable donations never go by wire transfer to a bank account — always use the official donation page.

framing: You got a thank-you email from a charity you supposedly donated to.

Dear Friend,

Thank you for your generous $75 donation to Hope Kids Foundation last quarter. Your gift helped provide school supplies to 3 children.

As we enter our year-end campaign, would you consider doubling your impact with another gift?

Hope Kids Foundation

red_flags.label: No record of ever donating to this organization

Scammers send these broadly hoping some recipients will assume they forgot donating

explanation.safe_move: Check your bank or credit card records. If there is no $75 charge from this org, do not engage.
explanation.consequence: You make an additional donation based on a fake prior gift — your money goes to scammers.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Check your records before acting on any thank-you for a donation you do not remember making.

framing: You got a DM from a crypto wallet service.

message.body: Your CoinVault wallet has been flagged for suspicious activity. To restore access, you must verify ownership by providing your 12-word recovery phrase at: coinvault-verify-wallet.com. Failure to verify within 2 hours will result in permanent wallet lock.

red_flags.label: Support via DM, not through official app
coinvault-verify-wallet.com is not the real site

explanation.short: Your seed phrase is the master key to your entire wallet — no legitimate service ever needs it.
tells: Your seed phrase gives complete control over your wallet to whoever has it
No legitimate crypto service requests your seed phrase for any reason
Real wallet support happens through the official app, never via social DM
The domain is a phishing site designed to capture your phrase
explanation.safe_move: Never enter your seed phrase anywhere except when recovering your wallet locally. Close this tab immediately.
explanation.consequence: Entering your seed phrase gives the attacker full control. All funds are drained within minutes.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Your seed phrase is like a master password and bank PIN combined — guard it absolutely and share it with no one.

framing: A stranger DMed you about a crypto opportunity.

spot_flag_options.label: Requires a fee to withdraw earnings

message.body: Hi! Join our VaultChain liquidity mining program. Guaranteed 15% weekly returns on your deposit. Minimum $500 to start. Over 8,000 members already earning. Withdraw anytime — no lock-up period. DM for invite link.

red_flags.label: Withdraw anytime promise that will later have fees
Invite-only via DM adds artificial exclusivity

explanation.short: No investment legitimately guarantees 15% weekly returns — that is 780% annually, which no real asset produces.
tells: Guaranteed returns in any investment are a scam signal — all real investments carry risk
15% weekly is mathematically unsustainable for any legitimate platform
Withdraw anytime promises disappear when you try — fees and taxes appear first
Unverifiable member counts are used to create social proof from nothing
explanation.safe_move: Block and ignore. Report the account to the platform.
explanation.consequence: You deposit $500 and see fake gains on a dashboard. When you try to withdraw, new fees appear that you must pay first. The platform eventually disappears.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Guaranteed investment returns do not exist — any promise of them is a scam.

framing: You got this email about unclaimed crypto.

spot_flag_options.label: Requires a fee to release the funds

message.subject: Unclaimed cryptocurrency: $4,200 Awaiting Your Claim
message.body:
Our records show $4,200 in cryptocurrency linked to your email address from an inactive wallet. To claim and transfer to your wallet:

1. Verify identity at nexcoin-assets.net/claim
2. Pay the $89 processing fee via gift card
3. Receive your $4,200 within 24 hours

NexCoin Asset Recovery

red_flags.label: Requires a fee to receive funds — advance fee fraud
Fee must be paid via gift card
No mechanism exists to link cryptocurrency to email addresses
nexcoin-assets.net is not a real exchange

explanation.short: cryptocurrency is not linked to email addresses — this unclaimed funds story is entirely fictional, and the fee is the actual theft.
tells: cryptocurrency wallets are identified by cryptographic addresses, not email addresses
Paying a fee to receive money is the advance fee fraud pattern
Gift card fees are untraceable and the preferred method of scammers
The 72-hour expiry creates urgency to prevent you from researching
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and delete. There is no unclaimed cryptocurrency. Report to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov.
explanation.consequence: You pay $89 in gift cards and receive nothing. No cryptocurrency exists — it was fabricated to justify the fee.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: You never pay fees to receive money — any request to do so is advance fee fraud.

framing: Someone who seems to be a crypto expert reached out.

spot_flag_options.label: Invites you to a private trading group
Shows a track record of past wins
Starts with a small suggested investment

message.body: Hey, I noticed you follow some crypto pages. I run a private VIP signal group — we are up 340% this year. I share my exact trades. First month is free. Just $150 to join after. Here are my recent wins [screenshot]. DM me if you want in before I close the group this week.

red_flags.label: Paid private signal group with entry fee
Trade screenshots can be fabricated easily

340% annual returns are not credible for signal groups

tells: Trade performance screenshots can be edited in minutes

Real traders do not cold-DM strangers to share their edge
explanation.safe_move: Do not pay. Real trading education comes from established, regulated sources — not social DM groups.
explanation.consequence: You pay $150. The signals are low quality or nonexistent. The group eventually vanishes or the fee increases.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Paid signal groups are a fee collection business, not a trading business — the only consistent winner is the group owner.

framing: You got this from your bank in an email.

Horizon Bank Security

tells: A QR code hides its URL, making it a perfect tool for sending you to a phishing site

explanation.safe_move: Do not scan. Log into your bank account directly through the app or by typing the URL you already know.
explanation.consequence: The QR code leads to a fake login page. Your credentials are captured and the account is accessed.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verify account alerts by logging into your bank directly — never by scanning a QR code from an email.

framing: You got this text claiming to be from the tax agency.

red_flags.label: The tax agency never contacts taxpayers by text message

framing: A text arrived claiming to be from Federal Benefits.

tells: The Federal Benefits Agency does not suspend Federal Benefits numbers

The $4.35 bait amount is small enough you would not hesitate to pay without checking

Pay via CashLink to $westviewrentals. Once received, we will schedule your viewing.

Westview Property Management

red_flags.label: Large non-refundable fee before you have even seen the unit
Rental application fee via CashLink — no accountability
Viewing is scheduled after you pay, not before
Non-refundable framing means you lose the money no matter what

explanation.short: Application fees for rentals you have never seen, paid via peer payment apps, are almost always scams.
tells: Legitimate application fees are charged after you tour the property and decide to apply
Real property managers use professional platforms — not personal CashLink accounts
A non-refundable fee before viewing means you have zero recourse if the unit does not exist
The high-demand framing creates artificial competition to pressure fast payment
explanation.safe_move: Never pay a rental fee before visiting the property. Verify the management company through independent searches.
explanation.consequence: You pay $150 and either no viewing is ever scheduled, or the unit turns out to be unavailable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Tour first, pay fees after — any rental that requires payment before viewing is a scam.

framing: A rental inquiry turned into this exchange.

spot_flag_options.label: Listing is significantly nicer than anything in the price range
No mention of a formal lease
Unit is described as pet friendly

message.subject: Re: Studio Rental $700/mo - Downtown
message.body: Hi! Yes the studio is still available. It is 650sqft, fully renovated kitchen and bath, in-unit laundry, rooftop access, and parking included — all for $700/mo. I just want a good tenant. No lease necessary for the first month, just pay and move in. When can you start?

red_flags.label: Luxury amenities at an impossibly low price
No lease for first month removes legal protection
Pay and move in without any formal process
Property manager using QuickMail with no business credentials

explanation.short: If a rental is dramatically underpriced with luxury features, it almost certainly does not exist at that price — the listing is stolen.
tells: Scammers copy photos from real listings and post them at below-market prices
No lease means no legal recourse when the property turns out to be unavailable
The willingness to skip formal process is designed to make you feel like a trusted tenant
You arrive to move in and find the property belongs to someone else or is already occupied
explanation.safe_move: Search the listing photos using reverse image search. Visit the property before paying anything.
explanation.consequence: You pay first and last month's rent. The property is not available, and the person who took your money is unreachable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Prices that seem too good to be true are too good to be true — always reverse image search rental photos.

framing: A property manager sent you lease documents.

spot_flag_options.label: Signing link requires creating an account with personal details
Lease is sent as an attachment

message.subject: Your Lease is Ready to Sign - Unit 12C
message.body:
Congratulations! Your application has been approved for Unit 12C.

Please sign your lease at the link below. You will need to create an account using your SSN and bank account number for identity verification before signing.

lakeview-rentals-portal.com/sign

Lakeview Rentals Leasing Team

red_flags.label: Lease signing requires SSN and bank account upfront
Real e-sign platforms do not require your SSN to sign
Bank account number is not needed to sign a digital document
lakeview-rentals-portal.com may not be the real property site

explanation.short: Lease signing platforms like SignSecure require only your name and email — never your SSN or bank account number.
tells: Legitimate e-signature platforms identify signers by email, not SSN
Collecting SSN and bank details under the guise of identity verification is identity theft
Real property portals use established e-sign services, not custom portals
Once submitted, your SSN and account number enable immediate identity and financial fraud
explanation.safe_move: Contact the real property management company through a number you find independently to verify the signing process.
explanation.consequence: Your SSN and bank account number are used to open fraudulent credit accounts and drain your bank.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: E-signature platforms only need your email — never provide your SSN or bank account to sign a document online.

framing: You got this text about a package.

spot_flag_options.label: Requires a small fee to release your package
Asks you to update your address

message.body: NexShip: Package held due to incomplete address. Pay $1.79 release fee at nexship-delivery-update.com/pay to receive today. Tracking: NSP-440192

red_flags.label: Carriers do not charge fees to release held packages
nexship-delivery-update.com is not a real carrier site
Sent from a random full phone number, not a short code
$1.79 bait fee captures your full card details

explanation.short: Real carriers do not charge fees to deliver packages — the fee is a pretext to capture your card number.
tells: No shipping carrier charges a release fee for an address issue — they contact the sender
The domain is not affiliated with any real carrier
Legitimate carrier texts come from 5-6 digit short codes, not full phone numbers
The $1.79 feels trivial but your card is then used for larger unauthorized charges
explanation.safe_move: Track any expected packages on the carrier official website using a tracking number you already have.
explanation.consequence: Your card details are captured and used for larger unauthorized charges.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Package release fees do not exist — any text charging one is a scam.

framing: You got this email about your computer.

spot_flag_options.label: Asks you to install remote access software
Claims a virus was detected on your device

message.subject: Critical: Malware Detected on Your Device
message.body:
Our systems have detected malicious software on your device that is attempting to access your NexCloud account.

To remove it immediately, please call our security team at 1-855-555-0167 and allow them to connect remotely to your device to run our removal tool.

Do not restart your computer until this is resolved.

NexCloud Security

red_flags.label: Asks you to allow remote access to your computer
nexcloud-device-alert.com is not the real service domain
Software companies do not proactively scan your device
Do not restart instruction creates dependency on their help

explanation.short: No legitimate software company remotely monitors your device and calls you to fix viruses — that call grants scammers full control of your computer.
tells: Cloud services do not scan your local device for malware
Remote access gives the scammer complete control — they can steal files, install malware, and access banking
The do not restart instruction creates urgency and dependency
Real security alerts direct you to your account dashboard, not a phone call
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and delete. Run your own antivirus scan if concerned. Never grant remote access to an unsolicited caller.
explanation.consequence: The remote session gives scammers full computer control. They access banking, steal files, and often install persistent malware.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never grant remote computer access to someone who contacts you unsolicited — legitimate support does not work that way.

framing: You got this text about your account.

spot_flag_options.label: Asks you to share a one-time code you just received
Asks you to reply with a code
Claims to be from the support team

message.body: TrustBank: We detected a login attempt. A verification code was sent to your phone. Reply with the code to confirm it was you and restore access.

red_flags.label: Asks you to share a verification code via text
The code being requested was just triggered by the scammer
Sent from a full phone number, not a bank short code
Asking you to reply with a code is not how banks verify

explanation.short: The scammer triggered a real login attempt on your account — the code they want is the one protecting you from them.
tells: The verification code was sent because someone tried to log into your account
Sharing that code hands the scammer the key to bypass your authentication
Real banks never ask you to reply with your verification code via SMS
Banks use short codes for alerts — a full phone number sender is suspicious
explanation.safe_move: Never share a verification code with anyone. If you receive an unexpected code, someone is trying to access your account — log in directly and change your password.
explanation.consequence: You reply with the code. The scammer uses it to log into your account and lock you out.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A verification code is your lock — anyone asking for it is trying to pick it.

framing: An urgent password reset email arrived.

spot_flag_options.label: You did not request a password reset
Email contains a link to reset password

message.subject: Password Reset Requested - Act Within 30 Minutes
message.body:
A password reset was requested for your DataVault account. Click below to set a new password:

[Reset My Password]

This link expires in 30 minutes. If you did not request this, your account may be compromised — still click the link to secure your account.

DataVault Security Team

red_flags.label: You never requested this password reset
Tells you to click even if you did not request it — dangerous instruction
datavault-security-alerts.com is not the real service domain

explanation.short: Telling you to click even if you did not request the reset is the key tell — legitimate services say to ignore emails you did not request.
tells: Real password reset emails say to ignore if you did not request it — not click anyway
The click-even-if-not-you instruction is designed to capture credentials regardless
The sender domain is a variant, not the real service
If you did not request the reset, simply ignore the email — your account is fine
explanation.safe_move: If you did not request a reset, ignore the email. If concerned, log into the service directly by typing the URL yourself.
explanation.consequence: The reset link leads to a fake login page. Your credentials are captured and your account is taken over.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: If you did not request a password reset, do not click anything in the email — just ignore it.

framing: You got this text out of nowhere.

spot_flag_options.label: Must pay a fee to claim your prize
Prize must be claimed within 24 hours

message.body: Congrats! Your number was selected in the NovaMart Weekly Giveaway. You have won $500 in gift cards. Claim at novamart-winners.net/claim before midnight. Ref: NVM-WIN-2024-8812

red_flags.label: You never entered any NovaMart giveaway
novamart-winners.net is not the real company site
Sent from a random full phone number

explanation.short: You cannot win a contest you never entered — this prize is fabricated to get you to a phishing site.
tells: Legitimate sweepstakes require entry — you cannot win one you never joined
The claim site is a phishing page that will request personal or payment information
Real company giveaways are announced through official company channels, not random texts
The midnight deadline is pressure to act before you think clearly
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and delete. You did not win anything.
explanation.consequence: The claim site requests personal information and payment details to receive your prize, which never arrives.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: You cannot win a contest you never entered — unsolicited prize notifications are always scams.

framing: A text thread with someone claiming to be your bank.

thread.body: Horizon Bank Fraud Dept: We flagged a $1,400 charge at Vertex Electronics. Did you make this purchase? Reply YES or NO.
No, that was not me.
Thank you. To protect your account, a specialist will call you in 2 minutes. Please have your debit card ready to verify your identity.

message.body: [Thread: Bank fraud alert asking you to confirm a charge, then requesting card details by phone]

red_flags.label: Asks you to have your debit card ready for a callback
Initiates a callback to collect information
Sent from a full phone number, not a short code

explanation.short: Real bank fraud teams never call you and ask for your card details — they already have them.
tells: Legitimate bank fraud alerts use short codes, not full phone numbers
Real fraud departments never need you to read back your card number — they have it on file
The callback setup hands you to a scammer posing as a banker
If you receive a real fraud alert, hang up and call the number on the back of your card
explanation.safe_move: Do not wait for the callback. Hang up and call Horizon Bank using the number on the back of your card.
explanation.consequence: The callback is from a scammer who collects your full card number, CVV, and PIN to make fraudulent charges.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Your bank never calls you to ask for your card details — if that happens, hang up and call the number on your card.

framing: A bank texted you about suspicious activity.

thread.body: TrustBank Alert: Suspicious login detected from Lagos, Nigeria. Your account has been temporarily locked. Reply UNLOCK to restore access.
UNLOCK
To verify your identity, please provide your full account number and the last 4 digits of your SSN.

message.body: [Thread: Bank lock notification that escalates to requesting account number and SSN]

red_flags.label: Asks for your full account number via text
Requests SSN digits to verify identity
Unlocking an account via text reply is not how banks work

explanation.short: Banks never unlock accounts or verify identity by asking for your account number and SSN over text.
tells: Account verification requires you to call the bank or visit a branch — not reply to a text
Your bank already has your account number — asking for it proves this is not your bank
SSN digits via SMS are immediately usable for identity theft
The UNLOCK reply confirmed your number is active and engaged
explanation.safe_move: Do not reply with any information. Call your bank directly using the number on your card.
explanation.consequence: Your account number and SSN are used to take over your account and open fraudulent credit lines.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Your bank verifies you by asking security questions you set up — never by asking for your account number or SSN over text.

framing: You received this SMS thread from your bank.

thread.body: Granite Credit Union: A new payee was added to your online banking. If this was not you, reply STOP to block the transfer.
STOP
Transfer blocked. To secure your account, we need to verify your online banking password. Please reply with your current password.

message.body: [Thread: Fraud alert that ends with a request for your banking password via text]

red_flags.label: Asks for your banking password via text
No bank ever asks for your password — they cannot and should not know it
Sent from a full phone number, not a credit union short code

explanation.short: No bank or credit union ever asks for your password — ever. This is the clearest possible sign of fraud.
tells: Banks operate on hashed passwords they cannot read — asking for it proves this is not your bank
Replying with your password gives full account access to the scammer immediately
Legitimate fraud blocks are handled by freezing transactions, not asking for credentials
The STOP reply was used to build trust before the escalating credential request
explanation.safe_move: Stop replying immediately. Call your credit union using the number on your card or their official website.
explanation.consequence: Your banking password is captured and your account is accessed and drained.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No financial institution ever asks for your password — receiving that request means you are talking to a scammer.

framing: Your bank texted you about a charge.

thread.body: Apex FCU: Did you authorize $890 at Vertex Electronics on card ending 4412? Reply YES or NO.
NO
Card ending 4412 has been blocked. A new card will arrive in 3-5 business days. Call 800-555-0100 if you need immediate assistance.

message.body: [Thread: Fraud check from credit union short code that blocks card without asking for sensitive info]

green_flags.label: References your specific card ending in 4412
Never asks for password, SSN, or account number
Card is blocked and replaced — no action needed from you

explanation.short: Legitimate bank fraud alerts use short codes, reference your specific card, block the card automatically, and never ask for credentials.
tells: 5-digit short code is how real banks send automated alerts
Referencing card ending 4412 proves the bank knows your specific account
Real fraud response blocks the card and handles the issue — it does not ask you to do more
A callback number for assistance is provided, but no sensitive action is demanded
explanation.safe_move: Reply YES or NO as asked. Your card is already protected. Call the number if you need a faster replacement.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring a real fraud alert allows the unauthorized charge to process and additional fraudulent use to continue.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate bank fraud alerts are automated, use short codes, reference specific card digits, and never ask for credentials.

framing: A text thread about wire transfer fraud.

thread.body: Horizon Bank: We detected an outgoing wire transfer of $3,200 to an unknown account. If unauthorized, reply CANCEL immediately.
CANCEL
To cancel the wire we need to verify your identity. Please provide your full name, date of birth, and the last 6 digits of your account number.

message.body: [Thread: Wire transfer alert that escalates to requesting identity details]

red_flags.label: Requests your date of birth via text
Asks for last 6 digits of account number
Sent from full phone number, not a short code

explanation.short: Banks cancel wire transfers internally without needing your DOB or account digits — those are being collected for identity fraud.
tells: Your bank can cancel outgoing wires without asking you for your own account details
DOB plus account digits is enough for identity verification at many institutions — enabling account takeover
Real bank fraud teams reach you via your registered contact, not a random number
The CANCEL reply confirmed your number is active and responsive
explanation.safe_move: Stop the thread and call your bank directly using the number on your card or their official website.
explanation.consequence: Your DOB and account digits enable the scammer to authenticate as you and take over your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Wire cancellations are handled internally by your bank — they never need you to text them your identity details.

framing: You got a text from your bank about an international charge.

thread.body: TrustBank: Is this you? $340 charge in Amsterdam on card ending 7821. Reply 1=Yes 2=No.
Card ending 7821 frozen. Dispute filed automatically. New card ships in 5-7 days. To expedite: call 800-555-0118.

message.body: [Thread: Legitimate international fraud check with automated card freeze and no credential requests]

green_flags.label: Sent from a verified bank short code
Dispute filed and card frozen automatically
Never asks for credentials, SSN, or full account number

explanation.short: This is what a real fraud alert looks like — short code, specific card reference, simple reply, automatic action, no credentials requested.
tells: Short code sender is the standard for real bank automation
Card ending 7821 shows the bank knows exactly which card was used
Numbered replies are clear, quick, and require no sensitive input
The bank handles the dispute automatically — your only decision is Yes or No
explanation.safe_move: Reply 1 or 2 as appropriate. Call the number if you need to expedite the replacement card.
explanation.consequence: Not responding to a real fraud alert allows unauthorized charges to continue on your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real fraud alerts are simple — a yes/no question from a short code, with automatic protection once you respond.

framing: A text claimed your bank account was accessed from overseas.

thread.body: Ridgeline Bank Security: Your account was accessed from Vietnam. As a precaution we have limited transfers. To restore full access click: ridgelinebank-security.net/verify
I did not do that.
We understand. Please complete verification at ridgelinebank-security.net to restore access within 2 hours or your account will remain limited.

message.body: [Thread: Bank security alert with a phishing link and a 2-hour restoration deadline]

red_flags.label: Link to ridgelinebank-security.net is not the real bank site
2-hour threat to force fast action without scrutiny
Full phone number instead of bank short code

explanation.short: Real bank security restrictions are lifted by calling or visiting a branch — not by clicking a link in a text.
tells: ridgelinebank-security.net is a phishing domain designed to capture your credentials
The 2-hour deadline is artificial — banks do not set time limits for security verification
Full phone number senders are suspicious for bank communications
Clicking the link leads to a fake login page that captures your credentials
explanation.safe_move: Do not click the link. Call Ridgeline Bank directly using the number on your card.
explanation.consequence: You click the link, enter your banking credentials on a fake site, and your account is taken over.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Bank account restrictions are resolved by calling — never by clicking a link in a text message.

framing: An email thread about your work account.

thread.body: IT Security Team: We detected unusual sign-in activity on your account from an unrecognized device. Please verify by clicking the link in this email.
I did not sign in from another device.
Thank you for confirming. Click here immediately to lock out the unauthorized session and reset your credentials: corp-it-verify.net/secure

message.subject: Urgent: Unauthorized Sign-in Detected
message.body: [Thread: IT security alert escalating to a phishing link for credential reset]

red_flags.label: corp-it-verify.net is not the company IT domain
IT security emails should come from the company domain
Urgency framing to lock out unauthorized session

explanation.short: Corporate IT teams send security alerts from company domains — never from external sites.
tells: Your real IT team communicates from company email addresses, not external domains
corp-it-verify.net is a phishing site designed to capture work credentials
Real unauthorized access alerts are handled through your company's actual IT portal
Work credential phishing is used to gain access to company systems and email
explanation.safe_move: Contact your IT department directly through official internal channels to verify the alert.
explanation.consequence: Your work credentials are captured. Attackers access company systems, email, and potentially customer data.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: IT security emails come from your company domain — external domain IT alerts are always phishing.

framing: An email about a shared document.

thread.body: Sandra Torres has shared a document with you: "Q4 Budget Review.xlsx". Click to view.
I clicked the link and it is asking me to sign in.
Yes, sign in with your work email to access the shared file. Your credentials are needed to verify you are an authorized viewer.

message.subject: Sandra Torres shared a file with you
message.body: [Thread: Shared document notification that prompts work credential sign-in on a fake page]

red_flags.label: Share notification from nexcloud-docs-share.net, not the real service
Clicking leads to a credential sign-in page
Sandra Torres may not be a known colleague

explanation.short: Shared document phishing is one of the most effective attacks because it mimics a normal workflow.
tells: The share notification comes from nexcloud-docs-share.net, not the real document platform domain
Entering credentials on a third-party site hands them directly to attackers
Real document shares come from the platform itself (e.g., the real NexCloud domain)
If you do not recognize Sandra Torres, the share is likely unsolicited
explanation.safe_move: Do not enter credentials. Contact Sandra Torres directly through a known channel to verify she sent a document.
explanation.consequence: Your work credentials are captured and used to access company systems and email.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verify document shares with the sender through a separate channel before entering any credentials.

framing: An IT alert about your password expiry.

thread.body: IT Help Desk (helpdesk@yourcompany.com): Your password expires in 7 days. Log into the employee portal at portal.yourcompany.com to update it before it expires.
Can I do it on my phone?
Yes — go to portal.yourcompany.com on any device. Use your current credentials to log in and follow the prompts. Let us know if you run into issues.

message.subject: Password Expiry Notice - 7 Days Remaining
message.body: [Thread: Legitimate IT password expiry notice directing to the real company portal with no urgency or links]

green_flags.label: Email from the real company domain
Directs you to type the URL yourself, no clickable link
Never asks for your current password
Answers follow-up questions through normal IT channel

explanation.short: Legitimate IT password notices come from the company domain, direct you to the known portal, and never ask for your current password.
tells: Sender is from the real company domain, not an external site
Directing you to type the URL yourself prevents link-based phishing
No urgency — 7 days is reasonable notice without panic pressure
IT never asks you to email or text your current password
explanation.safe_move: Go to portal.yourcompany.com as directed and update your password.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring a real password expiry notice locks you out of your account when the password expires.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate IT security notices come from your company domain and direct you to portals you already know.

framing: An email about your email storage being full.

thread.body: NexCloud Mail: Your mailbox is 98% full. Undelivered messages are being queued. Click here to upgrade storage or verify your account to receive queued emails.
I do not want to upgrade. How do I just verify?
Click this link to verify and release your queued emails: mailbox-verify.nexcloud-support.net/verify. You will need to sign in with your email credentials.

message.subject: Mailbox Almost Full - Undelivered Messages Waiting
message.body: [Thread: Mailbox full alert that escalates to a credential phishing link]

red_flags.label: Verification link goes to nexcloud-support.net, not the real service
Fear of losing emails drives you to comply
Requires sign-in on an external domain

explanation.short: Mailbox full warnings that require external verification are phishing — real storage management happens in your account settings.
tells: Real email storage management is handled in your account settings, not external links
The fear of losing queued emails is emotional pressure to comply quickly
nexcloud-support.net is an external domain, not the real mail provider
Entering credentials on the external site captures them immediately
explanation.safe_move: Log into your email account directly and check storage in settings. Do not click verification links in storage warning emails.
explanation.consequence: Your email credentials are captured, giving attackers access to all your email, password resets, and linked accounts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Manage email storage through your account settings directly — never through verification links in storage warning emails.

framing: An email about your subscription being cancelled.

thread.body: DataVault: Your subscription was cancelled due to a payment issue. To reactivate your account and recover your files, verify your payment method within 48 hours.
I did not cancel. How do I fix this?
Log in here to reactivate: datavault-reactivate.com/login. Enter your credentials and update your payment method to restore access immediately.

message.subject: Your DataVault Subscription Has Been Cancelled
message.body: [Thread: Subscription cancellation notice with a reactivation phishing link]

red_flags.label: datavault-reactivate.com is not the real service domain
Threat of losing files drives panic compliance
Billing email from datavault-account-alerts.com, not real domain

explanation.short: Subscription cancellation phishing combines fear of data loss with a fake login page to capture credentials.
tells: The reactivation link goes to a domain other than the real service
File loss threats create emotional urgency that bypasses careful scrutiny
The billing sender domain is not the real service domain
Real subscription issues are resolved through your account on the official site

explanation.consequence: Your service credentials are captured and the attacker accesses your stored files and linked payment method.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Resolve subscription issues by logging into the service directly — never through links in cancellation emails.

framing: An email about a suspicious sign-in to your account.

message.subject: New Sign-in Detected - DataVault Account
message.body: [Thread: Legitimate sign-in notification with no links, no credential requests, and a real company domain]

green_flags.label: Email from security@datavault.com — the real service domain
If it was you, no action needed — no pressure
Security URL points to datavault.com itself

explanation.short: Legitimate sign-in alerts come from the real company domain, require no action if you made the sign-in, and never ask for credentials.
tells: security@datavault.com is the authentic sender, not a lookalike domain
Real alerts require no action from you if the sign-in was legitimate
The security URL points to the real site, not a third-party domain
No credentials are requested at any point in the exchange

explanation.consequence: Ignoring a genuine unauthorized sign-in alert allows the attacker to maintain access to your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real security alerts come from the actual company domain and require no credentials — just a yes or no from you.

framing: HR sent you a benefits enrollment reminder.

message.subject: Open Enrollment Closes Friday - Action Required

benefits-enrollment-portal.net is not the real HR portal
Directs you to enter employee ID and password on an external site

tells: HR communications come from the company email domain, not external sites
The benefits portal URL is not on the company domain

framing: Someone DMed you claiming to be from tech support.

message.body: [Thread: Tech support DM that escalates to requesting your account password]

red_flags.label: Asks for your current password — no support team ever needs this
Real support does not initiate contact via social DM
Your username was collected before the password request

tells: Real tech support uses admin tools — they never need your password
Proactive DM-based support contact is a hallmark of social engineering
