framing: A tech support agent contacted you about a virus.

thread.body: SignalOne Tech Support: Our system flagged your device sending unusual network traffic. We can run a remote scan to check for malware. Would you like to proceed?
Sure, how do we do that?
Download RemoteAssist Pro at remote-assist-pro.net and share the session code with me. I will connect and run the scan in about 10 minutes.

message.body: [Thread: Tech support contact leading to remote access software installation]

red_flags.label: Asks you to install remote access software
remote-assist-pro.net is not a known legitimate remote tool
Telecom provider does not monitor your device traffic and DM you

explanation.short: Your carrier does not monitor individual device traffic and will never DM you to offer a remote scan.
tells: Telecom providers do not analyze individual device traffic in real time and contact customers by DM
Downloading software from a third-party site and granting remote access gives the attacker full computer control
Remote access in this context is used to steal files, passwords, and install persistent malware
Legitimate remote support uses established tools like TeamViewer or AnyDesk through official vendor channels
explanation.safe_move: Do not download anything. If concerned about your device, run your own antivirus scan or contact your carrier through their official support line.
explanation.consequence: The remote session gives the scammer full control. Files, passwords, and banking credentials are accessed.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never install remote access software for someone who contacted you unsolicited — that is always a scam.

framing: You submitted a tech support ticket and this DM followed.

thread.body: Hi, this is Alex from DataVault support. I see you opened ticket #DV-84421 about syncing issues. Can you confirm the email address on the account so I can pull it up?
Yes, it is jsmith@email.com.
Got it. I can see your account and the sync error. This is a known issue on our end — I am pushing a fix to your account now. You should see it resolve within 30 minutes. No action needed from you.

message.body: [Thread: Legitimate tech support follow-up on a ticket you opened, resolving without credential requests]

green_flags.label: References a ticket number you actually created
Only asks for email address to pull up account — not password
Fix applied on their end — no action needed from you
Never requests password, SSN, or payment info

explanation.short: Legitimate support follows up on tickets you opened, asks only for account-identifying info like email, and never needs your password.
tells: The agent references a specific ticket number you created — proving they have context
Email address is sufficient for account lookup — no password or sensitive details needed
The fix is applied server-side with no action required from you
Real support closes tickets without requesting credentials
explanation.safe_move: This appears to be a legitimate support response. Allow the 30 minutes and verify the fix resolved the issue.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring legitimate support responses delays resolution of your actual technical issue.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real tech support references your ticket, asks only for identifying info like email, and resolves issues without your password.

framing: A tech support agent wants to help you with a billing issue.

thread.body: NovaMart Plus Support: We noticed a billing discrepancy on your account. To process a refund of $34.00, we need to verify your identity. Can you confirm your name and address?
Sure, it is Jane Smith, 123 Oak Street.
Thank you Jane. To complete the refund, I need your bank account number to process the transfer. We cannot use the card on file for refunds.

message.body: [Thread: Refund offer that escalates to requesting bank account number]

red_flags.label: Requests bank account number to process a refund
Cannot use the card on file excuse is false — all refunds go to original payment method
Name and address collected before the escalating request

explanation.short: Refunds always go to the original payment method — any service claiming otherwise and asking for your bank account is committing fraud.
tells: Every legitimate refund goes back to the original card or payment method used — always
No real company collects bank account numbers via social DM to process refunds
Your name and address were collected to build a profile before the financial request
The $34 refund is plausible enough to make you cooperate with the bank account request
explanation.safe_move: Do not provide your bank account number. Contact the company through their official website to verify any real refund.
explanation.consequence: Your bank account number is used for unauthorized withdrawals — far more than $34.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Refunds go to your original payment method — anyone asking for a bank account number to process a refund is stealing from you.

framing: Tech support reached out about your account being hacked.

thread.body: NexCoin Support: Your wallet was accessed by an unauthorized IP address. We need to lock it down immediately. Are you available to resolve this now?
Yes, what do I need to do?

To lock the unauthorized session, you need to temporarily transfer your balance to our secure holding wallet: nvlt9x8k3mQ2...wallet-address. You can transfer back in 24 hours once we clear the session.

message.body: [Thread: Crypto wallet support contact leading to a request to transfer funds to a holding wallet]

red_flags.label: Asks you to transfer crypto to their wallet address
Holding wallets are not a real security mechanism
Real crypto platforms do not DM you proactively about hacks

explanation.short: No legitimate crypto service will ever ask you to transfer your funds to a holding wallet — that is theft dressed as security.
tells: Legitimate security measures lock your account — they do not ask you to move funds
The wallet address given belongs to the scammer
Crypto transfers are irreversible — once sent, the funds are permanently gone
Real platforms handle account security server-side without requiring fund transfers from users
explanation.safe_move: Do not transfer anything. Secure your wallet by changing your password and enabling two-factor authentication on the official platform.
explanation.consequence: Your crypto balance is transferred to the scammer wallet. It is irreversible and unrecoverable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No security team ever asks you to transfer your crypto to a holding address — that is always theft.

framing: You reported a bug and got this DM back.

thread.body: Hi! I am from the NexCloud dev team. I saw your bug report (#4421) about the upload failure. Can you tell me what file type and size you were uploading when it failed?
It was a 2GB MP4 file.
That explains it — we have a file size cap issue in the current build. Fix is in the next release, expected Friday. Thanks for the report! No action needed on your end.

message.body: [Thread: Legitimate bug follow-up asking for technical details to reproduce the issue]

green_flags.label: References the specific bug report number you submitted
Asks only for technical details to reproduce the issue
Never asks for credentials or personal information
Gives a clear timeline and resolution without requiring anything from you

explanation.short: Legitimate developer support asks for technical details to reproduce a bug — never credentials or personal information.
tells: Bug report number reference proves they have your actual submission
Questions about file type and size are technical — not personal or financial
Resolution is communicated without requiring any action from you
Real developers fix bugs on their end — they do not need your account access
explanation.safe_move: Engage with the technical questions as needed. This is normal bug triage.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring legitimate bug follow-up slows down resolution of an issue that may affect others too.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate tech support asks technical questions about your issue — not credentials or personal information.

framing: Someone claiming to be tech support offered to help.

thread.body: Hi, I noticed you posted about having trouble with your NexCloud account. I am from the support team. I can help resolve this quickly if you share your 2FA backup code.
Really? How did you find my post?
We monitor mentions of our service to help users. The backup code lets us verify your identity without your password. It is just for verification.

message.body: [Thread: Support agent finds your public post and requests your 2FA backup code]

red_flags.label: Monitors your posts to offer help — not how real support works
Handle is @nexcloud_help_desk_2 — numbered handles are often fake

explanation.short: Your 2FA backup code bypasses all two-factor authentication — sharing it gives complete account access to whoever has it.
tells: A 2FA backup code is the emergency key to your account — it bypasses your authenticator app entirely
Real support verifies identity through your registered email, not backup codes
The numbered handle (@nexcloud_help_desk_2) suggests a fake account mimicking the real support handle
Legitimate companies do not monitor social posts and cold-DM users to offer account help
explanation.safe_move: Do not share your backup code. Report the account. Contact support through the official platform only.
explanation.consequence: Your backup code gives the attacker full account access, bypassing your password and authenticator entirely.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Your 2FA backup code is as powerful as your password — guard it absolutely and share it with no one.

framing: You receive a series of texts claiming to be from a tax agency.

thread.body: URGENT: Federal Tax Authority has flagged your account for underpayment of $1,247 for tax year 2023. Failure to respond within 48 hours will result in wage garnishment.
A warrant for your arrest has been authorized pending payment. To avoid escalation, call 1-888-555-0142 immediately to speak with a resolution officer.
Your case number is FTA-2024-88234. Payment must be completed via gift cards or wire transfer to suspend the warrant. Act NOW.

red_flags.label: Threatens arrest warrant over a tax debt
Requests payment via gift cards or wire transfer
Real tax agencies do not initiate contact by text message
Each message escalates fear to prevent rational thinking

explanation.short: Tax agencies send paper mail first and never demand gift card payments or threaten arrest by text.
tells: Tax authorities contact you by postal mail, not SMS
No government agency accepts gift cards or wire transfers for tax debts
Arrest warrant threats are a scare tactic — tax debt is civil, not criminal
Legitimate case numbers can be verified on the official agency website
explanation.safe_move: Hang up or ignore. Look up the real tax authority number on the official government website and call it yourself if you have concerns.
explanation.consequence: Victims buy hundreds of dollars in gift cards and read the numbers aloud, which is immediately drained by the scammer.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Gift card payment requests from any government agency are always scams — no exceptions.

framing: You get a series of emails claiming you are owed a tax refund.

thread.body:
Subject: Tax Refund Notice — $847 Pending

Our records show you are owed a refund of $847.00 from the previous tax year. To receive your refund, you must verify your identity at the link below within 72 hours.

[Verify and Claim Refund]

Federal Tax Refund Office

Reminder: Your refund of $847.00 expires in 24 hours. Provide your bank routing number and account number for direct deposit to complete the claim.

[Complete Your Refund]

Final notice: Your unclaimed refund will be forfeited at midnight. Complete your bank verification now to receive your funds.

[Claim $847 Now]

red_flags.label: Requests bank routing and account number to process refund
fedtax-refund-portal.com is not an official government domain
Tax refunds do not expire or get forfeited if unclaimed
Each email adds more urgency to prevent skepticism

explanation.short: Tax refunds are issued automatically — no agency emails you to claim them or asks for your bank details by email.
tells: Official government sites use .gov domains — no exceptions
Refunds are issued by paper check or direct deposit on file — not claimed via email
Expiring refund deadlines do not exist — this is fabricated pressure
Providing bank details hands scammers access to drain your account
explanation.safe_move: Check your real tax account status on the official .gov website. Never provide bank details in response to an email.
explanation.consequence: Your bank account details are used to initiate ACH withdrawals, draining your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Official government agencies only use .gov domains — any other domain in a government email is a scam.

framing: You get texts claiming your social benefits account is suspended.

thread.body: ALERT: Your federal benefits account has been suspended due to suspicious activity. Call 1-888-555-0177 within 24 hours to reactivate.
A temporary hold has been placed on your monthly payments. To restore access, provide your full name, date of birth, and benefits ID number when you call.
Failure to call will result in permanent suspension and forfeiture of benefits. This is your final notice.

red_flags.label: Benefits agencies do not text about account suspensions
Requests your name, date of birth, and ID number
Threatens permanent loss of benefits to create panic
Provides a callback number you did not initiate

explanation.short: Government benefits offices send paper letters — they do not text about suspensions or request PII by phone.
tells: Benefits agencies communicate via postal mail for account issues
Providing your name, DOB, and ID number enables identity theft
No benefits program permanently suspends with 24 hours notice via text
Always call the number on your official benefits card or letter, never a number from a text
explanation.safe_move: Do not call the number in the text. Find the official contact number on your benefits documents or the official government website.
explanation.consequence: Your personal information is used to redirect your benefit payments to the scammer's account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Benefits offices use postal mail — any text about a suspended benefits account is a scam.

framing: You get emails claiming a customs package is being held.

thread.body:
Subject: Package Detention Notice — Action Required

A package addressed to you has been detained by customs pending duty payment and documentation review. Reference: NCO-2024-448812.
To release your package, a customs duty fee of $38.50 is required along with a copy of your government-issued ID to verify ownership. Upload your ID at: customs-verify-id.com/upload
Final notice: Your detained package will be destroyed in 48 hours if duties are not paid. Complete payment and ID verification immediately.

red_flags.label: Requests you upload a government-issued ID to a website
Customs duties are not collected via email payment links
national-customs-clearance.com is not an official government domain
Package destruction threat is fabricated to create panic

explanation.short: Uploading your government ID to an unofficial site enables identity theft — real customs agencies use official .gov portals.
tells: Official customs communications use .gov domains
Real customs fees are handled through official carrier or government portals, not email links
Uploading your ID to an unofficial site gives scammers everything needed for identity theft
Package destruction in 48 hours is fabricated pressure
explanation.safe_move: If you believe a package is genuinely held, contact the shipping carrier directly using their official website.
explanation.consequence: Your ID photo is used for identity theft — new accounts, loans, and fraud conducted in your name.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never upload your government ID in response to an email — only to verified official government portals you navigate to yourself.

framing: You get escalating texts claiming police have a warrant for your arrest.

thread.body: NOTICE: An arrest warrant has been issued in your name for failure to appear for jury duty. Case #: JD-2024-55610.
You have the option to pay a $350 fine to clear the warrant before officers are dispatched to your address. Call 1-888-555-0161 immediately.
Payment can be made via gift cards or prepaid debit card. Have the card numbers ready when you call. Officers will be dispatched in 2 hours if unpaid.

red_flags.label: Requests gift card or prepaid debit payment for a legal fine
Sheriff offices do not text about arrest warrants
Real warrants cannot be cleared with a phone payment

explanation.short: No law enforcement agency accepts gift cards or prepaid cards as payment for fines — this is always a scam.
tells: Law enforcement serves warrants in person — they do not text about them first
Court fines are paid through the court system, not via phone to a sheriff's number
Gift card payment requests from law enforcement are never legitimate
The 2-hour dispatch countdown is pure psychological pressure
explanation.safe_move: Hang up. If genuinely concerned about jury duty, call your local court clerk directly using a number from the official court website.
explanation.consequence: Victims buy hundreds of dollars in gift cards reading numbers to the caller who pockets the funds immediately.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Gift card payment to law enforcement is impossible — any such request is a scam.

framing: You get texts about a new health benefits card.

thread.body: Important: Your federal health benefits card is expiring. A new card has been issued and requires confirmation before it can be activated.
To receive your new card, confirm your current member ID, date of birth, and last four digits of your Federal Benefits Number.
Without confirmation, your coverage will lapse on the 15th. Reply CONFIRM or call 1-888-555-0143 to complete your update.

red_flags.label: Asks for the last four digits of your Federal Benefits Number
Threatens coverage lapse to create urgency
Health benefit agencies mail new cards — they do not text to confirm them
Sent from a regular phone number, not an official short code

explanation.short: Sharing even partial Federal Benefits Numbers by text enables identity theft — benefits offices send cards by mail automatically.
tells: New benefits cards are mailed automatically — no confirmation call or text is required
Providing partial SSN, combined with other info collected, enables identity theft
Threatening lapsed coverage creates panic that overrides skepticism
Official agencies do not ask for SSN digits by text or inbound phone call
explanation.safe_move: Call the number on the back of your current benefits card or on official paperwork if you have concerns about coverage.
explanation.consequence: Your SSN digits and personal details are used with data from other breaches to open fraudulent accounts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never share any digits of your Federal Benefits Number in response to a text or unsolicited call.

framing: You get emails saying you have been selected for a government grant.

thread.body:
Subject: You Have Been Selected — $8,500 Federal Relief Grant

Congratulations. Based on your tax records and income profile, you have been selected to receive an $8,500 federal relief grant. No repayment required.
To claim your grant, a one-time processing fee of $99 is required to verify your banking information for secure direct deposit. Pay at: federal-grant-process.com/fee
Your grant will be canceled if the processing fee is not submitted within 48 hours. This is a one-time opportunity.

red_flags.label: Requires upfront fee to receive a government grant
federal-relief-grants.com is not an official government domain
Government grants require an application — they are not assigned by email
Grant cancellation deadline is fabricated pressure

explanation.short: Government grants require applications and never charge processing fees — any fee to claim a grant is a scam.
tells: You cannot be selected for a grant you never applied for
Real government grants never require upfront fees
Official grant programs use .gov domains
The $99 fee is the actual theft — the $8,500 grant does not exist
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and delete. If interested in real grant programs, search only at grants.gov.
explanation.consequence: You pay the $99 processing fee, which goes directly to the scammer. No grant is ever received.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any grant that requires an upfront fee is a scam — real grants are free to receive.

framing: You get texts asking for disaster relief donations after a recent news event.

thread.body: Families left homeless after the flooding in the southeast need your help. Donate to Disaster Aid Now and 100% of your gift goes to survivors.
Donate now at disasteraidnow-giving.com. We accept all major cards and gift cards for immediate disbursement to affected families.
Every hour counts. A matching donor will double your gift if you donate before midnight tonight.

red_flags.label: Accepts gift cards as a donation method
Disaster Aid Now cannot be verified as a registered charity
No charity can legitimately claim 100% goes to recipients
Midnight matching donor deadline is a common fake urgency tactic

explanation.short: Legitimate charities do not accept gift cards and can always be verified through charity watchdog sites.
tells: Gift card donations are a scammer hallmark — real charities do not accept them
100% to recipients is impossible — all charities have operating costs
Disaster-response charities appear within hours of news events, including fake ones
Verify any charity at Give.org or Charity Navigator before donating
explanation.safe_move: If you want to help with a disaster, donate directly to established organizations you can verify through Charity Navigator or Give.org.
explanation.consequence: Your donation goes entirely to the scammer — no aid reaches any disaster survivors.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verify any charity before donating — especially ones that appear right after news events.

framing: You get a series of fundraising emails for a children's hospital.

thread.body:
Subject: A child named Marcus needs your help today

Marcus, 7, was diagnosed with leukemia last month. His family cannot afford treatment. Your gift of just $25 covers one day of his care.
Update on Marcus: His treatment begins Friday. We are still short of our goal. Please donate today — every dollar gets us closer to his recovery.
Final update: We raised enough for Marcus's first week of treatment thanks to donors like you. Please give again to continue his care. [Donate Now]

red_flags.label: Individual child stories cannot be independently verified
Bright Hope Children's Fund mimics real charity names but is unregistered
Claims goal was met but immediately asks for more donations
Uses a specific child's story to bypass rational evaluation

explanation.short: Fake charities use emotional child stories because they are difficult to verify and hard to say no to — always check registration first.
tells: The goal being met but immediately asking for more is a consistency red flag
Real charities are registered and verifiable — search the name at state charity registries
Emotional individual stories are a common manipulation technique in charity fraud
Verify before giving: Charity Navigator, GuideStar, or your state attorney general's charity registry
explanation.safe_move: Search the charity name on Charity Navigator or GuideStar. If it does not appear, do not donate.
explanation.consequence: Donations go entirely to the scammer. No child is being helped.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify a charity's registration before donating — emotional stories are designed to bypass this step.

framing: You get texts from a veterans support organization.

thread.body: Our veterans are sleeping on the streets tonight. Veterans Forward Fund provides emergency housing and meals to homeless vets. Can you help with $20?
Reply YES to have a donation agent call you, or donate directly via gift card — call 1-888-555-0122 to provide the code.
Your support means a veteran won't go hungry tonight. This is your last chance to make a difference before our midnight deadline.

red_flags.label: Asks you to call and provide gift card codes as a donation
Veterans Forward Fund cannot be found in charity registries
Legitimate charities do not send agents to call you after you text YES
Midnight donation deadlines are fabricated pressure

explanation.short: Gift card codes as donations are always scams — no registered charity collects donations this way.
tells: Real charities accept payments through verified payment processors, not gift card codes over phone
Verified veteran charities can be found at charitynavigator.org
A donation agent calling you after a text reply is not how any legitimate charity operates
Midnight deadlines do not apply to charitable giving
explanation.safe_move: If you want to support veterans, donate to verified organizations through their official websites.
explanation.consequence: Gift card codes are read to the caller and immediately redeemed. No veterans receive any aid.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Gift card codes as charitable donations are always scams — period.

framing: You get fundraising emails for an animal rescue organization.

thread.body:
Subject: 47 dogs rescued from a hoarding situation — they need medical care NOW

We rescued 47 dogs from deplorable conditions. Many need surgery. We're overwhelmed. Your $30 covers one dog's initial vet exam.
We are almost out of funds. Without your help, we may have to surrender these animals to an underfunded shelter. Help us keep them safe — [Donate Now]
A generous donor will match all gifts dollar-for-dollar until midnight. Your $30 becomes $60 for the rescued dogs. Time is running out.

red_flags.label: PawSafe Animal Relief cannot be verified as a registered organization
Midnight matching donor deadline is a common fake urgency tactic
Threat of surrendering animals is emotional manipulation
No physical address, phone number, or verifiable contact information

explanation.short: Real animal rescues have verifiable locations and charity registrations — always check before donating to emotional rescue appeals.
tells: Legitimate rescues have physical addresses and verifiable nonprofit status
Midnight matching donor deadlines are almost always fabricated
Emotional animal rescue stories are commonly exploited by fraud operations
Search the organization name and your state's charity registry before donating
explanation.safe_move: Search for verified local animal rescues or national organizations through Charity Navigator before donating.
explanation.consequence: Your donation goes to the scammer. No animals are rescued.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verify any charity's nonprofit registration before donating, especially after emotional rescue appeals.

framing: You get texts from a cancer research fundraiser.

thread.body: Hi! I'm volunteering for Cancer Hope Alliance's annual fundraiser. We fund breakthrough research for pediatric cancer patients. Can I count on your support today?
Amazing — our researchers are so close to a breakthrough. A $50 gift covers a full day of lab testing. You can donate securely at cancer-hope-give.com or via gift card.
Our fundraiser ends tomorrow. Will you help us reach our goal? Even $10 makes a difference. Text GIVE to donate.

red_flags.label: Offers gift cards as a donation method
Real charity volunteers do not cold-text strangers
Cancer Hope Alliance is not in recognized charity databases
Vague breakthrough claim is designed to create emotional investment

explanation.short: Real charity volunteers do not cold-text people — and gift card donation options signal a scam, not a charity.
tells: Charitable organizations do not solicit donations via unsolicited text messages
Gift card donation options are a definitive scam indicator
Real cancer research charities have verifiable track records on Charity Navigator
Vague research breakthroughs are used to justify donation urgency without accountability
explanation.safe_move: Ignore. If you want to support cancer research, donate directly to a verified organization through their official website.
explanation.consequence: Donations enrich the scammer. No research is being funded.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Gift card options for charitable giving are always a scam signal.

framing: You get emails from someone claiming to be a pastor asking for urgent help.

thread.body: Dear friend, I hope this message finds you well. I am reaching out because our congregation is facing a crisis — our church building's roof collapsed and families have nowhere to gather.
We need to raise $4,000 for emergency repairs by Friday. I am asking trusted members of our community to help. Could you send a gift via wire transfer or gift card? God bless you.
Any amount helps. Please keep this between us as we do not want the congregation to worry. I will explain everything when we meet in person. Reply for payment details.

red_flags.label: Asks you to keep the transaction secret from others
Requests gift cards or wire transfer for a church repair
A church pastor using a personal QuickMail for official financial requests is suspicious
Combination of urgency and secrecy is a social engineering red flag

explanation.short: Requests to keep financial transactions secret combined with gift card payments signal manipulation, not genuine need.
tells: Secrecy requests are designed to prevent you from getting a second opinion
Churches handle funds through transparent processes, not individual wire transfers or gift cards
Scammers impersonate trusted community figures to exploit loyalty
Verify any urgent financial request with the person directly via a known phone number before sending anything
explanation.safe_move: Call the person directly using a phone number you already have — do not use contact info from the email. Verify the request before sending anything.
explanation.consequence: You send gift cards or wire funds believing it is helping a trusted community figure. The scammer receives the funds.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any request for secrecy combined with urgent payment is a social engineering red flag.

framing: A social media friend sends you messages asking you to share a fundraiser.

thread.body: Hey! I know this is out of nowhere but my nephew was in a car accident and is in the ICU. The family set up a fundraiser. Would you be willing to share it?
Here's the link: gofundme-relief-help.com/jason-recovery — even sharing helps so much. The family is devastated and insurance won't cover everything.
If you can donate even $10 that would mean the world. They're good people going through the worst time. I wouldn't ask if it wasn't real.

red_flags.label: gofundme-relief-help.com is not the real crowdfunding platform
The account sending the message may be hacked or fake
I wouldn't ask if it wasn't real — preemptively deflects skepticism
The accident story cannot be independently verified

explanation.short: Lookalike crowdfunding URLs combined with messages from possibly compromised accounts are a common donation scam setup.
tells: The URL mimics a crowdfunding platform but is not the real site
Friends' accounts are frequently hacked to send scam messages to trusted contacts
Pre-deflecting skepticism ("I wouldn't ask if it wasn't real") is a manipulation tactic
Verify by calling the friend directly before clicking or donating
explanation.safe_move: Text or call the friend directly using a number you already have to verify they actually sent this. Do not click the link.
explanation.consequence: The link leads to a phishing page or a fake crowdfunding clone that captures card details.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verify any financial request from a social media friend by calling them directly — their account may be compromised.

framing: You inquire about an apartment listing and receive these emails.

thread.body: Hi! Thanks for your interest in the 2BR at 4412 Maple Ave. The rent is $1,100/mo, utilities included. I'm currently working overseas as a missionary, so I'll need to handle this remotely.
I'd love to get you moved in quickly. To hold the apartment, I just need a $600 security deposit via wire transfer. I'll mail you the keys once I receive it.
Several other people are very interested. If you want to secure it before the weekend, please send the deposit today. I'll send the lease agreement once the transfer clears.

red_flags.label: Landlord claims to be overseas and cannot show the property
Requests wire transfer deposit before any lease agreement is signed
Promises to mail keys after receiving money — never showing the property
Claims other applicants are interested to pressure quick payment

explanation.short: Never send a deposit for a property you have not seen in person — overseas landlords who mail keys are always scammers.
tells: Legitimate landlords can always arrange a showing or designate a local contact
Deposits are paid after signing a lease, not before
Wire transfers are irreversible — this is why scammers insist on them
Keys are never mailed in exchange for a wire transfer
explanation.safe_move: Never pay a deposit without first seeing the property in person and signing a proper lease. Walk away from any listing that cannot be shown.
explanation.consequence: You wire the deposit and never receive keys. The listing does not exist or belongs to someone else.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never send money for a property you have not physically visited.

framing: You find a sublet listing on social media and message the poster.

thread.body: Hi! Yes the room is still available. $850/mo, furnished, near downtown. I'm relocating for work next month so I need someone reliable to take over the lease.
I can't show it right now since I'm already in my new city, but I can send you photos and a virtual tour video. The building manager can let you in once we've agreed on terms.
A few others are interested. To take it off the market, I just need a $400 holding fee via PeerSend. I'll refund it if the virtual tour doesn't meet your expectations.

red_flags.label: Cannot arrange an in-person showing before payment is required
Requests payment app deposit before any lease is signed
Claims other people are interested to pressure quick payment
Promises a refund if you're not satisfied — refunds never come

explanation.short: No legitimate sublet requires payment before an in-person showing — if you cannot see it first, do not pay.
tells: A genuine sublettor can always arrange access through the building manager before collecting money
Payment app holding fees are non-recoverable and a common advance fee scam technique
Competing interest pressure is used to prevent you from thinking clearly
Refund promises from strangers on social media are not enforceable
explanation.safe_move: Insist on viewing the property in person before any payment. If that is not possible, move on to the next listing.
explanation.consequence: You send the holding fee and are then told the deal fell through. The scammer keeps your money.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: See any rental property in person before sending any money — no exceptions.

framing: You inquire about a vacation rental you found online.

thread.body: Thanks for your interest in our beachfront property in Clearwater! $175/night, sleeps 8. Check-in Jul 18-25 is available. Beautiful views, private pool, fully equipped kitchen.
To confirm your reservation, I'll need 50% upfront ($612.50) via wire transfer or gift card. The balance is due 7 days before arrival. I'll email the check-in details once payment is received.
Several families are looking at the same week. To hold your dates, please transfer today. I can accept bankcard gift cards — just call with the numbers.

red_flags.label: Requests gift cards for vacation rental payment
Requests wire transfer deposit before any rental contract exists
Legitimate vacation rentals use established booking platforms, not personal QuickMail
Will only provide check-in details after payment is received

explanation.short: Vacation rentals booked outside established platforms via gift card or wire transfer are almost always fake listings.
tells: Legitimate vacation rentals are booked through established platforms with fraud protection
Gift card payments for vacation rentals are never legitimate
No check-in information until after payment means you have no recourse if the property does not exist
Personal QuickMail for a vacation rental business with no verifiable address is a red flag
explanation.safe_move: Book vacation rentals only through established platforms that offer buyer protection. Never pay via gift card or wire transfer.
explanation.consequence: You arrive at the address to find the property does not exist or belongs to someone else who knows nothing about a rental.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Book vacation rentals only through established platforms — never via wire transfer or gift card.

framing: You post looking for a roommate and receive these messages.

thread.body: Hey! I saw your roommate post. I'm looking for a place starting next month. I'm a grad student, very clean and quiet. Would love to learn more about the space.
Sounds great! One thing — I'm traveling for research right now and can't see it in person yet. My aunt offered to PeerSend you my deposit and first month to hold it. Would that work?
She will actually send you a bit extra by mistake — just send back the difference to her QuickSend once it lands. Really sorry for the inconvenience, she always does this.

red_flags.label: Sends more than the amount and asks you to send back the difference
Payment comes from a third party, not the actual applicant
Cannot view the space in person before committing funds
Asks to secure the space with full payment before any lease or meeting

explanation.short: Overpayment scams send a fake check or payment then ask you to refund the difference — by the time the original bounces, your refund is gone.
tells: Overpayment and send-back-the-difference is a classic check fraud / payment scam pattern
Payments always come from the person who will live there, not a third party
The payment will be reversed after you send the "refund" — leaving you liable
No legitimate roommate secures a place without seeing it or meeting you
explanation.safe_move: Never accept payment from a third party and never send money back before confirming the original payment fully cleared. Require in-person meeting before any payment.
explanation.consequence: The initial payment is reversed or bounced. Your refund of the "extra" goes to the scammer and is unrecoverable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: If someone pays you extra and asks for the difference back — stop. This is always a scam.

framing: You get texts from a landlord you applied to work with.

thread.body: Congratulations! Your application for the unit at 229 Birchwood Ct has been approved. We just need your security deposit of $1,400 to finalize your move-in date.
To process quickly, please send the deposit via wire transfer to the account we will provide, or purchase gift cards and call us with the numbers. Deadline is Friday.
We have other applicants on the waitlist. If we do not receive the deposit by Friday, we will proceed with the next applicant. Please confirm your intent today.

red_flags.label: Requests gift cards for a security deposit
Requests wire transfer for a security deposit before move-in
Claims waitlist will take your unit to pressure immediate payment
Requests deposit payment before providing a signed lease

explanation.short: Security deposits are paid via traceable methods after signing a lease — never by gift card or wire to an unknown account.
tells: Legitimate property management companies accept checks or traceable electronic payments
Security deposits are collected when you sign the lease, not before
Gift card deposit requests are always fraudulent
Verify any property management company's address and reviews before sending money
explanation.safe_move: Request a lease before any payment. Deposits should always be paid via check or established payment platforms with receipts.
explanation.consequence: You send the deposit and the scammer disappears. You have no lease and no recourse.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never pay a deposit before you have a signed lease in your hands.

framing: You get emails from a housing assistance program.

thread.body:
Subject: Housing Assistance Approval — Action Required

You have been pre-approved for our housing assistance program that covers up to 80% of rent for qualifying applicants. Units are available in your area.
To complete your enrollment, a $75 background check and application processing fee is required. Pay at affordable-homes-apply.com/fee. Approval is not final until the fee is received.
Your pre-approval expires in 48 hours. If we do not receive your fee by then, your slot will be reassigned to the next applicant. Complete enrollment now.

red_flags.label: Legitimate housing assistance programs do not charge application fees
affordable-homes-assistance.com is not an official government housing site
You were pre-approved for a program you never applied to

explanation.short: Real government housing assistance programs are free — any program charging an upfront fee is a scam targeting people who need help most.
tells: Government housing programs never charge fees to apply or enroll
You cannot be pre-approved for a program you never applied to
Official housing programs use .gov domains
The fee is the scam — no housing assistance follows
explanation.safe_move: Contact your local housing authority directly using contact information from your local government website. Real programs are free to apply to.
explanation.consequence: You pay the $75 fee and receive nothing. The scammer targets people in vulnerable housing situations.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any housing assistance program that charges a fee is a scam — real programs are always free to apply to.

framing: You email about a rental listing and receive these replies.

thread.body: Thank you for your interest. The 3BR home is available immediately at $1,350/mo. I am currently on a mission trip and my property manager has moved to another state, so I am handling this directly.
I will arrange for a lockbox with the key so you can do a self-tour. To receive the lockbox code, I just need the first and last month's rent ($2,700) to confirm you are serious.
Once funds clear, I will text you the lockbox code. If the home does not match the listing, I will personally refund every dollar. I am a man of faith and my word is my bond.

red_flags.label: Requires payment before providing access to view the property
Landlord is overseas and unavailable to show the property
Appeals to religious identity to establish trust
Requests full first and last month upfront before lease or showing

explanation.short: Paying for a lockbox code before seeing a property is paying for nothing — the code never comes or opens an empty unit.
tells: Legitimate landlords arrange showings before collecting any money
The overseas mission trip is a common fiction used in rental scams
Religious trust appeals are used to lower your guard, not a sign of legitimacy
No lease, no background check, and full payment upfront are all red flags
explanation.safe_move: Never pay to receive access to view a property. If a landlord cannot show it before payment, move on.
explanation.consequence: You send $2,700 and never receive a lockbox code. The property owner has no idea their listing is being used by a scammer.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: You should view a property before paying anything — including a deposit to see it.

framing: You get a series of emails claiming you won an international lottery.

thread.body:
Subject: Congratulations — You Have Won $450,000

Your email address was randomly selected in our annual Global Prosperity Lottery. You are entitled to $450,000 USD. Claim reference: GWF-2024-77412.
To release your winnings, a transfer facilitation fee of $285 is required to cover international banking clearance. Pay at global-winners-claim.com/fee to initiate your transfer.
Your prize is being held in escrow. Unclaimed funds expire after 10 days. Complete your fee payment to avoid forfeiture of your $450,000 award.

red_flags.label: Requires upfront fee to release lottery winnings
You never entered any lottery or contest
global-winners-foundation.com is not a real organization
Unclaimed fund expiry creates pressure to pay the fee quickly

explanation.short: You cannot win a lottery you never entered, and real winnings are never released in exchange for upfront fees.
tells: Legitimate lotteries never charge fees to release winnings
You cannot win a contest you did not enter
The fee is the scam — no $450,000 exists
Escalating emails with deadlines are used to collect fees before skepticism sets in
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and delete. You have not won anything.
explanation.consequence: You pay the $285 fee and are then asked for additional fees. No prize is ever received.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any prize that requires upfront payment is a scam — real winnings do not come with fees.

framing: You get texts claiming you won a sweepstakes.

thread.body: NovaMart: You've been selected as our weekly sweepstakes winner! Your prize: a $1,000 NovaMart gift card. Reply YES to claim.
Congrats! To receive your gift card, a $15 shipping and handling fee applies. Pay at novamart-prizes.com/shipping. Card will arrive within 5-7 business days.
Your gift card is ready to ship! Complete your $15 payment today — unclaimed prizes are forfeited after 48 hours.

red_flags.label: Requires payment of a shipping fee to receive a prize
Legitimate sweepstakes do not notify winners by unsolicited text
novamart-prizes.com is not the real company's domain
48-hour forfeiture deadline creates pressure to pay

explanation.short: Shipping fees to receive prizes are always a scam — real prizes are delivered without extra charges to winners.
tells: Real sweepstakes winners are notified via official company channels, not random texts
Shipping fees to claim a prize are a classic advance fee scam variant
The $15 fee is the actual theft — no gift card will arrive
Verify any sweepstakes claim through the company's official website
explanation.safe_move: Ignore. Real sweepstakes do not require payment to receive your prize.
explanation.consequence: You pay the $15 fee and never receive a gift card. Your card details may also be used for future charges.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real prizes never require shipping fees or processing payments — any such request is a scam.

framing: You get emails claiming you won a car in a raffle.

thread.body:
Subject: You Are Our Grand Prize Winner — 2024 Luxury SUV

Congratulations! Your raffle ticket #LX-2024-48812 was drawn as our grand prize winner. You have won a 2024 luxury SUV valued at $67,000.
To claim your vehicle, a prize transfer tax of $1,450 must be paid upfront, as required by federal prize regulations. Provide your bank details at: auto-prize-claim.com/transfer
Failure to pay the transfer tax within 72 hours will result in forfeiture of your prize and reassignment to the alternate winner. Complete your claim now.

red_flags.label: Requires upfront payment of prize taxes before receiving the prize
luxury-auto-raffle.com and auto-prize-claim.com are not legitimate organizations
You never purchased a ticket for this raffle
Federal prize tax regulations do not require upfront payment to the prize giver

explanation.short: Prize taxes on real winnings are paid to the tax agency after receiving the prize — not upfront to the prize organization.
tells: Real prize taxes are settled with the tax agency, not paid upfront to the prize giver
You cannot win a raffle you never entered
The $1,450 fee is the scam — no car exists
Federal regulations do not require winners to prepay taxes to claim prizes
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and delete. You have not won a car.
explanation.consequence: You pay the $1,450 and are often asked for additional fees. No vehicle is ever received.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Prize taxes on legitimate winnings are paid to the tax agency — never upfront to the prize giver.

framing: A social media account DMs you claiming you won a giveaway.

thread.body: Congratulations! You were randomly selected from followers who liked our post last week. You've won a $500 NovaMart shopping spree! DM us to claim.
To send your prize, we just need your full name, mailing address, and email. Also, a $10 gift card processing fee is required to activate your reward code.
We have multiple winners to process today. Please send the processing fee and your info within 2 hours or your prize will go to our backup winner.

red_flags.label: Requires a processing fee to activate a prize
The account has a handle that mimics a real brand but is not verified
Requests full name, mailing address, and email for a prize
2-hour deadline creates pressure before you can verify the account

explanation.short: Real brand giveaways are posted from verified accounts and never require fees or personal details via DM.
tells: Real company giveaway accounts are verified — check for the verification badge
Processing fees to activate prizes are always scams
Providing your home address to an unverified account enables further targeting
Real giveaway winners are announced publicly, not via DM
explanation.safe_move: Check if the account is actually the official verified brand account. If in doubt, navigate to the brand's real social profile and check their actual giveaway posts.
explanation.consequence: Your home address and personal details are collected for targeting. The $10 fee is stolen. No prize arrives.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real brand giveaways never require fees or your home address via DM — look for the verified badge.

framing: You get texts about a mailer scratch card you recently received.

thread.body: Congratulations! Our records show you scratched a winning ticket from our recent mailing. Your prize: $2,500 or a vacation package. Call 1-888-555-0131 to verify.
Your prize number 4-7-2 shows a $2,500 cash win. To receive your check, a $50 processing and verification fee is required. Provide a credit card number when you call.
This is your final notification. Processing fees not received by end of day will result in prize forfeiture. Call now to claim your $2,500.

red_flags.label: Requires credit card for a fee to claim a scratch card prize
Unsolicited mailer followed by text and phone pressure is a known scam pattern
Cash or vacation package choices are used to make the prize feel certain
End-of-day forfeiture deadline is fabricated pressure

explanation.short: Everyone who receives these mailers has a "winning" ticket — the prize does not exist, and the fee is the entire point.
tells: Mass mailer scratch cards are all "winners" — the prize is contingent on paying the fee
Providing credit card details to claim a prize hands the scammer your full card information
Two-choice prizes (cash or vacation) create certainty that prevents dismissal
Real lottery or sweepstakes prizes never require a fee to be paid via phone
explanation.safe_move: Hang up. Throw away the mailer. You have not won anything.
explanation.consequence: Your credit card details are captured when you call. Charges appear within days.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Scratch card prize fees are always scams — the fee IS the scam, not a processing formality.

framing: You get emails about a reward for completing a survey.

thread.body:
Subject: Your $750 Survey Reward Is Ready

Thank you for completing a consumer survey last month. Your $750 reward has been approved and is ready for redemption. Click below to claim.
To receive your reward, select your preferred payout method and provide your shipping address for verification. A $6.97 S&H fee applies for physical reward cards.
Your reward expires in 24 hours. Enter your card details now to cover the S&H and confirm your delivery address: consumer-reward-claim.com/claim

red_flags.label: Requires credit card for S&H to claim a survey reward
consumer-survey-rewards.com and consumer-reward-claim.com are not legitimate
You may not remember completing any such survey
24-hour expiry creates pressure to enter card details quickly

explanation.short: Survey reward S&H fees are designed to capture your credit card details — the $750 reward does not exist.
tells: Shipping fees on rewards are how your card details are captured
Legitimate survey rewards are fulfilled without requiring payment
The small S&H amount seems trivial but your card is then used for larger charges
Real survey reward emails come from verifiable organizations with official domains
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and delete. Real survey rewards do not require payment to receive.
explanation.consequence: Your card details are captured via the "S&H" form and used for larger unauthorized charges.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never enter card details to claim a reward — real rewards are sent without fees.

framing: A crypto account DMs you about winning a giveaway.

thread.body: Congratulations! You have been selected for our annual CoinVault user appreciation giveaway. Your prize: 0.05 BTC (approx $3,200). To claim, verify your wallet address.
To release your BTC reward, you must first send 0.001 BTC to our verification address to confirm your wallet is active. Your 0.05 BTC will be sent immediately after.
This giveaway ends in 4 hours. Unclaimed BTC rewards will be reallocated. Send your verification amount now to receive your full prize.

red_flags.label: Requires sending cryptocurrency to receive a larger crypto prize
The account name mimics CoinVault but is not the verified official account
4-hour deadline prevents you from researching the account
Verification amount is a pretext to steal crypto with no prize sent back

explanation.short: Any crypto giveaway that requires you to send crypto first to receive more is always a scam — you send, they keep, nothing returns.
tells: Send-to-receive crypto giveaways have zero legitimate examples — 100% of them are scams
Real crypto platforms never require a verification send to release a prize
Impersonator accounts are created to look like official brand accounts
Crypto transactions are irreversible — once sent, the funds are gone
explanation.safe_move: Ignore. Never send cryptocurrency to receive a larger amount. Navigate to the real platform directly to check for any legitimate promotions.
explanation.consequence: You send the verification BTC and receive nothing. Crypto transactions cannot be reversed.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any crypto giveaway requiring you to send funds first is 100% a scam — always.

framing: You are selling a bicycle and receive these messages from a buyer.

thread.body: Hi! I'm very interested in the bike. Is it still available? I can offer your full asking price. I'm out of town but can arrange shipping.
Great! I'll pay via QuickSend. My cousin can pick it up and ship it for me. Can you ship it once I pay? I'll send extra for shipping costs.
I sent $380 — your asking price plus $80 for shipping. Once you get the funds, just send $80 to my cousin's QuickSend so he can arrange pickup. Thank you!

red_flags.label: Pays more than asking price and asks you to send back the difference
Buyer is always out of town and cannot meet in person
Uses a third party cousin to create a reason for extra payment
Wants you to ship before you have confirmed funds

explanation.short: The overpayment plus send-back request is a classic scam — the original payment will be reversed, leaving you liable for the amount you forwarded.
tells: Overpayment with a refund request is the defining pattern of check/payment fraud
Legitimate buyers either meet in person or use escrow services
The "cousin pickup" is a fiction to justify the extra payment
Payment app funds can be reversed via fraud dispute, leaving you out the forwarded amount
explanation.safe_move: Only sell in person with cash or verified payment. Never send money back to a buyer before funds fully clear.
explanation.consequence: The original payment is reversed. You have forwarded $80 of your own money to the scammer.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any buyer who overpays and asks for money back is running a scam — walk away immediately.

framing: You are selling a laptop online and get these emails from a buyer.

thread.body: Hello, I saw your laptop listing. I am interested in buying it. Is it still available? I will pay your asking price plus shipping. I am a serious buyer.
Perfect. I will pay via QuickSend Business which holds funds in escrow for seller protection. You will receive a payment confirmation email. Once you get it, ship the item.

Payment Confirmation: David K has sent $450 for your laptop. Funds are held in escrow. To release payment to your account, first ship the item and send the tracking number to: release@quicksend-escrow-protection.com

red_flags.label: quicksend-escrow-protection.com is not a real escrow or payment service
Requires shipping before payment is released — you risk losing the item
The payment confirmation email is fake — no funds exist
Buyer chooses the payment method to control how you are deceived

explanation.short: Fake escrow confirmation emails are sent to trick you into shipping — the funds do not exist and you lose the item.
tells: Real escrow services are established companies, not custom domains set up for one transaction
Payment app escrow is not a real feature of standard payment apps
Once you ship, you lose all leverage — the "escrow" disappears
Verify any payment by logging into your actual payment account, not by checking an email
explanation.safe_move: Verify funds in your actual payment account before shipping anything. Never ship based on an email confirmation alone.
explanation.consequence: You ship the laptop. The escrow email was fake. You have no item and no payment.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify payment in your actual account before shipping — email confirmations can be faked.

framing: You are buying furniture from a marketplace listing and message the seller.

thread.body: Hi! Yes the sectional is still available, $400. Beautiful condition, barely used. I need to sell quickly because I'm moving next week.
I can hold it for you with a $100 deposit via PeerSend. Just to make sure you're serious. The rest is cash on pickup. Does that work?
A few others are interested but I'd rather sell to someone local. Send the $100 deposit today and I'll take it off the market. Moving truck comes Saturday.

red_flags.label: Requests deposit before you have seen the item in person
Moving deadline creates pressure to pay quickly
Claims other interested buyers to pressure your decision
Payment app deposits for marketplace items are not recoverable if item does not exist

explanation.short: Marketplace deposits via payment apps before seeing an item are not recoverable if the seller disappears.
tells: See any item in person before paying any amount
Moving deadlines and competing buyers are common pressure tactics in marketplace scams
Payment app transactions are treated as voluntary and are difficult to reverse
Legitimate sellers accept payment at pickup, not before
explanation.safe_move: Arrange a viewing first. Only pay at the time of pickup when you have the item in hand.
explanation.consequence: You send $100 and the seller stops responding. The listing disappears.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never send a marketplace deposit before seeing an item — pay at pickup only.

framing: You are trying to buy concert tickets from someone online.

thread.body: Yes I have 2 floor tickets for the show on the 22nd. Paid $220 each, selling for $180 each. They're digital tickets so I can transfer them instantly.
I'll transfer the tickets as soon as I receive payment. Pay $360 to my CashLink and I'll send the transfer immediately after. I have great feedback, been doing this a long time.
The show is almost sold out everywhere. This is your best bet for floor seats. Send payment now and I'll get the tickets to you within minutes.

red_flags.label: Requires full payment before transferring tickets
Payment app transactions for tickets from strangers are not recoverable
Claims good feedback but provides no verifiable proof
Show being sold out creates pressure to skip verification

explanation.short: Digital tickets should be transferred first or simultaneously — paying first to a stranger for tickets often results in fakes or no delivery.
tells: Legitimate ticket resellers use established platforms with buyer protection
Payment before ticket transfer leaves you with no recourse if tickets never arrive
Self-reported feedback from a stranger is not verifiable
Sold-out urgency is designed to prevent you from checking verified resale platforms
explanation.safe_move: Buy tickets through verified resale platforms with buyer guarantees. Never pay a stranger via payment app for tickets.
explanation.consequence: You send $360 and receive no tickets, or receive fake tickets rejected at the door.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Buy event tickets only through verified platforms with buyer protection — never via payment app to a stranger.

framing: You are inquiring about a puppy for sale.

thread.body: Yes we have 2 golden retriever puppies available, 8 weeks old, fully vaccinated, AKC registered. $800 each. We can ship to your area safely.
To reserve your puppy, we need a $200 deposit via wire transfer. The balance and shipping ($150) are due when the puppy ships. We'll send photos and vet records once you're confirmed.
We have another family also interested in the same puppy. To hold her for you, please send the deposit today. She is adorable and won't last long.

red_flags.label: Requests wire transfer deposit to ship a pet you have never seen in person
Vet records and photos only provided after deposit is received
Another family is interested creates pressure to pay before verifying
No option to visit and see the puppy before purchasing

explanation.short: Pet shipping scams use emotional appeals and fake puppies to collect deposits and shipping fees that are never refunded.
tells: Reputable breeders allow visits — always see a pet in person before purchasing
Vet records should be provided upfront, not after payment
Wire transfer deposits for pets are non-recoverable
Additional shipping fees are a secondary extraction after the initial deposit
explanation.safe_move: Only buy pets from breeders you can visit in person. Require all documentation before any payment.
explanation.consequence: You pay the deposit and possibly the shipping fee. No puppy arrives. The scammer is unreachable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never pay to ship a pet you have not seen in person — visit the breeder first.

framing: You matched with someone on a dating app who says they are a soldier overseas.

thread.body: Hello. I know this is sudden but I came across your profile and felt I had to reach out. I'm a colonel stationed in Germany. It gets very lonely here. Would you be open to talking?

I feel such a connection with you already. I've been thinking about what life looks like after I return. I have savings but they're frozen in a military account until I'm stateside. It can be frustrating.
My transport leave was approved but I need to pay a small processing fee to get my funds released and buy my ticket home. Is there any way you could help? I would pay you back within days of landing.

red_flags.label: Claims to be military overseas — a very common romance scam persona
Mentions frozen funds that will be released soon to prime a loan request
Promises quick repayment once back in the country
Builds strong emotional connection quickly before making a financial ask

explanation.short: Military romance scams follow a script: soldier overseas, frozen funds, small loan to come home — the soldier and the return never materialize.
tells: Active duty soldiers do not pay for their own leave transport
Military accounts are not frozen — this story is a fabricated setup for a loan request
Building intense emotional connection quickly before asking for money is the core romance scam pattern
Reverse image search the profile photos — they are typically stolen from real people
explanation.safe_move: Never send money to someone you have only met online, regardless of how real the connection feels. Reverse image search their photos.
explanation.consequence: You send money for the ticket. More requests follow — medical bills, customs fees, more transport costs. The person never arrives.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never send money to an online romantic interest, no matter how real the connection feels.

framing: You have been emailing someone from a faith-based dating site for several weeks.

thread.body: I feel so comfortable sharing with you. Since losing my husband three years ago, I have not felt this kind of warmth with anyone. You remind me so much of him — patient, kind, faithful.
I have been planning to visit my daughter in the UK and I thought — what if I came to see you first? I have the flights priced and I am so excited. This feels like something real.

I am devastated — my card was frozen due to fraud on the account, just days before my flight. I hate asking this. Could you help cover the ticket ($680)? I will transfer everything back the moment I arrive.

red_flags.label: Card is conveniently frozen just before a planned visit
Has never met you in person despite strong expressed feelings
Promises to repay immediately upon arrival — arrival never happens
Weeks of emotional investment before the financial request

explanation.short: Weeks of emotional connection followed by a last-minute emergency financial ask is the defining pattern of romance fraud.
tells: The timing of the card freeze — just before visiting — is not coincidence, it is the script
Romance scammers invest weeks or months building trust before the ask
The visit will always be prevented by a new emergency even if you pay
The identity being presented is fabricated using stolen photos and borrowed life stories
explanation.safe_move: Do not send money. If you want to continue the relationship, insist on a verified video call before any financial discussion.
explanation.consequence: You send $680 for the flight. A new emergency prevents the visit. More requests follow.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never send money to someone you have only met online — the emergency timing is always intentional.

framing: You have been chatting with someone you met through social media who talks about investing.

thread.body: It's so nice connecting with someone who appreciates real conversation. I have been doing really well with my investments lately — my uncle teaches finance in Hong Kong and shared a strategy with me.
I don't share this with many people but I trust you. The platform is called CoinHarvest Pro — I started with $500 and made $3,200 in three weeks. I can walk you through it if you want.
Your account shows a great return! But to withdraw, the platform requires a 15% tax deposit first. It's standard. I had to do it too. Once you pay, everything releases immediately.

red_flags.label: Requires upfront tax payment to withdraw your investment profits
Romantic contact introduces an investment opportunity — pig butchering pattern
Cites a family expert abroad as the source of the strategy
CoinHarvest Pro cannot be verified as a legitimate regulated platform

explanation.short: This is pig butchering: a romantic connection grooms you to invest on a fake platform, then blocks withdrawals unless you pay a fabricated tax.
tells: Building romance then introducing investments is called pig butchering — one of the most financially devastating scam types
The platform's profits are fictional numbers on a fake dashboard
Withdrawal tax fees are the final extraction — you cannot withdraw because the money was never real
The family expert abroad is a stock element of this script
explanation.safe_move: Stop all contact and do not send any additional funds. Report to the FTC and your financial institution immediately.
explanation.consequence: Victims lose tens of thousands of dollars paying fees to "withdraw" profits that were always fictitious.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any romantic contact who introduces investment opportunities is running the pig butchering scam.

framing: Someone reaches out after seeing you comment in a community group.

thread.body: Hi! I saw your comment in the neighborhood group and had to reach out. You seem like a genuinely thoughtful person. Not trying to be weird, just rare to find good conversation these days.

I've been going through a rough patch — my business partner cleaned out our joint account and I'm in a legal battle. Meanwhile I have a closing next week worth $40K but my attorney's retainer is due Friday.
I have no one to ask. I know this is a lot to put on a new friend. Even $500 as a short-term loan would get me through this. I would repay you the moment the closing funds clear.

red_flags.label: Builds brief rapport then quickly asks for money
Detailed hardship story designed to seem credible and evoke sympathy
Claims a large payoff is coming soon to justify repayment
Claims to have no one else to ask — creates obligation to help

explanation.short: Detailed hardship stories with an imminent payoff and no one else to ask are engineered to make lending money feel both urgent and safe.
tells: The entire interaction exists to build enough rapport for the loan request
The impending $40K closing is fabricated to make repayment feel certain
No one to ask isolates you as the solution to a manufactured crisis
This pattern works especially well in community groups where people already have a sense of shared trust
explanation.safe_move: Do not send money to anyone you have only met online, regardless of how credible their story sounds.
explanation.consequence: You send $500. The closing never happens. Contact stops immediately after payment.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A financial ask from someone you just met online — regardless of their story — is almost always a scam.

framing: You receive this email in your work inbox. Read it carefully before answering.

message.subject: Invoice #INV-2024-7741 — Payment Due
message.body:
Please find attached invoice #INV-2024-7741 for $3,847.00 for office supplies ordered in October. Payment is due within 5 business days. Wire transfer details are attached.

OfficeSupply Direct
Accounts Receivable

red_flags.label: officesupply-direct-invoices.com is not the real vendor domain
No such order was placed — this is a fabricated invoice
Requests wire transfer which is irreversible
5-day deadline on a large amount pressures quick payment

explanation.short: Fake vendor invoices target businesses, hoping someone pays without verifying whether the order was actually placed.
tells: Verify any invoice against actual purchase orders before paying
Lookalike vendor domains are created specifically to deceive accounts payable staff
Wire transfers cannot be recalled once sent
Real vendor invoices come from domains matching the vendor's actual website
explanation.safe_move: Contact the vendor directly using contact info from their real website — not from this email — to verify the invoice.
explanation.consequence: The wire transfer is sent to the scammer's account. Wire transfers are nearly impossible to reverse.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify invoices against purchase orders before payment — especially wire transfers.

framing: You receive this email appearing to be from your regular software vendor.

message.subject: Urgent: Software License Renewal — Expiring in 48 Hours
message.body:
Your NexSoft Enterprise license expires in 48 hours. To avoid service interruption, complete renewal payment of $6,200 via the link below.

[Renew License Now]

Failure to renew will disable your software access. Contact renewals@nexsoft-license-renewals.net with any questions.

NexSoft Licensing Team

red_flags.label: nexsoft-license-renewals.net is not the real vendor domain
Threatens immediate service interruption to drive panic payment
Payment link in email bypasses normal purchase order process
$6,200 renewal with a 48-hour deadline prevents proper verification

explanation.short: Software vendors send renewal notices weeks in advance through official channels — 48-hour ultimatums from lookalike domains are scams.
tells: Legitimate software renewals come from the vendor's real domain with advance notice
Service interruption threats create urgency that bypasses verification
Large payments should always go through a proper purchase order, not an email link
Log into the vendor's real website to check your actual license status
explanation.safe_move: Log into your vendor account directly. Call the vendor using the number on their official website to verify.
explanation.consequence: Payment is sent to the scammer. Your actual software license was not expiring.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verify software renewal notices by logging into the vendor's real account — never pay via an email link.

framing: You get this email claiming to be from your accounting software provider.

message.subject: Action Required: Update Payment Method to Avoid Suspension
message.body:
Your LedgerPro subscription payment failed. To keep your account active and avoid data loss, update your billing information within 24 hours.

[Update Payment Method]

After 24 hours, your account will be suspended and data may be permanently deleted.

LedgerPro Customer Success

red_flags.label: Threatens permanent data deletion to create panic
ledgerpro-billing-update.com is not the real service domain
Links to update billing information — leads to a credential or card phishing page

explanation.short: Legitimate SaaS companies give ample notice for billing failures and direct you to your account dashboard — not a link in an email.
tells: Real billing failure notices come from the company's actual domain
Data deletion threats are exaggerated to create panic and bypass skepticism
Update billing links in emails lead to phishing pages — always go directly to the service
Log into your account dashboard directly to check subscription status
explanation.safe_move: Navigate to the service's real website yourself and log in to check your subscription status.
explanation.consequence: The update billing link leads to a phishing page. Your card and login details are captured.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never click billing update links in emails — always log into the service directly.

framing: You receive this invoice from your office cleaning service.

message.subject: Invoice #CS-1194 — October Services
message.body:
Hi Team,

Please find attached Invoice #CS-1194 for October cleaning services — $420.00, due November 15.

You can pay by check to the address on file or ACH to the account on the invoice. Please reach out with any questions.

Thank you,
CleanSpace Services
Phone: (555) 203-4410

red_flags.label: Sender domain matches the business name
Real contact phone number is included
Invoice amount is consistent with expected service costs
Requests check or ACH — not wire transfer or gift cards

explanation.short: This is a legitimate invoice — the sender domain matches the business, a real phone number is provided, and standard payment methods are requested.
tells: Domain matches the vendor name — no hyphenated subdomain tricks
Phone number allows independent verification of the invoice
Check and ACH are standard business payment methods, not red flags
Invoice amount is reasonable for the described service
explanation.safe_move: Verify the invoice against your service agreement and pay through your normal accounts payable process.
explanation.consequence: No scam here — this is what a legitimate vendor invoice looks like.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate invoices have matching domains, verifiable contact info, and standard payment methods.

framing: You receive this email appearing to change payment details for a regular supplier.

message.subject: Important: Updated Banking Details for Future Payments
message.body:
Dear Valued Customer,

Please be advised that our banking details have changed effective immediately. All future payments should be sent to our new account:

Bank: Horizon Financial
Account: 7741882293
Routing: 021000089

Please update your records. Our previous account will be closed by month end. Contact us at the number below if you have any questions.

Parkway Supply Finance Team

red_flags.label: Bank account changes sent only by email should always be verified by phone
Effective immediately creates urgency before verification can happen
Previous account closing soon adds pressure to switch quickly
Business email compromise redirects payments to scammer accounts

explanation.short: Redirecting payments to new bank accounts via email is the core technique of business email compromise fraud — always verify by phone.
tells: Legitimate vendors communicate banking changes through multiple channels and allow time to verify
Immediate effect leaves no time for verification — this is intentional
Call the vendor at a number from your own records, not from this email
Even if the email domain looks real, the vendor's email may be compromised
explanation.safe_move: Call the vendor at a phone number from your existing records — not from this email — to verify the banking change before updating anything.
explanation.consequence: You update the payment details. Next invoice payment goes to the scammer's account. The real vendor is also a victim.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify banking detail changes with a phone call to a number you already have on file.

framing: You get this renewal notice for your team's project management tool.

message.subject: Your TaskFlow subscription renews in 14 days
message.body:
Hi there,
