To update your payment method or plan, log into your account at taskflow.io/billing.

Questions? Contact support@taskflow.io or visit our help center.

TaskFlow Billing

explanation.short: This is a legitimate renewal notice — 14 days advance notice, no urgent action required, and directs you to the real domain to manage your account.
tells: Sender domain matches the service domain (taskflow.io)
14-day advance notice is ample time to verify — no artificial urgency
No action is required — the charge is automatic, which is normal for subscriptions
Directs you to the real website to manage billing, not to click a payment link
explanation.safe_move: Log into your account at the real domain to verify the renewal if needed.
explanation.consequence: No scam here — this is a model example of a legitimate renewal notice.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate subscription renewals give advance notice and direct you to the real service website.

framing: You applied to several jobs recently and get this email.

message.subject: Job Offer — Remote Customer Success Role — $65K
message.body:
Congratulations! After reviewing your profile on JobBoard, we would like to offer you a Customer Success Representative position. This is a fully remote role paying $65,000/year.

To get started, please complete a brief online interview at talentbridge-screen.com. If selected, equipment will be shipped to you and an initial check for supplies will be mailed.

TalentBridge Recruitment

red_flags.label: Mails a check for supplies — classic overpayment scam setup
talentbridge-careers-hiring.com is not a real recruiter domain
Job offer made before any interview or screening
Equipment shipped to your address sets up additional scam steps

explanation.short: Unsolicited job offers that mention mailing a check for supplies are always the setup for an overpayment check fraud scam.
tells: Legitimate jobs require interviews before offers
A check mailed for supplies is the setup: you deposit it, buy gift cards, send codes — check bounces days later
Lookalike recruiter domains are created to look professional but are unregistered
Real companies ship equipment through corporate accounts, not by mailing personal checks
explanation.safe_move: Ignore. Search the company name separately to find their real website and verify they have real open positions.
explanation.consequence: You deposit the check and buy gift cards for "supplies." The check bounces. You are liable for the full amount.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any job that mails you a check and asks you to buy things with it is a scam.

framing: You receive this message after uploading your resume to a job site.

message.subject: Immediate Placement Available — $28/hr Data Entry
message.body:
Dear Job Seeker,

We have reviewed your profile and have an immediate data entry position available at $28/hr, fully remote. This role requires no experience.

To proceed, a background check is required ($49 processing fee) payable at bgcheck-portal.com/apply. Once cleared, your start date will be confirmed within 24 hours.

Global Staffing Partners

red_flags.label: Requires you to pay for your own background check upfront
global-staffing-partners.net and bgcheck-portal.com are not legitimate
High pay for no-experience remote work is unrealistically attractive
24-hour start date after fee payment creates urgency

explanation.short: Legitimate employers pay for background checks — any job that requires you to pay for your own is a fee harvesting scam.
tells: Real employers absorb background check costs — candidates never pay
High hourly pay for zero-experience remote work does not reflect real market rates
The fee site is separate from the staffing company — both are controlled by the scammer
24-hour placement promises are unrealistic and designed to prevent research
explanation.safe_move: Never pay fees to get a job. Report the listing to the job site where you uploaded your resume.
explanation.consequence: You pay $49 for a background check. No job follows. Your card details may also be used for further charges.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Employers pay for background checks — any job that makes you pay is a scam.

framing: You applied for a marketing coordinator role two weeks ago and get this response.

message.subject: Interview Invitation — Marketing Coordinator
message.body:
Hi,

Thank you for applying for the Marketing Coordinator role at Meridian Creative. We'd love to schedule a 30-minute phone screen with you.

Please use the link below to pick a time that works for you:
[Schedule Interview]

Looking forward to speaking with you.

Samantha L.
Talent Acquisition, Meridian Creative Group

explanation.short: This is a legitimate recruiter email — it responds to an application you made, comes from a matching domain, and only asks you to schedule a call.
tells: Domain matches the company name without extra hyphens or keywords
Responds to an application you actually submitted
Only asks for your time — no fees, equipment, or checks mentioned
Named recruiter with a title adds verifiability
explanation.safe_move: Proceed with scheduling the interview. You can verify the company is real by searching their website before the call.
explanation.consequence: No scam here — this is what a legitimate recruiter follow-up looks like.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate recruiters ask for your time, not your money.

framing: You receive this job offer from a company you did not apply to.

message.subject: Offer Letter — Remote Administrative Assistant
message.body:
Dear Candidate,

We are pleased to offer you the Remote Administrative Assistant position at $55,000/year. Your profile was recommended to us by a mutual contact.

Before your start date, we require the following to process your onboarding:
— Copy of your driver's license
— Federal Benefits Number for payroll setup
— Direct deposit banking information

Please reply with these details at your earliest convenience.

NovaCorp HR Department

red_flags.label: Requests Federal Benefits Number before any interview or formal offer
Requests copy of driver's license by email
Requests banking information before employment is confirmed
You never applied to this company

explanation.short: Collecting your SSN, ID, and banking details before any interview is identity theft, not onboarding.
tells: Legitimate employers collect tax documents (including SSN) only after you formally accept and begin onboarding through secured systems
Driver's license copies are never sent by reply email to an unknown sender
No company makes a job offer without an interview to someone who never applied
The combination of SSN, ID, and banking info enables full identity theft and account takeover
explanation.safe_move: Do not reply. This is an identity theft attempt disguised as a job offer.
explanation.consequence: Your SSN, ID, and banking details are used to open fraudulent accounts, file false tax returns, and drain your bank account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never send your SSN, ID, or banking details to an employer before you have formally started.

framing: You get this message from a company after connecting on a professional network.

message.subject: Exclusive Opportunity — $80K Remote Analyst Role
message.body:
Hi, we came across your profile and believe you'd be a perfect fit for a Senior Data Analyst role we're filling urgently. $80K base, fully remote, flexible hours.

This role will not be posted publicly. Interested candidates must complete a skills assessment at skylink-assess.com/apply and provide a $25 assessment processing fee.

Spots are limited. Respond within 48 hours.

SkyLink Talent Acquisition

red_flags.label: Requires a fee to complete a job skills assessment
"Not posted publicly" creates exclusivity to prevent research
skylink-consulting-careers.net is not the real company domain
48-hour deadline prevents careful verification of the company

explanation.short: Legitimate employers never charge candidates assessment fees — this is a fee harvesting scam using exclusivity as cover.
tells: Real employers pay for assessments — candidates never pay
Not-posted-publicly claims make you feel special and prevent external verification
Lookalike career domains are created to appear professional
48-hour response windows are designed to prevent you from researching the company
explanation.safe_move: Ignore. Search the company name separately to find their real website and verify they are a real company with real openings.
explanation.consequence: You pay $25 and receive no job. Your card details may be used for additional charges.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No legitimate employer charges candidates to complete an assessment.

framing: You are currently employed and receive this message from a headhunter.

message.subject: Confidential: VP-Level Opportunity — $180K
message.body:
We are conducting a confidential search for a VP of Operations for a growth-stage company. Based on your background, you are one of five candidates we are considering.

Due to the confidential nature of this search, we require a refundable $150 engagement deposit to verify your seriousness before sharing the company's identity.

This is refundable at any point if you choose not to proceed. Reply to express interest.

Premier Executive Search

red_flags.label: Requires a deposit to be considered for a job
Promises refundability — refunds from scammers do not happen
Company identity withheld until deposit is paid — prevents verification
One of five candidates creates artificial status and urgency

explanation.short: Executive recruiters are paid by the hiring company — candidates never pay engagement deposits for any legitimate search.
tells: Legitimate executive recruiters earn placement fees from employers, never candidates
Withholding the company name until payment is received prevents you from verifying the search
Refundable deposit promises from strangers online are not enforceable
Real executive searches require extensive interviews, not deposit payments
explanation.safe_move: Decline. Real executive recruiters do not charge candidates. If genuinely interested, research the firm independently.
explanation.consequence: You pay $150. The recruiter goes silent. The company and opportunity do not exist.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Recruiters are paid by employers, never by candidates — any deposit request is a scam.

framing: You get this text while using your bank's mobile app.

message.body: TrustBank Alert: Your account has been suspended due to unusual activity. Verify your identity at trustbank-secure-verify.com within 2 hours to restore access.

red_flags.label: trustbank-secure-verify.com is not the real bank website
Real bank alerts use 5-6 digit short codes, not full phone numbers
2-hour deadline to verify or lose account access is pressure tactic
Banks do not ask you to click SMS links to verify your identity

explanation.short: Banks use short codes for alerts and their own secure app — they never text you a link to an external site to verify your identity.
tells: Bank text alerts come from 5-6 digit short codes, not full phone numbers
Your bank app is the proper place to address any account issues
Lookalike bank domains are designed to steal login credentials
Two-hour deadlines are used to prevent you from calling the real bank first
explanation.safe_move: Open your bank app directly or call the number on the back of your card. Do not click the link.
explanation.consequence: The link leads to a fake bank login page. Your credentials are captured and account is accessed.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never click SMS links claiming to be from your bank — open the app or call the card number directly.

framing: You get this email about unusual activity on a streaming account.

message.subject: Unusual Sign-In Detected — Verify Your Account Now
message.body:
We detected a sign-in to your StreamNow account from an unrecognized device in another country. To protect your account, verify your identity by clicking below within 24 hours.

[Verify My Account]

If you do not verify, your account will be locked and your subscription canceled.

StreamNow Security Team

red_flags.label: streamnow-account-alert.com is not the real service domain
Threatens subscription cancellation to add financial loss to the fear
Foreign login alert is a common hook to create alarm and urgency
Verification links in emails lead to phishing pages, not real service sites

explanation.short: Streaming services have in-app security tools — they do not send email links to external sites to verify your identity.
tells: Real streaming security alerts direct you to log into the actual service
The domain has extra words added — the real service domain would be much simpler
Subscription cancellation threat adds financial urgency beyond just account access
Log into the service directly to check for any real alerts or active sessions
explanation.safe_move: Log into your streaming account directly using your bookmark or by typing the URL. Check security settings there.
explanation.consequence: The link leads to a fake login page. Your email and password are captured and used to access your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: For any account security issue, always navigate directly to the service — never via a link in an email.

framing: You just logged into your email from a new laptop and get this.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate security notification — it matches your recent activity, requires no action, and directs you to the real service domain.
tells: Sender domain matches the service (mailvault.com)
Notification matches your actual recent activity — you just logged in
No action required if it was you — no urgency or threat
Directs you to the real website, not an external link
explanation.safe_move: No action needed if you recognize the login. If you did not recognize it, go to the service directly using your bookmark.
explanation.consequence: No scam here — this is a model security notification.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate security notifications require no action if you recognize the activity, and never link to external sites.

framing: You get this text while you are at the grocery store.

message.body: Your CoinVault account has been flagged for suspicious withdrawal. Your funds have been frozen. Call 1-888-555-0155 immediately to unlock your account before funds are seized.

red_flags.label: Threatens seizure of funds to create maximum panic
Provides a phone number to call — you will be talking to the scammer
Legitimate financial service alerts use short codes, not full phone numbers
Frozen funds narrative creates urgent pressure to act immediately

explanation.short: Financial platforms do not text from full phone numbers about fund seizures — the callback number connects you to the scammer, not the real company.
tells: Legitimate financial alerts come from verified short codes or the official app
Calling the provided number connects you to the scammer who will ask for account credentials or remote access
Fund seizure threats are designed to create panic that overrides skepticism
Log into the service directly or call the number on your debit card to check your real account status
explanation.safe_move: Do not call the number. Log into your account directly or call the customer service number from the official website.
explanation.consequence: You call the number and speak with the scammer who guides you to share your account credentials or 2FA codes.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never call a number from a text about a frozen or flagged account — use the official contact info from your account.

framing: You receive this email about your email account storage.

red_flags.label: mailvault-account-notice.com is not the real service domain
Threatens account deactivation and data loss

Claiming incoming messages are blocked creates urgency without verification

explanation.short: Storage notifications come from the real service domain and direct you to log in — not to click links in emails from lookalike domains.
tells: Real service notifications use the same domain as the service itself
If you are actually near storage limits, you will see warnings when logged into the service
Account deactivation for storage issues has a longer grace period than 24 hours — this urgency is fabricated
Click the real service bookmark to check your actual storage status
explanation.safe_move: Log into your email account directly to check actual storage usage. Ignore this email.
explanation.consequence: The storage management link captures your email login credentials.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Check storage or account issues by logging in directly — never via email links.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate password reset email — you requested it, it comes from the real domain, and correctly says to ignore it if you did not request it.
tells: You actually requested this reset — solicited emails are expected
Domain matches the real service (mailvault.com)
Correctly advises to ignore if you did not request it — not to click anyway
1-hour expiry is reasonable and standard for password resets
explanation.safe_move: Click the reset link to complete your password change.
explanation.consequence: No scam here — this is what a legitimate password reset looks like.

framing: You get this email about an antivirus subscription.

Calling the provided number connects you to a tech support scam operation

framing: You receive this email about a streaming service charge.

tells: Real payment failure notices come from the service's actual domain
Update billing links in emails lead to phishing pages — go to the service directly
Log into the streaming service directly to check your actual payment status
48-hour suspension threats create urgency that overrides careful domain checking
explanation.safe_move: Log into the streaming service directly using your bookmark. Check payment settings there.

framing: You receive this email from a cloud storage service.

tells: Domain matches the service (clouddrive.com)
7 days notice is ample time to verify or cancel
No urgent action required — charge is automatic as expected
Directs you to the real website path to manage your subscription

explanation.consequence: No scam — a model subscription renewal notice.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate renewal emails give advance notice, use the real domain, and don't require urgent action.

framing: You get this text while watching TV.

tells: Check your bank app — if no charge appears, the text is fraudulent
Real streaming services use short codes and direct you to the app or website
Calling the number connects you to a scammer who will ask for card details to process a refund
Inflated amounts ($99.99 vs typical $15) are designed to alarm you into calling immediately
explanation.safe_move: Check your actual bank statement. If no charge appears, delete the text. Never call numbers from unsolicited texts.

explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always check your actual bank statement before calling any number from a text about a charge.

framing: You receive this email about a VPN subscription you do not remember signing up for.

Check your real card statement — if no $189 charge appears, this email is entirely fake

Annual renewal amount ($35.88) is consistent with typical password manager pricing
No urgent action required — auto-charge is normal for annual plans
Directs you to a specific account page on the real domain, not an external link
explanation.safe_move: No action needed. If you want to cancel, go to keysafe.io/account/billing using your bookmark.
explanation.consequence: No scam — a legitimate renewal notice for an expected annual charge.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate renewal emails match the expected service, amount, and direct you to the real domain.

framing: You parked your car and found a flyer with this QR code information, then received this text.

message.body: ParkEasy: Your parking session at Lot C begins now. To avoid a $75 fine, complete payment by scanning the QR code on the meter or at parkeasypay-lot-c.com. Session expires in 30 minutes.

red_flags.label: $75 fine threat creates panic to pay quickly without verifying
parkeasypay-lot-c.com is not a legitimate parking payment site
QR codes on meters may be stickers placed by scammers over real ones

explanation.short: Scammers place fake QR code stickers over real parking meter codes — always verify the URL before entering payment details.
tells: QR stickers on parking meters are a known physical scam — check if the code is a sticker over an original
The URL the QR leads to should match the real city or parking service — always check before paying
A fine threat creates panic that overrides careful URL inspection
Real city parking meters have consistent URL patterns you can verify
explanation.safe_move: Before entering card details, verify the URL matches the official city or parking service website. Look for tampering on the meter.
explanation.consequence: Your card details are captured on the fake payment page. The real parking authority has no record of your payment.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify the URL a QR code leads to before entering any payment information.

framing: You scan a QR code at a restaurant table and receive this on your phone.

message.body:
[Website prompt]

Welcome to Bistro 44! To view our menu, please verify you are a real customer by entering your email and creating a quick PIN. This helps us prevent bots from accessing our ordering system.

[Email field] [Create PIN]

URL shown: bistro44-menu-verify.com/access

red_flags.label: A restaurant menu should not require your email or a PIN to view
bistro44-menu-verify.com is not the restaurant's real website
Bot verification pretext is used to justify collecting credentials
Restaurant QR codes should go directly to a menu, not a login page

explanation.short: Restaurant QR codes lead directly to menus — any QR that asks for credentials first should be treated as a phishing attempt.
tells: Real restaurant QR codes open menus immediately — no login or verification needed
The domain should match the restaurant's real website — check before entering anything
Email + PIN collection enables account attacks using your credentials elsewhere
A scammer's QR sticker may have been placed over the real restaurant's code
explanation.safe_move: Do not enter any information. Ask the restaurant for the real menu link or a paper menu.
explanation.consequence: Your email and PIN are collected for credential stuffing attacks on other accounts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Restaurant QR menus never require login — any QR asking for credentials is a phishing attempt.

framing: You receive this email with a QR code about a package delivery.

message.subject: Action Required: Scan QR to Confirm Delivery Address
message.body:
Your NovaMart order is ready to ship but requires address confirmation. Please scan the QR code below or visit the link to update your delivery address within 24 hours.

[QR CODE IMAGE]

URL: novamart-address-confirm.com/update

Order will be held after 24 hours.

NovaMart Delivery

red_flags.label: QR codes in emails are used to bypass link-scanning security tools
novamart-shipping-update.com and novamart-address-confirm.com are not real
Address confirmation is a pretext to collect personal and payment information
Order being held after 24 hours creates urgency

explanation.short: QR codes in emails are used specifically to evade email security filters — the domains they link to are almost always phishing sites.
tells: Legitimate delivery services direct you to your account on their real website, not QR codes in email
QR codes in emails hide the destination URL until you have already scanned them
Address confirmation is a pretext to collect your real home address and payment details
Check your real account on the retailer's website to see actual order status
explanation.safe_move: Log into your account directly at the real retailer's website to check your order. Do not scan the QR code.
explanation.consequence: The QR code leads to a phishing site collecting your address, card details, or login credentials.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never scan QR codes in emails about orders — check your account directly on the real website.

framing: You receive a social media message with a QR code asking for charity donations.

message.body:
We are collecting emergency relief donations for flood victims. Scan the QR below to donate. Every dollar helps displaced families get food and shelter. Donations are tax deductible.

[QR CODE]

Donate at: relief-aid-flood-fund.com

red_flags.label: Relief Aid Network cannot be verified as a registered nonprofit
QR codes in social DMs for donations are a high-risk pattern
Claiming tax deductibility for an unregistered organization is false
Flood victim urgency exploits compassion to bypass verification

explanation.short: Disaster charity QR codes on social media are a common scam — always verify an organization through charity registries before donating.
tells: Only verified nonprofit organizations can offer tax deductibility — unregistered groups cannot
Verify any charity at Charity Navigator or GuideStar before donating
QR codes bypass URL inspection — the destination is unknown until scanned
Disaster relief scams appear within hours of news events
explanation.safe_move: Verify the organization at Charity Navigator. If verified, donate through their official website directly.
explanation.consequence: Your donation goes to the scammer. No disaster victims receive any aid.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify charity registrations before donating, especially after disaster events.

framing: You get a text with a QR code about verifying a crypto wallet.

message.body:
CoinVault: Security alert. Your wallet requires re-verification due to a system migration. Scan the QR below or visit coinvault-secure-migration.com to complete verification within 48 hours or your funds will be locked.

[QR CODE]

red_flags.label: Crypto wallets do not require re-verification via SMS QR codes
coinvault-secure-migration.com is not the real platform domain
Funds locked threat creates maximum urgency for crypto holders
System migration pretext is used to make verification seem necessary

explanation.short: Crypto platforms do not migrate wallets via SMS QR codes — this is designed to steal your seed phrase or credentials.
tells: Real platform migrations are announced within the app with no action required from users
QR codes from text messages for crypto verification always lead to phishing sites
The site collects your seed phrase or login credentials to drain your wallet
Log into the real platform through your bookmarked URL to check for any real alerts
explanation.safe_move: Log into the crypto platform directly through your bookmark. If no migration alert exists there, this text is fraudulent.
explanation.consequence: The QR leads to a site that collects your login credentials or seed phrase. Your wallet is drained.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never verify a crypto wallet via a QR code from a text — check the real platform directly.

framing: You get this text from a government-looking number.

message.body: NOTICE: Your vehicle registration has expired. Pay the $42 renewal fee at dmv-online-renew.com to avoid a $200 fine. Act before Friday.

red_flags.label: dmv-online-renew.com is not an official state government domain
DMV departments send paper mail, not unsolicited texts
$200 fine threat creates urgency to pay without verifying
Friday deadline prevents you from calling the real DMV to verify

explanation.short: DMV agencies communicate by postal mail — any text about vehicle registration is a phishing attempt using a lookalike domain.
tells: Official government sites for your state use .gov domains
DMV notices come by postal mail, not SMS
Check your real registration status on your state's official .gov DMV website
The $200 fine threat creates panic that overrides careful domain inspection
explanation.safe_move: Check your registration status on your state's official .gov DMV website. Ignore this text.
explanation.consequence: Your card details are captured on the fake payment site. Your registration remains expired.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government agencies use .gov domains — any other domain claiming to be government is a scam.

framing: You get this email about unclaimed property.

message.subject: You Have Unclaimed Funds — Claim Before Expiry
message.body:
Our records show you have $1,847 in unclaimed property registered under your name. Claim your funds at state-treasury-claim.com/verify before the 90-day expiry date.

Provide your full name, date of birth, and Federal Benefits Number to verify your identity and initiate transfer.

State Treasury Office

red_flags.label: Requests Federal Benefits Number by email to claim funds
state-treasury-claims.com and state-treasury-claim.com are not .gov domains
Real unclaimed property programs do not expire — this is false pressure
Requests name, DOB, and SSN — complete identity theft package

explanation.short: Real unclaimed property programs are free to search and never ask for your SSN by email — they use secure portals on .gov domains.
tells: Official unclaimed property programs use .gov domains
You can search unclaimed property yourself at your state's official website for free
SSN, name, and DOB submitted by email enables full identity theft
Unclaimed property does not expire — the 90-day expiry is fabricated pressure
explanation.safe_move: Search for unclaimed property yourself at your state's official .gov unclaimed property website. Never provide SSN by email.
explanation.consequence: Your SSN, DOB, and name are used for identity theft — new accounts, tax fraud, and financial crime.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Search for unclaimed property yourself on .gov sites — never provide your SSN in response to an email.

framing: You signed up for text alerts from your state unemployment office and get this.

message.body: StateBenefits: Your biweekly certification is due by Sunday. Log into your account at benefits.state.gov to complete it. Reply STOP to opt out.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate government text — it comes from a short code, links to a .gov domain, and only asks you to log into your real account.
tells: Sent from a short code (77311), not a full phone number
Links to a .gov domain — official government websites
You signed up for these alerts — this is expected and solicited
No urgency threat, no fees, no requests for personal information
explanation.safe_move: Log into your state benefits account at the official .gov website to complete your certification.
explanation.consequence: No scam — a legitimate government benefit reminder.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate government texts use short codes and only link to .gov domains.

framing: You get this email claiming to be about your stimulus payment.

message.subject: Your Economic Relief Payment Is Ready
message.body:
Dear Citizen,

Your federal economic relief payment of $1,400 has been approved and is ready for disbursement. To receive your payment, verify your banking information at federal-payment-verify.com within 5 business days.

Federal Relief Payment Office

red_flags.label: Requests banking information to disburse a government payment
federal-relief-payment.com and federal-payment-verify.com are not .gov domains
You did not apply for or expect this specific payment
Bank verification is a pretext to capture account details

explanation.short: Government payments are issued automatically through the tax agency using tax records — no agency emails you to verify banking details.
tells: Government payments use banking information already on file with the tax agency
Official government communication uses .gov domains
Providing banking details to an unofficial site enables direct ACH withdrawal
If you are owed a real payment, it will arrive without any action required
explanation.safe_move: Check the status of any real government payments at irs.gov or through your official tax account.
explanation.consequence: Your banking details are used to initiate fraudulent ACH withdrawals from your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government payments are issued automatically — no agency emails you to verify banking details.

framing: You get this text claiming your driver's license is about to be suspended.

message.body: FINAL NOTICE: Your driver's license will be suspended in 24 hours due to an unpaid traffic violation. Pay $89 at dmv-violation-pay.com to prevent suspension. Case: MV-2024-77812.

red_flags.label: License suspension notices come by postal mail, not unsolicited texts
dmv-violation-pay.com is not an official government domain
24-hour suspension threat is fabricated to create immediate panic
Government agencies do not send suspension notices from personal phone numbers

explanation.short: Driver's license suspension notices come by certified mail — any text claiming 24-hour suspension is a phishing attempt.
tells: DMV suspension notices are sent by certified postal mail with appeal rights
Official DMV websites are state .gov domains — not .com domains with DMV in the name
A 24-hour deadline on a legal action is legally implausible
Verify any real traffic violations through your state's official DMV website directly
explanation.safe_move: Check your license status on your state's official .gov DMV website. Ignore this text.
explanation.consequence: Card details entered on the fake payment site are captured. No real violation is resolved.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: License suspension notices come by mail — texts about 24-hour suspensions are always scams.

framing: You get this text after shopping at a mall last weekend.

message.body: Congratulations! Your receipt from NovaMart entered you in our Spring Giveaway. You won a $500 gift card! Claim at novamart-spring-winners.com before Sunday.

red_flags.label: novamart-spring-winners.com is not the real store website
Real brand prize notifications use official channels, not random phone numbers
Sunday deadline creates pressure to claim before verifying the site
The claim site collects card details to receive your prize

explanation.short: Real brand giveaways are announced on official websites and social channels — not via text from unknown numbers linking to lookalike domains.
tells: Check whether the URL matches the real brand's actual domain
Real giveaway notifications come through the brand's official app, website, or receipt — not random texts
Claim sites for prizes always ask for payment details to deliver your reward
Search the brand's actual website to see if this promotion is listed there
explanation.safe_move: Check the brand's real website to see if this promotion exists. If it's not listed there, it's a scam.
explanation.consequence: The claim site captures your card details to cover a small delivery fee, then charges larger amounts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verify any prize promotion on the brand's real website before entering any information.

framing: You receive this email about winning a cash prize.

message.subject: You Have Won $10,000 — Claim Your Prize
message.body:
Dear Winner,

Your email was randomly selected from 2.4 million entries in our Global Generosity Sweepstakes. You have won $10,000 USD.

To claim, provide your full name, home address, and phone number and pay a $195 release fee at winners-claim.net/verify.

Claim within 72 hours.

Winners Circle Foundation

red_flags.label: Requires a fee to release cash prize winnings
winners-circle-foundation.net and winners-claim.net are not legitimate organizations
Collecting home address, phone, and fee enables both theft and physical targeting

explanation.short: You cannot win a sweepstakes you never entered, and real prizes never require release fees.
tells: The $195 release fee is the entire scam — no $10,000 exists
Collecting home address enables follow-up mail scams and physical targeting
Legitimate sweepstakes have verifiable rules, sponsors, and contact information
72-hour deadlines prevent you from researching whether the organization is real
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and delete. You have not won anything.
explanation.consequence: You pay $195 and provide your home address. Further fees follow. No prize is ever received.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any prize that requires an upfront fee is a scam — real prizes never cost you money to receive.

framing: You get a text saying you won a gift card from a survey.

message.body: You completed a survey last month and won a $250 NovaMart gift card! Visit survey-rewards-hub.com/gift and enter code GIFT250 to claim. Expires in 48 hours.

red_flags.label: survey-rewards-hub.com is not a real survey company domain
Vague reference to a survey you may or may not have completed
Claim code creates the illusion of a real prize waiting for you

explanation.short: Survey reward links in texts almost always lead to phishing sites that harvest card details under the pretense of a small delivery fee.
tells: Real survey rewards are fulfilled through the survey platform you used — not random texts
The vague survey reference covers the fact that you may never have completed one
The claim code creates a false sense that a prize is already assigned to you
Claim sites typically ask for card details to cover a small S&H fee to deliver the gift card
explanation.safe_move: If you want to verify, check the actual survey platform's website you used — not this link.
explanation.consequence: Your card details are captured to cover a small delivery fee, then used for larger unauthorized charges.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Survey reward texts are almost always phishing attempts — check the real survey platform instead.

framing: You entered a raffle at a local community event last weekend.

message.subject: Raffle Winner — Spring Fair 2024
message.body:
Congratulations! Your raffle ticket #4412 was drawn as a winner at Saturday's Spring Fair. You've won a $50 restaurant gift card.

Please stop by the community center office at 88 Park Ave between 9am-5pm this week to pick up your prize. Bring this email as confirmation.

Westside Community Center Events Team
(555) 204-3310

explanation.short: This is a legitimate prize notification — you actually entered this raffle, it comes from a community org domain, and requires you to pick up the prize in person.
tells: You entered this raffle in person at a real event
In-person pickup means no card details or fees required
Phone number provided for verification
No urgency, no fees, no links to enter information
explanation.safe_move: Visit the community center during business hours with this email to pick up your prize.
explanation.consequence: No scam — this is what a legitimate small raffle prize notification looks like.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate local prize notices don't require card details — you pick up prizes in person.

framing: You get this email out of the blue.

message.subject: Second Notice: Unclaimed Prize — $50,000
message.body:
This is your second and final notice regarding your unclaimed international lottery prize of $50,000 USD. Your ticket number 7-7-2-4-9 was drawn in our quarterly global lottery.

To claim, contact our claims agent at +44 7911 123456 or claims@intl-claims-office.com. A processing deposit of $450 is required to release your winnings.

International Lottery Commission

red_flags.label: You never entered any international lottery
$450 processing deposit to receive $50,000 is a classic advance fee pattern
Second and final notice is fabricated — no first notice was ever sent
International phone number from a lottery you never entered

explanation.short: International lottery scams always require a fee to release the prize — the fee is the scam, and the prize does not exist.
tells: You cannot win a lottery you never entered
Processing deposits for lottery winnings are always fraudulent
Second notices for prizes you never knew about are fabricated urgency
Real lotteries deduct taxes from winnings — they do not charge upfront fees
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and delete. You have not won anything.
explanation.consequence: You pay the $450 and are asked for additional fees. No prize is ever received.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any lottery prize requiring upfront payment is 100% a scam — real prizes do not cost money to receive.

framing: You get this text on a Saturday morning.

message.body: FINAL NOTICE: Your $1,200 reward from the National Consumer Survey is expiring today. Visit consumer-reward-redeem.com/final to claim. This is your last chance.

red_flags.label: Today expiration creates maximum urgency before you can think clearly
Vague reference to a national consumer survey you cannot verify
consumer-reward-redeem.com is not a legitimate survey company
Final notice framing implies prior notices you never received

explanation.short: Final notice texts for rewards expiring today are designed to prevent any research or verification before you click and enter your details.
tells: Same-day expiration leaves no time to verify the survey company or URL
Final notice implies history that does not exist — manufactured pressure
The reward claim site will ask for card details to cover a delivery fee
Real survey rewards come through the platform used to complete the survey, not random texts
explanation.safe_move: Ignore. You have not won a reward.
explanation.consequence: The claim site captures card details. Your card is used for unauthorized charges.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Urgency is the enemy of good judgment — same-day prize expiration texts are always scams.

framing: Two emails about your account. Which one is the scam?

messages.label: A
messages.subject: Suspicious login detected — verify now
messages.body:
We detected a login from an unknown device. Click below immediately to verify your identity or your account will be locked.

[Verify Account Now]

MailVault Security Team

messages.label: B
messages.subject: New sign-in to your account
messages.body:
A new sign-in was detected from Chicago, IL. If this was you, no action needed. If not, visit mailvault.com/security to review.

MailVault Security

red_flags.label: A: mailvault-login-alert.net is not the real service domain
A: Verify immediately or be locked — creates panic
B: Uses the real service domain (mailvault.com)
B: Says no action needed if it was you — no false urgency

explanation.short: A uses a lookalike domain and demands immediate action with a lock threat. B comes from the real domain and requires no action if you recognize the login.
tells: Lookalike domains with extra words are a primary phishing indicator
Legitimate security alerts say "no action needed" — phishing alerts create urgency
B directs you to the real service domain path, not an external link
explanation.safe_move: B is legitimate — no action needed if you recognize the login. Ignore A and delete it.
explanation.consequence: Clicking A leads to a fake login page that captures your credentials.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate security emails never demand immediate action under threat of lockout.

framing: Two texts about a bank transaction. Which one is the scam?

messages.label: A
messages.body: TrustBank: Did you authorize a $312 charge at FuelMart? Reply YES or NO to confirm. Reply STOP to opt out.

messages.label: B
messages.body: ALERT: Your TrustBank account has been suspended due to fraud. Click trustbank-secure-hold.com NOW to restore access before 6pm.

red_flags.label: B: Real bank fraud alerts come from short codes, not full phone numbers
B: trustbank-secure-hold.com is not the real bank website
B: Restore before 6pm creates panic pressure
A: Short code (22799) is how real bank fraud texts arrive

explanation.short: A is a legitimate fraud confirmation — short code sender, simple yes/no, no link. B is a scam — full phone number, lookalike domain, and a time-pressure threat.
tells: Bank fraud texts come from 5-6 digit short codes, never full phone numbers
Legitimate fraud alerts ask yes/no — they do not threaten suspension with a link
Lookalike bank domains are the defining feature of banking phishing
explanation.safe_move: Respond to A as appropriate. Ignore and delete B — call the number on your card if you have concerns.
explanation.consequence: Clicking B captures your bank login credentials on a phishing page.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real bank alerts use short codes and ask yes/no questions — scam alerts use full numbers and links.

framing: Two emails about job opportunities. Which one is the scam?

messages.label: A
messages.subject: Interview Invitation — Marketing Coordinator
messages.body:
Thanks for applying. We'd love to schedule a 30-minute phone screen. Please use the link to book a time.

Samantha L., Talent Acquisition

messages.label: B
messages.subject: Remote Position — $28/hr — Start Immediately
messages.body:
We reviewed your profile and have an immediate data entry role. No experience required. A $49 background check fee is required to proceed.

Global Staffing Partners

red_flags.label: B: Requires you to pay for a background check — employers pay this
B: Unrealistically high pay for zero experience remote work
A: Responds to an application you submitted
A: Only requests your time for an interview — no fees

explanation.short: B requires a fee that employers always pay, and offers unrealistic pay for zero experience. A is a standard recruiter follow-up to a real application.
tells: Candidates never pay for background checks — employers absorb this cost
High-pay zero-experience remote work does not exist at legitimate companies
A responds to an actual application and only asks for your time
explanation.safe_move: Proceed with A. Ignore and report B to the job site.
explanation.consequence: Paying B's fee results in no job and possible additional card charges.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any job that requires you to pay anything upfront is a scam.

framing: Two invoice emails arrive in your work inbox on the same day. Which is the scam?

messages.label: A
messages.subject: Invoice #PS-2244 — October Delivery
messages.body:
Please find Invoice #PS-2244 attached for your October supply order — $1,840. Payment due Nov 15 via check or ACH to our account on file.

Parkway Supply Accounts

messages.label: B
messages.subject: Updated Banking Details — Action Required
messages.body:
Please update your records — our banking details have changed effective immediately. All payments should now be wired to the new account below. Previous account closes this week.

Parkway Supply Finance

red_flags.label: B: Banking detail changes sent by email alone should always be verified by phone
B: parkway-supply-billing.net differs from the real vendor domain
B: Effective immediately prevents verification before updating records
A: Standard invoice with normal payment methods and matching domain

explanation.short: B is a business email compromise attempt — lookalike domain, banking redirect, immediate effect. A is a normal invoice from the real vendor domain.
tells: Banking changes emailed from a slightly different domain is a classic BEC pattern
Immediate effect prevents the call-to-verify that would expose the fraud
Call the vendor at a number from your own records to verify any banking change
explanation.safe_move: Pay A normally. Call Parkway Supply at a number you already have to verify whether B is legitimate before changing any payment details.
explanation.consequence: Updating to B's account redirects your next payment to the scammer. Wire transfers are irreversible.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verify all banking detail changes by phone call to a number from your own records.

framing: You are expecting a package. Two texts arrive. Which one is the scam?

messages.label: A
messages.body: Your package is out for delivery today. Track at ShipTrack.com/track or reply with your 10-digit tracking number for updates. Reply STOP to opt out.

messages.label: B
messages.body: NexShip: Package held — incomplete address. Pay $1.99 release fee at nexship-delivery-update.com/pay to receive today.

red_flags.label: B: Carriers never charge fees to release a held package
B: Real carrier alerts use short codes, not full phone numbers
B: nexship-delivery-update.com is not a real carrier site
A: Short code, no fee, standard tracking instructions

explanation.short: A is a standard delivery alert — short code, no fees, track on the real site. B charges a fee to release your package, which carriers never do.
tells: No carrier charges a fee to release a package for address issues — they contact the sender
Real carrier texts use short codes
The small fee captures your card for larger unauthorized charges
explanation.safe_move: Track via A as directed. Ignore B — if genuinely concerned about a package, track it on the carrier's real website.
explanation.consequence: Paying B's fee captures your card details and uses them for larger unauthorized charges.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Carriers never charge fees to release packages — any text asking for one is a scam.

framing: Two password reset emails. Which one is the scam?

messages.label: A
messages.subject: Password Reset — Act Within 30 Minutes
messages.body:
A reset was requested. Click below. This link expires in 30 minutes. If you did NOT request this, still click to secure your account.

[Reset Password]

messages.label: B
messages.subject: Password reset requested
messages.body:
We received a request to reset your password. Click the link to set a new one.

[Reset Password]

This link expires in 1 hour. If you did not request this, ignore this email — your password has not changed.

red_flags.label: A: Tells you to click even if you did not request the reset — dangerous
A: datavault-security-alerts.com is not a real service domain
B: Correctly tells you to ignore the email if you did not request it
B: Comes from the real service domain (mailvault.com)

explanation.short: A tells you to click even if you did not request the reset — the opposite of what a real service would say. B correctly says to ignore if you did not request it.
tells: Real password reset emails say ignore if you did not request it — scam emails say click anyway
A's lookalike domain and 30-minute urgency are additional indicators
B follows the expected pattern for legitimate password resets
explanation.safe_move: If you requested a reset, use B's link. Ignore A entirely.
explanation.consequence: Clicking A leads to a fake login page where your credentials are captured.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: If a password reset email tells you to click even if you didn't request it — that's always a scam.

framing: Two texts about government accounts. Which one is the scam?

messages.label: A
messages.body: StateBenefits: Your biweekly certification is due Sunday. Log into benefits.state.gov to complete it. Reply STOP to opt out.

messages.label: B
messages.body: ALERT: Your federal benefits account has been suspended. Call 1-888-555-0177 within 24 hours. Provide your member ID, date of birth, and last 4 SSN to restore access.

red_flags.label: B: Requests SSN digits, DOB, and member ID by text
B: Government alerts use short codes, not full phone numbers
B: Threatens account suspension to create panic
A: Short code and .gov domain — standard government text alert pattern

explanation.short: A is a legitimate government reminder — short code and .gov domain, no PII requested. B asks for SSN and personal details via text, which government agencies never do.
tells: Government agencies never request SSN or DOB via text message
Short codes are used by real government text programs
.gov is the only legitimate domain for US government services
explanation.safe_move: Respond to A by logging into the .gov site. Ignore B — if concerned about benefits, call the number on your benefits card.
explanation.consequence: Calling B connects you to a scammer who collects your personal details for identity theft.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government agencies never request SSN or personal details via text.

framing: Two charity fundraising emails. Which one is the scam?

messages.label: A
messages.subject: Help us care for 12 rescued dogs
messages.body:
Last week we rescued 12 dogs from a neglect case. They need vet care and we need your help. Your $25 covers one dog's first exam. Donate at brighthoperescue.org/give.

We are a 501(c)(3) org — EIN 47-1234567.

messages.label: B
messages.subject: 47 dogs need surgery NOW
messages.body:
We rescued 47 dogs and are overwhelmed. A donor matches all gifts dollar-for-dollar until midnight. Without your $30 today, we may have to surrender them.

Donate now — time is running out.

red_flags.label: B: No EIN, registration number, or verifiable nonprofit status provided
B: Midnight matching deadline is a fabricated urgency tactic
B: Threatening to surrender animals is emotional manipulation
A: Provides EIN — verifiable nonprofit registration
