OfficeSupply Direct Accounts Receivable

red_flags.label: B: officesupply-direct-invoices.com adds "invoices" — not the real vendor domain
B: Requests wire transfer — irreversible and preferred by scammers
B: $3,847 with no verifiable purchase order reference
A: Matching domain, standard payment terms, verifiable phone number

explanation.short: A is a normal recurring service invoice. B uses a lookalike vendor domain and requests wire transfer for a large unverified amount.
tells: Vendor domains with extra words like "invoices" appended are a BEC red flag
Wire transfers are preferred by scammers because they are irreversible
Verify all invoices against purchase orders before payment
explanation.safe_move: Pay A through normal accounts payable. Verify B by calling the real vendor using a number from your own records before paying.
explanation.consequence: Wiring B's payment sends funds directly to the scammer's account. Wire transfers cannot be recalled.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verify large wire transfer invoices by phone before payment.

framing: Two package tracking texts arrive. Which one is the scam?

messages.label: A
messages.body: ShipTrack: Your package is out for delivery. Estimated arrival: today by 8pm. Track: shiptrack.com/NSP-440192. Reply STOP to opt out.

messages.label: B
messages.body: NexShip: Your package is held due to incomplete address. Pay $2.49 at nexship-delivery-update.com/pay to release your delivery today.

red_flags.label: B: Shipping carriers never charge fees to release packages
B: Carrier delivery texts use short codes, not full phone numbers
B: nexship-delivery-update.com is not a real carrier website
A: Short code sender with a real tracking number and service domain

explanation.short: A is a standard delivery notification from a short code. B charges a fee to release a package — which no carrier ever does.
tells: No shipping carrier charges fees to release packages for address issues
Real carrier texts use short codes and their real domain
The small fee is a pretext to capture your card for larger charges
explanation.safe_move: Track your package via A's link. Ignore B.
explanation.consequence: Paying B's fee captures your card details for larger unauthorized charges.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: No carrier charges delivery release fees — any text doing so is a scam.

framing: Two people you matched with online send you messages. Which one is the scammer?

messages.label: A
messages.body: Hey! I saw you also like hiking — did you do the lakefront trail last weekend? I was there Saturday morning. Also, are you free for coffee sometime this week?

messages.label: B
messages.body: I feel such a strong connection with you already. I am stationed overseas and my savings are in a frozen military account. Could you help with my transport home — $680 ticket? I'll repay you immediately when I land.

red_flags.label: B: Military overseas persona is the most common romance scam archetype
B: Frozen military funds is a scripted setup for a loan request
B: Requests money before any in-person contact
A: References a real local activity and proposes in-person meeting

explanation.short: A wants to meet in person — normal. B asks for money before meeting and uses the military-overseas-frozen-funds script.
tells: Military overseas romance scams follow a predictable script
Any money request before in-person meeting is a romance scam indicator
Real romantic connections progress toward meeting — scam connections progress toward money
explanation.safe_move: Coffee with A is fine. Never send money to B — end contact.
explanation.consequence: Sending B $680 leads to more requests. The person never arrives.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never send money to an online romantic interest before meeting in person.

framing: Two tax-related emails arrive in the same week. Which one is the scam?

messages.label: A
messages.subject: Notice CP12 — Changes to your return
messages.body:
We made a change to your 2023 tax return. Review the details and respond if needed at irs.gov/account. If you agree with our change, no response is needed.

Internal Revenue Service

messages.label: B
messages.subject: $847 refund expiring — provide banking details
messages.body:
Your $847 refund from 2023 expires in 72 hours. Provide your routing and account number at federal-payment-verify.com to receive your funds.

Federal Tax Refund Office

red_flags.label: B: fedtax-refund-portal.com is not a .gov domain — all real Tax Agency communications use .gov
B: Tax Agency never requests banking details by email
B: Tax refunds do not expire — fabricated urgency
A: Comes from irs.gov, no action needed if you agree, directs to real account portal

explanation.short: A is from irs.gov and requires no action if you agree. B uses a non-.gov domain and requests banking details for a refund that does not expire.
tells: All Tax Agency communication uses .gov domains only
The tax agency never requests banking details by email — refunds use information already on file
Tax refunds do not expire with a 72-hour deadline
explanation.safe_move: Verify A at irs.gov/account. Ignore B completely.
explanation.consequence: Providing banking details to B enables direct ACH withdrawal from your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Tax Agency communications only come from .gov domains — anything else is a scam.

framing: Two messages about investment opportunities arrive. Which one is the scam?

messages.label: A
messages.body: Congratulations! You've been selected for our user appreciation giveaway — 0.05 BTC. To receive it, send 0.001 BTC to our verification address to confirm your wallet is active. Full prize sent immediately after.

messages.label: B
messages.body: I've been doing really well with CoinHarvest Pro — my uncle in Hong Kong taught me the strategy. I started with $500 and made $3,200 in three weeks. Want me to walk you through it?

red_flags.label: A: Send crypto to receive more crypto — 100% always a scam
A: Account name mimics a brand but is unverified
B: Romantic/friendly contact introducing investment opportunity is pig butchering
B: CoinHarvest Pro cannot be verified as a legitimate regulated exchange

explanation.short: A is an obvious crypto giveaway scam — send to receive more is 100% fraudulent. B is subtler but is the setup for a pig butchering investment scam.
tells: Crypto giveaways requiring verification sends are universally fraudulent
B's friendly investment introduction is the pig butchering entry point — you will be guided to deposit on a fake platform
Both are scams, but A is more immediately obvious — B operates over weeks or months before the extraction
explanation.safe_move: Ignore both. Never send crypto to receive more. Do not invest on platforms introduced through social contacts.
explanation.consequence: A drains whatever you send immediately. B will eventually ask for a withdrawal fee on fictitious profits.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any crypto giveaway requiring you to send first is a scam. Investment opportunities from online contacts are almost always fraud.

framing: Two texts arrive about your financial account. Which one is the scam?

messages.label: A
messages.body: TrustBank: $45.00 purchase at GrocerMart approved. Balance: $1,247.83. Reply STOP to opt out.

messages.label: B
messages.body: Your TrustBank account shows unauthorized access. Click trustbank-verify-identity.com NOW to verify or your account will be frozen within 1 hour.

red_flags.label: B: Banks use short codes for alerts — full phone numbers are a red flag
B: trustbank-verify-identity.com is not the real bank website
B: 1-hour freeze threat creates maximum urgency
A: Short code sender with routine transaction confirmation — standard bank alert

explanation.short: A is a routine transaction confirmation from a bank short code. B uses a full phone number, lookalike domain, and 1-hour freeze threat — all scam indicators.
tells: Bank alerts always come from 5-6 digit short codes
One-hour freeze threats are used to prevent you from calling the real bank
Lookalike bank domains are the most targeted phishing category
explanation.safe_move: Note A as normal. Ignore B — if concerned, call the number on the back of your bank card.
explanation.consequence: Clicking B leads to a fake bank login page. Your credentials are captured and account accessed.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Bank fraud alerts use short codes and ask yes/no questions — links with urgency are always scams.

framing: You donated to two charities last month. Which donation receipt is from a scam organization?

messages.label: A
messages.subject: Tax receipt for your donation — #BHR-4412
messages.body:
Thank you for your $50 donation on October 22. This serves as your official tax receipt.

Bright Hope Animal Rescue
501(c)(3) EIN: 47-1234567
Donation #BHR-4412

messages.label: B
messages.subject: Thank you for helping Marcus
messages.body:
Your donation is making a difference for Marcus. Thank you for your generosity. Our work continues — please consider donating again to help children like Marcus.

Bright Hope Children's Fund

red_flags.label: B: Receipt has no EIN — required for tax deductibility claims
B: Receipt immediately asks for another donation
B: Name mimics A's legitimate charity but is a different organization
A: Provides EIN, receipt number, and date — all required for legitimate tax receipts

explanation.short: A is a proper donation receipt with a verifiable EIN. B has no EIN, no receipt number, and immediately solicits another donation.
tells: Legitimate donation receipts include EIN, receipt number, and donation date for tax purposes
Missing EIN means the organization may not be a registered nonprofit
Immediately soliciting another donation in a receipt is a pressure tactic used by fraud operations
Both organizations have similar names — scammers copy legitimate charity names to borrow credibility
explanation.safe_move: Keep A's receipt for taxes. Verify B at Charity Navigator before any further donations.
explanation.consequence: B is collecting donations but not registered as a nonprofit — your donation may not be tax deductible and funds may not go to any charitable purpose.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Donation receipts must include an EIN — no EIN means no verified nonprofit status.

framing: Two emails about government money arrive. Which one is legitimate?

messages.label: A
messages.subject: You've been selected — $8,500 Federal Relief Grant
messages.body:
You've been selected for an $8,500 federal relief grant based on your tax profile. A $99 processing fee is required to claim within 48 hours.

federal-grant-process.com/fee

messages.label: B
messages.subject: Your 2023 refund has been processed
messages.body:
Your 2023 federal tax refund of $312 was processed and will arrive by direct deposit within 5 business days to the account on file.

Tax Agency.gov

red_flags.label: A: Government grants never require upfront processing fees
A: federal-relief-grants.com is not a .gov domain
A: Government grants require applications — unsolicited selections do not exist
B: Tax Agency .gov domain, refund processed automatically to account on file — no action needed

explanation.short: A requires a fee and uses a non-.gov domain — neither is possible for a legitimate government grant. B is a routine Tax Agency refund notification from irs.gov.
tells: Government grants are always free to receive — fees are the scam
Official government sites use .gov exclusively
Refunds from the tax agency are processed automatically to accounts on file — no action needed
explanation.safe_move: Expect B's refund within 5 days. Ignore A entirely.
explanation.consequence: Paying A's $99 fee results in more fee requests. No grant is received.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government grants never have processing fees — .gov domain is the only legitimate source.

framing: Two recruiters reach out about your resume. Which one is the scam?

messages.label: A
messages.subject: Opportunity match — Project Manager role
messages.body:
Hi! I came across your profile and think you'd be a strong fit for a Project Manager position with one of our clients. Would you have 15 minutes for a quick call this week?

Sarah M., Skyline Staffing

messages.label: B
messages.subject: Immediate Remote Role — $28/hr — Apply Today
messages.body:
We reviewed your resume and have a remote data entry role at $28/hr. No experience needed. A $49 background check fee is required to proceed.

Apply at bgcheck-portal.com/apply

red_flags.label: B: Candidates never pay for background checks — employers do
B: Application goes to bgcheck-portal.com — unrelated to the staffing company domain
B: $28/hr for zero-experience remote work is unrealistically high
A: Only asks for 15 minutes of your time — no fees or documents

explanation.short: A is a standard recruiter outreach asking only for your time. B requires a fee and sends you to a separate domain — both are scam indicators.
tells: No legitimate recruiter ever charges candidates to proceed
Separate application domains from the recruiting company are a red flag
Unrealistically high pay for no experience is a lure, not a real opportunity
explanation.safe_move: Take A's call if you are interested. Ignore and report B.
explanation.consequence: Paying B's $49 fee results in no job. Your card may be used for additional charges.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate recruiters ask for your time, not your money.

framing: Two texts about winning something. Which one is the scam?

messages.label: A
messages.body: NovaMart: Your in-store raffle ticket #NM-4412 was selected. Visit any NovaMart location to claim your $25 gift card. No expiration.

messages.label: B
messages.body: You've won a $1,000 NovaMart gift card! Claim at novamart-prizes.com/shipping — a $15 S&H fee applies. Prize forfeited after 48 hours.

red_flags.label: B: Shipping fee to receive a prize is an advance fee scam
B: novamart-prizes.com is not the real store website
B: 48-hour forfeiture deadline is fabricated urgency
A: In-store pickup of a verified raffle prize with no fee

explanation.short: A is a real raffle win with in-person pickup. B requires a shipping fee and uses a lookalike domain — the fee is the scam.
tells: Real prizes are claimed without fees
Short code sender vs full phone number is the sender pattern difference
Lookalike brand domains for prize claims are phishing sites
explanation.safe_move: Pick up A's prize in-store. Ignore B.
explanation.consequence: Paying B's $15 fee captures your card for larger unauthorized charges.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any prize that requires payment to receive is a scam.

framing: Two emails about software and device security. Which one is the scam?

messages.label: A
messages.subject: Scheduled scan complete — no threats found
messages.body:
Your weekly ShieldGuard security scan completed on November 7. No threats were found. Your device is protected.

No action needed. ShieldGuard

messages.label: B
messages.subject: Critical Malware Alert — Remote Removal Needed
messages.body: Malicious software was detected on your device accessing your NexCloud account. Call 1-855-555-0167 to allow our team to connect remotely and remove it. Do not restart your computer.

red_flags.label: B: Requests remote access to your computer — grants scammer full control
B: Cloud services cannot detect malware on your local device
B: Do not restart creates dependency and escalates fear
A: Scan result requiring no action — standard antivirus notification

explanation.short: A is a routine scan completion from real antivirus software — no action needed. B requests remote access using an impossible claim (cloud detecting local malware).
tells: Cloud services have no visibility into your local device files
Remote access grants the scammer complete control of your computer
Do not restart creates artificial urgency and dependency on the scammer's help
explanation.safe_move: Note A and do nothing further. Ignore B — run your real antivirus if concerned.
explanation.consequence: Calling B grants remote access. Scammer installs persistent malware, steals banking credentials, and may demand payment to remove the access.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Cloud services cannot see local malware — any such claim is a tech support scam.

framing: You apply for housing assistance and receive two responses. Which one is the scam?

messages.label: A
messages.subject: Pre-approved — Housing Assistance — Act in 48 Hours
messages.body: You have been pre-approved for our housing assistance program covering 80% of rent. Complete enrollment by paying a $75 application fee at affordable-homes-apply.com/fee within 48 hours.

messages.label: B
messages.subject: Your application is in queue — Reference #CH-4412
messages.body:
Thank you for your housing assistance application. Your application (Reference #CH-4412) is being reviewed. Processing takes 4-6 weeks. We will contact you by mail with updates.

City Housing Authority, (555) 301-5400

red_flags.label: A: Housing assistance programs never charge enrollment fees
A: affordable-homes-assistance.com is not a government .gov domain
A: Pre-approved for a program you never applied to
B: .gov domain, realistic timeline, communication by mail — standard government process

explanation.short: A charges a fee for free housing assistance from a non-.gov domain. B is a legitimate government housing authority with a realistic timeline and .gov domain.
tells: Government housing programs are always free to apply to — fees are a scam
.gov is the exclusive domain for US government agencies
Realistic 4-6 week timelines and mail follow-up are consistent with real housing programs
explanation.safe_move: Wait for B's process to proceed. Ignore A — contact your local housing authority using their official .gov contact if you need information.
explanation.consequence: Paying A's $75 fee results in no housing assistance. The scammer targets people in vulnerable situations.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government housing assistance is always free to apply to — any fee is a scam.

framing: You are selling electronics online. Two buyers offer to purchase. Which is the scammer?

messages.label: A
messages.body: Hi! I'm interested in the laptop. I'm local — could we meet at the library Saturday afternoon? I'll bring cash. Does $380 work for you?

messages.label: B
messages.body: I'll pay your full asking price. I use QuickSend Business Escrow for seller protection — you'll get a confirmation email. Ship after you receive it. I'll cover shipping.

red_flags.label: B: QuickSend Business Escrow is not a real service — the confirmation email will be fake
B: Ship after receiving an email — not after verifying funds in your real account
B: Immediate full price acceptance with no questions is a scammer pattern
A: Local, in person, cash — the safest transaction type

explanation.short: A wants to meet in person with cash — ideal. B uses a fake escrow service and wants you to ship based on a spoofed confirmation email.
tells: Payment app escrow is not a real feature — scammers create the term to sound legitimate
Always verify payment by logging into your own account, not by checking an email
Buyers who accept full price immediately and want to ship remotely are almost always scammers
explanation.safe_move: Meet A at the library. Decline B or require in-person transaction only.
explanation.consequence: You ship the laptop based on the fake escrow email. No payment ever arrives.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verify payment by logging into your account — never based on an email confirmation alone.

framing: You see two emails about subscription charges in your inbox. Which one is the scam?

messages.label: A
messages.subject: Your TaskFlow subscription renewed — $89.00
messages.body:
Your TaskFlow Business plan renewed today for $89.00. To manage your subscription, visit taskflow.io/billing.

TaskFlow Billing

messages.label: B
messages.subject: ShieldGuard renewed — $349.99 charged
messages.body: Your ShieldGuard Antivirus subscription renewed for $349.99. If you did not authorize this, call 1-888-555-0148 immediately for a full refund within 24 hours.

red_flags.label: B: shieldguard-renewal-center.com is not the real software domain
B: $349.99 is inflated to create alarm and urgency to call
B: Callback to cancel connects to a scammer doing a fake refund operation
A: Expected renewal amount from the real service domain — no action required

explanation.short: A is your expected monthly subscription renewal from the real domain. B is a fake renewal notification designed to make you call a scammer.
tells: Check your card statement — B's $349.99 charge likely does not appear
Real antivirus renewals come from the software's own domain
Calling B connects you to a tech support scam operation
explanation.safe_move: Manage A at taskflow.io/billing. Check your real card statement before calling anyone about B.
explanation.consequence: Calling B connects you to a scammer who requests remote access to process your refund.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Check your actual card statement before acting on any unexpected charge notification.

message.subject: Wire confirmation — reference WR-4419823
message.body:
Hello Maria,

This is confirmation of the outgoing wire you initiated today at 10:42 AM ET.

Amount: $38,250.00
Beneficiary: CLOSING ESCROW (last 4: 7712)
Reference: WR-4419823

If you did NOT initiate this wire, call the number on the back of your BluePeak debit card within 24 hours. Do not reply to this email; this mailbox is not monitored.

BluePeak Wire Operations

explanation.short: Legitimate wire confirmation — matches something you actually did, directs you to the back-of-card number, no link to click.
tells: Sender domain matches the bank exactly — not a lookalike
No login link or 'click here to cancel' — asks you to call the number on your own card
Large dollar amounts legitimately trigger 24-hour review windows at banks
References a specific reference number and escrow beneficiary — the kind of detail an attacker usually can't fake
explanation.safe_move: If you did initiate this wire, no action needed. If not, call the number printed on the back of your card — never a number from the email itself.
explanation.consequence: No risk from reading this email. If the wire was unauthorized, a fast callback can still claw it back within the bank's recovery window.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Large-dollar urgency from a bank is a red flag only when the sender domain, the callback channel, or both look off. A matching domain + back-of-card callback is the legit shape.

green_flags.label: No embedded phone number or link
Directs you to number on back of card
Includes reference number you can verify in online banking

message.body: NationPost: Delivery attempted for pkg 9412 711X 6899. No one home. Reschedule free in the app or at nationpost.com/reschedule. Reply STOP to opt out.

explanation.short: Real carrier reschedule message — from the carrier's real short code, with the carrier's actual domain and no urgency or fee.
tells: Sender is a 5-digit short code (47832), not a random 10-digit number
Domain is the carrier's real root domain (nationpost.com), not a hyphenated lookalike like nationpost-reschedule.com
No fee, no urgency timer, and no demand for personal info — just 'use the app or website'
Includes a STOP opt-out, which is a legal requirement for legit US business SMS
explanation.safe_move: If you're expecting a package, open the carrier's app (not the link) and reschedule there. That avoids link-sniffing entirely and verifies the notice independently.
explanation.consequence: No risk if you use the app. Clicking the link in this specific message is also safe because the domain matches, but the app-first habit makes you immune to lookalike scams.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Short-code sender + real root domain + STOP opt-out = legit carrier pattern. Train yourself to check those three.

message.subject: Your Helix password expires in 48 hours
message.body:
Hi Devon,

Your Helix network password expires on Friday at 5:00 PM.

Rotate it before then via single sign-on at sso.helix-corp.com/account. You'll be redirected through the standard Okta flow and prompted for MFA.

If you've rotated recently and think this is a mistake, reply to this email or ping #it-help in Slack.

— Helix IT

explanation.short: Legit internal IT notice — sender matches company domain, sends you through the real SSO flow with MFA, offers a human-verifiable channel (Slack, reply-to).
tells: From-address domain matches the company's domain exactly
Link goes to sso.helix-corp.com (the company's real SSO subdomain) — not a lookalike like helix-corp-sso.com
Mentions the real MFA flow rather than asking for the password in the email
Offers a known secondary channel (Slack #it-help) for verification
explanation.safe_move: Instead of clicking the link, open your browser and type the SSO URL directly, or launch it from your company bookmark. Both lead to the same place and remove any lookalike-domain risk.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring this legitimately will lock your account at password expiry. Reporting it as phishing wastes IT time but causes no damage.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legit password-expiry emails go through SSO and mention MFA. A scam version would ask you to enter your current password directly or send you to a lookalike domain.

message.body: Hey — following up on your application for the Senior Backend role at Ledgerly from March 14. The hiring manager would like a 30-min intro call this week. Here's my calendar: ledgerly.com/careers/priya-r. No take-home, no prep needed — just a chat. Let me know if you'd rather we find another time.

red_flags.label: Direct-messaged by someone you don't know personally

explanation.short: Legit recruiter follow-up — references a specific application you actually submitted, scheduling link uses the company's real domain, no payment or personal info asks.
tells: References a specific role and application date — the kind of detail only an internal recruiter has access to
Scheduling link is on the company's real careers subdomain, not a third-party form
No request for bank info, SSN, 'onboarding fee,' or gift cards — the usual job-scam tells are all absent
Tone is casual and no-pressure — 'just a chat' contradicts the urgency scammers rely on
explanation.safe_move: Verify by searching the recruiter on the company's careers page or LinkedIn before scheduling. If the match checks out, book the call through the link.
explanation.consequence: No risk. A legit intro call is how most hires start — declining out of caution could cost you a real opportunity.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real recruiters reference specific applications and use company domains. Fake ones invent generic roles, rush you, and ask for money or personal data upfront.

green_flags.label: Mentions specific role and date you applied
Scheduling link on company's real domain
No fee, bank info, or SSN request

message.subject: Your 2025 tax docs — secure upload link
message.body:
Hi Sam,

Time to get your 2025 returns rolling. I've set up your folder in our secure portal.

Upload your W-2s, 1099s, and the business mileage log here: portal.finchleycpa.com/u/sam-k-2025

The portal requires your email + a one-time code — same flow as last year. If you have questions, reply to this email or call the office at the number on my card.

Jordan

red_flags.label: Asks you to upload sensitive docs

explanation.short: Legitimate CPA message — ongoing relationship, real firm subdomain, references the exact auth flow used previously.
tells: Sender domain matches the CPA firm's real domain
Portal link uses the firm's real subdomain (portal.finchleycpa.com), not a hosted form on a third-party domain
Mentions 'same flow as last year' — establishes continuity with a prior legit interaction
Offers multiple verification paths (reply-to, phone on card)
explanation.safe_move: Confirm by calling the number on Jordan's business card (not a number in the email). Once verified, use the portal.
explanation.consequence: No risk if the portal is real (it is). A 'swap this invoice for a new bank account' email would be the scam version — this is the opposite.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legit CPAs use their own firm's portal with MFA. Scammers impersonating CPAs push you to generic file-sharing links or ask you to wire fees to a new account.

green_flags.label: Uses MFA same as prior year

message.body: MetroHealth: Reminder — Dr. Chen on Thu Mar 20, 2:15 PM. Reply C to confirm, R to reschedule, or tap metrohealth.org/mychart to message the office. STOP to opt out.

explanation.short: Legitimate appointment reminder — short code sender, real domain, multiple response paths (including non-link ones), no request for personal or payment info.
tells: Short-code sender (83919) with a recognizable hospital system name
Domain is metrohealth.org, the hospital's real root domain — not metrohealth-mychart.com or similar
Offers a keyword response (C/R) — you never need to click anything if you don't want to
No request for date of birth, SSN, or payment info in the text
explanation.safe_move: Reply C to confirm or open MyChart in your own bookmark. Avoid clicking the link even though it's legit — the habit matters more than this one message.
explanation.consequence: No risk. Missing the reminder might cost you a visit; clicking the link here is fine but sets a habit that could hurt you against a lookalike later.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Healthcare reminders use short codes, real root domains, and keyword replies. If a 'reminder' asks for DOB, SSN, or a copay payment link, that's the scam version.

message.subject: Your mortgage servicer is changing — action may not be required
message.body:
Dear Ms. Hart,

Effective May 1, the servicing of your BluePeak mortgage (loan ending 8841) will transfer to Rivermark Home Loans. Your loan terms, interest rate, and balance do not change.

Your monthly payment will stay the same. After May 1, please send payments to Rivermark (details in the attached transfer notice and the letter mailed to your home address on file).

A federal 60-day grace period protects you from late fees if you accidentally pay the old servicer during the transition.

Questions? Call the number on your most recent BluePeak statement or visit bluepeak.com/servicing-transfer.

BluePeak Loan Servicing

explanation.short: This looks like a classic 'change the payment destination' scam — but mortgage servicing transfers are a real, federally-regulated thing. It's legit.
tells: References your loan's last-4 and a specific effective date
Cites the federal 60-day grace period — a real RESPA provision scammers usually don't know about
Also mentions a physical letter mailed to your address — legit transfers must be sent by mail, not email alone
Directs verification to the phone number on your existing statement, not a new number in the email
explanation.safe_move: Verify in two ways: (1) call the number on your most recent paper statement, and (2) check that the physical letter arrives within ~15 days. If both match, the transfer is real.
explanation.consequence: Sending payments to the wrong place during a servicer transfer is protected by the 60-day grace — you won't be penalized. But falling for a scam that imitates this pattern sends your mortgage payment to a thief.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real servicer transfers: federal grace period mention, physical letter, last-4 of loan, verification via existing statement phone. Scam versions skip the letter and push urgency.

green_flags.label: Notes that a physical letter is also being mailed

message.body: Northbridge CU Fraud: Did you attempt $412.18 at 'SHELLNOR GAS #1119' at 3:12 AM? Reply YES or NO. No links. If you did not attempt, call us at the number on your card. STOP to opt out.

explanation.short: Legit card fraud alert — short code, reply-only (no links), and directs you to call the number on your card rather than a number in the text.
tells: Short-code sender and 'No links' literally in the body — attackers almost never write that
YES/NO keyword reply flow — the standard fraud-alert pattern from real card networks
Transaction is specific: merchant, amount, time — and unusual (3 AM)
Callback instruction is 'number on your card', not a number provided in the SMS
explanation.safe_move: If you did not make the charge, reply NO, then call the number on the back of your card. Don't Google the credit union's fraud line — scammers buy ads on common searches.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring a real fraud alert can let further charges through. Replying YES when you didn't make the charge greenlights the thief.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real fraud alerts: short code, YES/NO reply, no links, card-back callback. Fake versions push a 'call this number NOW' with a new phone number embedded in the text.

message.subject: Q2 assessment — due May 15
message.body:
Dear Cedarwood Homeowner (Unit 214-B),

Your Q2 assessment of $487.00 is due May 15.

Pay via the owner portal at portal.cedarwoodhoa.com (check or ACH, no card fees). Paper coupon also mailed separately.

Questions? The board's quarterly meeting is April 28 at 7 PM in the clubhouse, or email bookkeeper@cedarwoodhoa.com.

Thank you — Cedarwood HOA Management

red_flags.label: Specific dollar amount with due date

explanation.short: Legit HOA assessment — identifies your unit number, sender matches management company, portal on real subdomain, offers multiple non-link verification paths.
tells: References your unit number — detail only the management company has
Sender domain matches the HOA management company
Mentions a paper coupon mailed separately — legit HOAs almost always send both
Points to a scheduled board meeting as a verification venue
explanation.safe_move: If you haven't received the paper coupon yet, wait for it or call the HOA's existing phone number. The scheduled board meeting is another way to verify the amount.
explanation.consequence: No risk. Missing a legit HOA payment incurs late fees, not security loss.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legit HOA invoices include your unit number, mention paper backup, and point to real-world verification (meetings, on-file phone). Scams invent generic 'community fees'.

message.body: Hi — SkyBridge here. Your flight SB842 on Apr 19 has a schedule change: now departs 6:45 AM (was 8:10 AM). You're rebooked automatically. Open the SkyBridge app to confirm or switch to a later flight at no cost. DM replies aren't monitored — use the app or skybridgeair.com/manage.

explanation.short: Legit schedule-change DM — verified handle, sends you to the app/real domain, explicitly says DM replies aren't monitored (real brands gate support to official channels).
tells: References a specific flight number and a before/after time
Explicitly tells you DM replies aren't monitored — scammers want you to reply to them
Directs to the app or real root domain, not a shortened link or third-party site
No 'pay $75 change fee' or 'verify your payment method' — the usual scam shape is absent
explanation.safe_move: Open the SkyBridge app (not the link) and check for the change there. If the flight matches, the DM is real.
explanation.consequence: No risk if you use the app. Schedule changes are real and common; over-reacting by calling a number from the DM could expose you to a support-line scam.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real airline DMs about schedule changes push you to the app. Fake ones push you to call a number or pay a change fee via the DM itself.

green_flags.label: No change fee or payment ask

message.subject: yourshop.com renews in 14 days
message.body:
Hi,

yourshop.com will auto-renew on April 30 for $14.99/yr using the card ending 4419.

Manage or cancel at gatekeepdomains.com/account (log in, then Domains → Renewals). Reply to this email if the card on file has changed.

Gatekeep Domains

explanation.short: Legit domain renewal reminder — references your specific domain, card last-4, sends you to the real root domain with a normal account flow.
tells: Specific domain name that matches what you actually own
Last-4 of card on file matches what you have
No 'your domain has been suspended, pay within 24 hours' fake-urgency
Link goes to the root domain, not a lookalike or separate 'renewal' domain
explanation.safe_move: Log in directly via a bookmark (not the email link) and verify the renewal date and card. Adjust if needed.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring a real renewal past the due date lets the domain expire — costly if someone else grabs it.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legit renewal emails give you 2-4 weeks of notice and point to your account dashboard. Scam versions compress the timeline ('act in 24 hours') and ask you to pay via a new link.

message.body: GreenGrid: Outage credit of $18.42 applied to acct ****4822 from the Apr 3 storm. See details in your next bill or at greengridenergy.com/account. STOP to opt out.

explanation.short: Legit utility credit notification — short code, account last-4, references a specific documented event, doesn't ask for anything.
tells: Short-code sender
Account last-4 matches your actual utility account
References the Apr 3 storm — a verifiable real-world event
Asks for nothing — no 'click to confirm', no login prompt, no payment info
explanation.safe_move: Nothing to do. Verify against your next bill if you care. The credit just shows up.
explanation.consequence: No risk. Refund-bait scams usually ask you to click and enter banking info to 'claim' the refund. This one applies automatically.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real credits are applied automatically. If a 'refund' message asks you to click a link and enter bank info to receive money, that's the scam version.

message.subject: Your 2025 donation receipt — thank you
message.body:
Dear Tomás,

Thank you for your $150 donation on March 8, 2025. Clearpath Aid is a registered 501(c)(3) (EIN 82-1119422). This receipt is valid for tax purposes.

Receipt PDF: clearpathaid.org/receipts/2025-T-KTN-8842

No goods or services were provided in exchange for this contribution.

Clearpath Aid

explanation.short: Legit donation receipt — references a donation you actually made, includes EIN, uses charity's real domain, and includes the IRS-required 'no goods or services' language.
tells: Donation amount and date match what you actually gave
Includes the EIN, which you can verify at IRS.gov's tax-exempt lookup
Includes the IRS-mandated 'no goods or services' language — small detail legit nonprofits always include
Link uses the charity's real root domain
explanation.safe_move: Save the receipt for taxes. If uncertain, verify the EIN on IRS.gov independently.
explanation.consequence: No risk. Real tax receipts help you at filing time.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legit donation receipts include the EIN and the 'no goods or services' line. A charity-fraud scam wouldn't know to include those.

green_flags.label: Amount and date match your records

message.body: Hi — we reviewed your appeal (ref #A-771822) on the suspension of @tomas_k. The account has been reinstated. It may take up to 15 minutes to reflect. No action is needed from you. Policy details: swarmfeed.com/help/appeals. DMs are not a support channel — use the Help Center for anything else.

red_flags.label: DM claiming to be from support

explanation.short: Legit support DM — references your actual appeal number, sends you to the official help center, explicitly says DMs aren't support.
tells: Reference number (A-771822) matches something you actually submitted
'DMs are not a support channel' — a real brand gate against impersonation
Uses real root domain for policy link
No request for password, 2FA code, or payment to 'expedite' the reinstatement
explanation.safe_move: Check your account directly to confirm the reinstatement. If it's back, the DM was real.
explanation.consequence: No risk. Support-impersonation scams typically ask you to 'verify identity' by sending a 2FA code or resetting a password. This does neither.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real support DMs (when they happen) reference your ticket and point you to the help center. Fake ones ask for 2FA codes or fees to 'restore' the account.

green_flags.label: Policy link on real root domain

message.subject: Your email appeared in a new data breach
message.body:
Hi,

We monitor public breach data for subscribers. Your address tomas.k@protomail.com appeared in a new breach disclosed April 2: 'NovaForum 2025'.

What to do:
• Change your NovaForum password if you reuse it anywhere.
• Review activity at sentrywatch.io/dashboard (log in, no link needed here).

This is informational only — we never ask for your passwords, and we don't send password-reset links. Reply if you want to unsubscribe.

SentryWatch

explanation.short: Legit breach notification — from a service you subscribed to, explicitly says it never sends password-reset links (the scam shape it could be mistaken for).
tells: You actually subscribed to SentryWatch (pretend-scenario for this drill)
Tells you to log in at your own dashboard rather than clicking a password reset link
Explicitly calls out that it never sends password-reset emails — a direct inoculation against phishing that imitates it
Mentions a specific, nameable breach ('NovaForum 2025') you can verify independently
explanation.safe_move: Log into SentryWatch via your bookmark, not the email. If the breach matches, change any reused passwords.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring it leaves you exposed if you reuse passwords. Falling for a phishing version of this email could give up your password to the attacker.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legit breach notices tell you to log in to your dashboard via your bookmark. Fake ones include a 'reset your password here' link — which is exactly how the scam works.

green_flags.label: From a service you actually use
Explicitly does not send reset links

message.body: NovaWireless: Your plan upgrade to Unlimited+ is active as of today. New price $65/mo starts next billing cycle. Manage in the Nova app. Reply HELP for help, STOP to opt out.

explanation.short: Legit plan-change confirmation — short code, confirms something you actually did, points to the app rather than a link.
tells: Short-code sender
Confirms a specific plan change and new price — details scammers usually can't guess correctly
No link at all — points to the app
HELP/STOP keywords present, which is a legal requirement for business SMS
explanation.safe_move: Open the Nova app to confirm. If it matches, ignore. If you didn't change your plan, contact support via the app.
explanation.consequence: No risk. Ignoring a real confirmation is harmless. Over-reacting by calling a phone number you Googled could expose you to a carrier-impersonation scam.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real carrier confirmations are app-first with no link. Fake ones contain a link to 'manage' your plan.

message.subject: Payment issue — your plan will pause April 28 if not updated
message.body:
Hi Chen,

We couldn't process your April payment for Lumea Premium ($12.99) on the Visa ending 0817.

Update your payment method at lumea.com/account/billing (log in — we'll never ask for your card number by email).

Your plan continues through April 28. After that, it pauses — no cancellation, just paused — until you update the card.

Lumea Billing

explanation.short: Legit subscription-billing email — references card last-4, uses the real root domain, explicitly says it won't ask for card info by email.
tells: Card last-4 matches the card you actually have on file
Explicitly states 'we'll never ask for your card number by email' — inoculates against phishing that imitates it
Deadline is 2+ weeks out, not 'act now'
'Paused, not cancelled' — realistic subscription language
explanation.safe_move: Log in via your bookmark and update the card. Never click 'update card' links in emails, even legit ones — the habit protects you from scam versions.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring it pauses the service, which is reversible. Falling for a scam version hands your new card number to an attacker.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legit billing emails say 'log in to update' and warn against card-info-by-email. Scam versions embed a form asking for full card numbers.

green_flags.label: Warns against card info by email

message.body: Riverton Library: Overdue reminder — 'The Overstory' was due Apr 2. Please return or renew. Fines cap at $5. Manage at rivertonlib.org/myaccount. STOP to opt out.

explanation.short: Legit library overdue reminder — short code, real .org domain, specific book title, fine is a realistic cap (not a $500 'collections' scam).
tells: Short-code sender
Specific book title matches something you actually checked out
Fine cap is $5 — realistic, not the $200+ 'collections fee' scam version
.org domain matches the real library
explanation.safe_move: Return or renew via the library site (bookmark it, don't click). The fine is tiny and resolvable in person.
explanation.consequence: Ignoring a real overdue notice costs you at most a small fine. Falling for the scam version ('pay $275 collections fee') costs you a credit card number.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real library fines max out at a few dollars. Any 'library' message demanding hundreds is a scam.

green_flags.label: References a specific item checked out
